Del Mundo, Rowaldo R.
column10: The application of solar photovoltaic (PV) technology is becoming popular due to increasing awareness and concern in the environment. The PV experience in the Philippines since the early '80s have indicated that many failures in PV systems can be attributed to the lack of standards in PV products and in systems design, installation, operation and maintenance practices. column9:
The Fundamentals of Environmental Engineering Education
Marino M. Mena
column10: The engineer plays an important role in development and change. However, the traditional concept of development has been a compromise between what is technically feasible and what is considered economically attractive. The increasing environmental problems associated with engineering development projects has made the engineering profession modify this traditional concept. This means that, today, development has to be a compromise between what is technically feasible, what is considered economically attractive, and what is acceptable on environmental grounds. If engineers can be  educated and trained with regard to the important environmental issues of the day, they would then be better prepared to understand the environmental consequences of their actions. This is the essence of the environmental education and training of engineers.

column10: Solid waste management is an environmental problem. As such the problem has many aspects : social, technical, economic, financial, political. Globalization : its impact on environment 
Marino M. Mena
column10: This paper only focuses on the impact of globalization on the environment. The term "globalization" is interpreted as a "free market concept that calls for a borderless world" - the definition adopted by the organizers of the 2002 EWC/EWCA International Conference in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia. The term "environment" as used here only refers to the natural environment and not in its broadest sense which could include, for example, the internal and external forces which have an effect on a company's operation and success. The paper specifically respond to the organizers' question : Can the Asia-Pacific nations prepare themselves to minimize the negative effects and maximize the positive advantages?" My answer to this question is "yes, provided the measures as recommended herein are considered as integral part of globalization". The challenge of globalization with respect to the environment is how to have adequate and implementable environmental management program while pursuing the economic thrust of globalization which is to avail of the advances in science and technology so as to expand the businesses, to increase productivity with products of global quality, and thus, to provide better and more opportunities for the people in a global context. Globalization will challenge our people to gear up for competition. However, environmental management has to be an integral part of the overall effort. The resources of the nature and the quality of the environment hae to be sustained because they belong to the past, the present, and the future.

column10: Sustainable development is the challenge of meeting human needs for natural resources, industrial products, energy, food, transportation, shelter and effective waste management while conserving and protecting environmental quality and the natural resource base essential for future development. Meeting such a challenge requires the concerted effort among all sectors of society in order for us to achieve broader social needs. The education sector has an important role to play in this effort. Engineering Education for the Environment : A Continuing Challenge in the 21st century 
by Marino M. Mena
column10: Responding to the increasing demands of modern society for a better and sustainable environment will continue to be a major global challenge in the 21st century. In the field of engineering society calls for engineering who are environmentally educated and who will be able to balance technical with socio-economic-ecological efficiency in their planning, design, construction, operation, maintenance and monitoring of engineering projects. These demands have broadened the role in society of the engineering profession and as a consequence, have increased the responsibilities of engineering education. Both sectors, in cooperation with the other sectors of society, have to work together for a better and sustainable environment. How these two sectors can effectively respond to the environmental challenge is the main focus of this paper.
column11: Engineering education

column9:
Notes on the Philosophy and Concept of Ecology and Sustainable Development 
by Marino M. Mena
column10: Sustainable development, although basically an economic-growth concept, is ecology-related. Sustainable development is the challenge of meeting human needs for natural resources, industrial products, energy, food, transportation, shelter, and effective waste management while conserving and protecting environmental quality and the natural-resource base essential for future development. Meeting such a challenge requires the concerted effort among all sectors of human society : the government and the private sectors, the entrepreneurs and the industrialists, the producers and the consumers, the professionals and the scientists, the teachers and the students, the NGOs and the people. The concept of sustainable development aims to achieve economic growth without destroying the natural-resource base essential for meeting the needs of the future generations. Man cannot achieve this goal if he does not recognize the reality that he is part of a life-support system composed of the atmosphere, water, minerals, soil, plants, animals and micro-organisms that function together to keep the whole system viable. It is therefore obvious that sustainable development and ecology are inter-related. The following discussion focuses on this philosophy.

column9:
Trends and perspectives in Philippine Engineering Education towards the next millennium 
by Marino M. Mena
column10: As the world gears towards the new millennium Y2K it is essential for us to make an assessment of our past achievements and to identify the changes that we have to make in order to respond to the demands of the future. One of the areas which has to be dynamic to keep pace with the rapid advances in scientific knowledge is education in general and engineering education in particular. This paper focuses on trends and perspectives in engineering education in the Philippines in general and at the College of Engineering, University of the Philippines in particular. The new millennium will have challenges at various geographical levels which will have implications on engineering education. It is important that the changes are made at the proper time and in the right direction.
column11: Engineering education--Philippines

column9:
Global trends and issues in civil engineering education
by Marino M. Mena
column10: Societies throughout the world are aware of the achievements in civil engineering. It is recognized world-wide that the civil engineer is one major instrument in development and change and plays a fundamental role in shaping the world. But development is now more and more a compromise between that which is technically possible, that which is considered economically attractive, and that which is acceptable on environmental grounds. The concept of sustainable development has emerged in the last decades of the 20th century and is gaining global acceptance. Information and communication technology (ICT) is now a global tool for development. We are moving rapidly towards on e-environment. The questions are : What are the implications of these global trends and issues on civil engineering education? Is the present professional education and training for the civil engineer adequate to meet these new challenges? How could the civil engineering education be able to respond to these challenges? This paper aims to answer these questions and hopefully will be useful to civil engineering education planners, administrators and educators.

column9:
Setting objectives and strategies for technical education 
by Marino M. Mena
column10:
A former deputy minister of education in the 1980s said : "The Philippine educational system is irrelevant and elitist, is not attuned to the needs of the times, and is oriented more to the welfare of the rich". (Bulletin Today, February 16, 1980)
In the case of engineering education programs in the Philippines, we also often hear people from industry saying that "real" engineering is not that which is practiced in the classroom with fully specified problems and specific answers obtainable by analytical methods. There is considerable confusion and misunderstanding both in industry and among educators concerning the roles to be played by engineering and/or technician programs and by their graduates. This situation results in the employment of engineers who are performing as technicians of technologists. This mismatch leads to the perception that engineering graduates lack the knowledge and skills to meet the needs of industry. It is the purpose of this paper to address this problem. It discusses the processes involved in the setting up of objectives and the strategies for the attainment of these objectives.

column9:
Thirty-six years of engineering research in energy (1957-1993) 
by Ibarra E. Cruz, Ph.D.

Presented at the NRCP pre-Diamond Jubilee Conference. November 24, 1993 (as a Professorial Chair Lecture)
column10: Thirty-six years of engineering research in energy are reviewed, covering a period that started in 1957, when the engineering graduate program at the University of the Philippines was in its earliest stages, up to the present time. Titles and brief descriptions of research projects which are known to the author are presented. Discussed are problems of inadequate funding and inexperienced researchers tackling ambitious studies in the early years. Recounted are the euphoria of the golden years when the energy crisis brought a deluge of research money in the mid-70ss, and the frustrations of the lean years of declining research funds due to a softening of petroleum prices in the mid-80s. A story is told of the struggle of graduate students and faculty members of the U.P. College of Engineering to conduct laboratory experiments under less than ideal conditions and of the failures and the successes leading to national energy programs that aimed at the widespread use of producer gas, alcohol and coconut oil as substitute fuels for gasoline and diesel. The story is concluded with a forecast of researches that should be continued in 1994 to the year 2000, with high hopes that the accomplishments of the past thirty-six years will be continued and expanded.

column9:
Disaster risk management background of dmaps for infrastructure 
by Benito M. Pacheco, PhD, PE 

Philippine Engineering Journal
PEJ 2007; Vol. 28, No. 2 : 1-28
column10: The first part of this paper is a situationer for the Philippines in the most recent (2009) UN global assessment report that ranked it among the first 14 of approximately 200 countries and economies whose population is most at risk from earthquakes, cyclones, landslides, and floods. Second is an introduction of DMAPS for Infrastructure, a new (2009) public-private partnership agreement for disaster mitigation, adaptation, and preparedness strategies for infrastructure, involving scientists and engineers in the Philippines; this may be viewed as having evolved from the Philippine Institute of Civil Engineers DMAPS that was first conceptualized five years earlier (2004), and DQRP that was first institutionalized four years even before (2000), both volunteerism programs by Filipino civil engineers. Third and main part is a framework of concepts and terminologies that the author proposes for engineers and scientists to harmonize among themselves and with professionals in finance, medicine, and other disciplines. The author proposes that fundamental issues common to various professional disciplines concerning the management of risk be associated with four general steps of risk management, easily remembered as R-I-S-K : Recognize, Impute an estimate, Survey over time, and Keep inside tolerance range. The particular type of risk needs to be clearly recognized (in step 1 of risk management), before deciding on the ordinal or other level of measure to impute an estimate (in step 2 of risk management). The older paradigm of managing disaster meets the newer paradigm of managing risk, at the step of surveying the risk over time (in step 3 of risk management), by virtue of the recognition that risk factors do evolve. Risk reduction is cited as the preferred mode of keeping disaster risk inside the tolerance range, yet many risk control modes indeed exist (in step 4 of risk management) from many viewpoints. Highlights of the four steps of the risk management spiral are summarized; ideally, each complete cycle of risk management R-I-S-K brings down the level of uncertainty and reduces the risk. It is proposed that the DMAPS for Infrastructure public-private partnership program be viewed in this framework. In appendices, this paper includes three essays that offer an alternative way of appreciating risk management concepts and terminologies.

Computer simulation of fuel consumption in driving cycles
by Gino T. Bautista, B.S.M.E
May 1988
column10: A computer model for simulating fuel consumption of a road vehicle over any prescribed driving cycle was developed using steady-state performance data from chassis dynamometer tests. The method is simple and does not require sophisticated chassis dynamometer test facilities. The method also eliminates variances due to driving style. It was determined that the predictions of the computer model agree closely with fuel consumption figures from actual road tests. The use of steady-state performance data from chassis dynamometer tests was found to be suitable in predicting fuel consumption of driving cycles. This study and its conclusions are limited to diesel engine driven road vehicles.

Progress report no. 10 of the ESA-UNET Subproject B : research on industrial wastewater treatment.
March 1, 1997 - May 31, 1997
by Mark Noort
Project Coordinator, CICAT (Centre for International Cooperation and Appropriate Technology)
Delft University of Technology
column10: Work progressed on the finalization of research reports and publications. Regular communication was maintained on these subjects. The different participating universities started preparing plans for future activities after this project phase along the lines sketched in the mid-term workshop report. A proposal was made to hold a final workshop at ITB Bandung in November and submitted to the European Union. Preparations of the workshop are in progress. During the workshop in Bandung the final results of the projects will be presented and plans will be made for future activities. The core future activity will be joint research with industry from each participating ASEAN country. The output from project A on technology transfer will be used as input for the formulation of plans for further network development and interaction between universities and industry for the specific topic of industrial wastewater treatment. The output of the workshop will be a clear strategy on how to tackle this issue and what should be the role of the participating universities, companies and RIET in future activities, like joint-ventures, technology transfer and dissemination of research results. The final workshop will also provide an excellent opportunity for interaction with Indonesian industry and for a discussion on the experiences in the joint formulation of research plans with industry from each ASEAN country. Mr. Chu of the University of Malaya and Mr. Noort of the TU Delft participated in the general ESA-UNET network meeting in Aachen in June.

column3: Central Azucarera Don Pedro (CADP) Sugar Factory Project
column10:
The Central Azucarera Don Pedro (CADP) sugar refinery is designed to produce 550 tons of refined sugar and 30 tons of final molasses daily. The adjoining CADP raw sugar factory shall supply 50% of the total annual raw sugar requirements while 50% shall be taken from outside sources.

The proposed project site shall be located inside the existing 40 hectare CADP raw sugar factory in Barangay Lumbangan, Nasugbu, Batangas. The plant shall occupy a total land area of 7,200 m2. The total capital investment of the project is P 500 million. The workforce needed by the project during construction shall vary at different stages of the project ranging from 20 to 642 men. During operations, workforce requirement is approximately 120 men. Hiring of locally available qualified men during the implementation of the project shall be prioritized.

The processes involved in sugar refining are affination, carbonation, filtration, decolorization, evaporation and crystallization. The total water requirement of the sugar refining process shall be taken from Lian River at a rate of 0.9 m3/sec. A spray pond shall be provided to recycle the cooling water especially during dry months where Lian River flow is low.

The construction phase of the project will last for one year. The construction site preparation is minimal. It only involves the demolition of the existing warehouse in the site. Build-up of localized suspended particulate matter concentration will only occur within the vicinity of the project site. Noise levels may increase but will be confined within the CADP compound. 

During operation, the wastewater effluents generated can be classified into spent acids, spent brine and gas scrubber wastewaters. The existing wastewater treatment scheme of CADP shall be used but with some improvements. An installation of an additional ash settling pond to accomodate the gas scrubber effluent and maximum utilization of wastewater or irrigation by acquisition of mobile irrigation pumps and sprinklers are recommended. 

The workers health and safety shall be protected by the existing CADP House Safety Rules and Regulations. Construction safety and goodhousekeeping manuals were developed. Workers health are monitored by conducting annual physical examination. Training on fire prevention and control are regularly conducted. Personnel protective devices are provided to all employees of CADP involved in plant construction and operation. 

An environmental monitoring program is recommended to assure that wastewater facilities are working on their expected efficiency, provide a record for future plans of improving the treatment scheme and to seek compliance with the DENR standards.

column10:
The proposed Dry Process Plant has a rated capacity of 3500 TPD or 1 Million Ton Clinker/year will be an expansion of the existing two units (wet & dry) process plant of Davao Union Cement Corporation (DUCC), located at Bo. Ilang, Davao City. The proposed plant is a modernization of the existing plant and will be constructed adjacent to the existing kiln facilities. The proposed dry process plant has an air suspension in preheater with precalciner and kiln feed system that minimize false air, which has a lower fuel consumption compared to the existing wet & dry process plant. Coal firing facilities that utilize more effectively the coal rejects that the existing wet process plant currently produces. 

The new plant will share compound with the existing DUCC plant so that there will be no additional clearing of the area covered. 

Cement manufacture is the process where in limestone, shale and silica components are ground together in proportion of approximately 87% limestone, 9% shale and 4% Silica. After grinding, the mixture called the raw meal is homogenized and fed to a Rotary Kiln where it is burned at approximately 1450 deg. centrigrade. The output product of the kiln is called cement cliner. It is cooled, crushed and then ground with 5% gypsum proportion by a ball mill to produce portland cement. The clinker could also be ground with pozzolanic material & slag to produce other types of cement like pozzolan or primero. The finished product is pneumatically conveyed to the packhouse for packing and delivery. 

The probable environmental impact here will be on the nearby communities including the residents of Davao City and other neighboring towns which will be benefited with job opportunities and other money generating pursuits in life, as well as other socio-economic benefits. 

Another environmental impact here is on the dust emission during the operation of the plant. Such air pollution is being taken cared-of and controlled by implementing measures, such as installation of electrostatic precipitators, dust collecting and multi-cyclones before every emission points. Process water will continue to be taken from Ilang River beside the plant with appropriate water treatment of water before it is utilize as coolant for the grinding mills, kiln and other equipments. The used cooling water will be collected in a setling pond and shall be recycled so that no water is discharged outside the plant and make-up water from the river will be at a low level and volume. Other major equipments will be enclosed in a building to abate noise pollution.

column9: (1970, March 23) U.P. Master Development Plan
column10:
The Master Development Plan for the Diliman Campus of the University of the Philippines at Quezon City was prepared by the Office of Campus Planning in 1965-66. Its principal aims was and is to serve as the principal base for a continuous, comprehensive, and ultimate development program. This report is a revised and updated edition of that Plan, as taken from the students' point of view. 
At the start of the second semester, the CE 80 class was organized into a mock consulting firm in order to evaluate and update the 1966 UP Master Plan. After a series of lectures and orientation conducted by Prof. Felisberto Reyes, Dr. Goodman, Dean Aurelio Juguilon, Prof. Perlito C. Reyes and Prof. Delfin Zapanta, the class was subdivided into teams and sub-teams.
Upon the organization of the class, a survey of what has been accomplished by way of implementation of the UP Master Plan. A report on this regard has already been submitted and is included here in the earlier part for comprehensiveness. Intensive studies and researches on the original charts, schemes, and other documents of the Office of Campus Planning were undertaken to evaluate all conditions and factors bearing on the preparation of a comprehensive campus development plan. Emphasis of the investigations were on the changes, if any, of the conditions and factors affecting the plans. No major revisions are recommended onthe Land Use Plan. 
As students intested in the fundamentals of planning, the team also covered the general context of campus current problems, development, objectives, planning guidelines and the rationale behind the proposals- most of which were carry-overs from the original edition. 
The proposals presented here took cognizance of the setbacks and problems that besetted the original plan. Consideration of relevant data and factors were, in no small measure, in-debted to the following : Director Tomacruz of the President's Advisory Committee on Campus Planning and Development, Engineer Jose Giron, Engineering Department of the Office of the Physical Plant, Prof. Perlito C. Reyes, Architect Novenario, and numerous other individuals without whom this report would not have been possible.

column3: 1984 Revised Code of the University of the Philippines (2nd Draft)
column10:
Consonant to the fundamental principle of democratic law-making that the sentiments of those who are to be governed by the rules must be taken into consideration, the various sectors of the university community were represented in the Commitee created to revise the University Code. The different constituencies were then provided copies of the completed first draft. Public hearings were subsequently conducted in the four autonomous campuses for wider and more systematic discussion of the proposed rules. 
Within the constraints of law and reason, this second draft of the proposed Revised University Code reflects the results of the discussions. While all interests and opinions could not be accomodated, revisions and modifications were incorporated to safeguard high academic standards, to render the university processes more open to democractic participation, to enhance individual and institutional academic freedom, to protect the rights of individual members of the academic community against the tyranny of numbers, to simplify and clarify procedures whether they pertain to personnel action or disciplinary proceedings, and to expand benefits where reasonably allowed by limited resources.
In the face of sometimes irresponsible conflicting claims, the Code Committee continued to be guided by the principles that animated the preparation of the first draft in the formulation of the provisions of the second draft.

column10: The publication of a revised code of the University of the Philippines has been a pressing need for the orderly administration and operation of the University since the reestablishment and transfer of its principal campus from Manila to Quezon City. The old Code was designed and intended for the University of pre-war days. It was sufficiently adequate under the conditions then existing. But after the War it became obviously out of place and outdated in several respects under different surroundings and before novel problems obtaining on the new campus. The marked increase in student enrollment, the changed physical environment, and the new academic atmosphere had called for a revision of many parts of the old Code. The Board of Regents, therefore, upon the recommendation of the University President, decided to update the University regulations so as to fit them to present needs and conditions and to make them more responsive and better suited to the problems of the University. The work of revision lasted over a period of one year. After this work was finished, it was submitted to the Board of Regents for consideration. About six months had elapsed before this body finally approved it on January 9, 1961. The revised Code took effect on February 15, 1961 when the Board confirmed its previous action.

Updated copy as of April 19, 2001
column10:
Preface
This publication was prepared pursuant to Administrative Order No. 260, dated October 22, 1974, designating the U.P. Law Center "to collate, update and publish the Revised Code of the University of the Philippines System preparatory to the comprehensive revision of said code."
The execution of this task was by no means easy. Firstly, there was the difficulty of searching for the relevant laws, resolutions, and executive and administrative orders. This problem was particularly met with respect to the period from 1961-1969, when the University System still had no Official Gazette. For this period, the Center heavily relied on materials provided by the Office of the President and the Office of General Services. For the years following this period, the Center had to depend mostly on the Official Gazette in additon to materials made available by the above-mentioned offices. 
Difficulty of access to the needed materials was not the only problem. As difficult, if not more difficult, was the selection of the materials, which were numerous, and how to incorporate them in the provisions of the Code. This problem was especially encoutered in connection with laws, resolutions and executive and administrative orders which, while not expressly amending, modifying, amplifying, superseding, or repealing certain provisions of the Code, have the implied effect of doing so. Some of these simply cannot be incorporated in the Code without entailing substantial revision of entire chapters, even titles, of it. At the same time, they are so imporant that they cannot be left unaccounted for. 
In view of this problem, the Center incorporated in the provisions of the Code only those laws and Board resolutions which expressly amend the charter and the Code and such of those which do so impliedly as can be incorporated without entailing the problem adverted to above. As to other laws, resolutions and executive and administrative orders, the Center has no recourse except to resort to the use of footnotes and appendices. Many of the footnotes make reference to the appendices while others explain or amplify certain provisions of the Code in the light of developments since 1961. 
This work does not claim to be free from error. It is entirely possible that there might be some emission of important rules or resolutions which can qualify for incorporation in the Code or as appendices. But, on the basis of the available materials, the Center certainly did its best to make this edition of the COde as exhaustive, accurate and useful as possible, not only as a guide for the University officialdom and populace but as a material for the projected revision of the Code. 
Acknowledgment must be made of the special contribution of the Division of Research and Law Reform of the U...Law Center to which this job was entrusted, particularly Prof. Esteban B. Bautista, Assistant Head of the Division, who prepared the manuscript; Prof. Sulpicio Guevara, Division Head, who went over the manuscript and made valuable suggestions; the Division's clerical force - Mr. Primo S. Arinas, Mrs. Zenaida P. Cruz, Miss Elizabeth O. Rivera, and Mrs. Milagros A. Gallardo - who did the stenciling and helped in the proof-reading; and the Administrative Division which tool care of the mimeographing and binding.

column9: A proposal to establish a computating facility for computer-aided engineering, design, and manufacturing in the College of Engineering, University of the Philippines
column10:
The establishment of a Training and Research Facility for VLSI Technology and Systems is an urgent and pressing need if the country's goal of infusing microelectronic technology before the year 2000 is to be achieved. 
The funding requested is US$529,500 over a period of three years excluding counterpart funds to be requested from the Philippine government. This may seem a large amount at first glance, however, this what is needed. Corporations spend many times more just to maintain their momentum. With this comparatively small amount, the proponent aims to accelerate the industry's momentum.
Specifically, local industry will benefit from this project in the following ways : 1) a ready pool of young talent; 2) availability of training for their in-house pool of engineers; 3) a low risk means by which to sample VLSI technology; and 4) a local pool of experts whom they may tap as they climb the learning curve of VLSI. All of these translate to a highly conducive environment for the local electronics industry to grow. 
The project is also beneficial to the donor country aside from the goodwill generated by its interaction with Philippine project members. Significant will be the exposure received by the donor country's related industries. This is the crucial time since few cooperative ties have been established in the area of microelectronics and more so in the field of VLSI design. It would be to the advantage of the donor country if the software and foundry services were sourced there. 
In summary, it is the sincere conviction of the proponent and his colleagues that given such a facility with the direction set for it in this proposal, the Philippine's electronics industry will be on the right path towards a better future.

Presidential Task Force Report on Science and Technology Development
column10: The "strategic importance of science and technology in socio-economic development", basically in the "attainment of quality and productivity in the country's industry and agriculture" is fittingly emphasized in a recent report to the President of the Philippines. It specifically states that "acquiring science and technology manpower and financial support is the most important task for science and technology development". The College of Engineering in the University of the Philippines can contribute directly to the attainment of making the Philippines a newly industrialized economy at the turn of the century by training the best students in engineering specifically at the graduate level, in fields relevant to the priority production sectors identified by the Presidential Task Force, such as, energy, electronics, metals and engineering, information technology and processing industries, among others.

column9: (1998, August 31) Memorandum No. 51 : Prior notice about new academic programs before actual processing through academic bodies
column10: Please inform us, through the Chancellor, about any new academic program you may wish to propose for final approval by the Board of Regents. That is : inform us about the concept before it is actually submitted to the appropriate academic bodies, from the Departmental level to the University Council. By concept is meant a brief working proposal : what the new program is, why it should be established, and possible budgetary requirements. By new academic program is meant a new curricular program or course of study (not individual subjects nor revisions of already existing programs); or a new certificate, post-baccalaureate, or non-degree course of study.

We, the students of the U.P. College of Engineering, in order to establish a relevant Student Council that shall promote student welfare and advance student rights, develop, direct, and channel our Engineering skills, knowledge, and talents towards nation-building, and commit ourselves to the interests of the Filipino people, do hereby ordain and promulgate this Constitution.

Subject : Committee to draft operational guidelines for laboratory facilities in the College of Engineering
column10: The College Executive Committee at its meeting held on December 3, 1980 approved to form a committee with you as a member. The task of the committee is to draft a set of operational guidelines that could be uniformly adopted by the various departments with laboratory facilities in the College of Engineering. The purpose of such guidelines is how twe can effectively utilize the College laboratory facilities in support of our functions, namely : teaching, research and extension services and at the same time make such facilities available to the National Engineering Center through the various centers under its umbrella. It is noted that the College of Engineering and the National Engineering Center have a mutual commitment to help each other. It is felt that it is necessary to adopt sufficient guidelines that will allow an effective utilization of the College laboratory facilities to the best interest of both entities.

column9: (1979, October 10) Invitation to send whatever information to be published in the Graduate School newsletter
column10:
The Graduate School of the University of the Philippines is launching a monthly newsletter which we hope to release within the first week of each month starting this October. 
This project is expected to close the communication gap between the Graduate School and the thirty-two (32) units of the University System offering graduate programs, as well as to provide a forum for graduate faculty and graduate student dialogue. 
The Graduate School Newsletter will be produced by the Graduate School staff, initially with Mr. Francisco E. Dumlao, as Editor; Dr. Emerenciana Y. Arcellana, as Adviser; and Dr. Joventino D. Soriano and Dr. Leopoldo Y. Yabes, as Consultants. 
In this regard, we wish to invite your College/Unit to send to us whatever information you would want to be published. This invitation is also extended to your graduate faculty and graduate student association(s). Manuscripts preferably inEnglish should be submitted as soon as possible, and if possible not later than the last week of every month for publication in the succeeding month.

column9: (1979, October 3) 2nd Indorsement
column10:
Respectfully returned to the Vice President for Academic Affairs with the following information.
During the first semester of schoolyear 1979-80 Dr. Edgardo S. Pacheco of our college opened an experimental class in ES 11 (Statics of Rigid Bodies) using the Personalized System of Instruction (PSI). This system of instruction is described in the announcement which is herewith attached. 
The services of tutors are essential in the Personalized System of Instruction. Graduate fellows who are assigned to assist in teaching will serve as tutors in PSI classes. 
Dr. Pacheco reported very encouraging results of the PSI experiment he is currently conducting. He will again teach ES 11 next semester using PSI. He also plans to write the study guides and other materials for ES 13 (Mechanics of Deformable Bodies I) so it can be offered using PSI during the first semester of school year 1980-1981.
column11: Mena, Marino M.

column9: (1979, September 24) Increase of graduate fellows allowance from P400 to P700 per month
column10:
I have the honor to recommend that the living allowance of graduate fellows in the College of Engineering be increased from P400.00 to P700.00 per month effective November, 1979. To be entitled to this increase, the graduate fellow must render fifty hours of service per month, assisting in the research and teaching activities of the College of Engineering. This service shall be rendered during the period of the fellowship. 
Presently, our graduate fellows are not required to render any service to the college. However, the living allowance of P400.00 per month is so small that, for a number of years now. there are fewer applicants than there are available slots. By increasing this allowance to P700.00 per month and at the same time requiring the rendering of service, we feel that the fellowship will become more attractive to graduate students and more beneficial to our college.
column11: Mena, Marino M.

column9: (1980, January 9) Encyclopedias recently published by MacMillan Publishing Company
column10:
We would like to bring to your attention two encyclopedias recently published by Macmillan Publishing Company, New York, which should be in every engineering library : 
1. Encyclopedia of Statistics. Edited by Wm. H. Kruskal and Judith M. Tanur. 2 volumes, 1978. This is the most extensive reference work on statistics in print, covering the development of modern statistical methods and describing the operations, interpretations, and applications of the various statistical techniques. 
2. The Great Soviet Encyclopedia. 31 volumes, plus indexes. Vol. 1 to 22 already published; other volumes to be published within the next two years. The Russian edition, now being published in serial form, is the prime source of information in more than 600,000 libraries in the U.S.S.R and the Macmillan translation is the prime source in English on the Soviet Union. Of the more than 100,000 articles, 10,000 are on technology; 1,900 on mathematics; 3,500 on Chemistry; and 4,200 on Fine Arts and Architecture.
column11:
Makabenta, Eduardo
Mena, Marino M.

column9: (1980, February 8) Engineering Week celebration
column10:

Highlighting the festivity is the Engineering March on Monday, February 11, at 9:00 AM to be participated in by the engineering community together with students from other colleges. If news hasn't reached you, these colleges worked for their own college week and made them coincide with ours so that February 11-15 virtually becomes a University Week. 
Enclosed is the program of activities for our week-long celebration, carefully planned to suit the interest and the needs of the engineering populace : the students, faculty, administration and non-academic personnel. With this year's theme -- PAGKAMULAT, PAGKAKAISA at PAGKILOS ng INHINYERA PARA SA SAMBAYANAN, we hope to come up with a successful and more meaningful affair.

Specifically, we urgently need your support and help in organizing some of the technical sessions for the Conference as discussed in Item 2 of the memorandum. column10:
The Office of Student Affairs of the University of the Philippines has started to pave a way to channel the boundless energies of fraternity members into positive and creative endeavors and away from destructive misadventures which end up with fratmen, or even innocent by-standers, maimed or killed. 
Last year, we initiated, with the enthusiastic cooperation of the fraternity leaders themselves, an inter-fraternity conference whereby the blueprint of a forum for all fraternity leaders to work out mutual problems and plan projects of common interest was successfully set up. 
This year's crop of fraternity leaders wish to continue the worthwhile effort and seek to actualize the establishment of a really effective inter-fraternity council. 
We are aware of your own deep concern for the physical safeties of our fraternity men and your desire to see bright, but otherwise restless, young men pursue meritorious activities that will re-inforce their formal education in the University. It is, for this reason, that we are inviting you to join hands with us in making a SECOND INTER-FRATERNITY CONFERENCE a reality. 
The Conference was scheduled by the fraternity leaders to be held on September 12-14, 1980. We expect to have 90 fraternity members attending it, for which we estimate to spend P20,000.00.

column9: (1981, September 30)
column10:
In reply to your letter of September 8 I am enclosing a calander of the College of Graduate Studies and Research as well as a calendar of our Biomedical Engineering group. I am also enclosing applications forms for admission to the Graduate School. The possibility of obtaining financial support for graduate students depends strongly upon the field the applicant chooses. There are areas where relatively more money is available and of course fellowships can be easier granted. Other areas are less fortunate. The best approach would be for the individual to submit an application form outlining the field in which he/she would like to work. The form is then circulated among the members of the department who express their view with regard to vacancies, availability of funds, etc. I would like to repeat that the reply depends very much upon the academic standing of the applicant and the recommendations he received from your school or other institutions in his home country. 
I would like to emphasise that the information I gave you refers basically only to Engineering. This does not mean that positions outside the College of Engineering are not available, but that there are fewer and more difficult to obtain. I received since my return two applications, one from a biochemist and one from a microbiologist. I passed these on to the respective departments but there were no vacancies for the coming year. The applications were incomplete and I am advising the applicants to submit an application in a formal way on the necessary forms in the hope that some positions might become available later on in other departments.

column9: (1981, November 20) Support a Research Professor in Engineering
column10:
Attached herewith is a proposal for UPERDFI to support a "Research Professor in Engineering."
It is a fact that research in the College is not really as great as desirable. Despite availability of funds for research, our faculty shy away from them because the activity takes them away from more financially rewarding activities. The faculty cannot really be faulted for this attitude because man is an economic being and must support his family's ambitions. 
In an effort to rectify the situation, this proposal would require the recipient to render only 3 hours a week for teaching but would undertake no other activity except research. A remuneration of P6,000 a month is proposed so that there will be no need for him to seek side lines to satisfy his financial needs. 
The Research Professorship would probably require a Board of Regents action. There will probably be many other problems. Nevertheless, the idea is being presented for whatever it is worth.

column9: (1982, February 25) Proposed Curriculum Development (Phase II)
column10:
The technical committee on the Ladder-type Curriculum in the Mining Sciences Project of the Ministry held a meeting last February 5, 1982 to draw up details and comments on the proposed curriculum development (Phase II). It was pointed out that there is an increasing need for skilled manpower in the mining industry for the next (5) years or more. 
Two options were considered for curriculum development. The first is the certificate program wherein a skilled worker will finish in a certain period of time after which he can go back to work in his company. It maybe noted that the certificate for masteral program has been abolished. The other option is the institutionalized training program to be participated by qualified candidates from the mining companies. 
Another issue concerns the demands for mining workers abroad. The fluctuation of workers who have already undergone training in the curriculum might endanger the domestic supply of skilled miners. There is a possibility, however, of increased foreign exchange earnings due to this factor. 
It was also suggested that there is a possibility that other technical/vocational schools could offer the Ladder-type Curriculum in their respective institutions unlike what has been originally proposed which limit the curriculum only to the University of the Philippines. 
In this connection, transmitted herewith for your comments and recommendations is the project proposal by UPCB and BMG. Also enclosed are copies of the Survey of Skilled Manpower Requirements of the Mining Industry in Ilocos Region and the highlights of the workshop/seminar on Ladder-type Curriculum held in Baguio City on 6-8 September, 1978 for your reference.
column11:
Mena, Marino M. 
Valerio, Jesus D.

column9: (1982, March 9) Modular or Ladder Type Curriculum
column10:
Dean, I have always been in favor of a modular or a ladder type curriculum even if the plan basically emphasizes the "dropping out" feature. 
In U.P. Baguio's proposal, a number of things have to be considered.
1. The graduates will basically be technicians of the trade school level. Under the CRAP criterion "Is the program University level in nature?" The proposal will be unacceptable. 
2. The proposal envisions the students to advance to the BS Geol or BSEM  degree programs after their 3rd year and then expect to graduate after only 1 or 2 years more, respectively. 
- To enter this quota degree programs, will the UPCAT be waived for them?
- The BS programs require 67 units of gen. educ.; 53 units of engg sciences and service courses; 12 units of Spanish; 41 units of specialization courses. Will there be enough room for all of these? Even graduates of other Engineering courses like Met E find it very difficult to finish BSEM in 2 years. 
3. The course envisions answering a very wide variety of technical skills without or with minimum in service training in the mines. Technical skill can only be acquired through practice, never through lectures or books. If the proposal requires use of company production equipment, I can see very little excuse for dispensing with in-service trainng in from of the proposal. If company equipment will not be used, enormous equipment and physical facilities will have to be provided. Envisioning UP Baguio maintaining Bulldozers, Cranes, Hoists, Alimak raise climbers locomotions, a railway and a quarry to practice on boggles the imagination.
column11: Ordillas, M. U.

column9: (1982, May 4) One Day Seminar Work shop - "Seminar-Workshop on the Report of the Management Review Committee on the Organizational Structure and Management of the University of the Philippines System"
column10:
In its 943rd meeting on July 31, 1981, the Board of Regents has authorized President Edgardo J. Angara to "conduct a comprehensive review and appraisal" of the : (a) academic programs; (b) organizational structure and management; and, (c) land use and infrastructure development. Pursuant to this authority, the President issued Administrative Order No. 58 (August 18, 1981) creating the Management Review Committee to conduct "an appraisal and audit of the organization and management functions of the University of the Philippines System, in order to achieve modern and progressive, effective and economical, and coordinated and simplified organization and management operations appropriate to a multi-campus University. 
The MRC has already submitted its report to the President and there is now a need to get the reactions and recommendations of the various sectors of the University including the Central Administration Officials, Deans, Directors, Faculty Members, Non-Academic Staff, Administrative Personnel and Students. 
In this regard, the MRC, PDS and the UPMEC are sponsoring a one-day "Seminar-Workshop on the Report of the Management Review Committee on the Organizational Structure and Management of the University of the Philippines System", to be held on 19 May 1982 (Wednesday) at the Seminar Room, Asian Institute of Tourism. The major topics to be discussed in the seminar are : (1) Concept of the University of the Philippines System; (2) Relation Units; (3) Organization and Management of Academic Units, including Research and Extension Centers / Institutes. The objectives of the seminar are : (1) To discuss the report of the MRC on the organizational structure and management of the University; (2) To identify and discuss other issues and problems related to the organizational structure and management of the University which were not resolved or covered in the report; and, (3) to formulate recommendations on the report of the MRC. 
The seminar is the first of the series of seminars/discussions on the report of the MRC which the UPMEC in collaboration with MRC and PDS is planning to undertake. The other topics to be discussed in the future are : (1) Planning and Financing; (2) Manning and Personnel Management; (3) Library Administration; (4) Auxilliary Enterprises and Services; (5) Supply Management; (6) Records Management; (7) Building, Utilities and Grounds Administration; and (8) Community Services.

column9: (1982, June 4) Resource justification and plan of operation for the Proposed Bachelor of Computer Science Program
column10:
This is a proposal to institute a four-year undergraduate program, the Bachelor of Computer Science Program, to be jointly offered by the Department of Engineering Sciences, the Department of Electrical Engineering, and the Department of Mathematics. The program aims to implement a baccalaureate curriculum in computer science with emphasis on software design and to equip graudates of the program with reasonable mastery of the fundamental ideas of computer science and of the associated skills so that these graduates can be effective in practice and can overcome on their own the rapid onset of obsolescence so common in this field. Undergraduate students whose field of interest is computer hardware may instead pursue a B.S. in Electrical Engineering degree with senior option in Computer Engineering. The current masteral program in Computer Science shall be phased out in the meantime because present resources are inadequate to support both baccalaureate and masteral programs. 
Started in school year 1975-76, the Master of Engineering in Computer Science Program is an existing joint graduate program of the Department of Engineering Sciences and the Department of Electrical Engineering of the College of Enginering. It was designed to provide training in the major fields into which the entire computer science discipline had differentiated during the late sixties and early seventies. The curriculum requires 36 units of coursework on software and hardware areas of the discipline, with particular emphasis on software. Holders of Bachelor of Science degrees in engineering, mathematics, statistics, and other natural sciences wre admitted to the program. The main objective of the program is to equip the students with the knowledge and skills that qualify or else upgrade their status as systems analysts, systems programmers, and at best, applications software, database or information system designers and administrators, complementary to their baccalaureate and work experience qualifications.
column11: Gregorio, Martin V.

column9: (1982, June 14) UNDP Questionnaire on TCDC Information Referral System (INRES)
column10:
One of UNDP's major undertakings towards the promotion of technical cooperation among developing countries has been the establishment of an Information Referral System (INRES). In 1977 and 1978, with the cooperation of the governments involved, INRES produced and distributed directories of Technical Services available in developing countries for TCDC activities. Based on our collective experience to date, UNDP has further improved and redesigned the system. 
We now plan to develop an enquiry facility at UNDP Headquarters, in New York that would permit quick and thorough search for relevant information in order to respond to enquiries received through cable or mail. To make INRES more responsive to TCDC needs, the improvements will concentrate on (a) expanding the coverage to a larger number of organizations and institutions; (b) broadening the information available within the areas of training/education, research and technological development, consultancy and expert services as well as scientific and technological information services; (c) inclusion of specific TCDC experience of organizations and institutions in any of the fields covered by INRES.
To collect the requisite information, a questionnaire has been designed and, in consultation with the Government's TCDC Focal Point, is being sent to selected institutions and agencies having the necessary capability and capacity for TCDC. 
Your institution/agency has been included among the above, and we would very much appreciate your having the enclosed questionnaire completed (following the attached detailed instructions for completing the forms) and returned to us as soon as possible but preferably no later than 30 June 1982. 
The degree to which the TCDC Information Referral System will be able to respond effectively to enquiries and needs of institutions and agencies within the developing countries will very much depend upon the information obtained through these questionnaires. We would therefore be grateful for your cooperation in this exercise.

column9: (1982, September 13) Invitation to participate in a workshop on the Ecological Information Decision System to be held on 19-20 October, 1982 at the AIT House, Diliman, Quezon City.
column10:
The National Environmental Protection Council (NEPC) is currently involved in the development of analytical and evaluation techniques that incorporate ecological considerations in planning and decision-making. One such methodology called the Ecological Information Decision System (EIDS) is being tested by NEPC as to its suitability to land-use planning and decision-making in the country. The EIDS has been called the ecosystem approach to planning of human settlements to signify its heavy emphasis on ecological relationships and functions and the constraints these impose on the planning process. 
A major component of the EIDS is the development of a comprehensive list of ecological factors to be considered in ascertaining the suitability of various development sites to different land uses such as residential, industrial-commercial and agricultural uses. For each prospective land use, there will be certain factors of greatest importance and these can be selected. Moreover, there will be a ranking of importance and so the factors can be assigned numerical values. The output of the EIDS consists of land-suitability decision maps which are numerical overlays of ecovalue maps of the various ecological factors considered. 
In connection with this, we are cordially inviting you to participate in a workshop on the EIDS to be held on 19-20 October, 1982 at the AIT House, Diliman, Quezon City. The objectives of the workshop are : (i) verification of the list of ecological factors developed; (ii) assignment of relative weights to each of the ecological parameters; (iii) determination of relevant land-use decision maps to be produced by the project.

column9: (1985, January 15) Comments on the Comprehensive Report on the System-Wide Conference on Graduate Studies in the University of the Philippines
column10:
The concluding paragraph of the report might fail to give the reader an accurate picture of what transpired during the Conference. In fact, it can be misleading. An effort must be made to state more clearly the following : 
1. During the Conference Plenary Session, a motion to approve the recommendations of Workshop Groups A. B and C was not carried. What the Conference approved was the motion to receive the reports of the Workshop Groups and transmit the same to University authorities without recommending their approval or disapproval. 
2. The discussion in Group C was characterized by controversy and disagreement particularly on the question of a graduate faculty. The first resolution was passed only after it was clarified that the statement "....the Graduate School shall have its faculty" simply meant that faculty members teaching graduate courses shall be given additional designations as faculty members of the Graduate School.

column10:

a. To meet the urgent needs of engineering schools and industry for professionals with advanced training in engineering. 
b. To develop innovative educational programmes, teaching materials and methods of instruction specifically designed for the Philippine setting which may serve as models for other engineering schools.
c. To provide a pool of technical experts to which public and private entities may turn for assistance in the solution of their engineering problems.
d. To develop technologies that utilize local resources and which are adapted to the needs of a developing country. 
e. To identify technology-oriented ecological problems and to support studies aimed at their solutions.

column9: (1985, January 28) 2nd Indorsement
column10:
Respectfully returned to Vice Chancellor Paz G. Ramos, the attached communication regarding the holding of review classes, with the following comments : 
1. Firstly, the undersigned believes that educational institutions are universally recognized as the credentialing agent within our society. Such a credentialing responsibility extends beyond the traditional bachelor's, master's and Ph.D. degrees. Thus, any credentials to be awarded as a result of the accumulation of educational experience in the classrooms of academic programs, in-plant courses, short courses or correspondence courses should be from an educational institution. 
2. If the envisioned classes can be classified as part of a continuing education program for the purpose of upgrading the knowledge of the engineering graduate and preparing him to better practice his engineering profession, this is a responsibility of the academic institution.
3. Pursuant to the premise in the No. 1 and subject to the conditions in No. 2, the undersigned has no objection to the offering of such short courses. For example, a short course in design can be offered to include the recent developments and strengthen the engineer's capability in this field. Similar courses can be developed in other areas of interest depending on the needs of the target groups. 
4. However, let us not call it "review classes". If the intent is just to review what the engineer already learned in his five-year BS program, the undersigned feels that it is not necessary.
5. In summary, continuing education is a responsibility of the academic institution and should be institutionalized.
column11: Mena, Marino M.

column9: (1985, April 19) Engineering Shops (Welding Shop Section) was entered by robber (s)
column10:
I am reporting that the Engineering Shops (Welding Shop Section) was entered by robber (s) last night April 17, 1985, possibly between 7:00 p.m. to 6:00 a.m. the following day, April 18, 1985. This is the second time this month that the Shops has been robbed. The first incident was on April 11, 1985, which the undersigned has reported to the Dean, and the second time is April 17, 1985. 
The equipment lost in this incident is : 
a. One (1) set of OX-AC HOSE, complete with pressure gauge (tank and hose gauges) and mixing chamber components. Total cost of the above set is P1,600.00
Since all the door entrances of the Engineering Shops were not tampered of forced open, and all the windows on the first floor are provided with iron grilles, the undersigned and his staff with the help of the investigating unit of the UP Police Force surmised that the possible point of entry are the windows without iron grilles on the second floor (mezzanine) of the Textile Building located on the southern end of the building. , facing Roces Avenue. This area is where the reserach program (Gasifier) of Dr. Ibarra Cruz is located. 
To minimize the illegal entrance of unauthorized people in the Shop, the undersigned in consultation with Dr. Garcia recommends that these ungrilled windows be provided with iron grills. Approximate cost for iron bars including welding rods is about P13,000.00. This amount does not include cost for labor and installation. Maybe labor could be jointly provided by the personnel of the Engineering Shop and the M.E. Power Laboratory.
column11:

column9: (1985, April 24) Ad Hoc Committee on Manufacturing Engineering submit the following consensus and recommendations concerning its tasks
column10:
The undersigned, in behalf of the other members of the Ad Hoc Committee on Manufacturing Engineering, respectfully submit the following consensus and recommendations concerning its tasks for your consideration : 
1. That Manufacturing Engineering is a field or discipline where expertise needs to be developed int he country today.
2. That the U.P. College of Engineering should have a share inthe responsibility for training manpower in this field.
3. That in consideration of the needs, the availability of academic programs in the field in other institutions, and the capabilities of the College, both existing and pontetial, the College can best make a contribution through graduate education. 
4. That an MS Program in Manufacturing Engineering appears to be appropriate, timely and attractive to offer
5. That the MS (Mfg. Eng'g) Program should be an interdisciplinary program that should draw the involvement of at least the ChE, EE, IE, ME and Met. E. departments. 
6. That the MS (Mfg. Eng'g) Program should be open to any baccalaureate degree holder in engineering provided the usual admission requirements (grade average, etc.) are met. 
7. That the MS (Mfg. Eng'g) Program should feature core courses in Product Design, Manufacturing Facilities Design, Materials Engineering, Human Resource Engineering, Engineering Economics, and Production Planning and Control. This should also conform with College requirements through the inclusion of a course on Numerical Methods and a course on Optimization (Applied Math). 
8. That the MS (Mfg. Eng'g) program should offer specialization options and the suggested ones are as follows : 
(a) Machine Design
(b) Product Design
(c) Management of Manufacturing Systems
9. That graduate courses in product design be made available in at least four departments namely ChE, EE, ME and Met. E. 
10. That a mechanism should now be put forth into motion to study the detailed design, resource requirements and feasibility of an MS (Mfg. Eng'g) Program at the College. In connection with this, existing graduate courses should be reviewed to study the possibility of adopting those which would fit into the envisioned program.

column9: (1980, November 19) Attached Proposal for Continuing Education Program in Electrical Engineering
column10:
1. Both the College of Engineering and the National Engineering Center recognize the need. While the College of Engineering has been offering formal continuing education programs (MS and Diploma programs), the National Engineering Center has been sponsoring non-formal continuing education programs through a series of seminars and workshops. 
2. In areas where we still don't have existing programs, we institute such programs based on the need and our capability to offer such programs.
3. We recall that in 1979, for example, the College of Engineering and the National Engineering Center extended to the Philippine Institute of Civil Engineers their facilities for the Continuing Education Seminar on Structural Analysis and Design of Small Dams. 
4. In principle this proposal of IIEE is the in line with the common efforts of the College of Engineering and the National Engineering Center in providing opportunities and facilities for continuing education to the members of the engineering profession. 
5. With regards therefore to the proposed program by the Institute of Integrated Electrical Engieering (IIEE), we suggest that we invite IIEE's duly authorized representative to a meeting with Dr. Luis M. Alarilla, Jr. representing the College of Engineering, and Professor Fortunato T. De la Peña, representing the NEC, for the purpose of discussing the details of the program. column9: (1982, January 14) Comments to the recommendation of the Committee to Review Academic Programs that the National Engineering Center be attached to the College of Engineering.
column10:
This is in reply to your letter dated January 13, 1982 asking for our comments to the recommendation of the Committee to Review Academic Programs that the National Engineering Center be attached to the College of Engineering. We agree with this recommendation. 
It will be recalled that in my memorandum to you dated May 20, 1980, I explained that the College of Engineering and the National Engineering Center cannot be independent for the following main reasons :
1. Presently, the two units are dependent on common resources : staff, equipment and facilities. Unless a formal linkage is therefore established, we have two "independent" units which are dependent on the same resources and under such a situation, conflicts involving authority over personnel and priority to use of equipment and facilities and other problems are likely to arise.
2. The College of Engineering, in recognition of its responsibilities, has to continue improving its capability in performing its three functions namely, teaching, research and extension service. It was precisely for this purpose that the College of Engineering established the U.P. Industrial Research Center, the National Hydraulics Research Center, the U.P. Building Research Services, the Transport Training Center and the Training Center for Applied Geodesy and Photogrammetry. And yet, under the present set-up, all these Centers were absorbed by the National Engineering Center. Thus, the College of Engineering will now be dependent on the National Engineering Center in carrying out its programs and activities especially those related to research and extension services. Should there be no formal linkage between the two units, the College of Engineering may find the need in the future to reinstitute the facilities that were absorbed by the NEC in order to perform the three above-mentioned functions which we feel are vital and necessary to the growth and progress of the College. 
It will further be recalled that the faculty and administrative personnel of the College of Engineering in a meeting held on June 27, 1980 recommend to make the two headships concurrent positions as an alternatie arrangement for linkages between the College of Engineering and the National Engineering Center. This alternative was recommended as an initial step without prejudice to the consideration of other alternatives in the future. 
We agree with the Committee's recommendation that "all research and extension units shall be attached to academic degree-granting colleges/units to allow for the immediate interchange of resources among teaching, research and extension units and to make knowledge derived from research and extension readily available to academic departments." However, some problems may arise in the case of research centers which are multidisciplinary in character. It may be difficult to identify an academic college to which such centers can be best attached without reducing their effectiveness in carrying out their research activites.
column11:
Alfonso, Dr. Oscar M. 
Mena, Marino M.

column9: (1982, February 15) Comments on CRAP Recommendations
column10:
I wish to transmit herewith for your information and consideration the comments and suggestions from the U.P. College of Engineering on the CRAP recommendations. This updates our previous report of January 15, 1982 which we submitted to Executive Vice-President Alfonso in response to his memorandum of January 4, 1982. The commens in said previous report were those of the College Executive Committee while the attached comments include those of the faculty as obtained in a faculty meeting held last Thursday, February 4, 1982. 
First of all, we welcome and support your efforts in initiating a review of existing university programs in the light of new directions towards which these programs must be addressed to as well as the adoption of revised and new guidelines/ criteria which university programs must satisfy so that the university will be more responsive to the interests and needs of the many constituencies it serves. We agree that (a) the university has to be very much concerned about what to teach and how to teach it: (b) the university, in the face of new demands in a changing society, has to recognize greater responsibilities than a few years ago; and (c) the university has to be recognized nationally and internationally as a leader in many matters relating to the academic field. To meet these new responsibilities, we also believe that the university has to use new approaches and to establish new priorities even if new risks have to be taken. It has been said that if there are no controversies or tensions involved in what we are doing, then we can't be doing anything much. If this is the test, then the controversies or tensions created by the CRAP are welcome signs. However, while we agree that new ideas will continue to be needed, they must be requierd to go through the mill of objection and analysis. This is the trial heat which true innovations must survive. It is in the spirit that we are pleased to react to the CRAP project. 
Secondly, we also wish to inform you of the survey that the U.P. College of Engineering conducted in 1980-1981 for the purpose of obtaining the opinions of educators, practicing engineers, government and private employers of engineers as well as future engineers regarding the needs and requirements of the future will serve as a reasonable basis for establishing practical guidelines for defining the long-term goals of engineering education in the Philippines. It is intended to identify and prioritize specific goal areas which are broadly classified into three categories namely, those related to (1) national tasks, (2) development of professional skills and (3) development of professional attitudes. Specifically, we want to attain the following aims : 
(1) To determine the basic educational needs and goals of engineers, not only for the immediate future but also for the decade ahead.
(2) To determine the scope of education necessary to fill these needs that can best be accomplished at the undergraduate and graduate levels.
(3) To study the adequacy of present engineering education and to consider possible improvements.
(4) To study the need and scope of continuing education programs. 
The engineering programs can then be reviewed in the light of newly identified areas and priorities. Changes can be introduced either to improve the existing programs or to institute new ones to meet new responsibilities. It was partly because of the result of this survey why we decided to propose a new program (MS and Ph.D.) in the field of Energy as well as a Bachelor degree in Computer Science. Attached is also a copy of the results of the survey.

column9: (1983, January 3) An assessment of the direct admission of freshmen at the College of Engineering
column10:
This is in connection with the direct admission of freshmen at the College of Engineering which was started last June, 1982. It will be recalled that the College of Engineering is one of the professional schools which participated in trying the effectiveness of the policy with regards to the direct admission of freshmen. An assessment of our experience is hereby being submitted for the purpose of identifying some problem areas which were encountered and recommending solutions to these problems. 
First of all, the assessment of the experiment's success or failure has to be made within the context of the objectives. I recall that the primary objective of the experiment is to decongest the registration at the College of Arts and Sciences and thus, reducing the difficulties encountered by the students during the registration period. Against this background, permit me to review the registration procedure for freshmen as applied during the first and second semesters of academic year 1982-83 and to identify the problems that were encountered during the registration process. Attached herewith is the flow chart for freshmen registration. 
Problems : 
1. It will be noted that it takes at least three (3) days for a freshman to complete his registration. Two (2) days are during the advanced registration period,a dn at least one (1) day during the regular registration period. 
2. The registrants get Form 5 (Long Form) only during the regular registration period. Yet some were told upon enlistment in CMT that the Long Form 5 is required. 
3. Those on free schedule go back to their advisers in engineering when the sections are closed. Yet the engineering advisers do not have the information on what sections are still open. Thus, the students have to go back and forth from CAS to Engineering. 
4. There were many who would take the free schedule than the blocks. 
5. The advisers assigned for freshmen have longer registration assignments because of the advanced registration. 
Recommendations :
1. The time required for registration can be shortened through better planning and coordination. In this connection, we strongly recommend that the College of Engineering, in consultation with the Office of the Dean/Secretary and Departments concerned at the College of Arts and Sciences, prepare a reasonably good estimate of CAS subjects needed by engineering students. On the basis of this approved list of subjects, the required number of classcards will be issued to the College of Engineering through the College Secretary and the distribution of these classcards will be done at the College of Engineering. The adoption of this recommendation will very much simplify the registration process and provide the solution to the above-mentioned problems because it will mean that:
(a) there is no need for advanced registration; 
(b) the freshmen and sophomore engineering students do not have to go to the College of Arts and Sciences for classcards except for very exceptional cases;
(c) some of the steps in the registration process are no longer necessary which will shorten the time required for registration.
2. Some of the engineering departments will be advised to have some engineering courses at the first and second year levels so as to decrease the demand for AS subjects at these levels.
3. A continuing assessment of the registration process will be made for the purpose of improving the existing procedure. 
We hope that the above recommendations merit your favorable consideration. Should you wish to discuss this with our staff in person, we would be very glad to meet with you and to discuss these problems which I am sure are of common interest.
column11:
Angangco, Dean Ofelia R. 
Mena, Marino M.

Please accept our appreciation for initiating the dialogue with the construction industry. It is only through this continuing dialogue that we may be able to update our academic programs to meet the needs of industry.

column9: (2002, April 2) Volume 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 of my "Thoughts of Wisdom"
column10:
I have an engineer friend who said to me : "The place in the college where I stayed most is the Engineering Library." This is perhaps true to many of our students. 
It is an old saying that it is not so much to know as to know what and where to find. The library is the first that usually comes to mind when one is confronted with the problem of where to find. It has been said that all that man has thought, all that man has done, the experience that has been bought with the sufferings of 3 hundred generations - al are garnered up for us in the world of books. 
I am pleased to share with you and the users of the Engineering Library these five volumes of my thoughts. You may be interested to read what I wrote on page 13 of Volume 3 on "Engineering Library : Its Role". The five volumes are : 
Volume 1 : Values of Life
Volume 2 : Education : The Key to Succcess
Volume 3 : Education and The Engineering Profession
Volume 4 : Mathematics : Its Usefulness in Our Day to Day Living and in Engineering
Volume 5 : Jokes and Lessons in Engineering Education
