column9: Expansion of the Philippine Autosomal Short Tandem Repeat Population Database for DNA-based Paternity Testing by Sheena Marie B. Maiquilla, Jazelyn M. Salvador, Gayvelline C. Calacal, Frederick C. Delfin, Kristina A. Tabbada, Henry B. Perdigon, Minerva S. Sagum, Miriam Ruth M. Dalet and Maria Corazon A. De Ungria
column10: Recent developments in forensic DNA technology have led to the expansion of the Philippine reference database of autosomal Short Tandem Repeat (aSTR) DNA markers currently being used by the DNA Analysis Laboratory, Naturla Sciences Research Institute of the University of the Philippines for DNA-based paternity testing. A total of 502 unrelated individuals from different regional centers of the Philippines were analyzed at 15 aSTR DNA markers, which include the 13 DNA markers that comprise the Combined DNA Identification System (CODIS) of the US Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI). Population statistical parameters were calculated and determined. Expansion of the population database resulted in the detection of additional alleles of the markers included in the database, increased power of discrimination and power of paternity exclusion compared to a smaller-sized Philippine database (n=100) that was reported previously (De Ungria et al. 2005, Salvador et al. 2007).
column11: human DNA typing, paternity testing, Philippines database, population genetics

column9: A Contribution to the Taxonomy of Mastogloia (Class Bacillariophyceae) in the Philippines by Milagrosa R. Martinez-Goss and Luisito T. Evangelista
column10: Nine species of Mastogloia found on the brown seaweeds, Sargassum spp., in two study areas in the Philippines (Lian, Batangas and Plaridel, Quezon) are described. Mastogloia smithii Thw. ex W. Sm. is added to the list of new record for the country.
column11: Brown seaweed, Batangas, Epiphytic diatoms, Mastogloia, Partecta, Quezon, Sargassum

column9: Physico-Mechanical Properties and Durability of Thermally Modified Malapapaya [Polyscias nodosa (Blume) Seem.] Wood by Juanito P. Jimenez, Jr., Menandro N. Acda, Ramon A. Razal, and Ponciano S. Madamba
column10: The study investigated the use of thermal modification to improve physico-mechanical properties and decay resistance of Malapapaya [Polyscias nodosa (Blume) Seem.] wood. Results showed that wood properties were altered with exposure to high temperature and long treatment duration. Wood color changed from light yellowish brown to very dark brown with increasing treatment temperature. No defects due to heat treatments were observed. Treatments resulted in improved dimensional stability as indicated by the reduction in water absorption and thickness swelling of treated materials. Decay resistance against white rot [Fomes lividus (Kalchbr.) Sacc] and brown rot [Lenzites striata (Swartz ex Fries)] fungi improved significantly at the highest treatment combination of 220 degree celcius - 120 minutes. Mechanical properties as measured by modulus of rupture and toughness were significantly reduced.
column11: Decay fungi, Dimensional stability, Durability, Malapapaya, Physico-mechanical properties, Thermally modified wood

column9: Influence of Castor Oil-Based Polyurethane on Physico-Chemical Properties of Calcium Silicate Cement by Eduardo R. Magdaluyo, Jr., Clodualdo M. Aranas, Jr., and Jovilyn D. Masiglat
column10: Polyurethane from castor oil and toluene diisocyanate was activated in the presence of calcium silicate cement paste, with varying equivalent free isocyanate (NCO) to equivalent free hydroxyl (OH) moiety and cement to water ratio. It was shown that as the NCO/OH ratio and percent cement mixture were increased, the compressive strength and bulk density of the obtained polymer-modified calcium silicate cement materials improved significantly. The increase in the strength and density is explained by low porosity and the presence of polyurethane hard segments in the polymer structure. Moreover, the powder x-ray diffraction and surface morphology investigations of the castor oil-based polyurethane-reinforced cement composites revealed the interpenetration of the polymer macromolecules in the cementitious matrix. The Ca2+ from the portlandite phase was readily available to react with the hydroxyl groups from the polyurethane macromolecule and excess monomer resulting in calcium oxide-terminated diisocyanate.
column11: Calcium silicate, Castor oil, Composite, Polyurethane-reinforced cement

column9: Development of Floor Tiles from Philippine Bamboos by Marina A. Alipon, Edna B. Bauza, and Gilberto N. Sapin
column10: The study determined and evaluated some important physical and mechanical properties of floor tiles from kauayan tinik (Bambusa blumeana J.A. & J.H. Schultes) and giant bamboo [Dendrocalamus asper (Schultes f.) Backer ex Heyne] glued with urea formaldehyde (UF) and polyvinyl acetate (PVAc) without and with preservative treatments (deltamethrin, borax and boric acid, and chlorpyrifos) as well as their comparative costs. Standard procedures were used to test and evaluate the properties of the bamboo floor tiles such as relative density (RD), moisture content (MC), hardness (H), thickness swelling (TS), abrasive resistance (AR), and glue bond (GB). Kauayan tinik and giant bamboo are both acceptable for flooring although the former has generally better properties than the latter. UF-glued kauayan tinik treated with either deltamethrin or borax and boric acid is preferred over the others in term of physical and mechanical properties, as well as cost of glue and preservative combinations.
column11: Bamboo floor tiles, Bambusa blumeana, Dendrocalamus asper, polyvinyl acetate, physical and mechanical properties, urea formaldehyde

column9: Evolutionary significant units of gray-sided vole (Myodes rufocanus) in Hokkaido, Japan by Anna Pauline O. de Guia and Takashi Saitoh
column10: The gray-sided vole, Myodes rufocanus (=Clethrionomys rufocanus) is a widespread species in Hokkaido, Japan. We applied an integrative approach to determine adaptive and neutral genetic variation in the gray-sided vole populations to identify full or partial Evolutionarily Significant Units. We surveyed 38 mainland populations and six island populations. The mitochondrial DNA control region was analyzed for neutral genetic variation and cranial measurements were taken as a proxy for adaptive variation. Data on neutral genetic variation indicate that most island populations are differentiated from the mainland populations. The islands of Teuri and Yagishiri, Rishir, and Kunashiri can be regarded as partial ESUs based on their unique haplotypes. Partial ESUs based on morphological features can be identified in the eastern part of mainland with populations from Akkeshi and Shibecha. A full ESU designation can be given to Daikoku Island based on its unique haplotype and unique morphological feature.
column11: Adaptive variation, ESU, Mitochondrial DNA, Morphometrics, Neutral genetic variation, Small mammal

column9: The effect of egg consumption on lipid profile among selected 30-60 year-old filipino adults by Celeste C. Tanchoco, Liza N. Infante, Marietta P. Rodriguez, Mina Grace C. Aquino and Consuelo L. Orense
column10: Egg a rich source of protein, contains vitamins and minerals. However, egg is also high in cholesterol. The objective of this study is to determine the effect of daily egg consumption on blood lipid profile of selected 30-60-year-old free-living Filipino adults. One hundred fifteen normocholesterolemic males and females in a cross-over study were randomly assigned to either an egg-eating regimen or a no egg-eating regimen for three months. After a two-week wash period, one regimen was exchanged for the other for another three months. Fasting venous blood samples were drawn to determine blood lipids before and after each period. The marginal means of serum cholesterol increased after the initial phase of intervention for both treatment groups, with cholesterol values higher for those who started with egg. Participants with normal weight who started with eggless diet exhibited higher serum cholesterol except in the final period, compared with those who started with egg. In the univariate analysis, age showed as the factor that influenced total cholesterol in the two study regimens. In both regimens, total cholesterol and LDL- cholesterol slightly increased. but HDL-cholesterol and triglycerides decreased. The GLM procedure revealed that the blood lipid levels were not significantly affected by treatment (egg or no egg), sequence (egg first or no-egg first) and time (egg eating or no-egg eating) factors. Consumption of up to one egg per day is unlikely to have substantial increase in blood lipid levels.
column11: Adults, age, cross-over design, HDL, LDL, regular egg consumption, sex, total cholesterol

column9: Selection of arbuscular mycorrhizal inoculants for improved growth of somatic embryo-derived Liriodendron tulipifera (Magnoliaceae) Plantlets by Nelly S. Aggangan, Yong-Wook Kim, Sim-Hee Han, So-Young Park, and Heung-Kyu Moon.
column10: To select arbuscular mycorrhizal (AM) fungi for large scale inoculation programs, somatic embryo (SE) derived yellow poplar (Liriodendron tulipifera L.) plantlets were inoculated during ex-vitro, with spores of AM fungi Gigaspora margarita, Glomus macrocarpum, Glomus etunicatum, Glomus fasciculatum from the Philippines, Glomus intraradices from INVAM, USA and mixed species extracted from rhizosphere soil of a Populus stand in Suwon (AMKFRI) and Carex growing in mine tailings in Bonghwa (AMM6), Korea. AMKFRI consists of Glomus and Gigaspora while AMM6 had Glomus and Acaulospora. Plantlets were grown in autoclaved peat perlite vermiculite medium and incubated under growth room conditions for six weeks and later in a glasshouse. Generally, mycorrhizal plants grew better with greener and broader leaves than the non-mycorrhizal counterpart. After 14 weeks in a glasshouse, roots of all mycorrhizal seedlings were colonized with AM fungi where G. intraradices and G. margarita colonized 63 and 54%, respectively, of the root system. G. intraradices, G. margarita, G. macrocarpum and AMM6 increased the highest root and total dry weight but comparable with that by AMM6. G. fasciculatum inoculated plants had the highest P concentration and P, Zn and Cu uptakes. AMM6 promoted the highest N,K, Na and Mg uptakes. The results show that AM fungi, irrespective of origin (either from the Philippines, USA, and Korea), can establish a symbiotic association with SE-derived yellow poplar plantlets. However, capabilities in promoting plant growth, biomass and nutrient uptake differed. The Philippine G. fasciculatum and the Korean AMM6 are the most promising AM inoculants that can be used on SE-derived yellow poplar plantlets during ex-vitro. G. margarita, G. etunicatum. G. macrocarpum, G. intraradices and AMKFRI promoted an intermediate plant growth. Their effectiveness and persistence should be determined in degraded field conditions.
column11: Acaulospora, Gigaspora, Glomus, Liriodendron tulipifera, Somatic embryogenesis

column9: Structural characterization of Buri (Corypha utan Lam.) Petioles by Rosalie M. Calapis, Vivian C. Daracan, Stella Villa A. Castillo, Wilfredo M. Carandang and Willie P. Abasolo
column10: Anatomical analysis was conducted on Buri (Corypha utan Lam.) petioles in order to generate baseline data about the anatomy of buri petioles where buntal fibers are extracted and to determine the origin of these fibers. It is with hope that this baseline data can contribute in maximizing its yield in terms of quality and quantity, and suggesting other usage of wasted buntal fibers such as in pulp and papermaking industry. Vascular bundle and ground parenchyma area percentages were analyzed using advanced digital imagery. Variation both along the length (base (B), middle (M), top (T) and across the radius (outer periphery (PO), core (C), inner periphery (PI)) of the petiole were considered in the evaluation. Fiber morphology was also conducted. Analysis showed that the petiole is made up of vascular bundles and ground parenchyma cells the proportion of which was highest at the middle and top portion, respectively. The bundle was actually the buntal fiber itself. Bundle diameter was highest at the basal portion while its element percentages were greatest in the middle portion. The structure of the bundle was unique because of the two fiber caps enveloping the other elements. This provided the vascular bundle enough strength and protection to withstand manual extraction including the weaving process. Considering both the quantity and quality of buntal, it is suggested that farmers can prioritize harvesting from the middle, basal and finally, the top portion. Fiber morphology values were highest in the outer periphery, but it varies along the height of the petiole. Furthermore, fiber characteristics showed acceptable values for pulp and paper manufacture; hence, the petiole itself together with the waste buntal could be best suited for this purpose to maximize its utility.
column11: Buntal fibers, Corypha utan, Fiber measurements, Ground parenchyma, Vascular bundle

column9: On Bravais Colorings Associated with Periodic and Non-Periodic Crystals by Ma. Louise Antonette N. De Las Peñas and Enrico Paolo C. Bugarin
column10: In this work, a theory of color symmetry is presented that extends the ideas of traditional theories of color symmetry for periodic crystals to apply to non-periodic crystals. The color symmetries are associated to each of the crystalline sites and may correspond to different chemical species, various orientations of magnetic moments and colorings of a non-periodic tiling. In particular, we study the color symmetries of periodic and non-periodic structures via Bravais colorings of planar modules that emerge as the ring of integers in cyclotomic fields with class number one. Using an approach involving matrices, we arrive at necessary and sufficient conditions for determining the color symmetry groups and color fixing groups of the Bravais colorings associated with the modules Mn=Z [exp(2i/n)], and list the findings for M15=Z[exp(2i/15)] and M16=Z[exp(i/18)]. In the second part of the paper, we discuss magnetic point groups of crystal and quasicrystal structures and give some examples of structures whose magnetic point group symmetries are described by Bravais colorings of planar modules.
column11: Bravais colorings, color symmetry, magnetic point groups, periodic and non-periodic crystals, planar modules, quasicrystals

column9: Physico-Chemical and Microbiological Paramaters in the Deterioration of Virgin Coconut Oil by Ian Ken D. Dimzon, Melodina F. Valde, Jaclyn Elizabeth R. Santos, Mark Joseph M. Garrovillas, Henson M. Dejarme, Jo Margarette W. Remollo, and Fabian M. Dayrit
column10:
The deterioration of virgin coconut oil (VCO) due to physico-chemical oxidation and hydrolysis and microbiological processes was studied. The physico-chemical oxidation of VCO in the air at room temperature was negligible. Oxidation of VCO was observed only in the presence of air, UV radiation, ferric iron (Fe3+), and high free fatty acid (FFA) content. Chemical hydrolysis was performed at varying moisture levels and temperatures. The rate of hydrolysis to produce FFAs was measured using 31P NMR under conditions of saturated water (0.22%) and 80 degree celcius was found to be 0.066 umol/g-hr (expressed as lauric acid). At 0.084% moisture and 80 degree celcius, the rate of FFA formulation was found to be 0.008 umol/g-hr.
A low moisture levels (<0.06%), VCO was stable to microbial decomposition. However, at higher moisture levels, there was an increase in the formation of organic acids, in particular, lactic acid, dodecanoic acid, succinic aci, acetic acid, and fumaric acid, indicating that microbial action had occured. The most important conditions that influence the physico-chemical and microbial degradation of VCO are moisture, temperature, and the presence of microorganisms. These degradation processes can be minimized if the moisture level is maintained below 0.06%.
column11: Hydrolysis, microbial deterioration, oxidation, virgin coconut oil

column9: Proto-ubiquitin : A Bayesian Prediction of an Ancient Protein during the Prokaryotic-Eukaryotic Transition by Custer C. Deocaris, Lovette F. Cunanan, Richelda A. Galapia and Maria A. Endriga
column10: Only recently have biologists been able to apply mathematical and biochemical tools to preview lifestyles of ancient life forms and "travel back in time." In this paper, we describe an ancestral reconstruction of ubiquitin to determine its molecular properties during the rise of the eukaryotes. Although ubiquitin is one of the most conserved proteins in eukaryotes, no ubiquitin homolog has been found in prokaryotic genomes sequenced thus far. In an attempt to derive the ancestral ubiquitin, or proto-ubiquitin (proto-Ub), we applied Bayesian statistical theory to estimate posterior probabilities of protein sequences from a minimum evolution tree of 30 extant species. The inferred ancestral sequence was 100% homologous with the ubiquitin of Brugia malayi, a parasitic nematode. Among its 76 amino acides, only nine residues have undergone amino acid modification. As no major structural and functional changes happened during the evolution of ubiquitin, we hypothesize that the stressful conditions that led to the creation of this gene after the "Great Oxidation Event" 2.4 billion years ago may have already been "buffered" to date.
column11: Bioinformatics, eukaryotes, paleobiochemistry, planetary biology, protein evolution

column9: Mesoporous Hybrid Organosilica Materials Functionalized with Biphenyl Moiety by Eduardo R. Magdaluyo, Jr., Raymond V. Rivera Virtudazo, Leonard P. dela Cruz, Emily V. Castriciones, and Herman D. Mendoza
column10: The synthesis of well-defined structures of inorganic silica framework systems functionalized with organic moiety renders these systems versatile in designing new materials. We have obtained in our laboratories a mesoporous hybrid organosilica material incorporating a biphenyl moiety from the condensation of 4,4'-bis (triethoxysilyl) biphenyl monomer with tetraethoxysilane and 1,2-bis (triethoxysilyl) ethane in basic medium. The polymerization was carried out via surfactant-templated method. Infrared spectroscpic analyses showed that the biphenyl moiety was covalently integrated in the polymeric organosilica structure, and X-ray diffraction investigation confirmed the reflection of two-dimensional hexagonal symmetry lattice. The incorporation of this organic moiety is limited by the proportion of 1,2-bis (triethoxysilyl) ethane. The interplaner spacing was decreased with increasing amount of organosilane incorporation. Morphology of the hybrid xerogel material was in the form of agglomerated particles composed of rope-like orientation. Our results indicated that by manipulating the synthesis conditions, it is possible to tailor and tune the structural and textural properties of the obtained mesoporous hybrid material, allowing some possible flexibility in their design for specific applications.
column11: Surfactant-templated, Polymerization, Organic-inorganic, Synthesis

column9: Evaluation of Silica Sol-Gel Microcapsule for the Controlled Release of Insect Repellent, N,N-Diethyl-2-methoxybenzamide, on Cotton by Ann Shireen Chan, Judy del Valle, Kendricks Lao, Christian Malapit, Modesto Chua and Regina C. So
column10: N-N-diethyl-2-methoxybenzamide, an insect repellent compound, was incorporated into cotton textile using the sol-gel method. Evaluation of the results of four different sol-gel formulations showed that a lower condensation rate resulted in significantly better encapsulation. Also investigated was the encapsulation in the presence of other polymers like polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA), polystyrene (PS) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) that prolong the release of the repellent. The sol-gel formulation, SiO2 : polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA) (1:0.8), gave smooth fabric and good encapsulation of the insect repellent compound. PS and PVA resulted in cloths which are aesthetically undesirable. Cloths coated with sol-gel PVA gave the best repellent release profile.
column11: Functionalized textile, N,N-diethyl-2-methoxybenzamide, sol-gel

column9: Cross-Species Amplification of Shorea Microsatellite DNA Markers in Parashorea malaanonan (Dipterocarpaceae) by Myralyn A. Abasolo, Edwino S. Fernando, Teresita H. Borromeo and Desiree M. Hautea
column10: Sixteen (16) microsatellite DNA markers previously developed for two Bornean species of Shorea (Dipterocarpaceae), viz. 5 from Shorea curtisii (Shc) and 11 from Shorea leprosula (Sle), were tested on Philippine Parashorea malaanonan for cross-species amplification. Initially, these loci were tested in Parashorea malaanonan from the Makiling Forest Reserve on Luzon Island. All five Shc loci had amplification products, three (3) were polymorphic and two (2) were non-polymorphic. Of the 11 Sle loci, five (5) were polymorphic, three (3) were non-polymorphic, two (2) did not have amplification products, and one (1) had multiple bands. Four loci were further tested on individuals of Parashorea malaanonan from a plantation on Leyte Island. High levels of genetic variability were observed. Cross-transferability was 75%. Expected hetrozygosity (He) ranged from 0.39 to 0.82. Results indicate potential use of these markers derived from Shorea in studies of genetic diversity assessments in Parashorea malaanonan.
column11: Cross transferability, Dipterocarpaceae, Genetic diversity, Parasahorea malaanonan, Shorea, SSR

column9: Paper mill sludge as fiber additive for asphalt road pavement by Erlinda L. Mari, Ma. Salome R. Moran and Cesar O. Austria
column10: This study evaluated the properties of stone mastic asphalt mixtures made with paper mill sludge from four paper mills, as well as wastepaper, as fiber additive. Marshall specimens were prepared with asphalt content of 4.5, 5.0, 5.5, 6.0 and 6.5% and sludge or fiber contents of 0.2, 0.3, 0.4 and 0.5 percent. Properties tested were bulk specific gravity, stability, flow, air voids, voids in mineral aggregates and voids filled with asphalt. Effects of asphalt and fiber contents on flow and stability were analyzed statistically. Asphalt contents between 5 and 6 percent and sludge or fiber contents between 0.3 and 0.5% from any of the four paper mills resulted in Marshall specimens with properties generally passing the Department of Public Works and Highways specifications for both medium and heavy traffic road pavement.
column11: Asphalt, Fiber, Marshall specimens, Paper mill sludge

column9: Selection of high ethanol-producing saccharomyces cerevisiae strains, their fermentation properties, and genetic differentiation based on rep-PCR and mt rDNA-PCR by Irene G. Pajares, Francisco B. Elegado, Jose Paolo V. Magbanua and Asuncion K. Raymundo
column10: Ten out of 37 Saccharomyces isolates statistically gave higher ethanol yields after 24 hours using 20% (total sugars) unsterile molasses medium. Saccharomyces cerevisiase 2025 and 2023 produced the highest ethanol concentrations of 8.7% and 7.5% (v/v), respectively. In sterile molasses medium, ethanol production decreased with these two isolates, but increased with S. cerevisiase 2013, 2012, 2008, 2010 and 2014, with ethanol concentrations of 9.17, 8.88, 8.84, 8.44 and 8.31% (v/v), respectively. After 3 days incubation, all ten isolates survived at 40 degree celcius but only S. cerevisiase 2031 tolerated up to 15% (v/v) exogenous ethanol. S. cerevisiae 2010 and 2014 were resistant to the toxin produced by killer strain S. cerevisiae K1 (ATCC 60728). Combining the various test parameters, S. cervisiae 2008, 2010 and 2014 would be ideal for industrial ethanol production and thus fingerprinted. Amplification of the mitochondrial small subunit ribosomal DNA (mtSSU rDNA) by polymerase chain reaction (PCR) detected polymorphisms among the S. cerevisiae strains. Cluster analysis using UPGMA-SAHN was able to group the high from the low ethanol producers but other properties such as ethanol and temperature tolerance were not distinctly separated. Repetitive sequence-based PCR analysis of the ten selected isolates using REP primers (rep-PCR) also clustered the three best S. cervisiae isolates.
column11: Cluster analysis, DNA fingerprinting, Ethanol, Polymerase chain reaction, Saccharomyces cerevisiase

column9: Biocatalytic Synthesis of Diethanolamide Surfactants Under Mild Reaction Conditions by Galilee Uy Semblante, Modesto Tan Chua and Soma Chakraborty
column10: Sythesis of fatty acid diethanolamide was carried out by the condensation of diethanolamine with lauric acid, decanoic acid and octanoic acid in the presence of Novozym 435. The influence of solvent, time, temperature and agitation on the reaction rate was studied. Formation of lauroyl diethanolamide was complete in 6h in acetonitrile at 50 degree celcius whereas in toluene the reaction was not complete in 6h. Increase of reaction temperature from 50 degree celcius to 70 degree celcius increased the reaction rate. Proper agitation of the reaction mixture enhanced the reaction rate. Reaction in solution appeared to be more efficient than the reaction in solvent free system. The surface active property of the fatty acid amides was studied by determining their critical micelles concentration. The critical micelle concentration of lauroyl diethanolamide, decanoyl diethanolamide and octanoyl diethanolamide were found to be 0.63mM, 1.10mM, 1.45mM respectively in deionized water. Micelles of lauroyl diethanolamide were less than 1um in size.
column11: Diethanolamide, Novozym 435, Biocatalysis

column9: Influence of Growth Rate, Elevation and Sunlight on the Anatomical and Physico-Mechanical Properties of Plantation-Grown Palasan (Calamus merrillii Becc.) Canes by Willie P. Abasolo and Olga C. Lomboy
column10: The influence of growth rate, elevation and sunlight exposure on the properties of plantation-grown palasan canes was verified in order to promote the utilization of cultivated canes to encourage the establishment of more palasan plantations. Properties evaluated were fiber length, wall thickness, fiber distribution, ovendried specific gravity, Modulus of Elasticity (MOE) and Modulus of Rupture (MOR) using standard procedures. Growth rate ranged from 0.36 to 3.79 m/yr, elevation was from 10 to 980 masl and sunlight exposure from 20 to 90.28%, showing that palasan plants can thrive in varying site conditions. Among the properties evaluated, only fiber percentage was moderately affected by both growth rate (r=0/53) and amount of sunlight exposure (r=0.51). Elevation , on the other hand, moderately influenced wall thickness (r=0.45). Mechanical properties of the cane were unaffected by the three parameters. Therefore, the study proved that palasan plant is an ideal plantation species because it thrives in any kind of site and its properties are minimally affected by the major site characteristics such as elevation and sunlight exposure. Thus, it is recommended that more palasan plantations be established to provide a sustainable supply of raw canes to the rattan furniture industry.
column11: Cell wall thickness, elevation, fiber length, fiber percentage, growth rate, modulus of elasticity, modulus of rupture, specific gravity, sunlight exposure

column9: Correlates of Body Image Satisfaction Among Economically Depressed Urban Filipino Woman by Mildred T. Aquino, Consuelo L. Orense, Celeste C. Tanchoco, Sofia V. Amarra, Merlyn G. Tajan and Evelyn O. Dela Cruz
column10: The study assessed body image satisfaction with diet, body mass index (BMI), waist circumference (WC), waist-hip ratio (WHR), age, income, and education among 251 women in a depressed urban barangay. Results showed that most women were younger, had low, education, and had no occupation. Using BMI, 28.7% were overweight and obese, and 11.6% were underweight. Using WHR and WC, 59% and 13.5%, respectively, were at risk. Six out of ten women were not satisfied with their body image. Nutrient intake and adequacy between women who were satisfied and those who were not satisfied with their body image were not significantly different. Adequacy of intake was below the Recommend Energy and Nutrient Intake (RENI) for most nutrients except niacin. Women satisfied with their body image had lower BMI, WC, and WHR. Body image satisfaction was associated with BMI, WC and WHR but WHR was the only significant variable affecting image satisfaction. Android or central body adiposity affects body image satisfaction among women because of the change in body shape brought by fat deposition.
column11: Body image, Body mass index, Nutrient intake-adequacy, urban Filipino women, waist-hip ratio, waste circumference

column9: Effects of Epiphyte Density on Seagrass Leaf Turnover Rate by Maria Luisa S. Orbita and Hiroshi Mukai
column10: An experimental investigation on the effects of epiphyte density on leaf turnover rate was carried out on four temperate seagrass species : Zostera japonica, Zostera Marina, Zostera asiatica, and Phyllospadix iwatensis. The four seagrass species were cultured in outdoor tanks under ambient conditions and leaf turnover rates were measured with the presence and absence of epiphytes. The leaf turnover rates of Z. japonica, Z. asiatica, and Z. marina when epiphytes were removed were significantly lower, but the same seagrass species exhibited faster leaf turnover rates under the control condition. The leaf turnover rate of Z. japonica, Z. marina, and Z. asiatica within the control and experimental conditions was significantly affected by the changes in the epiphyte density, while no significant effect was detected in P. iwatensis. This study shows that high epiphyte density enhanced high leaf turnover rates exhibited by Zostera species, as the adaptive mechanism against epiphytism.
column11: Epiphytism, leaf turnover, phyllospadix iwatensis, zostera asiatica, zostera japonica, zostera marina

column9: Effects of Different Bleaching Conditions on Bending Tolerance of Two Rattan Species (Calamus merrillii Becc. and C. caesisu Blume) by Edgar E. Devera, Ramon A. Razal, Stella Villa A. Castillo and Portia G. Lapitan
column10: Following bleaching treatment under varying conditions, scraped poles of sika (Calamus caesius Blume) and palasan (C. merrillii Becc.) were tested to determine their breaking radius of curvature. Variables include bleaching agents, soaking time, and temperature using a 3 x 3 x 3 factorial in a Completely Randomized Design experiment. Bent poles that were least and most negatively affected by bleaching, along with unbleached control samples, were taken for chemical analysis and fiber measurement. Flexural properties of sika were unaffected by the different temperature levels employed while palasan was only affected by the bleaching agent used. Intearctions among the three variables were found to be insignificant in sika but were highly significant in palasan. Proximate chemical analysis showed that the bending tolerance of sika and palasan poles that was most negatively affected by bleaching treatment has significantly lower holocellulose content but higher ash, hot water, and 1% NaOH soluble fractions than the control and the least affected poles. Fiber analysis revealed that only the fiber length of palasan was affected by bleaching.
column11: Bending tolerance, Bleaching, Breaking radius, Fiber measurements, Proximate chemical analysis, Rattan

column9: Assessing the impact of fish cage culture on Taal Lake (Philippines) Water and Sediment Quality Using the Zebrafish Embryo Assay by Arnold V. Hallare, Patricia Ann Factor, Erica Katrina Santos and Henner Hollert
column10: The present study investigated the impact of fish cage aquaculture on the quality of lake water and sediment using a contact assay with zebrafish (Danio rerio) embryos. Fertilized zebrafish embryos were exposed to surface waters and whole sediments collected from three sites of varying levels of stress from Taal Lake, Philippines. Gonzales was the reference site, while Bañaga and Balakilong were sites of heavy aquaculture. No significant developmental differences were observed in water samples collected from all sites. Very high survival rates, high hatching success rates, and few abnormalities were observed in all water samples. All values did not differ significantly from the negative control. Similarly, zebrafish embryos exposed to sediments from the reference site and from sediment control also developed normally and showed no significant defects. However, a significant reduction in both survival and hatching success rates, and a considerable increase in rates of abnormalities, were recorded from sediments obtained from the aquaculture sites. The observed embryotoxic responses were discussed in relation to the presence of contaminants such as ammonia and copper which have settled in the sediments from unconsumed feeds. Results of the study clearly demonstrate that fish cage culture has a harmful impact on lake quality (particularly the sediment phase). We also recommend the use of an effect-directed analysis as a next-level approach to identify specifically other individual toxicants responsible for the teratogenic effects.
column11: Ammonia, Embryotoxicity, Fish cage, Heavy metals, Sediment quality, Taal Lake, Zebrafish

column9: Computational studies of dipyridodiazepinones as human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) reverse transcriptase inhibitors by Reiner G. Villavicencio and Junie B. Billones
column10: A series of dipyridodiazepinone derivatives that are highly active against wild-type and K103N-Y181C double mutant reverse transcriptase were computationally investigated. Quantitative structure-activity relationship (QSAR) studies, metabolic studies, and docking simulations were performed on these compounds. The logP value and the HOMO-LUMO energies of the compound were found to be critical for activity. The most probable biotransformation reactions include n5/N11-dealkylation, apliphatic hydroxylation/oxidation at the ethoxy connector, and aromatic hydroxylation in the dipyridodiazepinone nucleus and the aryl group. Docking studies showed that the inhibitor must have at least two aromatic structures to maintain interactions with the Tyr181-Tyr188 pocket and the Tyr318 region. The nucleus of the molecule must be a hydrophobic structure to interact with Val179 and Gly190 and must be substituted with H-bonding functional groups to interact with Lys101 and Lys/Asn 103.
column11: Docking simulations, E-State, Quantitative-Structure Activity Relationship (QSAR)

column9: A Structured Approach to Designing the Balanced Scorecard : A Case Study by Anna Bella D. Siriban-Manalang, Ph.D.
column10:
This paper presents a structured approach and methodology that uses the identity and business framework of the organization for the design of a balanced scorecard. It defines a sequential structured approach which starts from analyzing the entity with respect to its environment and multi-customer linkages in order to create a holistic integrative performance measure for the organization. It is applied to a system classified under the information technology industry which services multiple client companies belonging to another industry. 
The process starts with the company recognizing its need for a holistic integrative approach to determining a performance measurement system that will show the status of strategies and goals achievement. The company reports regularly to higher members of the organization about its organization for the past period just completed. In one of its strategic planning sessions with its top management, the leaders of the company through the assistance of this consultant pinpointed the appropriateness of Kaplan and Norton's Balanced Scorecard approach to performance management. The paper progresses by transforming the qualitative measures in the initial design of the scorecard into quantitative indicators that can be monitored continously. The study concludes by identifying the targetrs and weights for each indicator.

column9: Filipino Phoneme Recognition Trained on the Filipino Speech Corpus & TIMIT by Rowena Cristina L. Guevara
column10: This paper describes the experiments and results in the phoneme recognition of the Filipino Speech Corpus (FSC) using three sets of training data : TIMIT, FSC and TIMIT plus FSC. Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs) were trained on these three sets of data. The resulting MLP weights were used to compute the corresponding phone posterior probability of speech samples from the FSC and TIMIT. Using three sets of language models, phone models and phone a priori probability, a Start Synchronous decoder was used to align the phones from the posterior probability. A comparison of the cross validation accuracy of the MLP training with the resulting phoneme recognition rates leads to two important conclusions about the development of a bilingual speech recognition system. First, the acoustic model of TIMIT plus FSC may be derived from a simple combination of the training data from the two corpora. Second, the language model of TIMIT plus FSC is not a simple combination of the language models of the two corpora.
column11:

column9: Performance of Code Position Modulation for 2.4GHz IEEE 802.15.4 PHY in Indorr Multipath Channels by Joel S. Marciano, Jr.
column10:
This paper provides an introduction to the IEEE 802.15.4 standard, which specifies the physical (PHY) and medium access control (MAC) layers of low bit-rate wireless personal area networking (LR-WPAN) communication systems. We also present results of simulations representing our initial efforts in characterizing the performance of the physical layer (PHY) of the IEEE 802.15.4 Wireless Personal Area Network (WPAN) Standard in a wireless channel characterized by multipath propagation and additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN). In particular, the studies are conducted on the 2.4 GHz PHY, which specifies code position modulation (CPM) to achieve spectrum spreading of a maximum 250kbps data over a transmission bandwidth of 2MHz. A simple code correction technique using a look-up table was implemented at the receiver and the bit error rate (BER) is calculated for the JTC residential, commercial and office multipath propagation models. The results indicate that CPM provides a performance gain of between 4-11dB over conventional narrowband signaling at moderate signal-to-noise-ratio-per-bit levels. 
We also present a summary of development activities conducted at the Communications Engineering Laboratory (CEL) of the Department of Electrical and Electronics Engineering of the University of the Philippines Diliman (UP EEE) in the area of LR-WPAN.
column11:

column9: Short-Term Load Forecasting Using Holt-Winter's Method and Kalman Filtering by Allan C. Nerves
column10: A comparative study of two methods for short-term forecasting of substation load is presented. Holt-Winter's method predicts the load using a weighted average of past load values that decrease exponentially with time. The Kalman filtering method with fading-memory implements a two-stage forecast where a first-stage mean is first determine and a second-stage correction is applied in real time using the forecast errors from the early hours of the day. Two years of hourly load data for an 110 kV substation are used to develop and test the forecasting models for the two methods. The performance of each method is evaluated using mean absolute error, mean absolute percent error, and mean square error.
column11:
Exponential smoothing
Kalman filtering
Load forecasting
Substation load

column9: Videophotometric Velocimeter Using Temporal Cross-correlation by Luis G. Sison, Ph.D.
column10: Frame-by-frame analysis is the most established and accurate technique for measuring blood flow in microcirculation using videophotometry. Automated analysis software such as Capiflow, which are based on temporal cross-correlation, have been shown to have no significant difference in mesured velocity compared to frame-by-frame analysis. However, these comparative studies did not investigate the causes and modes of possible failure in the temporal cross-correlation algorithm, nor did they study the effect of gross subject motion. In this first of a series of reports, we show that the temporal cross-correlation algorithm applied to single-point intensities typically yield errors  of less than 3 frames. However, significant error may result when the intensity history does not have significant variation during the time periods of interest, or when there is a significant background difference between the selected start and end points.
column11:

Microcirculation
Velocimeter

column9: A Familiarization With Gold and the Jewelry Metals by Eligia Dela Paz Clemente
column10: Traditionally, gold has been known for its use as a jewelry metal and has placed second to copper in importance as ornament only because of its cost and relative rarity. Aside from these two metals, however, platinum, silver, and nickel have also been identified in jewelries and ornaments. This paper aims to familiarize the reader with the different properties of each of the metal mentioned as well as alloys of gold commonly used in making jewelry. A short description of how gold is refined as well as the common analytical techniques for determining the amount of gold in jewelry is also included. To assist in identifying the authenticity of a piece of jewelry and give an estimate of its purity in karats, a short description of the touchstone method is also included in this paper.

column9: E-wastes in the Philippines : Issues and Measures by Genandrialine L. Peralta
column10: The continuous dependence on electronic equipment at home and in the workplace has given rise to a new environmental challenge : electronic wastes. Electronic wastes or e-wastes refer to electronic products that no longer satisfy the needs of the initial purchaser. These can include a wide variety of goods such as computers, cellular phones, TVs, refrigerators, air conditioners, washing machines, video cameras, etc. These equipment contain hazardous materials such as lead, beryllium, mercury, cadmium, and chromium that pose both an occupational and environmental health threat. Although electronic equipment are considered safe during use, the potential for release of their toxic ingredients increase during storage or disposal. Because of the growing number of discarded electronics resulting from rapid product obsolescence, this type of waste is an emerging concern among developing countries. In the Philippines, a study estimates the current and future volume of E-wastes with focus on televisions, refrigerators, air conditioners, washing machines and radios. Data from the National Statistics Office (NSO) serve as input to a simple end-of-life model for each electronic type. Then, mathematical equations are derived incorporating other factors such as volume of electronics in use, current end-of-life management practices, serviceable years of the product and disposal behavior of consumers. The accurate estimation of E-waste generation would be useful in policy making as well as in designing an effective management scheme to avoid potential threat as health impact or environmental pollutant. Preliminary estimates show that at the end of 2004, approximately 3 million units will become obsolete and by 2010 this amount will increase 1.5 times.
column11:
Electronic wastes

column9: Filipino Word Recognition Using a Medium-sized Dictionary by Rowena Cristina L. Guevara
column10: This paper describes the design, implementation, testing and analysis of errors in the development of word-level recognition of the Filipino Speech Corpus (FSC) using a medium-sized dictionary. A Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) was trained on discrete utterances of the words subcorpus of the FSC. The resulting MLP weights were used to compute the corresponding phone posterior probability of discrete utterances of the test data from the FSC words subcorpus. Using a language model, phone model, dictionary and phone a priori probability, a Start Synchronous decoder was used to decode the phone posterior probability into words. Analysis of the decoded words reveals the error pattern in the recognition system. Error correction schemes are recommended for post-decoding, which may be applied to the decoder output to improve the accuracy of recognition.
column11:

column9: GIS and Multi-agent systems for Interactive Design of Policy in Natural Resource Management by Rhodora Gonzalez, Anne Mottet, Sylvie Ladet, Claude Monteil, Michel Etienne, Annick Gibon
column10: Increased agricultural abandonment and fast-growing tree species are transforming the landscape of Villelongue, a village located in the peripheral area of the Pyrenees National Park (France). The consequences on the future of the local agro-pastoral systems, on biodiversity, on tourism, as well as the benefits that may be derived from this naturally occurring process are not yet well founded. GIS were initially created to help scientists and local policy makers bring forth images of the changed environment that they manage together. MAS modelling techniques in linking natural and social processes were further used to better understand how the changes came about and how they are inter-related. Using diagrams, scientists debated with the government's land planning officer about the aspects to consider in getting a better picture of the problem situation; it highlights the strengths and weaknesses of interdisciplinary efforts to inform natural resource management policy, Further directions are posed.

column9: Strain profile effects on the time rate of consolidation settlements by Mark H. Zarco
column10: Consolidation settlements are a major consideration in the design of structures founded on soft clays. At present, analytical procedures used to estimate the rate at which these settlements occur are limited to cases involving uniform strain conditions under constant surcharge. In this paper, two procedures that consider effects of non-uniform strain profile on the time rate of consolidation settlements due to a time-dependent load are presented. Numerical experiments performed indicate that the rates of consolidation settlement predicted by the modified methods are in better agreement with field experience when compared to current analytical methods.

column9: Effect of Moving Storm Rainfall on Soil Erosion and Sediment Transport from Watersheds by Guillermo Q. Tabios, III
column10: This paper presents results of a simulation study to study the effect of storm rainfall movement on soil erosion and sediment transport from watersheds. The moving storm rainfall fields are stochastically generated using a Neyman-Scott process, space-time rainfall model. This model is used to generate spatially - and temporally-varying storm rainfall fields at different speeds and directions. For the different rainfall fields generated, the amount of soil erosion from overland flow planes and amount of sediment transported in the channels are calculated using the KINEROS2 model of the Agricultural Research Service of the U.S. Department of Agriculture. The KINEROS2 model is an event oriented, physically-based model to simulate the various watershed processes especially interception, infiltration, surface runoff and erosion from overland flow planes and channels for a given storm rainfall event. Two different watershed shapes are considered : a del-shaped watershed and an elongated watershed. This study shows that there are differences in flow and sediment hydrographs at different speeds and directions of the storm rainfall movement for the two watershed shapes considered.

column9: Novel ways of enhancing the properties of natural materials by Alberto V. Amorsolo, Jr.
column10: Natural materials from plant, animal and ground sources have been useful to mankind since the dawn of civilization. However, the new generation of high technology products require materials with unusual combination of properties which become harder to find in natural materials. New and novel ways to overcome this limitation are therefore being devised to either significantly enhance the properties of these natural materials or transform them into hybrid structures with the desired combination of properties. These methods make use of the usual principle of changing the material property through a change in composition and/or structure (usually done at the microscale level). However, one of the breakthrough principles in creating a radically different set of materials from existing ones lies in reducing the physical dimensions of conventional materials to the nanoscale level. The development of a reliable technology for the creation of this new generation of nanomaterials appears to be major challenge for materials scientists and engineers of this century.
column11:
Enhancement
Nanomaterials
Natural materials
Material properties

column9: Regionalized Flood Frequency Distributions and Parameters for Philippine Rivers Using L-Moments by Leonardo Q. Liongson
column10: This paper presents the results of the application of the method of L-moment ratios to obtain the probability distribution of the annual maximum flood peaks in two water resources regions in the Philippines. The parameters of the annual maximum floods such as the mean annual flood, are expressed in terms of regression relationships with basin properties such as catchment area and other basin properties. The Generalized Extreme Value or GEV is selected as candidate model for both single-site and regional flood frequency analysis, by using the L-moment ratio diagram. The L-moment ratios are regionalized by weighted-averaging according to the length of records of stations, after observing their low correlation with drainage area. Regional growth curves, or the equation of the ratio (T-year flood/mean annual flood) versus the return period T, are afterwards derived by substituting the regional average L-moment ratios in the GEV distribution.

column9: Rhythm-aided, Discrete-word Filipino Speech Recognition by Rowena Cristina L. Guevara
column10: Filipino speech is syllable-timed. In this paper, syllable properties, rhythm and statistics were investigated. A subcorpus of three-syllable words from the Filipino speech Corpus with 6 speakers was used as experimental data. Several approaches to improving the performance of a Filipino speech recognition system using speech rhythm are described. These approaches will be used for designing dictionaries for decoding the phone posterior probabilities, and designing the post-processing stage of the decoder output in the next discrete-word Filipino speech recognition system of the Digital Signal Processing Laboratory.

Filipino speech corpus

column9: Theoretical and Practical Aspects of Infiltration Gallery Planning and Design by Peter M. Castro
column10: The range of yields that may be expected through wet and dry periods is initially estimated for the candidate site. Alternative layouts of gallery pipes are configured in accordance with this range of yields. A groundwater flow model is employed to obtain the expected production rate for each alternative. Promising configurations are selected for design of pipe layouts, sizes and pumping schemes. Site investigations should be comprehensive so as to avoid wasting time and resources on sites that would likely turn out infeasible. Gallery pipes in high yield sites should be designed to flow full, while in low yield sites the pipes would have free surface flow.

column9: Projecting electronic waste generation using an AI Artificial Neural Network Approach by Florencio C. Ballesteros, Jr., Recina Simon Professorial Chair
column10: The demand for new technology is unmatched at any time in human history. As people yearn for a better quality of life, industries are forced to churn out dizzying arrays of electronic goods to satisfy this demand. Consequently, consumer electronics are constantly being upgraded or scrapped as a result of technological advancement. Technology turnover has been so rapid that what are considered high-tech today are at the same time already on the verge of obsolescence. This situation has led to the generation of millions of tons of so-called electronic waste, more popularly known as e-waste. These products include household appliances, ICT and other business electronic equipment, lamps, and consumer and entertainment devices. The downside of the issue is that these materials contain hundreds of different materials that are toxic and hazardous thereby making recycling risky. Effective regulation requires an accurate estimate of the amount of e-waste generated in the country on an annual basis. This has not always been possible because of deficient recording mechanisms. This study aims to demonstrate a technique to estimate the generation rate of e-waste in the country using Artificial Neural Network (ANN). The independent variables from which the ANN learns the equation include among other factors population, GNP, household income level, educational attainment and power demand. ANN used 65% of the data while the remaining 35% was used to validate the model. The validated model was then used to project future e-waste generation.

column9: Preparation of potassium ferrate from sludge and its performance in arsenite removal 2 from water by Maria Lourdes P. Dalida, UP Engineering Fraulein Centennial Prof. Chair Award in Engineering
column10: An efficient and multifunctional chemical potassium ferrate (K2FeO4) has been successfully synthesized from drinking water treatment sludge (DWTS) based on a novel method via wet chemical route. The dried sludge was a viable starting material for ferrate synthesis due to its abundance in iron as confirmed by SEM/EDS. The ability of this hexavalent iron [Fe(VI)] solution in the removal of arsenite [As(III)] from water by oxidation and the subsequent arsenate [As(V)] coagulation by its reduction product was studied. Response surface methodology (RSM) particularly IV-Optimal statistical experimental design was used to investigate the effect of Fe (VI) dose and pH to percent As(III) removal. The response function coeffcieints were obtained from experimental data and it was shown that the predicted values and experimental results were in good agreement. The highest percent As (III) removal required a Fe (VI) dose of 100.04 uM and pH of 5.19. This research showed that IV-Optimal statistical design successfully determined the optimum major operating parameters for arsenic removal and it was concluded that the synthesized Fe (VI) is a promising chemical for the remediation of arsenic from solution by oxidation and regulation.

column9: Co-production of alternative fuels for the Philippines by Rizalinda L. de Leon, Semirara Professorial Chair
column10: A large fraction of the cost of both bioethanol and biodiesel production has been attributed to the cost of feedstock. Hence process improvements result in only marginal production cost reduction. More efficient utilization of feedstock by producing a second fuel can enable the recovery of feedstock cost. This study assesses the co-production of two alternative fuels from one feedstock. Two production processes are considered : (1) the co-production of biodiesel and bioethanol from copra and; (2) the cogeneration of bio-oil and bioethanol from sugar cane. A technology assessment is performed on each of the two production processes. This includes an assessment of the feedstock, the product market trends, technical and economic evaluation of the process, including an assessment of expected waste streams. The assessment of the feedstock involves an analysis of the alternative uses of feedstock and its supply. Product market trends are then inferred from historical consumption data of the conventional fuels that are to be substituted by the alternative fuels. This is followed by a technical evaluation of the process, particularly the calculation of mass and energy process inputs and outputs including waste streams. An economic assessment involves a total production cost estimation and profitability analysis.

column9: Degradation of reactive yellow 145 (RY 145) dye contaminated synthetic wastewater using electro-fenton based advanced oxidation processes by Mark Daniel G. De Luna, Enrique and Elena Ostrea UP Centennial Professorial Chair
column10: Conventional wastewater treatment technologies are unable to eliminate dye molecules from industrial effluents. This study demonstrates the effectiveness of electro-Fenton (EF) based AOPs to degrade Reactive Yellow 145 (RY 145) dye-containing synthetic wastewater. Effects of initial dye concentration, initial pH, H2O2 dosage, Fe2+ dosage, current density and process intensification by UV (4-UW UV lamps) and ultrasonic (20 kHz ultrasonic processor) irradiation on RY 145 removal by electro-Fenton process were investigated. A quartz cylindrical batch reactor with RuO2/IrO2-coated titanium plate electrodes was used. Maximum dye removals of 99%, 95% and 86% were obtained for sonoelectro-Fenton (SEF), EF and photoelectron-Fenton (PEF) respectively after 20 minutes with corresponding initial rates of 0.1731, 0.1448, and 0.1209 M mion-1 for 0.49 mM RY 145 dye-containing synthetic wastewater. Verification experiments of optimized parameters were in proximity with predicted values. For SEF, 99.8% RY 145 removal was achieved after 20 minutes at optimum conditions. Fast initial RY 145 degradation fit a pseudo 2nd order kinetic model. Small footprint, potential for complete degradation of target pollutant with minimum or no sludge production and short reaction time make electro-Fenton based AOPs a viable solution to the growing need for efficient treatment of dye wastewater.

column9: An innovative application of thermal storage for temperature regulation by Angela D. Escoto, HOLCIM Professorial Chair
column10: This paper discusses an innovative concept of utilizing a phase change material to regulate the temperature inside automobiles parked under the sun. Preliminary characterization of the phase change material will be done. A heat rejection system will be identified for continued operation while exposed to the heat source. Initial calculations will be presented.

column9: Generating new ideas by probing paradigms by Wilfredo I. Jose, Sy Ling Chat Professorial Chair
column10: Many techniques on creativity and innovation are available and they can generate new ideas. This paper presents a method, which I developed in the last four decades. In my first job I developed a technique in creativity, but I was unaware of the mechanism. I used the technique in my research when I transferred to UP as an instructor. Based on the term paradigm, I called my process, "probing paradigms". After 10 years, I found its application in formulating teaching strategies and developing innovations. I knew I had an effective method but I did not have the appropriate framework and theoretical basis. I applied the technique to researches in environment, biotechnology and biochemical engineering, energy, design engineering, and others. Starting in 2005, I developed the framework, and finally completed the necessary theoretical basis in 2009. Essentially, with this method, we probe the paradigm of a topic, an area of study, or a specific field. We systematically map them in our mind. Connecting ideas in our mind results in new ideas. We can consider our brain as a powerful neural network computer and by recycling the ideas, we can refine the selection of ideas. Some examples of the results are presented.

column9: Methane production and anaerobic treatment of sardines and mackerel wastewater in an expanded granular sludge bed (EGSB) reactor system by Analiza P. Rollon, UP KEM Global Dr. Luz Salonga Professorial Chair in Chemical Engineering
column10: As most municipalities in the Philippines are coastal, fish processing industry is economically important in the country. Many fish processing plants have to deal with problems associated with their wastewater and the need to comply with effluent standards. This study explored the possibility of treating fish processing wastewater using a high-rate system that produces energy in the form of methane gas. Fish processing wastewater contains high amounts of proteins and lipids, which are present as suspended solids. The results of this study showed that the hydrolysis of the particulate lipids is rate-limiting while that of proteins is relatively fast. For an influent wastewater containing about 4,000 mg/L total COD treated at 6 h hydraulic retention time (HR) and 16 kg/m-3 .d-1 COD loading rate in a one-phase expanded granular sludge bed (EGSB) reactor, 90% COD removal and 44% COD conversion to methane were achieved. In a two-phase EGSB reactor system operated at the same HRT and COD loading rate, 98% COD removal and 48% COD conversion to methane were achieved. A large part (46-50% of the influent COD was removed by adsorption to reactor surfaces and sludge particles. Thus, the hydrolysis of the particulate COD in the system must be optimized.

column7: Department of Computer Science
column9: Some issues in distributed computation in natural computing with membranres by Henry N. Adorna, Alexan Professorial Chair
column10: Membrane Computing is an unconventional computing paradigm introduced in the year 1998 by Gh. Paun. Associated with Membrane Computing is the computing model called P Systems. This computing model abstracts the functioning of a living cell, which could be single cell, or organized in tissues, organs or other types of cell population. In general, the internal compartmentalization which are hierarchically arranged plays a key role in the computation. This computing model performs computations in non-deterministic and (maximally) parallel manner. There are different variants of P Systems already introduced in the literature, since the year 1998. Surprisingly, the so-called hard problems in conventional computations, that is, via Turing machines is at most polynomially solvable (in time) at the expense of space by some appropriate P Systems. This model of computation attracts lots of attention from computing scientists. This paper considered a so-called distributed P Systems (dP Systems). dP Systems consists of at least two (2) P Systems communicating in order to perform a job. We discussed measures of complexity with respect to communication performed by the model in solving problem. Finally, a possibility for the computing model to be implemented in silico is proposed.

column9: Minimum total edge length and results on local search algorithms by Tristan M. Basa, Cerlito San Juan and Family Professorial Chair
column10: We consider a problem relation to optimal layout designs : Given a graph G (V, E), and a fixed set of points P1 [P] = [V], on a plane, how can we map V - P such that the resulting total edge length is minimal? This problem can be considered a special case of the general problem of TOTAL EDGE LENGTH wherein we minimize the total edge length for a general graph. We prove this special case to be NP-complete and apply well-know local search heuristics on the problem.

column9: Metaheuristics in flood disaster management and risk assessment by Vena Pearl Bongolan, Cesar A. Buenventura UP Centennial Professorial Chair
column10: A conceptual area is divided into land-use zones, each of which is allowed to evolve under a physical constraint. A risk assessment method is then used to identify the flood risk in each zone using the following risk factors : the area's urbanized area ratio, literacy rate, mortality rate, percent of population under poverty,. radio/tv penetration and the state of structural and non-structural measures. Vulnerability is defined as a weighted-sum of these factors. A 'penalty' is then imposed for reduced vulnerability. Optimization comparison are done with MatLab's Genetic Algorithms and Simulated Annealing; results show that simulated annealing finds 'extreme' solutions, while genetic algorithms produce more realistic designs for cities. In related research, we also explored prioritization schemes for the components of vulnerability, as well as experiments with allowing interactions between related, but independent components.

column9: Alternative scoring systems for the speech phoneme recognition as educational aid for the deaf and hearing impaired children (spread) application by Mario T. Carreon, MaritheGirbaud Professorial Chair
column10: The Speech and Phoneme Recognition as an Educational Aid for the Deaf and Hearing Impaired (SPREAD) application uses the Sphinx-4 voice recognition system to analyze the vocalization of the student and provide prompt feedback on their pronunciation. The packaging of the application as interactive game aims to provide additional motivation for the deaf and hearing impaired student through visual motivation for them to learn and appreciate speech. However, the scoring system of the application produced very low scores even on nearly accurate pronunciations. This paper will explore the reasons for this problem and will attempt to test alternative scoring systems in order to provide a more accurate assessment of the child's performance.

column9: A proposed database re-design for the University of the Philippines databases for open data access by Rommel P. Feria, Aber P. Canlas DJ Prof. Chair in Engineering
column10: Access to data is an integral part of any research activity. The University of the Philippines, as an academic institution, collected data, e.g. grades, class size, case studies. etc., that should be openly available, i.e., no permission needed, for researchers, both from within the university or otherwise. Due to the personal nature of some types of data, access is restricted and requires to be applied for and approved. There are studies on how to anonymize these data on the database layer, i.e., K-Anonymity, on established and large databases. For relatively small databases and/or new databases, this paper proposes a re-designing of the database schema with privacy and open access in mind. As an example, the paper uses a standard student records database schema and presents way to introduce open access whilst preserving the privacy of the student.

column9: Challenges and lessons in bridging the digital divide by Cedric Angelo M. Festin, Dado Maria and Banatao Institute Professorial Chair Award
column10: The Internet has been shown as an important tool for enhancing learning. The Philippines has not fully utilized this tool for varying reasons. Although Internet connectivity in the country has become more available, this is more true for urban areas. Access to affordable computers is also an issue. There is also the lack of a stable and affordable power supply for these computers. Moreover, the Internet is a relatively new medium that could be hard to navigate for those unfamiliar with it. We present our work that seeks to address two issues directly : access to affordable computers and assistance in navigating the Internet. Instead of using conventional desktop computers, we made use of thin clients. Aside from cost reduction, the use of thin clients also indirectly address the power issue. To address the second issue, we focus on the activity of using the Internet to augment formal learning, particularly for grade school Science and Mathematics education. With the assistance of the University of the Philippines NISMED, we have designed a framework that encompasses the classification and presentation of e-learning materials. We present the design and prototype of this framework and the challenges and lessons encountered in the process.

column9: Practical challenges in developing secure real-time streaming for mobile devices by Susan P. Festin, Jorge M. Consunji Professorial Chair in Engineering
column10:
Mobile devices have increasingly become more ubiquitous, We have also seen an increase in the types of applications that are being developed for these devices. These devices have transcended their original function as telephones and are now also used as small and portable computers. Securing mobile applications present the challenge of optimizing the still limited (compared to desktop computers) computational, storage and power on mobile devices. Thus, security primitives for encryption need to be as fast and as lean as possible. 
We present our recent work in developing a secure and real-time streaming application for two popular mobile platforms : iOS and Android. We focus on porting a new stream cipher, Rabbit, into these two platforms. Rabbit is designed to be lean and fast but since it is a new cipher, its use is still limited. Our experience has shownt that there are important challenges when developing secure applications for these platforms : openness and maturity of the platform APIs, availability of suitable codecs for audio and video, and access to lower level functions and/or hardware.

column9: Pursuit and evasion of cubic graphs by Nestine Hope S. Hernandez, MIESCOR/Cesar Nuguid Professorial Chair
column10: In this lecture, we discuss pursuit and evasion grame on cubic graphs. In a pursuit and evasion game, the pursuer tries to capture the evader while the evader tries to avoid capture. Cubic graphs are graphs in which all vertices have degree three, that is, exactly three edges are incident to a vertex. We consider the case where two pursuers attempt to capture the evader on a given cubic graph. All players have local visibility and pursuers can communicate with each other. We propose a winning strategy for the pursuers based on the positions of the players in the graph and the topology of their immediate neighborhood.

column9: Analysis of human-computer interaction attributes of geographic information systems by Ligaya Leah Lara-Figueroa, Mantaring-Gervasio Professorial Chair
column10: The purpose of this study was to investigate the current advances in human-computer interaction principles utilized by geographic information system applications. A survey of human-computer interaction concepts was conducted. Identification of attributes important for GIS applications was made. The analysis focused on identifying application components and features that make the experience of mapping easy and intuitive for the users.

column9: Folksonomy on collaborative use of rubrics on E-portfolio assessment system by Ma. Rowena C. Solamo, UPERDFI Professorial Chair
column10: As many people in the education sector are finding that standardized tests give little indication of what students can actually do in real world situations, they are looking at other means to assess learning of individuals. Performance-based assessment poses as an alternative which makes uses of rubrics as scoring or rating tool for products or performances. Projectrix, a cloud-based e-Portfolio Assessment System, is primarily built to collaboratively create and use rubrics as part of the learning assessment process where an online community defines links to work products and chooses people to assess them using rubrics. This research investigates the folksonomy, the resulting collective vocabulary with a focus on knowledge organization, within the collaborative use of rubrics as an alternative means to assess learning.

column9: On the controllability of the De St. Venant Equations by Adrian Roy L. Valdez, Beta Epsilon Professorial Chair
column10: The de Sr. Venant equations are first order quasilinear hyperbolic partial differential equations modeling the flow of fluid in one-dimension. They are good at capturing the dynamics of shallow water. A main problem being addressed in this paper is if, indeed, a flow can be steered from one state to another by putting controls on the boundaries of the flow. The paper surveys current results on the main problem by looking at which states already have proven existence of boundary controls. Moreover, some numerical implementations of such controls when they exist are presented. Finally, some open problems shall be discussed.

column9: On the bounds of unsecured RSA secret keys by Philip Christian C. Zuniga, Marlon Bacani Professorial Chair in Engineering
column10: We study in this research the bounds of RSA secret keys that make the RSA unsecured. Wiener was able to prove that if the magnitude of the secret key is less than 0.25, then the RSA is unsecured. It is conjectured that the actual bound is at 0.5. We extend in this paper Wiener's work, including the works of several other mathematicians to show a more extensive bound on unsecured keys, but in a special case where we assume that the factors of the RSA modulus have the same number of bits.

column7: Electrical and Electronics Engineering Institute
column9: Doodletalk : a collaborative application on a mobile device over existing XMPP servers by Rowel O. Atienza, PLDT Professorial Chair
column10: The need for Internet collaboration is fueled by businesses that do not have the time and resources to transport all the needed employees located around the world to work on a project or collaborate on an idea. Current implementations of Internet collaboration are video-conferencing, VoIP conferencing and text-based document conferencing, e.g. Google Docs. Together with the recent surge of people having mobile devices with touch screens and wireless Internet access such as smartphones, a new way of collaboration on mobile devices has become our goal. We propose an approach that uses a virtual whiteboard that all collaborators can see and modify in realtime. It provides an intuitive communication medium, uses less badnwidth than video or voice conferencing and enables its users to easily communicate ideas such as design, strategy or plan. To detect the presence of other peers in the Internet we use existing XMPP servers, e,g, GoogleTalk and Facebook. It minimizes the chance of a user having to sign up and setup their contacts, increases the reachability of the user and does not require us to maintain a separate server.

column9: Area and performance trade-off in a viterbi error correction and detection hardware by Anastacia Ballesil-Alvarez, Jose Segovia Professorial Chair
column10: The emergence of high-speed networks for exchange, processing and storage of digital information has continuously increased the demand for efficient and reliable data transmission and storage systems. Errors in these systems cannot be avoided, and as such, error detection and correction schemes are typically employed. One of the most common of these schemes is the Viterbi algorithm. In this paper, trade-off between area and performance of different Viterbi decoder and encoder hardware is presented and analyzed.

column9: Waveguide filters by Paul Jason R. Co, Vea Technology for All Centennial Professorial Chair Award
column10: The increasing demand for broadband wireless access would translate into a huge market for microwave circuits and systems that are an integral part of these wireless communication systems. These microwave circuits are the building blocks of the backhaul systems of these broadband systems, as well as integral parts of other wireless systems such as radars and satellite communications. One of the most important microwave blocks in these communication systems is the filter. The filter is a device that is used to remove undesired signals, which would otherwise degrade the performance of our system. This research deals with the design, fabrication and characterization of filters that are operating in the X-band (8-12GHz). Operating at these microwave frequencies require specialized techniques to be applied in the design and implementation of these filters. Two waveguide filter topologies were designed and fabricated; namely the post waveguide filter and the iris waveguide filter. The designs were fabricated locally through a local metal manufacturing company and were tested in the Wireless Communications Engineering Laboratory. The fabricated fileters showed satisfactory results; with bandwidths of 3% and return losses of at least 15dB.

column9: A power supply aggregation model of small electric cooperatives in the Philippine restructured power industry by Rowaldo D. Del Mundo, Benguet Management Corporation Professorial Chair
column10: The Electric Power Industry Reform Act of 2001 (R.A. 9136) also known as the EPIRA law has restructured the Philippine power industry into functionality unbundled units. The EPIRA law mandates the establishment of a competitive generation and supply sectors and to strengthen the regulation of franchised transmission and distribution utilities in order to ensure the security of power supply and to reduce the power cost to consumers. After ten years of implementation, there is no sign that the objectives of law will be achieved. This study has analyzed the behavior of the generation companies and distribution utilities in the Philippines in bilateral power supply contracting and their participation in the wholesale spot market to determine the reasons why the energy security objective of the law was not achieved. A new model of power supply aggregation of small distribution utilities in a restructured power industry was developed to solve the power supply security problem which will also achieve the least-cost objectives of the EPIRA law. The aggregation model is implemented in 21 Electric Cooperatives in Mindanao Grid.

column9: Multilevel extension of parallel sequence spread spectrum modulation scheme for high-speed rural point-to-point wireless backhaul by Joel Joseph S. Marciano, Dado and Maria Banatao Institute Centennial Professorial Chair Award
column10: A technique utilizing a multilevel extension of Parallel Sequence Spread Spectrum (PSSS) for high spectral efficiency communications is presented. It is capable of achieving spectral efficiencies of up to 12 bits/s/Hz; hence a data rate of 1 Gbps over a single carrier is possible by using the whole 84.5 MHz bandwidth of the 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi band. It is intended for line-of-sight, rural backhaul applications where heavy multipath is not a primary consideration. We show the results of simulation and the subsequent implementation of the technique in actual hardware using a flexible Software-Defined Radio platform. The simulated and actual measurement results show a peak-to-average power ratio of 7.66 dB and a bit error rate (BER) performance close to that of 4096-QAM in an AWGN channel. Unlike QAM, however, the use of multi-level PSSS allows the bit rate to be readily scaled down in situations where the BER is too high. Established links can also be easily extended to point-to-multipoint without a significant increase in complexity.

column9: Risk assessment of supplier bidding strategies in electricity markets by Allan C. Nerves, NAPOCOR Professorial Chair
column10: A methodology is developed to quantify the financial risk for a multi-unit generation company with bilateral contracts in a competitive electricity market, using Value-at-Risk as a risk measure. A Monte Carlo simulation technique is used to calculate the Value-at-Risk by modeling different pricing scenarios, bids and schedules of generation. An artificial neural network is used to forecast hourly market prices which determine generating unit schedules through a price-based unit commitment method using a genetic algorithm approach.
