An economic dispatch then determines local generation and purchase schedules using a linear programming solution. The profit and loss distribution that is obtained from the Monte Carlo simulations becomes the basis for Value-at-Risk calculations. Bidding strategies are formulated from a sensitivity analysis of the Value-at-Risk for various market conditions.

column9: Solar charge controller for group PCS by Manuel C. Ramos Jr. Dado and Maria Banatao Institute Professorial Chair Award
column10: The use of solar power in rural areas is getting increasingly common nowadays. With a simple solar power system basically consisting of solar panels, batteries, and the load, it is relatively easy to setup and use solar energy to power lights, computers, and other electrically powered equipment. However, simple solar power systems are usually inefficient, i.e., they do not make full use of available solar energy. In some cases, lifetime of batteries are compromised making such systems economically inefficient. To address the shortcomings of simple solar power systems, we propose a smart solar charge controller to manage the system. Our system is designed to power personal computers and associated peripheral with the end goal of providing a completely solar powered networked classroom. Among the novel features of the smart solar charge controller are battery management with SOC tracking, high voltage DC bus, integrated DC bus communications and USB interface to the client computers.

column9: An integrated development environment for dual-core ARM7 architecture by Joy Alinda Reyes-Madamba, Manuel Lopez Professorial Chair
column10: Embedded systems today are targeting high performance computing with low power consumption to satisfy the growing needs of users. The latest technique found in these designs use multi-core technology on embedded processors, such as the ARM7. To fully utilize the capability of dual-core ARM7 processors, software tools such as compilers and assemblers are designed specifically to transform high-level language applications to run efficiently in the target processors. These tools are also used to help ARM7 designers in formulating new techniques to further improve the performance of their processors. In this research, an integrated development environment (IDE) uses a compiler-assembler for the dual-core ARM7 architecture for runtime memory simulations. It allows selective optimization to aid a processor designer in determining high-level algorithms that would take advantage of the dual-core ARM7 architecture

column9: Entrepreneurship and engineering education technology by Luis G. Sison, Globe Telecom Inc. Professorial Chair
column10: Entrepreneurship is widely acknowledged to be one of the major engines for growth and innovation. With companies like Apple and Facebook in the limelight, policy makers are trying to replicate the success of Silicon Valley but tend to focus more on the industrial policy side of development while ignoring the role of the technopreneurial culture. Despite UP Diliman's leadership in R&D (measured in terms of publications, personnel, and funding), many of our graduates, even in engineering, typically prefer to enter the profession through established technology companies, especially multinational corporations, leading to a discontinuity of technology transfer from the lab to industry. To encourage more students to be entrepreneurs, the campus has initiated different forms of support, including : participation in business plan competitions technology boot camps, technopreneurship classes, student events and contests such as the Engineering Marketing Congress; the campus technology business incubators : UP-Ayala TBI, Techportal at the UP-Ayala TechnoHub, and Open TBI, and the pre-incubation program at the DOST-UP Enterprise Center for Technopreneurship. In this work, we look at the impact of these initiatives and related factors, focusing on the startups in the TBI's and pre-incubation centers on campus.

column9: System for classroom scheduling by Tyrone W. Tai, Don Felipe Say and Therese Chua Say Professorial Chair
column10: Classroom scheduling is one of the more tedious tasks at the start of the semester. This involves arranging classes, room usage, faculty loads and possibly student hours subject to constraints and limitations. Poor scheduling often lead to load imbalance and class conflict/unavailability for students. As the department/institute expands its scope and capacity, the more complicated the task becomes. Studies have shown that a deterministic method for arriving at an optimal solution is not feasible due to the large number of parameters. This system aims to assist class schedulers by interpreting and presenting relevant information in a timely manner. Inputs to the system include room information, course information, faculty information, and student course demands. Features include room/faculty conflict notification, detection of common availability, as well as current faculty load. Schedule will be tested by simulating.

column9: Detection of millimeter movements using ultrasonic ranging and precise time synchronization in wireless sensor networks by Marc Caesar R. Talampas, Meralco Professorial Chair
column10: High accuracy movement detection is useful in many applications. One possible application is for landslide monitoring and prediction. Small movements in the millimeter range on a slope's surface are known to occur before an impending landslide. Observation of these millimeter movements at the soil surface is crucial for proper characterization of a landslide event. In this work, we developed a wireless sensor network platform that detects two-dimensional movements with sub-millimeter accuracy in a controlled environment. This was achieved through the use of omni-directional ultrasonic ranging, cross-correlation of ultrasonic pulses, and precise time synchronization between sensor nodes. The synchronization protocol is based on IEEE 1588, which was optimized for implementation in a wireless sensor network. Our experiment results show that the sensor node clocks can maintain worst-case synchronization error of at most one microsecond relative to the reference clock. This enables the system to detect movements with sub-millimeter accuracy, with initial distances of up to two meters between sensor nodes.

column7: Department of Geodetic Engineering
column9: Characterization of submarine groundwater discharge dynamics using radon monitoring and electrical resistivity tomography by Ariel C. Blanco, PHINMA Professorial Chair/Research Grant
column10: Significant amounts of nutrients can be delivered to the coastal waters through submarine groundwater discharge (SGD), threatening coral reefs. The dynamics of SGD must be well characterized to be able to adequately quantify loading of nutrients and contaminants. For this purpose, radon (222Rn) monitoring and electrical resistivity tomography (ERT) were utilized in Bolinao, Pangasinan and in Puerto Galera, Oriental Mindoro. In Puerto Galera Bay, radon inventory was monitored in 2 coves to estimate SGD rates. Measurements at a distance of about 100 meters from the shore yielded an average Rn concentration of 19 Bq/m3, with maximum of 39 Bq/m3 and minimum of 5 Bq/m3 during low tide and high tide, respectively. Based on one-day continuous radon measurement at two coves of the bay, groundwater flux in the range of 0.5-2.0 cm/hour. In Bolinao, time-lapse shore-parallel and shore-perpendicular ERT transect surveys were conducted to identify seepage zones. Continuous radon monitoring was simultaneously made in order to estimate SGD rates. These measurements were then compared against each other to examine the possibility of estimating groundwater flux from ERT measurements alone. Shore-parallel ER profiles showed the occurence of groundwater seepage at several areas during low tide only. On the other hand, the shore-perpendicular ER profiles showed that SGD mainly occurs at the beach-near shore reef interface. Increases in radon inventories corresponded well with higher increases in ER values.

column9: Land valuation using spatial-based support system by Florence A. Galeon, SR Metals Inc. Professorial Chair
column10: Professional valuers until now perform land valuation based on their intuition and experiences. A technique of valuing land more logically and quantitatively can be introduced to reduce the subjectivity. This paper envisions the development of a spatial-based support system to enhance and hasten the ferreting out of land values using the Direct Market Comparison Approach. A mathematical model developed using Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) and Goal Programming (GP) was utilized for calculation. Land value factors from literature and surveys of valuation specialists determine the significant parameters of the model. Flood Risk is added as one of the major parameters for this study. Spatial data are gathered and incorporated in a Geographic Information System (GIS) platform. This valuation method is tested to determine the land values of the informal settlement areas within the University of the Philippines Visayas Cebu Campus (UPVCC). The 13 hectares informal settlement areas are digitized from a high resolution QuickBird satellite image. Analysis tools in GIS are used to approximate the shapes of land parcels from Thiessen polygons. Initial samples gathered for validation show promising results, that at 95% CI, there is no significant difference between the system-generated values and the expert-determined values.

column9: Potential impact of tectonic motion on PRS92-Linked Cadastral Surveys by Epifanio D. Lopez, Felipe F. Cruz Professorial Chair in Geodetic Engineering
column10: The Philippine Archipelago, also known as the Philippine Mobile Belt (PMB) in the field of geology, is subjected to complex tectonic plate deformation processes along its western and eastern boundary zones. In the east, the Philippine Sea Plate subducts the PMB in an oblique west-northwesterly direction; while in the west, the Eurasia-Sundaland crustal block subducts the PMB in an easterly direction. Extensive collaborative plate convergence researches using Global Positioning Systems, such as the Geodynamics of South and Southeast Asia (GEODYSSEA) Project, have established that these crustal plate boundary convergence processes have been absorbed by the Philippine Trench/East Luzon Trench and the Manila Trench, respectively. Furthermore, the subduction processes have resulted in accumulated strain and movement along the left-lateral strike-slip Philippine Fault Zone (PFZ), extending for about 1200 km from northwest Luzon to eastern Mindanao. An analysis of the potential impact of the PFZ slip rate of ~2.5 cm/year (Aurelio et al, 1997; Barrier et al., 1991; Duquesnoy et al., 1994) on positioning accuracies in the Philippine Reference System of 1992 (PRS92) and PRS92-linked cadastral survey projects located along, or in the immediate vicinity of, the PFZ is the major objective of this study. Preliminary results of the effect of tectonic motion on the proposed PRS92 upgrade to zero-order accuracy (1:1,000,000) and densification within the International Terrestrial Reference Framework (ITRF) are also presented.

column9: Reducing heterogeneity in environmental and natural resources (ENR) data layers - a case study of Philippines' ENR data layers by Oliver T. Macapinlac, Alfredo L. Juinio Professorial Chair
column10: Science and engineering projects and researches rely on scientific data collected from different sources and context. In order to successfully utilize these data, investigators need to effectively access, decode, organize and interpret existing data to their process and methodology. The seriousness of these steps in data handling has varying levels of risk to the result of the process and thus to the community. Errors in data use and interpretation can be rooted from syntactic and semantic heterogeneity in data from different sources. The study focuses on reviewing the different data layers created and published by different national government agencies producing ENR datasets. In order to integrate these data layers, heterogeneity problems should be addressed. Two main tasks were performed to reduce complexities of these datasets. First, syntactic and structural similarities of geographic data were characterized to improve integration. Secondly, semantic of the data using ontologies were built by inferring relations between classes of data. Formal modeling languages such as UML will be used to document the result of the study. Geographical information from the Department of Agriculture (DA), Department of Environment and Natural Resources (DENR), and the National Economic Development Authority (NEDA) were used to demonstrate the approach.

column9: Shallow water hydrographic charting using high-resolution satellite imagery by Enrico C. Paringit, Prof. Jose Jovellana Professorial Chair in Engineering
column10: This paper introduces the needs, methods and applications of high-resolution satellite remote sensing in mapping coastal and near shore marine resources. Analysis of coastal environment using optical imaging sensors presents a very interesting challenge and intriguing paradox. To be able to perform coastal habitat mapping, one must first overcome the obfuscating effects of the water layer. Variable depth and the presence of optically-active constituents in the intervening water introduce errors in benthic cover classification. On the other hand, methods to estimate bathymetry from optical imagery is met with complications posed by the variety of benthic cover present in the coastal bottom and the non-uniform distribution of water quality. Furthermore, estimation of water quality parameters, though quiet established for the deep oceans, is not immediately applicable to shallow coastal zones due to the presence of river outfall sediments and visibility of bottom. Therefore, a three-pronged approach must be pursued to come up a simultaneous solution to bottom cover composition, depth and water quality retrieval from a single satellite imagery. Previously, such attempts were not possible due to limited number of bands. With the availability of WorldView-2 sensor, there is bright prospect to develop, apply and operationalize such technique. The main objective of this study is to develop a method to simultaneously retrieve bottom benthic cover, depth and water quality from tropical coastal reef using 8-band Worldview-2 data. The proposed method is a combination of spectral mixture model and two-stream radiative transfer model that estimates fractional cover and depth simultaneously. We introduce additional components for estimating the total suspended sediments and chlorophyll and quite possibly yellow matter (detritus). Field surveys were carried to obtain field benthic cover data, field bottom spectra and precise shallow-water bathymetry. The study area is in Bolinao, Pangasinan, Luzon, Philippines where a variety of corals and abundant seagrass meadows are present. This study area is also an active coastal fisheries zone making it an interesting site for doing studies. Results indicate relatively better performance of Worldview-2 image over Quickbird in classifying different benthic habitats. However, they have insignificant difference in depth estimation except horizontal resolution. Comparison of Worldview 2 images classification and bathymetry estimates in also reveal consistency in results. Further, we tested he efficacy of the additional in estimation optically-active water constituents.

column7: Institute of Civil Engineering
column9: Governance systems in infrastructure projects by Alexis A. Acacio, Cristy R. Hernandez Professorial Chair in Engineering
column10: Most infrastructure projects are executed with only the engineering part in mind. Due to this, certain lapses and problems arise due to a lack of clear cut governance mechanisms in the project. These mechanisms are discussed in this paper. In describing the governance process, the entire project life cycle is used as the baseline of analysis- taking into consideration the various stages of : Project concept; Project design; Project construction and; Project turn-over. The pitfalls of poor governance mechanisms are also discussed in this paper.

column9: Analysis of wave loading on protection works for a coastal mangrove planting zone by Eric C. Cruz, Semirara Professional Chair in Hydraulics
column10: Fully-grown mangroves provide natural protection to the coasts by acting as energy dissipation zones of waves that would otherwise approach the coast with full force. By trapping littoral sediments via their unique root system, they also effectively stabilize the shoreline. Results from various studies indicate, however. that mangroves in the country have decreased at an average rate of 4.7 hectares annually, thus exacerbating the vulnerability of the coasts particularly in the face of natural disasters and climate change. A community-engaged movement has embarked on a program to rehabilitate mangrove areas in the country and to plant mangrove saplings in new pilot areas. Due to the low success rate of earlier rehabilitation programs where the saplings are immediately exposed to the wave environment, it was recognized that protection works must be in place prior to transplanting. To increase the success rate of the program, it is necessary to provide temporary protection to young mangrove saplings. Once pilot planting zones are determined according to bio-physical criteria, the need for protection against waves is assessed from engineering analysis of the storm-induced wave climate at the site. This study focuses on the application of numerical simulations to determine the wave loading on a pilot mangrove planting zone, the development of design criteria and subsequent design of protection works to ensure a high survival rate of mangrove saplings.

column9: Greening of Philippine Concretes : Use of Mineral Admixtures by Nathaniel B. Diola, Jose Ma. Diego de Castro Professorial Chair
column10: Climate change mitigation and adaptation has become a driving force for industries to go green. The production of ordinary Portland cement (OPC), specifically the clinkering process, is known to contribute to carbon dioxide emissions. The use of mineral admixtures as partial replacement to OPC is one strategy adopted worldwide to lessen the requirement for OPC clinker. In the Philippines, a dramatic shift to the use of blended cements resulted after a Philippine National Standard for blended cement was adopted. This paper presents a review of the various mineral admixtures used in blended cements. It also discusses issues associated with the use of blended cements among which is the performance of blended cement compared to ordinary Portland cement. Type I ordinary Portland cement and two local brands of Type IP blended cements were used to create mortar samples, which were cured and tested. Durability parameters such as porosity, permeability and diffusivity were determined experimentally to characterize each type of cement. These tests were performed to aid in the selection of binders for durable concrete.

column9: A simple technique for considering the vintage of a building in analytically deriving fragility curves of archetype building frame models by Jaime Y. Hernandez, Jr., William Liu Professorial Chair
column10: Building fragility curves show the probability of exceedance of a particular damage state versus the ground motion intensity (in terms of PGA or MMI) due to an earthquake. However, the design standard for a specific building archetype changed several times as understanding of the behaviour of structures subjected to earthquakes improved. Thus, there exist in our building stock, buildings of the same archetype but have different "vintage" normally based on the structural code used during the time of construction. This paper presents a simple technique for considering the effect of the vintage of a building in analytically deriving fragility curves of archetype building frame models used for loss estimation in disaster risk management.

column9: Hydrological modeling studies for proposed flood-control dams in the Marikina River Basin by Leonardo Q. Liongson, Team Energy Professorial Chair
column10: Tropical Storm Ondoy (Ketsana) crossed Metro Manila and the adjacent river basins in a late wet-season episode of 2009, starting in the evening of September 25, 2009 and continuing into the next day of September 26, 2009. TS Ondoy brought very intense and heavy rainfall to the region - record amounts of rains fell over a very short time period of 12 hours to 24 hours, which are estimated to occur at an average annual frequency of 1 in 100 years or even higher, depending on the measuring location in the region. The rains generated record-magnitude flood flows and inundation in the Pasig-Marikina River Basin in Metro Manila, and the Laguna de Bay region. This paper describes the application of the distributed watershed rainfall-runoff model SWATCH in order to simulate the TS Ondoy floods and other floods of lower return periods (2 years to 100 years) in the upper Marikina River Basin, using as input the spatially-interpolated rainfall network data and available topographic, soil/geology and river-geometry data. The SWATCH model consists of a tree-network of several variable rectangular surface/soil/aquifer storage nodes and overland/channel flow links, with accounting of soil moisture, evapo-transpiration, infiltration and recharge to groundwater, and routing of overland and channel flows, interflow and baseflow into aggregated total streamflows. Using the model-computed inflow flood hydrographs, the options of single or cascade of flood-control storage dams (with selected reservoir-storage and spillway capacities) for attenuation of floods peaks and volumes, and downstream flood hazard reduction are initially evaluated by flood-routing studies.

column9: Qualitative and quantitative attributes of risk factors in disaster risk management by Benito M. Pacheco, Dr. Magdaleno B. Albarracin Jr. Centennial Professorial Chair
column10: Disaster risk management, including disaster risk reduction, being multi-disciplinary has borrowed concepts, terminologies and practices from many fields including insurance and financial risk management, community development, emergency management, medicine and engineering. This paper examines the evolution of Hazard, Exposure and Vulnerability as three generic risk factors that conveniently capture the major aspects of natural disaster risks including earthquake, windstorm, flood, landslide, and the like. In the analysis, both the qualitative and quantitative attributes of each risk factor are examined. The use of these risk factors is illustrated with the author's proposed RISK MAPR2 framework of action in DRM.

column9: Survival versus sustainability : challenges to environmentally sustainable transport by Hilario Sean O. Palmiano, Transport Training Center Professorial Chair
column10: Transportation in developing countries such as the Philippines is still largely characterized by undesirable practices and systems that are considered unsustainable. The use and proliferation of two-stroke motorcycles and tricycles, use of inefficient transportation modes, the paradigm of vicious cycle in road construction, high rate of road crashes, aging and smoke-belching vehicles, and high private car usage are among the problems. This study will try to investigate the inertia that makes it difficult to adopt EST amidst the largely accepted benefits of EST in many local government units (LGUs), even with the political support of national agencies. It hypothesizes that survival is still the predominant force which resists the adoption of EST.

column9: Numerical model to predict time to initiate concrete cover cracking due to corrosion by Norbert S. Que, Filemon Rodriguez Professorial Chair
column10: Corrosion of RC structures in marine environment presents an economic, not to mention structural, problem as repair and rehabilitation of corroded structures draw significant portion of the budget allotted for infrastructures. Once cracks start to form and propagate due to chloride ingress, the useful life of the structure is significantly diminished and expensive repair and rehabilitation is the only recourse to extend the service life of the structure. The ability to predict the time to initiate corrosion and the time to initiate cracking of the RC cover can provide useful information that allows one to put in place less expensive maintenance procedures to extend the service life of the structure. In this paper, a numerical model to predict the time to initiate concrete cover cracking is developed. Results of the model were compared to published experimental data and excellent agreements were recorded.

column9: Traffic congestion in Metro Manila : is the UVVRP still effective? by Jose Regin F. Regidor, Pozzolanic Philippines Inc. Professorial Chair
column10: The Unified Vehicular Volume Reduction Program (UVVRP), more popularly known as the "number coding" scheme was originally developed as a short-term travel demand management (TDM) measure in the light of on-going construction of transportation infrastructure projects in the 1990's. Although the scheme was not meant to be a long-term policy, the UVVRP is at present still implemented along major roads in Metro Manila albeit with some modifications like the mid-day window. With the completion of those major infrastructure projects, various transportation stakeholders, particularly those who are significantly disrupted by the UVVRP are critical of its continuous implementation, especially now that the authorities are leaning towards imposing the scheme on public transportation. A study was conducted in 2004 to evaluate the UVVRP. Several scenarios were developed to evaluate traffic performance for variations to the schemes. The conclusion at the time was that UVVRP was effective enough to address traffic congestion. With the present experience of traffic congestion along major roads throughout the day there is a clamor for revisiting the UVVRP and to determine if it is still effective in curbing congestion. This paper examines the UVVRP and presents the way forward for addressing congestion in Metro Manila.

column9: Relating the air permeability of mayon sand with its soil-water retention curve : measurements and modeling by Augustus C. Resurreccion, Edgardo S. Pacheco Professorial Chair in Engineering
column10: Air permeability (ka) and soil-water retention curve are two important parameters that are needed when simulating the movement of volatile organic chemicals in the subsurface. In this study, we measured the air permeability and soil-water retention curve of sand obtained within the vicinity of Mayon Volcano in the Philippines, across a wide range of matric potentials and soil-water contents. Samples were prepared (1) by adding a pre-determined amount of water to sand, left to equilibrate in a sealed bag, and repacked at a bulk density of 1.5 g/cm3 for air permeability; or (2) by saturating repacked 1.5 g/cm3 sands and draining to a desired matric potential in a sandbox or a pressure plate apparatus for the soil-water retention curve. In addition, independent data sets of air permeability where obtained on 1.5 g/cm3 sand samples drained to different matric potentials. The variation of ka with soil-air content showed an increase in ka during the drainage of the bigger pores (effective diameter > 0.33 mm) while no further significant increase in air permeability when effective pore size of less than 0.03 mm has been drained. A power law ka model was fitted into the ka data for > - 100 cm H2O (pF 2 where pF = log (- in cm H2O) while a constant value described the ka in the dry region where < - 100 cm H2O. A ka (pF) model was developed by combining the developed ka model with the van Genuchten soil-water retention model and tested on the independent ka(pF) data sets/. The developed model reasonably captured the behavior of the air permeability as a function of matric potential.

column9: Towards a safer intersection design by Ricardo G. Sigua, Prof. Emeritus Norbert S. Vila Professorial Chair
column10: Intersections play a major role in the road transportation system of any town or city. Good design and proper application of traffic control devices to strengthen the design concept used are vital to successful operation of intersections. The paper revisits and presents a single fundamental design concept : Guide all road users on the appropriate path and action they should take while approaching and/or negotiating an intersection. This basic concept, the author believes, would help our road designers and traffic managers in putitng order to many problematic intersections in Metro Manila and other towns and cities in the country.

column9: Need for climate change studies and parameters prior to climate change adaptation efforts in the Philippines by Guillermo Q. Tabios III, Dean Marino Mena Professorial Chair in Engineering
column10: This paper discusses and advocates the need for climate change studies and parameters in order to develop climate change adaptation efforts in the Philippines. There are 6 global climate change scenarios entertained by IPCC. This requires deciding what global climate change scenarios are appropriate for our country which should be a national effort. Once the climate change scenarios are selected, then proper guidelines and research on what GCM Model (result) to adopt since are over 20 or so GCM's currently available. Also, since GCM's are ran at spatial resolutions of 150-300 km by 150-300 km and are unable to resolve important sub-grid scale features such as clouds and topography, then there is the need to downscale GCM results to regional/local climate characteristics. With properly downscaled climate change variables, these should be converted into appropriate planning, design or management parameters useful in developing climate change adaptation measures. Unfortunately, there are no studies done as a national effort to develop policies and management strategies to address flood risks, drought risk, crop yield or hydropower generation under climate change. This paper also not only discusses uncertainties in climate change process itself but also natural climate variabilities and uncertainties in ecosystem and socio-economic-political changes.

column9: Using coco-peat as sorbent materials in removing heavy metals from aqueous solutions by Maria Antonia N. Tanchuling, Ciriaco Professorial Chair
column10: Many wastewaters especially those coming from industrial activities contain dissolved heavy metals such as lead, copper, cadmium, and arsenic. Various methods are being employed to remove the dissolved ions from the solution. One of the treatment processes used is adsorption where the wastewater is made to pass through a sorbent material for removal to take place. This study investigates the efficacy of the coco-peat as a sorbent material, both in its fresh state and as activated carbon. This study determines the capacity of the coco peat to remove Cu, Pb, Cd, and As ions from aqueous solution. Coco peat was activated employing chemical activation, using ZnCl2 as chemical activating agent. Activated carbon from coco peat was found to have a high BET surface area of 2,121 m2/g and pore volume of 1.398cm3/g. The products were also characterized by using thermo gravimetric (TG), Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), and Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra. Through batch experiments, the effects of various parameters such as contact time, initial metal concentrations, pH solution, adsorbent dosage, and temperature on the removal efficiencies were investigated. Coco peat was found to have high removal efficiency for Cu2+ and Pb2+.

column9: Yielding and collapse of buildings subjected to strong ground motions by Eric Augustus J. Tingatinga, Roger Murga Professorial Chair
column10: This study investigates nonlinear effects of gravity on the response of buildings subjected to strong earthquake-induced ground motions. Simple analytical building models are used to study the changes in the natural frequency, steady-state response, yielding and collapse. Governing equations are developed to take into account large displacements and numerical time-stepping methods are proposed to solve the equation. The preliminary study which emphasizes on elastic structures exhibiting geometric nonlinearity revealed that gravity decreases the natural frequency of the building and large displacement formulations showed that the natural frequency as well as response depends on ground motion amplitude. For the specific case of the shear-flexural building model used, for example, it was reported that the large amplitude response contains sub-harmonic components and damping decreases the higher mode contribution on the response. While the previous study is very important, the study of inelastic behavior is inevitable. To investigate yielding and collapse of buildings, an elastoplastic restoring force-displacement relation was used to emulate material nonlinearity. Numerical examples revealed that the decrease in restoring forces as a consequence of yielding causes the weight to become the dominant force in causing buildings to collapse.

column9: Estimation of emissions and fuel consumption of sustainable transport measures in Metro Manila by Karl B. N. Vergel, Maynilad Professorial Chair
column10: Environmentally sustainable transport (EST) strategy measures for Metropolitan Manila are assessed in terms of reduction in local emissions, greenhouse gas emissions and energy demand. Measures include vehicle inspection, engine replacement, use of alternative fuels an biofuels, non-motorized transport, travel demand management and improvement of public transportation. Travel activity in terms of traffic volume and travel speeds of the travel demand forecast model estimates and the database of the 1996 Metro Manila Urban Transportation Integration Study (MMUTIS), emission factors from the Vehicular Emission Control Planning (VECP) project and fuel consumption factors from MMUTIS and recent studies on jeepneys and buses are utilized to generate the baseline emissions of criteria pollutants (HC, CO, NO2, PM and SO2), carbon dioxide emissions and energy demand (diesel, gasoline, alternative fuel/biofuel) in 2010 and 2015. Emissions and energy demand are estimated for each strategy measure and compared with respect to the baseline values. Measures such as implementation of vehicle inspection, public transport improvement, travel demand management and switch to 4-stroke tricycles contributed to higher overall emission reductions.

column9: Community-based methods for landslide risk reduction : state of the practice by Mark Albert H. Zarco, Semirara Mining Corporation Professorial Chair
column10: Despite the availability of hazard maps, engineering intervention methods, and early warning systems; the socio-economic costs due landslides continue to increase every year in the Philippines. Very often, existing hazard maps are either inaccurate, outdated, or are too technical to be understood and used in a meaningful manner by members of the local community living in the affected area. In other cases, early warning systems are prone to pilferage and vandalism. Existing engineering measures are often costly and unaffordable by most communities. This paper highlights the role of community based methods for landslide disaster risk reduction. The main advantage of community-based methods is their abiilty to empower communities by developing their capacity to recognize, document and monitor potential hazard zones within their communities. Making communities develop their own risk reduction strategies gives them a sense of ownership which is essential in minimizing the loss of life, limb and property due to natural disasters. Furthermore, the resulting outputs are easier to understand, and can be used in a more meaningful way for disaster risk reduction purposes. Various community based strategies for identifying, assessing and monitoring landslide risk are discussed. Examples of the application of community-based methods for landslide risk reduction here and abroad are discussed, as well as the issues that arise when such methods are implemented in local communities.

column7: Department of Industrial Engineering & Operations Research
column9: A design of an integrated computer-based information system for the University of the Philippines Diliman by Edgardo G. Atanacio, Jesus Ferrer Professorial Chair
column10: The design and implementation of information systems in UP Diliman has always been done piece-meal and without systematic planning. This paper suggests an over-all design for an integrated, comprehensive computer-based information system for the whole of UP Diliman and proposes an organization for its information-related functions. The over-all system is broken up into modules such as an Academic Information Subsystem, Accounting Subsystem, etc. that can be implemented as the need becomes critical and when development resources can be made available.

column9: The UP College of Engineering's Role in Public Service by Aura C. Matias, UP Engineering Class of 1956
column10: Countries that achieve rapid development are those that adopted policies to promote science; technology and innovation. In the light of the commitment of the Aquino Administration to harness the full potential of S&T capabilities of our country, the UP College of Engineering (COE) is called upon to contribute its talent and resources towards rapid economic growth. The vision, mission and goals of the UP COE are presented to highlight its role in providing knowledge, progress and growth in the interest of public service.

column9: The four rationales of technology transfer : identifying technology transfer risks / Iris Ann G. Martinez, Analog Devices Professorial Chair
column10: Up until now, most technology transfer activities have been carried out on trial-and-error basis. However, recent studies have emerged to provide scientifically-based guides for technology transfer in recognition of the fact that technology transfer is important for global advancement. Along with these studies, this research aims to present a systematic methodology, called the Four Rationales of Technology Transfer, for identifying the risks of technology transfer. This methodology is derived from an empirical study conducted on 50 recently-implemented technology transfer projects. With the proposal of the methodology of Four Rationales of Technology Transfer and the presentation of the results of the empirical study, it is the hope of this research that future activities of technology transfer will become more successful.

column9: Triz-driven sustainable innovation and technology model for SMES by Nestor O. Rañeses, UPIEAA Professorial Chair
column10: While the small and medium industries (SMEs) numerically hogged the productive economic sectors at 99.6% of the total formal business establishments based on the 2009 NSO Survey, their economic contribution is still small to make them the primary engine for productivity improvement and economic growth. The lack and inadequate penetration, diffusion, and use of technology and innovation in starting, growing, and improving small and medium enterprises has been recognized as one of the major barriers for the slow growth of the SME sector. Using the Value Chain perspective and the TRIZ methodology, we present a TRIZ-driven sustainable innovation and technology model that aims to provide small and medium-sized entrepreneurs the mind-set and the behavioral model on how to secure and exploit technology and innovation to a trajectory of sustainable growth. Several cases will be presented.

column9: Productivity of gold production by small scale mining and extraction industries by Virginia Soriano, Jose P. Dans Professorial Chair
column10: The Philippine gold industry has contributed largely to the country's economy by supporting more than one million people, as well as generating and supporting thousands of small scale enterprises/ businesses in mining, processing and jewelry manufacturing. This study aims to determine the productivity levels and the factors that influence the productivity of small scale gold mining and extraction. Based on the documentation  and analysis of small scale gold production sites located at the provinces of Benguet, Camarines Norte and Davao, there are different mining and extraction practices and methods depending on the type of inputs such as materials, energy, labor and equipment and the amount of quality of output that can be produced at the shortest time possible. The different methods used for mining include underground, open pit, sinking tunnel and seafloor mining. The different methods observed for the extraction of gold involved the use of rather toxic substances such as cyanide, mercury or zinc. Key issues and challenges were identified based on the observed practices for the mining and extraction processes. Research was done on the potential productivity growth that may be driven by technological and organizational improvements.

column7: Department of Mechanical Engineering
column9: Ejector Powerplant System by Menandro Serrano Berana, Emerson Professorial Chair in Mechanical Engineering
column10: This paper presents the potential application of ejector and the efficiency improvement it brings to powerplants that utilize low-temperature renewable and recoverable heat sources. The ejector significantly increases the efficiency of the organic Rankine cycle (ORC) by increasing the turbine temperature drop which is made possible by the expansion, mixing and recompression processes in the ejector. The driving fluid in the ejector of the modified cycle is the high-pressure liquid in the separator that is just circulated back to the evaporator in the ORC. Fundamental thermodynamic analysis of the ejector powerplant cycle was undertaken. Ammonia and propane, which are both natural working fluids, were used in the analysis. The analysis was limited by considering that the lowest pressure in the system must be higher than the atmospheric pressure to avoid vacuum leak. The calculation results showed that propane and ammonia can both give maximum efficiencies at different evaporator temperatures and fixed condenser temperatures in the range of 20-27%. It has been shown that propane can give higher efficiences and lower velocities in the ejector than ammonia at considerably lower evaporating temperatures, leading to more economical, safer and simpler design and operation. The ejector system with ammonia and propane as working fluids offers environment friendly power generation from low-temperature sources at improved efficiency.
column11: Ejector, powerplant, propane, ammonia, organic rankine cycle

column9: Development of a diesel hybrid vehicle prototype by Gerald Jo C. Denoga, Emerson Professorial Chair in Mechanical Engineering
column10: The research is about the development of a diesel hybrid vehicle. A small concept car was prototyped aimed at achieving high fuel mileage while still satisfying other consumer needs like speed, aesthetics, safety, usability and comfort. The design is comprehensive, including lightweight chassis design, aerodynamic shell design, calculated passenger safety, and optimized power generation. The drive train is based on a series hybrid system, using a single cylinder diesel engine directly coupled to an electric DC generator. The electricity produced is channeled in part to a motor and a super capacitor, which, at certain points, will be used to power the motor. The key feature of the drive train is the control system that ensures a fixed power draw from the engine corresponding to the load and speed of the diesel engine at optimum brake specific fuel consumption. The prototype vehicle was able to achieve an actual on-road mileage of at least 50 kilometers per liter.

column9: LPG in dual-fuel SI and CI engine applications - effects on maximum poewr and fuel consumption by Edwin N. Quiros, Emerson Professorial Chair in Mechanical Engineering
column10: This paper presents findings on the use of LPG as a substitute fuel for a gasoline car sedan and a diesel-powered container crane. Maximum power at the wheels is investigated with the gasoline car running on either gasoline or LPG. Tests for maximum power at various gear ratios were conducted using a chassis dynamometer. On the other hand, a comparsion of fuel consumption using either diesel only or diesel with LPG was conducted on a container crane used in port collections. The tests involved moving a loaded container van over a prescribed series of motions simulating actual cargo handling and repeating these for a pre-determined number of cycles. Fuel consumption was measured gravimetrically at the end of the last cycle. These tests were performed to determine fuel cost savings opportunities if LPG is used for crane operation. Results of dynamometer tests with LPG fuel confirmed reduction of maximum power. One reason for this is the displacement of intake air by the vaporized LPG fuel. This is also one reason why an LPG convesion kit should be tuned to the vehicle where it is installed. Use of LPG with diesel fuel for a turbocharged diesel engine for crane operations indicated opportunities for savings due to overall lower fuel consumption and fuel costs. There were no noticeable operating issues experienced when operating on dual-fuel mode for the diesel engine.

column9: Design and production of a vehicle run by cordless drills by Joseph Gerard T. Reyes, Emerson Professorial Chair in Mechanical Engineering
column10: This work was brought about by a plan to design and fabricate an entry that would compete in a race sponsored by a power tools manufacturer. It also provided an opportunity to dwell in further research into the local production of vehicles using prime movers other than conventional internal-combustion engines. For this particular case, four cordless drills were specified as the source of motive power for this vehicle. The conditions considered in the design were safety of the driver; maneuverability, reliability and endurance of the vehicle; a light vehicle mass and a fixed budget. In order to satisfy the given conditions, the design incorporated proper vehicle dimensions, drivetrain and controls, the use of items which could be acquired off-the-shelf and the use of conventional manufacturing processes. A prototype was constructed at the UP College of Engineering and underwent trial runs prior to competition. The results of the trial runs and its performance at the competition showed satisfactory results.

column7: Department of Mining, Metallurgical and Materials Engineering
column9: Surface modification of polymers by plasma treatment in an ion shower facility by Alberto V. Amorsolo, Jr. Philex I Professorial Chair
column10: Low density polyethylene (LDPE) and Kapton polyimide (PI) films were subjected to surface modification in an ion shower facility using argon and nitrogen plasma. Surface modification can improve the wettability and adhesion properties of polymers to other materials like metal films. This variable screening study focused on the contact angle response as a measure of wettability, employing a replicated fractional factorial experiment (1/2 of 2 4) for each polymer in which the factors are accelerating voltage (0.5 kV, 3.5 kV), type of plasma source gas (argon, nitrogen), exposure time (10 min, 30 min) and gas filling pressure (13 mTorr, 48 mTorr). Additional characterization made use of Atomic Force Microscopy and Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) Spectroscopy in ATR mode. All treatments in the variable screening experiments successfully reduced the contact angle indicating creation of a more hydrophilic surface. Both main effects and two-factor interaction effects turned out to have statistical significance at the 95% confidence level among certain parameters. The interactionsfound in both polymer systems imply that meaningful optimization can only be acieved by considering that effects of factors are not simply additive but could either be synergistic or anti-synergistic in nature.

column9: Methane gas generation for dairy wastewater and expired produtcts via anaerobic digestion by Louernie F. de Sales-Papa, Dr. Jose C. Araneta UP Centennial Professorial Chair
column10: Dairy wastewater from the initial washing processes and expired products pulled out from the shelf have high concentrations of biochemical oxygen demand (BOD) and chemical oxygen demand (COD). Currently, these wastes are being stored awaiting treatment. During storage, the wastes already undergo biodegradation process. This study was done to simulate the biodegradation of the wastes and to determine if methane gas can be recovered during storage. Specifically, the study aimed to correlate the effect of COD values of the wastewater and expired products to the volume of methane gas generated in an anaerobic set-up. Each set-up of 1-liter capacity was monitored for 15 days without stirring and the volume of the generated gas was recorded. Anaerobic digestion was done without pH adjustment and seeding. Dairy wastewater was characterized (COD, pH and TSS) before and after treatment. The pH values and COD concentrations of the dairy wastewater samples ranged from 3.5-6 and 20,000-250,000 ppm respectively. The volume of gas generated was proportional to the COD concentration. A COD reduction of about 60% was achieved via anaerobic digestion.

column9: Advancing nanotechnology in the country through utilization of locally available nanomaterials by Leslie Joy L. Diaz, Integrated MicroElectronics Inc. (IMI) Professorial Chair in Engineering
column10: Nanotechnology has been the trend of research recently especially in materials primarily because of the unique properties and phenomena it brings out them, which typically are not observable in bulk dimensions. In the Philippines, a naturally occurring nanomaterial comes in the form of clay minerals, which, up to these days, have been mainly used for traditional applications such as pottery making and additives to cosmetics and agricultural fertilizers and other supplements. One of the hindrances to its utilization in nanotechnology is its purity because our material in the Philippines is often accompanied by oxides such as silica and iron oxide, among others. In this study, purification of nanoclay, specifically montmorillonite, from locally sourced clay and its utilization in fabrication of products for the environmental, semiconductor and energy applicants were investigated. It was shown in the characterizations of the material produced from nanoclay mixed with different polymer materials that addition of nanoclay enhanced and even modified to make them applicable to the applications mentioned above.

column9: Molecular sieve materials : their synthesis, properties and application by Eduardo R. Magdaluyo, Jr., Semirara Professorial Chair in Wall Mining
column10: A large number of molecular sieve materials have emerged in the past decade, with interesting structures, special types and aggregated states. They are usually composed of highly porous mineral or organometallic compounds, which can sieve out other molecules on the molecular scale. All the new developments in the syntheses and structural characterizations of the mesoporous materials open up an increasingly wider range of applications, from the traditional areas such as adsorption separation, catalysis and ion-exchange to electronics and nanotechnology. To describe a porous structure, several parameters may be used and these include the pore size and shape, the channel dimensionality, the composition and features of channel wall, among others. This paper covers the fundamental principles, synthetic strategies and techniques of the molecular sieve materials. The interesting properties associated to its structural framework and the different applications are also discussed.

column9: Heterocoagulation behavior of ultrafine hematite mineral particles with silica macroscopic collector in aqueous environment by Herman D. Mendoza, Philex Mining Corporation
column10: The attachment and detachment behaviors of ultrafine hematite particles (0.33 micron) onto silica macroscopic collector (104 micron) are investigated as a function of pH and electrolyte concentration using the column-bed method. The zeta-potential measurements showed that the iso-electric points (i.e.p.) of hematite and silica minerals are at around pH 2.0 and 7.2, respectively. Attachment of hematite particles onto silica is found to be optimum at conditions pH 3-6 when hematite particles have opposite surface potentials to that of silica collector. Detachment of hematite particles from silica collector is most effective at conditions pH 9-11 when the surface potentials of both hematite and silica particles are of the same negative potentials. It is also at these alkaline conditions that the attachment of hematite particles back to the silica surface is not possible because of the presence of an insurmountable energy barrier. There is a significant deceased of hematite particles attached onto and detached from silica collector as the concentration of the electrolyte is increased. The results are analyzed using the classical Darguin-Landue-Verveek-Overvick (DLVO) and Born repulsive energy theories. The mechanism established in this study can be used to explain mineral and material separation processes.

column9: Ceramic foam for the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants by Jeffrey John Venezuela, William Liu Professorial Chair
column10: The search for efficient yet inexpensive alternative methods of environmental decontamination has earned interest among researchers. Advanced photochemical oxidation (APO) processes, which include TiO2 as a photocatalyst, have flourished nowadays due to their wide application which includes wastewater treatment. This study focused on the design and implementation of an APO-based photochemical reactor utilizing macroporous cement-supported TiO2 nanotube with N and Fe3+ sensitizers. The hydrothermal method was employed in the synthesis of the nanotubes followed by doping with the sensitizers. Ceramic foams fabricated via a modified polymeric foam replication method using two different types of precursors were then coated with the doped TiO2. Characterization of the nanotube were then employed to determine its properties. The photocatalyticproperty of the ceramic foams were tested by determining the photodegradation of methyl orange upon exposure to white light. Transmission electron microscopy revealed the nanotube structure of titania after the hydrothermal treatment while XRD patterns confirmed the presence of anatase phase of TiO2 in the powders. The Brunauer-Emmet-Teller (BET) test results, on the other hand, showed a significant increase in the surface area which is typical of nanostructures. Photocatalytic tests on the TiO2-coated ceramic foams show a dependence of activity on the additoin of dopant and on the type of foam structure used. A reusability test on the foams suggests strong adhesion of the TiO2 on the foam and confirms its ability to withstand the rigors of practical application.

Effect of Temperature and Type of Inorganic Acid in Acidolysis of Epoxy and Polyurethane Thermosetting Resins by Jonas Karl Christopher N. Agutaya, Zarlon M. Bernardo, Lorenz Anthony T. Fernando, Timothy David T. Salmo, Terence P. Tumolva, Department of Chemical Engineering, University of the Philippines Diliman, Quezon City 1101 Philippines
column10: The study aims to determine the effect of temperature on the diffusion of strong inorganic acids, as a precursor to dissolution in epoxy and polyurethane thermosetting resins. Epoxy and polyurethane resins were immersed in 5M solutions of HNO3, H2SO4 and HCl at 30 degree celcius, 50 degree celcius and 80 degree celcius. From the relative changes in weight over a period of 12 days, the distribution coefficients were determined . Results showed an increase in the value of the diffusion coefficient with temperature. Moreover, both resins dissolved only in nitric acid at 80 degree celcius. In both resins, the nitric acid diffused the fastest and proved to be the most effective decomposing agent for the two polymers.

column9: Performance Comparison of Diesel and Auto-LPG Jeepney by Jose Gabriel E. Mercado, Department of Mechanical Engineering, University of the Philippines-Diliman, Quezon City, Philippines
column10: Jeepneys are the most common mode of public transportation in the Philippines but most jeepneys in the country are powered by diesel engines that produces harmful emissions to the environment. The Philippine government now promotes the use of alternative fuels in public transportation to lessen the harmful emissions and to make us less dependent on fossil fuels. This study shows that Autho-LPG powered jeepney is a good alternative to the diesel-powered jeepney in most routes in the Philippines because with respect to Fuel Economy the Auto-LPG powered jeepney is 22.26% better than the Diesel Jeepney.

column9: Mechanism Design of Work Loader for CNC Vertical Mill Using Graphical Synthesis Method by Jose, Ralph San Gabriel, Department of Mechanical Engineering, University of the Philippines, Diliman, Quezon City, 1101 Philippines

One-dimensional unsteady-state heat transfer in a window shade coated with microencapsulated phase change material by H.M.G. Tambago, Department of Chemical Engineering, University of the Philippines, Diliman, Quezon City, 1101 Metro Manila, Philippines
column10: Phase change materials (PCM) are thermal energy substances that can absorb or release a large amount of heat at a constant temperature at it undergoes phase transition. PCMs are used in a number of applications such as thermal regulation and improvement of heat transfer. Microencapsulated PCMs (MPCMs) may be incorporated in window textile and shade to reduce space cooling load requirements due to solar gain through windows. An evaluation of the thermal performance of a window shade coated with MPCM is presented by modelling the transport phenomenon in the material as a one-dimensional unsteady-state heat transfer. The effect of the type of PCM, encapsulating material and thickness, type of coating, fabric properties, and PCM loading on the thermal performance may be investigated through the model. The temperature distribution in the fabric after a period of simulation time, the progression of the temperature of a fabric end, and the heat flux from the fabric to the room were generated from a proposed method of solution to the model and showed that the MPCM-coated fabric exhibits a much improved thermal resistance compared to an uncoated fabric. Only a short simulation time was considerd using the proposed method, however, due to the limitation imposed by the stability condition that demands a large amount of computer memory. Another method of solution must be explored to complete the simulations and gain more useful insights to the performance of the MPCM-coated shade as a thermal regulator.

column9: Performance Evaluation of Selected Locally Available Cement in Stabilizing Soil for Pavement Base Purposes by Jaime Angelo S. Victor, Instructor, Geotechnical Engineering Group, Institute of Civil Engineering, University of the Philippines Diliman
column10: Cement treated base course are commonly used in high volume and load traffic areas, such as : airports, industrial areas, and major highways. Various materials such as soil, aggregates and recycled concrete may be treated with cement to improve its engineering properties. This study aims to investigate the effect of cement type and cement content to the strength properties of soil cement used as base courses of pavements. The strength parameter considered in this study is the soaked unconfined compressive strength, with minor testing for California Bearing Ratio (CBR). Soil samples from A-2-4 type of soil were used to produce cylindrical soil-cement specimens to be usef for standard compressive strength tests. Results show that for all cement types, there were significant increases in compressive strength of the soil cement samples for every 2% increment of cement content. In comparison with each other, there were significant 28-day strength differences among samples of varying cement type but with one cement content. Type I soil cement samples demonstrated high early strength and satisified the DPWH strength standard at the PCA recommended cement contents for design (5%, 7%, and 9%). With regard to the CBR, all the samples satisfied the DPWH standard of a minimum 110% CBR.

column9: Plane Stress Computation of 94% Aluminum Oxide Orthogonal Cutting Tool using Finite Element Analysis by Hannah Erika R. Ducusin, Department of Mechanical Engineering, University of the Philippines Diliman, Quezon City, Philippines

Selecting and Tuning a Temperature Feedback Controller for Water Heating Process by Kristian July R. Yap, Shayne N. Guelos, Mersabel L. Veciana, Department of Chemical Engineering, University of the Philippines-Diliman
column10: Temperature control is ncessary for temperature-specific industrial operations in order to meet optimum operating conditions based on the desired product specifications. In line with this, an experiment on temperature control was conducted to select an appropriate feedback controller and determine its tuning parameters. A first order plus dead time (FOPDT) model was employed to characterize the system. Tuning parameters were determined through computer simulation using SciLab (version 5.0.3) by trial-and-error. Results show that upon using a PI controller with Kc=30.00 and K1=0.71, a temperature deviation of about 2 degree celcius for the half pail set-up and a 10-minute settling time deviation for the full pail set-up occured. This was due to several factors such as the ideality and absence of mixing in the mathematical model, and the difference in the conditions of the environment. Though a PID controller was theoretically used, a PI-controlled system was found to be more suitable.
column11: Dead time, Time constant, PID controller, PI controller

column9: Land Use/Land Cover Map Generation of Panay, Guimaras and Negros Islands using Moderate Resolution Satellite Images by Jeark A. Principe, Assistant Professor, Department of Geodetic Engineering, University of the Philippines Diliman, Quezon City, Philippines 1101
column10: This paper presents the results of using a series of moderate resolution (30m) Landsat images to derive a general land use/land cover (LULC) map of the islands of Panay, Guimaras and portions of Negros. The said images were pre-processed for atmospheric correction, cloud and cloud shadow filling and removal of noisy pixels. The six optical bands of Landsat ETM+ and an additional built-up index band were used as inputs to image classification, in per scene basis, via the Maximum Likelihood classifier. The said index band was derived from the three original Landsat bands and was used to aid in differentiating built-up and bare soil pixels. The classified image has a mean overall accuracy of 85.41% and kappa coefficient of 0.819 while the derived LULC map from this classification showed a class distribution of bare soil, paddy fields, forest, built-up, grass and water areas covering 33.4%, 11.3%, 13.3% 2.2%, 30.5% and 9.3% of the total area of the three Philippine islands. The study demonstrated the use of freely-available moderate resolution Landsat images to derive LULC maps, a very important spatial data that is often used in many research studies dealing with environmental planning and protection.

column9: Thermal degradation kinetics and lifetime estimation of orthophthalic unsaturated polyester resins by Ralph P. Villa, Jonas Karl Chritopher N. Agutaya, Terence P. Tumolva and Masatoshi Kubouchi, Department of Chemical Engineering, University of the Philippines, Diliman, Quezon City 1101 Philippines, Department of Chemical Engineering, Tokyo Institute of Technology, Meguro-ku, Tokyo 152-8552, Japan
column10: In this study, the model-free isoconversional methods of Vyazovkin, Ozawa-Flynn-Wall (OFW) and Kissinger-Akahira-Sunose (KAS) together with the master-plot method were used to study the non-isothermal thermal degradation kinetics of orthophthalic unsaturated polyester (ortho-UP). Non-isothermal experiments at four different heating rates (2, 5, 10 and 20 K/min) were conducted using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). The experimental data was used to find the kinetic parameters (E, A and f (x) of this process. The dependence of the activation energy E with conversion, x were determined using the three isoconversional models. Likewise, the pre-exponential factor A and reaction model f(x) (or g(x)) were obtained using the master-plot method. Results show that the activation energy and pre-exponential factor for the thermal degradation of ortho-UP are E=144.036 kJ/mol and A=1.4595 x 10 10 min-1, respectively. A three-dimensional diffusion model, of the form g(x) = [1-(1-x) 1/3] 2, was determined to be the best kinetic model for this particular system. The consistency of the kinetic triplets [E, A and g (x)) were checked by comparing experimental and calculated x - T curves.
column11: Ortho-UP; TGA; Thermal degradation kinetics; Non-isothermal; Isoconversion; Model-Free

column3: UPdate Diliman : the community newspaper of UP Diliman, Volume XV, Number 1
column7:
Roman takes the helm as 19th UP President
The first woman president is the centennial president. 
It's all system go. Today, the University of the Philippines (UP) makes its date with history as Dr. Emerlinda R. Roman assumes the top post as 19th President. Roman is the first woman president of the 97 year-old institution. Serving a 6-year term, she enjoys the distinction of being the centennial president since three years from now in 2008, UP will celebrate its 100 years of existence. 
The women of Malolos 
Book Review
Profile 
Outgoing helmsman
So many things can happen in six years - the length of service by a UP president. Outgoing University helsman Francisco "Dodong" Nemenzo looks back at the maelmstroms he faced, as he tried not merely to keep the boat afloat, but decide which course it would take, and steady its sails to that direction, however strong the winds may have been. 
All in a day's work
On the 5-year watch of UPD Chancellor Emerlinda R. Roman (CERR), there has been relative quiet on campus : no discordant emotional debates over issues at the University Council meetings nor protest actions either. For at the heart of UPD's chancellor is a woman who seeks to set aside peripheral matters to address the more essential concerns. 
Research
The new"Nanay" by Bino C. Gamba
Female OFWs tell how it is. 
The government's inability to generate enough jobs and adequate income drives millions of Filipinos to work abroad and leave their families in search for greater monetary returns for their labor.

The women of Malolos* 
Fighting for right to education
by Dr. Milagros C. Guerrero
Department of History
College of Social Sciences and Philosophy
*A critique of the book, "The Women of Malolos" by Dr. Nicanor G. Tiongson, Ateneo de Manila University Press, 2004.
The period between 1850 and 1920, around which revolves Dr. Nicanor G. Tiongson's book is a particularly tumultuous one. Within this period took place the terror of 1872, the manifestation of 1888, the propaganda-reformist period, the Philippine revolution and the Filipino-American War.

In August 2004, President Gloria Macapagal-Arroyo announced the country was in fiscal crisis. Although the news did not come as a shock to most Filipinos, the admittance of the chief executive confirmed the people's fears that the Philippines was in dire straits.

column3: UPdate Diliman : the community newspaper of UP Diliman, Volume XV, Number 5
column7:
Devotion, sacrifice, and accountable performance
The investiture of Dr. Sergio S. Cao as 8th UPD Chancellor
By Irwin Allen B. Rivera
"You who refused to work in other companies and teach in other schools share with me the enduring faith that UP is an institution worthy of devoting the best years of our lives." Said Dr. Sergio S. Cao in his speech at the investiture rites on September 7 at the UP Film Institute (see full text of the investiture address on page 1A)
Presidential message
Among the University of the Philippines' seven constituent universities, UP Diliman (UPD) is the biggest in terms of the number of students, number of faculty, number of degree-granting units and number of academic program offerings. As the largest campus, it deserves a most competent, dedicated and able leader who will lead this campus toward contributing to UP's aspiration to be a distinguished Filipino university in the 21st century. 
Paglilingkod at pananatili sa UP nating mahal* by Sergio S. Cao, Chancellor
Kagalang-galang na lupon ng mga rehente, presidente Emerlinda Roman, your excellencies, members of the diplomatic corps, mga presidente at pinuno ng ibang unibersidad at kolehiyo, mga piling panauhin mula sa loob at labas ng unibersidad, magandang hapon sa inyong lahat. Salamat sa inyong pagdalo sa aking investiture bilang ikawalong Chanselor ng UP Diliman. 
Voice of UP Diliman*
By Oscar M. Alfonso
(Former member of the UP Board of Regents and retired UP Professor of History)
In the dusk of life, gifts of great moment come few and rare. Few come rarer thatn the singular honor and privilege accorded me today by Chancellor Sergio S. Cao of UP Diliman to present him during his investiture. Proof once more that pleasant surprises still lie in wait in late life. 
10-point agenda
UPD action plan (2005-2008)*
University of the Philippines President Emerlinda R. Roman laid down her administration's plans and projects for the University's next six years in UP Plan (2005-2011). The President's plan has three major components : commemorating the UP Centennial, strengthening UP's stature as a center of excellence and culture, and transforming UP into an efficient university. 
OCES meets the press
The executive staff of UP Diliman (UPD) led by Chancellor Sergio S. Cao was formally introduced to members of the media on September 1, at a press conference held at Katre Restaurant on Scout Lascano along Tomas Morato Avenue in Quezon City.

Reducing poverty can be realized through pro-poor development agenda by the national government such as scaling up investment in quality education and health, credit markets for education and small enterprises, an active population management program, sustained increase in labor demand coupled with producing highly-skilled workers and efficient social safety nets programs.
Douglass North and the crisis of institutions*
A casual glance at the Philippine dailies suggests that the country is a pit of institutional dysfunction by Raul V. Fabella, Ph.D. Former Dean, School of Economics
Manila, Philippines - A casual glance at the Philippine dailies suggests that the country is a pit of institutional dysfunction. The economy is growing but the growth is still largely externally driven by the overseas workers industry, which grew fortunately behind the government's back and beyond its grasp. In fact the most dynamic local sector, the BPO, grows precisely because it avoids government-provided services (ports and transport) and funnels its business into cyberspace courtesy of private telcos.

College of Social Sciences and Philosophy

Dr. Jonathan Malicsi, Professor of Linguistics, identified some factors in the evolution of Philippine English, in his Rafael Palma Centennial Lecture on August 8, entitled "Pinoy English : A Case of Language Drift."

Lagmay's research by the Philippine Council for Advanced Science and Technology Research and Development (PCASTRD) last December.

The faculty is the university's greatest resource.

Gauging the risk on heritage sites 
Tool to measure the vulnerability created by Anna Regidor, image by Leonardo A. Reyes
The facade of the Church of San Pedro Apostol in Loboc, Bohol collapsed after the earthquake in 2013. 
In October 2013, a magnitude 7.2 earthquake struck Bohol, killing 28 people and destroying some of the country's oldest and most iconic churches, including the Church of San Pedro Apostol in Bohol and the Basilica Minore del Santo Niño in Cebu.

Making policy decisions on the Philippine scientific enterprise system by Maureen Anne Araneta, images by Leonardo A. Reyes
Pouring outlay into research and development (R&D) should be a sound investment for economic growth. After all, scientific knowledge, when discovered and applied successfully, boosts labor productivity, creates wealth and enhances quality of life. The Philippines followed this premise by increasing budget allocation in the Department of Science and Technology (DOST) by 115.11 percent from 2009 to 2014. But why is the Philippines still dismally behind Singapore, Brunei Darussalam, Malaysia, Thailand and Indonesia in terms of Gross Domestic Product (GDP) per capita?

"Siglo," the electric vehicle created by Team Dagisik University of the Philippines, won 1st Prize in the Shell Eco-marathon Philippines 2017, held Feb. 4 and 5 at the Clark International Speedway in Pampanga.

Felipe P. de Leon Jr., dating Tagapangulo ng National Commission for Culture and Arts (NCCA) at kasalukuyang propesor ng Aralin sa Sining sa Kolehiyo ng Arte at Literatura ng UP Diliman (UPD), sa "Sampaksaan sa K'wentong Bayan," Peb 23 sa UP NISMED Auditorium.

Text and image by Benito V. Sanvictores Jr. 
One faculty member and four staff received the 2017 Service Awards from the UP Diliman (UPD) College of Social Work and Community Development (CSWCD) on Feb. 24 at the CSWCD Bulwagang Tandang Sora.

Philippine myths on the universe's origin by Haidee C. Pineda, images by Jefferson Villacruz

Abridged from the Asian Center original
The students, staff and faculty of the Asian Center, University of the Philippines Diliman, are deeply saddened by the passing of their teacher, colleague, and mentor, Reuben Ramas Cañete, Ph.D., last 17 February 2017. He was 50.

Te in elections held Apr. 6.

A study on the views on aging of Filipina migrants to New Zealand found that some older women have more relaxed beauty standards and still consider themselves successful in their lives.

Two Chinese scholars presented opposing views on China's territorial claims on the South China Sea (SCS) in a public lecture at the UP Asian Center.

Commencement Address by Benjamin E. Diokno, PhD
Secretary, Department of Budget and Management
Message to the Class of 2017 by Michael Lim Tan, Chancellor, UP Diliman
Ang ating panauhing pandangal Kalihim Benjamin Diokno; ang dating Pangulko ng UP Dr. Emmanuel Soriano, Pangulo Danilo L. Concepcion, mga opisyal ng administrasyon ng UP, mga kaguruan, mga magulang, lolo at lola, asawa't anak, kabarangay at nag-alay ng dugo at pawis at ang mga inalayan ng dugo at pawis, ang class of 2017, magandang umaga sa inyong lahat, maayong buntag sa atong tanan. Assalamo alaikum.

Ghodsinia, a summa cum laude (SCL) graduate of Molecular Biology and Biotechnology (MBB), has a weighted average grade of 1.173.

To succeed, there should be better integration among member states of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN) Economic Community (AEC).

Older Filipinos are generally not materially well-off, not well-educated and about half consider themselves to be of average health, but have poor use of health services.

Tugon ng UP Diliman sa Hamon ng COVID-19 by Bino C. Gamba, Chi A. Ibay, Mariamme D. Jadloc, Haidee C. Pineda, Anna E. Regidor and Benito V. Sanvictores Jr. 
Life, as most Metro Manila residents knew it, began its drastic change on Mar. 8, when President Rodrigo R. Duterte signed Proclamation No. 922, which formally declared a state of public health emergency in the Philippines and suspended classes in public and private schools in the National Capital Region (NCR) from Mar. 10 to 14. 
Coronavirus Pandemic Timeline
On December 31, 2019, the World Health Organization (WHO) reported cases of pneumonia detected in Wuhan, China. According to Wuhan Municipal Health, the cases occurred between Dec. 12 and 29. The virus was still unknown. 
As of October 26, 2020, COVID-19 had infected 43,345, 944, with deaths at 1,159,093. In the Philippines, cases stood at 370,028 with 6,977 deaths.

Engineering in speech and language by Michael Gringo Angelo R. Bayona
Decades ago, human-machine interaction existed mainly in the realm of science fiction. 
Deafness as ethnicity by Nestor T. Castro
The perceptions of cultural differentiation in the Philippines were traditionally reckoned through a combination of criteria, such as religion (e.g. Christian, Muslim, or "pagan"), domicile (e.g. concepts of highlander vs. lowlander, riverine or coastal peoples, sea-faring groups), phenotypic characteristics (e.g. Negrito vs. Mongoloid), and material culture (e.g. native costume, type of dwelling, musical instruments, weapons, customs and traditions, etc.). More important than these aforementioned factors, however, is the language that a particular group uses. Thus, varied communities are commonly referred as "ethnolinguistic groups"  instead of "ethnic groups."

column5: UP Diliman Information Office under the Office of the Chancellor
column7:
Marso 20-Abril 30
Terraforming
1F Galleries
Vargas Museum
The UP Vargas Museum, in cooperation with Art Informal and Box Plot, presents Terraforming, an exhibit of Mark Valenzuela's and Pablo K. Capati III's contemporary ceramics. 
Abril 5-11, 19-25
National Training of Trainers of Grade 4 Science and Mathematics Teachers for the K-12 Basic Education
Science Teachers Training Center NISMED
The sessions for elementary school mathematics are designed to highlight teaching mathematics through problem solving. For elementary school science, the emphasis will be on teaching through inquiry.
Abril 6-17
Teaching Preschoolers and Early Graders in the Context of the K-12 Curriculum
A seminar for preschool and primary teachers, administrators and tertiary level early childhood education (ECE) educators and organized by the Teaching in the Early Grades (TEG) area of the College of Education. 
Abril 7
Tragedy on Television : Aristotle's Revenge
College of Mass Communication A101 
5:30-7 p.m.
While tragedy as a genre seems increasingly irrelevant to cinema, the recent trend in television serial dramas revolving around a central "anti-hero" makes contact with central tenets of classical tragic theory. Dr. Joel Mann views examples from the recent dramatic series Breaking Bad and Sons of Anarchy through the lens of Aristotle's poetics, though other series in the same vein will be up for discussion (e.g., Mad Men, The Sopranos).
Abril 8-15
BS Interior Design ID123 
Table Setting Exhibit
CHE Lobby
College of Home Economics
8 a.m.-5 p.m. 
An open exhibit by the class of ID123 Decorative Arts and Soft Furnishings featuring table settings designed in various themes such as Filipino colonial-inspired breakfast and Mediterranean-style al fresco lunch. 
Abril 10
Tampisaw : An Interactive Exhibit
Palma Hall Rm. 400
12-3 p.m. 
Tampisaw is part of the UP Tomokai's 24th year celebration of cultural exchanges between the Philippines and Japan.
Abril 13-Mayo 11
(Lunes-Miyerkules-Biyernes)
