An introductory talk about HIV and free screening for UPD community
UP University Health Service Public Health Unit
8 a.m. - 4 p.m.
Behind Closed Doors : an uncensored talk about HIV 
A public lecture by Dr. Kate Leytritana
UP University Health Service
Conference Room 
2-4 p.m.
The talk and screening will be held as public service of the UP University Health Service, as part of their month-long anniversary celebrations. It is in cooperation with LoveYourself Inc. From one per day, the current rate of new HIV cases daily has risen to 38, with increasing incidence in the younger age group. The activity will address the clamor of students, staff and faculty to obtain more information about HIV/AIDS, as a significant health concern. 
Setyembre 29
JORNADA LEE
Panel discussions on Spanish language teaching
CAL Pavilion
9 a.m. - 5 p.m. 
The event is a day-long discussion among Spanish didactic experts of issues related to Spanish language teaching. Organized by the Department of European Languages of the College of Arts and Letters. Panel discussions will tackle issues related to foreign language education policies in the Philippines, Spanish linguistics, Hispanic literature, and Spanish didactics. Speakers on the different topics include experts from UP, University of Santo Tomas, Instituto Cervantes de Manila, Western Mindanao State University, and the University of Cebu.

column3:
"Ugnayan
Unibersidad ng Pilipinas Diliman. Tomo 4, Blg 9 (5 copies)"
column7:
Oktubre 3 
Curiosity, Adventure & Love
Cine Adarna, UPFI Film Center
2:30, 5 and 7:30 p.m. 
The movie is about a woman's journey through a century of love, war and discovery. 
A young American woman leaves her country and all security behind, to begin an adventure in the Philippines that would witness the birth of a nation,a cruel war and occupation and reconstruction. 
Oktubre 4-5
16th Philippine-Spanish Friendship Day Conference
Mapping Spaces and Identities in Spanish Colonial Philippines
NISMED Auditorium
8 a.m. - 5:30 p.m.
The conference aims to interrogate the numerous ways that spaces and identities were mapped during the Spanish colonial period. The most obvious would be navigational maps that served not merely as geographic tools of exploration but also of conquest. The history, influence and legacy of cartography and the shift in the use of maps from simple navigation to long-term colonization remain rich areas of historical investigation. 
The conference seeks to examine not the mapping spaces and identities per se but the various ways these were represented in concrete formats, such as cartographic maps, census or travel accounts, or even the demarcation of towns as recounted in founding documents. The annual Philippine-Spanish Frienship Day Conference is organized by the UP Department of History and the National Historical Commission of the Philippines. 
Oktubre 4
Passages
Abelardo Hall Auditorium
6:30 p.m.
The event celebrates UP Arco's 20th Year Anniversary and Reunion Concert. It is also to celebrate the passage of time and the beautiful music they have shared in 20 years. UP Arco, formerly the UP Chamber Orchestra, is one of the country's premiere orchestras that has distinguished itself in the careful study and performance of string orchestral music. In July 2015, UP Arco won the Silver Band Award (highest score in the string orchestra category) in the Bratislava International Youth Music Festival competition in Bratislava, Slovakia. In 2016, the group won the Gold Medal for the String Orchestra Category in the 2nd Music Festa Florence in Italy. The group was also given two Special Jury Awards - Best Musical Interpretation for their performance of Andante Festivo by Jean Sibelius and Best Female Soloist awarded to the group's concertmaster. UP Arco is under the baton of Prof. Edna Marcil Martinez. 
Oktubre 5
5th Interdisciplinary Book Forum : Sapantaha : Kalipunan ng mga Maikling Kwentong Spekulatibo at Imahinatibo 
Pav. 1131, Palma Hall
2:30 - 4 p.m.
The forum seeks to provide an academic venue in which to realize the imperative for dialogue and intellectual engagement across the various disciplines, which speaks directly to the Literature for Social Development (LSD)'s core agenda. Being the center for the study and development of creative and, to a certain extent, critical writing in the UP System, the Likhaan is in a unique position to carry out such an important academic mission. The Interdisciplinary Book Forum is held once every semester and constitutes a vital component of the UP Likahaan's LSD. 
Oktubre 10
Change and Continuity in China's Foreign Policy
A Public Lecture
UP CIDS Conference Hall
Lower Ground Floor, Ang Bahay ng Alumni 
1:30 - 4 p.m.
The Strategic Studies Program (SSP) of the University of the Philippines' Center for Integrative and Development Studies (UP CIDS), in partnership with the UP Diliman Department of Political Science, will hold a lecture on the emerging trends in China's foreign policy and its implications for Korea. The lecture is based on the findings of the studies conducted by SSP Research Fellows Dr. Meitling Li and Raisa E. Lumampao. The lecture is free and open to the public on a first-come, first -served basis. 
Oktubre 15-16
Bergman in Spain
Ingmar Bergman screenings at UP Film Institute
Cine Adarna, UPFI Film Center
In celebration of the centenary of Ingmar Bergman, the most iconic and influential Swedish filmmaker of all time, the Instituto Cervantes, in partnership with the Embassy of Sweden in Manila and the UP Film Institute, presents Bergman in Spain, one of the jewels stored in the 17th edition of Pelicula-Pelikula 2018, the largest Spanish Film Festival in Southeast Asia. 
Oktubre 17-19
Bukluran : Exploring the Food Science and Nutrition nexus towards achieving the United Nation's Sustainable Development Goals (UN SDG) 
Gusali 2, Alonso Hall 
College of Home Economics
8 a.m. - 5 p.m.
The 3-day event will serve as a platform for acquisition and exchange of new and existing ideas and information regarding the disciplines of food science and nutrition in achieving the UN SDG related to food security and availability. 
Oktubre 19
Ensuring a Square Meal : Women and Food Security in Southeast Asia
Book Launch and Panel Discussion
UP CIDS Conference Hall
Lower Ground Floor, Ang Bahay ng Alumni 
2 -4 p.m. 
The UP CIDS Program on Social and Political Change, UP Department of Political Science and World Scientific will launch a book that aims to interlink women and food security at the household, community, national and transnational levels in the Southeast Asian region. The event will also hold a panel discussion joined by two of the book chapter authors, Dr. Jorge Tigno, Professor at the UP Department of Political Science; Dr. Thelma Paris, Senior Adviser (Gender Research) at Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security in Southeast Asia; as well as the editor of the book, Dr. Theresa Devashayam, Associate Faculty at the Singapore School of Social Sciences. 
Oktubre 19 - Nobyembre 16
Stasis and Mobility : Nasa Dugo Natin 'Yan?!
Bulwagan ng Dangal
6 p.m. (Exhibit Opening)
The exhibition is an attempt to problematize the science of genetics through the visual arts. Genetics is the study of genes, genetic variation and heredity in living organisms. Colloquially, it refers to a unit of inheritance-such as physical traits passed on by parents to their children - color of the skin, hair, body-built and height. The exhibit also tries to answer the question : what is the relationship of genetics and culture? This is especially since both culture and genetics are considered two opposite poles in the same way many tend to perceive nature and culture as binary opposites. How do metaphysicians explain alterity and permanency : the self as the individual age changes and at the same time stays the same? The exhibit posits that the genetic make-up of the individual is both permanent and changing. 
Oktubre 25-26
Colloquium on HIV/AIDS in Southeast Asia
GT Toyota Auditorium
8 a.m. - 5 p.m.
HIV/AIDS in Southeast Asia is an interdisciplinary and intercultural colloquium exploring the diverse processes by which HIV/AIDS is constructed as a cultural phenomenon. The colloquium presents two keynote speakers and up to 20 papers on various topics related to the theme. It also aims to bring together academics from different disciplinary backgrounds with the aim of developing a network of scholars working in this area. 
Oktubre 26
Sawikaan 2018 : Salita ng Taon
Institute of Biology Auditorium 
8 a.m. - 5 p.m. 
Ang Sawikaan ay isang masinsinang talakayan para piliin ang pinakanatatanging salitang namayani sa diskurso ng sambayanang Filipino sa nakalipas na taon. Isang popular ng kumperensiya ang Sawikaan at mainam na forum sa pagtalakay ng halaga ng wika sa mga pangunahing isyung kinakaharap ng bayan. Dadaluhan ito ng mga mag-aaral, guro, midya at mga akademikong sumusubaybay sa development ng wikang pambansa. Sa pagtutulungan ito ng Opisina ng Tsanselor ng UP Diliman (UPD), UP Diliman Information Office at ng Filipinas Institute of Translation (FIT). Ang FIT ay isang samahang pangwika na karamihan sa kasapi ay mga guro ng UPD. 
Oktubre 30
Forum on Science, Technology and Society
Institute of Biology Auditorium
1:30 - 5 p.m. 
In celebration of the 200th anniversary of Mary Shelly's pioneering sci-fi novel "Frankenstein," or the Modern Prometheus, a lecture-forum that gathers a cross-disciplinary panel discusses this early 18th century novel and the modern-day issues it anticipated. The forum aims to engage the UP community in discourses about the body and human consciousness, biotechnology and social responsibility, robotics, artificial intelligence and the perils of science ungoverned by ethical norms.

column3:
Ugnayan
Unibersidad ng Pilipinas Diliman. Tomo 4, Blg 10
column7:
Nobyembre 3-13
TOFARM Film Festival 2018
Special Screening run at UPFI
2, 5 and 7 p.m. 
UP Film Institute showcases all the six films of TOFARM Film Festival 2018 namely : Tanabata's Wife; 1957; Alimuom, Kauyagan; Mga Anak ng Kamote; and Sol Searching with three features each day for the duration of the screening program. This festival aims to uplift the farmers and their professional development. It is most worthy and significant since this is the only film festival that showcases the lives, journeys, aspirations, trials and tribulations - failures and successes of the farmers and other stewards of agriculture. 
Nobyembre 3
Films by Carlo Enciso Catu
Cine Adarna, UPFI Film Center
UP Film Institute starts the month of November with a back-to-back screening of Carlo Enciso Catu's multi-awarded Ari : My Life with a King and the TOFARM Film Festival finalist Mga Anak ng Kamote. 
Nobyembre 7-9
Philippine Research Conference on Tourism and Hospitality 2018
Facing the Challenges of Overtourism
Holiday Inn, Baguio City
The conference aims to examine the evolution of tourism development in rural and urban settings, and the implications of perceived and actual impacts of tourism on the environment, the economy and the social fabric. Through stand-up paper presentations, workshops, and keynote speeches, the conference will discuss a range of topics including resident perceptions and attitudes on tourism development, governance issues and policy options to address over-tourism and mitigate the negative impacts of tourism. The conference is organized by the UP Asian Institute of Tourism in cooperation with UP Department of Hotel, Restaurant and Institution Management. 
Nobyembre 8-9
What Now? A Hundred Years After World War I
The UP Center for International Studies (UPCIS), in collaboration with the French Embassy, UP Film Institute, and the Department of European Languages of UP College of Arts and Letters, will be holding a series of activities to commemorate the Centennial of the end of World War I. The activities wish to encourage discussion on the Great War topics and its contemporary implications, inviting students to participate in the discourse on one of history's most transformative events. 
Nobyembre 8
Marx Bicentennial Lecture Series No. 11
A lecture by Dr. Francisco Nemenzo and Dr. Eduardo T. Gonzalez
UP CIDS Conference Hall, Lower Ground Floor, Ang Bahay ng Alumni
2 - 5 p.m.
To commemorate Karl Marx's 200th year, a group of academics and social movement activists have gathered to hold a series of lectures. For the Marx Bicentennial Lecture Series No. 11, former UP President Dr. Francisco Nemenzo will look at the early writings of Marx in his talk "Marx before Marxism : The Philosophical Premises of Revolutionary Theory" while Dr. Eduardo T. Gonzalez will talk about "Navigating Contemporary Marxian Thought in the Age of Populism".
Nobyembre 9
Rondalya Para Sa Lahat
A celebration of rondalla music as an enriching and inclusive community music
UP Abelardo Hall Auditorium
3-8 p.m.
In celebration of the 51st anniversary of the Music Education Department of the UP College of Music, a workshop-concert highlighting rondalla music as an enriching and inclusive community music will be conducted. The event will also feature a lecture-workshop by Maestro Celso Espejo about managing and training a rondalla and a concert featuring performing groups mentored using the Celso Espejo Method namely : Talayan Rondalla, OB Montessori Center, Timoteo Paez Elementary School Rondalla, UP Rondalla, Celso Espejo Rondalla and the NCCA Employees' Rondalla. 
Nobyembre 12
Filmmaking Masterclass with Italian Filmmakers
UP Film Institute Media Center Bldg. Film Studio 
1-6:30 p.m. 
A 1-day Italian cinema masterclass that serves as the educational component of this year's edition of Italian Cinema : From Venice to Manila. Topics include new soundtracks to new horizons by Franco Eco; philosophy of an ordinary cinematographer by Davide Mancori; and FVX design by Paolo Bertola. Organized by the UP Film Institute, in partnership with The Philippine Italian Association, all masterclasses are free of charge. Certificates will be issued upon request. 
Nobyembre 13
Off-Campus Housing Fair
Isabelo delos Reyes Auditorium
UP School of Labor and Industrial Relations
8 a.m. - 5 p.m.
The event aims to help UP employees think about their options on where to live and what to live in. Real estate developers will present residential options in Quezon City and nearby areas of Bulacan, Rizal and Laguna. Bank representatives are invited to present their home loan products and acquired assets. Pag-IBIG Fund is also invited to present their available loan programs and other services related to housing and to set up a Membership Assistance Booth. Interested employees may consult and interact with the representatives from the different property brands, banks and Pag-IBIG Fund. The event is open to all UP employees. The fair is organized by the Diliman Housing Ofice, under the Office of the Vice Chancellor for Community Affairs. 
Nobyembre 14-16
13th Philippine Linguistics Congress (13PLC)
UP National Institute for Science and Mathematics Education Development (NISMED)
The Philippine Linguistics Congress brings together scholars interested in the study of Philippine languages and linguistics to discuss current research and recent advances in the field. The conference looks into the application of lingustics findings and theories to other related disciplines, as well as contemporary comparative studies between Philippine languages and other languages. The conference also seeks to promote collaboration and further research on Philippine languages and lingustics. It also serves as a venue for the discussion of langauge-related issues in the country. 
Nobyembre 14
YO! sa UP 2018
CHRONO : TRAVERSE TOWARDS THE FUTURE
SM Clark Cinemas 
6-9 p.m. 
YO! sa UP, hosted by the UP Triskelions Grand Fraternity and Grand Sorority, is celebrating its 30th year with the theme, Chrono : Traverse towards the Future. Witness dance groups of UP Diliman Extension Program in Pampanga go head to head in showcasing their dancing prowess in a hip-hop dance competition like no other. Prepare for robotic dance steps, galactic-inspired costumes, and eclectic sounds, all in a tangled array of futuristic aesthetic. 
Nobyembre 16
The Cleaners
Cine Adarna, UPFI Film Center
7 p.m.
Directed by Hans Block and Moritz Riesewieck, the documentary film is about five "digital scavengers" among thousands of people outsourced from Silicon Valley whose job is to delete "inappropriate" content on the net. The film also divulges their struggles to come to terms with the new and diconcerting paradigm of the point of balance for social media to be neither an unlegislated space nor a forum rife with censorship. Evolving from a shared social vision of a global village to a web of fake news and radicalization, the film charts the rise and fall of social media's utopian ideology. 
Nobyembre 17
ARIA
Cine Adarna, UPFI Film Center
5 p.m.
Directed by Carlo Enciso Catu, the film is about a love story set against the backdrop of the Huk guerrilla movement during World War 2. 
Nobyembre 17
Going Back, Giving Back : 64 Years of Empowering People
UP SOLAIR Grand Alumni Homecoming 
Isabelo Delos Reyes Auditorium
UP School of Labor and Industrial Relations
5-9 p.m.
All Diploma in Industrial Relations and Master in Industrial Relatons graduates are cordially invited to the UP SOLAIR Grand Alumni Homecoming 2018. Reminisce the UP SOLAIR experience, reconnect with your classmates, professors and staff, and meet new friends! 
Nobyembre 21-23
Worker's Institute on Labor Laws (WILL) Class 174
Isabelo Delos Reyes Auditorium
UP School of Labor and Industrial Relations
9 a.m. - 5 p.m. 
The seminar will enable the participants to : 1) acquire basic knowledge about labor laws and legislation of the country; 2) apply the legal processes and techniques in their respective workplaces; and 3) understand and share experiences and practices involving labor laws and industrial relations. 
Nobyembre 22-23
Examining Philippine Media : Texts and Contexts
The National Communication Research Conference 2018
Cine Adarna, UP Film Center
7 a.m. - 6 p.m.
The 6th National Communication Research Conference (NCRC) examines our critical engagement with and consumption of the media within and across platforms. It features 65 papers selected through a blind review process as well as several plenary presentations on the conference theme of media monitoring as well as on pertinent concerns in communication and media research such as ethics, research dissemination, intellectual property and intellectual honesty. The NCRC, organized by the Department of Communication Research, in cooperation with the Philippines Communications Society, is a platform for students, faculty members and professionals to present and discuss their research papers before a community of scholars. 
Nobyembre 23
UP Writers Night 2018
In celebration of the 40th year of UPICW, Grand Homecoming of Fellows of the UP Writers Workshop
University Hotel
4-11 p.m.
As part of its 40th year anniversary gala, the UP Institute of Creative Writing's (UP ICW) yearly gathering will pay homage to both the country's up and coming, and established writers. The grand homecoming will feature events like the 18th Madrigal-Gonzalez Best First Book Award, 40@40 and Likhaan 12 book launch. Expect a special evening filled with memories; to reminisce "the good old days," and renew old friendships. Everyone is invited. Admission is free. 
Nobyembre 24
Ginoong Maria 
Special screening at UPFI
Cine Adarna, UPFI Film Center 
7 p.m.
The film is a black comedy inspired by true events. It is a fictionalo gangster tale and a parody of key figures in the current political arena. Dindo, now a family man, is being recruited back to a syndicate involved in the lucrative business of killing. He is torn between a personal ambition and his loved-ones-a wife and a daughter. These imagined characters are set against the background of a more surreal scenario : a world of extra-judicial killings, fake news, misogynistic and corrupt leaders and fanatic followers. 
Nobyembre 30
Supremo
Special screening at UPFI
Cine Adarna, UPFI Film Center
1 p.m.
The screening of this bio-drama film by Richard V. Somes is to commemorate the 155th birth anniversary of Gat Andres Bonifacio. The film depicts events in history and exemplifies Bonifacio's sacrifice, passion and courage as the founder of the Katipunan. The Katipuneros called him Supremo, the supreme leader of Filipino revolutionaries.

column3:
Ugnayan
Unibersidad ng Pilipinas Diliman. Tomo 4, Blg 11
column7:
Disyembre 1
Ang Pag-ibig Naghahari
Abelardo Hall Auditorium
7 p.m.
The concert is the final offering of the Abelardo Hall Concert Series' Second Season. It will feature the UP Concert Chorus and Baihana. The UP Concert Chorus has been in various international tours and choral competitions and has won numerous festival awards including the Grand Prix at the 6th International Krakow Choir Festival in Poland, and was ranked "Number 1 in the World" for Folk Choral Performance at the 2012 Festival Internacional de Cantonigros in Spain. Formed in May 2008, Baihana has been garnering awards such as the "Best Jazz Band" at the Boy Katindig Songwriting Competition in 2012 and the "Best Performance By A New Group" at the 26th Aliw Awards in 2013. Backed up by the dashing Debonair District, the all-female vocal trio-Anna Graham, Krina Cayabyab and Mel Torre-enjoy executing playful arrangements of vocal harmonies and simply dive into any kind of music genre. 
World AIDS Day with Pusit
Indie advocacy feature by Arlyn de la Cruz
Cine Adarna, UP Film Center
7 p.m. 
The UP Film Institute joins the commemoration of World AIDS Day through a special screening of Pusit. The film, directed by Arlyn de la Cruz, stars Jay Manalo, Kristoffer King, Roli Inocencio, Ronnie Quizon, Lehner Mendoza, Mike Liwag and Elizabeth Oropesa. Based on true accounts, the film is about a salon owner, an overseas worker, a model, a call center agent, a teenage boy, a son of a rich family, and a breadwinner raised by a single mother. The characters are connected and bonded both by hate, persecution and condemnation by a society that is blinded by its own guilt-a reality of the lives of persons living with HIV and AIDS. 
Disyembre 3
Film Screenings with Colombia
UPFI Special Features
Videotheque, UP Film Center
2 and 4 p.m.
The UP Film Institute, in partnership with Embassy of Colombia in the Philippines, will showcase selected Colombian films, namely, Keyla (2 p.m.) and Mama (4 p.m.)
Keyla is an adventure-drama about a young woman living on a Caribbean island searching for her father lost at sea. When her estranged family came to help, she must confront their dark past and this new family she never expected to embrace. The film is written and directed by Viviana Gomez Echeverry and starts Mercedes Salazar, Sebastian Enciso Salamanca and Elsa Whitaker Sanchez. Mama is about Victoria, a reserved and independent woman who apparently enjoys her loneliness. She receives an unexpected visit from her estranged daughter Sara. Sara requested Victoria to take care of her 7-year old daughter for a few days. This favor alters Victoria's life. 
Disyembre 4
Python for Data Science and Analytics
UP National Engineering Center
8 a.m. - 5 p.m. 
The 3-day course is a guide to learn the Python program for analyzing data, creating beautiful visualizations and utilizing powerful predictive analytic algorithms. Composed of lectures, hands-on exercises and real-world data case studies, the training gives an overview of how to use Python and Jupyter notebooks to extract, manipulate and present massive data for management decision-making. At the end of the training, the participants are expected to be confident enough to produce any type of business analytics analysis with Python. 
Disyembre 6
TALASTASAN SA KASAYSAYAN 
"Vietnam : The Chemical War" 
Silid Balatik, Pavilion 2, Palma Hall
2 p.m.
The UP Department of History, together with the Third World Studies Center, present a public lecture on history "Vietnam : The Chemical War." 
Silid Balatik, Pavilion 2, Palma Hall
2 p.m.
The UP Department of History, together with the Third World Studies Center, present a public lecture on history "Vietnam : The Chemical War."
Prof. David Biggs from the University of California at Riverside will deliver a lecture on Vietnam War that will examine the history of the chemical war in Vietnam and the relation of scientific development, particularly in chemistry, at that time. The talk will also examine Vietnam War within a more global context of the post-1945 development of pesticides, the Green Revolution and military escalation in Southeast Asia. 
Disyembre 6
Making Policies Matter : Voter Responses to Campaign Promises (with P. Keefer and F. Trebbi)
A public presentation by Julien Labonne and Cesi Cruz
Rm. 114, Encarnacion Hall
4 -5 p.m. 
Labonne and Cruz's presentation looks into the different responses of voters to the campaign promises made by political candidates. Their analyses also show the impact of campaign promises to the distribution of vote shares. This seminar is organized by the Philippine Center for Economic Development and UP School of Economics and is open to the public on a first-come, first-served basis. 
Disyembre 8
Honor : The Legacy of Jose Abad Santos
UPFI Special Screenings
Cine Adarna, UP Film Center
10 a.m., 1 and 5 p.m.
Showcasing the life and legacy of the late Chief Justice Jose Abad Santos, the film uncovers his numerous virtues that guided him from his humble beginnings, to his scholarly achievements, and eventually to his ascent into prominence. 
The film aims to inspire the younger generation by exposing them to the courageous, honorable and incorruptible nature of Jose Abad Santos - that they may emulate the same values in their own lives. His clean and upright public service record is one of the most outstanding in the history of Philippine governance. His love for the country was unparalleled-choosing death than siding with the Japanese Imperial Forces. His story is one that highlights patriotism, bravery and integrity in the face of overwhelming difficulty. 
Disyembre 8
Pamaskong Handog ng UP
UP Amphitheater
7 p.m.
The Philippine Philharmonic Orchestra (PPO) and UP's top soloists and choral groups will perform immortal orchestral selections, Christmas and inspirational music including Handel's Messiah, one of the grandest and best-known choral and orchestral works of Western music. More than 130 voices comprising soloists and UP choruses, under the musical direction of UP professor and Madrigal Singers choirmaster Mark Anthony Carpio, will be accompanied by the PPO under the baton of Dr. Herminigildo Ranera. 
Disyembre 8
Honor : The Legacy of Jose Abad Santos 
UPFI Special Screenings
Cine Adarna, UP Film Center
10 a.m., 1 and 5 p.m.
Showcasing the life and legacy of the late Chief Justice Jose Abad Santos, the film uncovers his numerous virtues that guided him from his humble beginnings, to his scholarly achievements, and eventually to his ascent into prominence. The film aims to inspire the younger generation by exposing them to the courageous, honorable and incorruptible nature of Jose Abad Santos - that they may emulate the same values in their own lives. His clean and upright public service record is one of the most outstanding in the history of Philippine governance. His love for the country was unparalleled - choosing death than siding with the Japanese Imperial Forces. His story is one that highlights patriotism, bravery and integrity in the face of overwhelming difficulty. 
Disyembre 8
Pamaskong Handog ng UP
UP Amphitheater
7 p.m.
The Philippine Philharmonic Orchestra (PPO) and UP's top soloists and choral groups will perform immortal orchestral selections, Christmas and inspirational music including Handel's Messiah, one of the grandest and best-known choral and orchestral works of Western music. More than 130 voices comprising soloists and UP choruses, under the musical direction of UP profesor and Madrigal Singers choirmaster Mark Anthony Carpio, will be accompanied by the PPO under the baton of Dr. Herminigildo Ranera. The choral groups include the UP Cherubim and Seraphim, UP Concert Chorus, UP Singing Ambassadors, UP Staff Chorale and UP College of Music Chorus Classes. UPD's yearly Christmas include a Messiah staging, which culminates in a grand-scale performance of "Hallelujah". 
Disyembre 10-13
UP CWGS 30th Anniversary Celebration
For its 30th anniversary, the UP Center for Women's and Gender Studies will be holding a week-long celebration with the theme #Kabog At 30 : Trailblazing in Equity and Equality. 
December 10
5 p.m. Zumbabae : Patuloy na Pakikipaglaban para sa Karapatang Pantao at the UP CWGS Courtyard
December 11
2 - 5 p.m. Participatory Video Showcase at the UP CWGS Conference Room
December 12
3 - 5 p.m. Dakila Awards at the Balay Kalinaw
5 - 9 p.m. UP CWGS Anniversary Night at the Balay Kalinaw
December 13
2 - 4 p.m. Review of Women's Studies vol. XXVIII Journal Launch at the UP CWGS Conference Room
Zumbabae and the Participatory Video Showcase are free and open to the public while the Dakila Awards, the UP CWGS Anniversary Night and the Review of Women's Studies vol. XXVIII Journal Launch are closed events. 
Disyembre 11-14
2nd Philippine Symposium on Freshwater Biodiversity and Ecosystems (2nd PSFBE) 
Freshwaters are fragile and finite
Institute of Biology, College of Science
The symposium will gather international and Filipino specialists in limnology, freshwater ecology, biodiversity and conservation biology. Keynote speakers are Dr. Teodoro Herbosa (Executive Vice President of the UP System) and John Morse (Clemson University, USA). The symposium tackle issues that confront freshwater biodiversity and ecosystems, such as overexploitation, climate change and population growth. It will also highlight the overwhelming species - rich freshwater ecosystems. 
Disyembre 13
UP Symphonic Orchestra Inaugural Concert
UP Amphitheater 
6 p.m.
The concert will feature a program of both classical and contemporary orchestral works for the Christmas season, including selections of Christmas arrangements by National Artist for Music Ryan Cayabyab. Several pieces will also bring together a grand chorus representing the UP campuses of pioneering members of the UP Symphony Orchestra (UPSO). UPSO, under the musical direction of Prof. Josefino "Chino" Toledo, consist of 65 members exclusively from the UP community : alumni, students, faculty members and staff from all UP campuses. The Orchestra functions as training ground for both composers and musicians, and as representative of not only the University but also of the country in the worldwide stage. 
Disyembre 14
Paglaot, Pagdaong
UPD Parada at Paligsahan ng mga Parol 2018
UP Academic Oval and UP Amphitheater 
5 p.m.
Parada at paligsahan ng mga pinakamagandang parol ng mga administratibo at akademikong yunit ayon sa temang "Paglaot, Pagdaong." Ipaparada ang mga parol paikot sa Academic Oval at magtatapos sa isang palatuntunan sa Amphitheater. Ito rin ay tatampukan ng fireworks display.

column3:
Ugnayan
Unibersidad ng Pilipinas Diliman. Tomo 5, Blg 2
column7:
Abril 1-27
The Nature of the Collection
1F Galleries, UP Vargas Museum
The Nature of the Collection exhibits some of the most notable paintings of the American and Japanese period in the Philippines done by the first Filipino National Artist, Fernando Amorsolo. The exhibition dwells on Amorsolo's practice in portraiture and landscape as it intersects with the collecting practice of his patron Jorge Vargas. Seen together, the history of painting and history of a collection come together to define a time in Philippine art history through the careers of both Amorsolo and Vargas. Speaking to this condition of embodied nature and labor is the video work of Tanya Villanueva titled "Safe Space, Sacred Space / How we work together." 
Abril 4
The Grammaticalization of Negative Stance Makers in Korean
Rm. 207, Palma Hall
2-4 p.m.
Organized by the UP Department of Linguistics in cooperation with the UP Korean Research Center, this special lecture will be delivered by Dr. Kyou-Dong Ahn of the HanKuk University of Foreign Studies, Seoul, South Korea. 
Abril 4
Bird of Prey
Cine Adarna, UPFI Film Center
7 - 9:30 p.m.
Renowned wildlife cinematographer Neil Rettig embarks on what could be the most challenging assignment of his career : to find and film the rarest eagle on the planet. The documentary explores the vanishing world of the Great Philippine Eagle and reveals the people determined to save it. The award-winning feature-length documentary follows Rettig's return to the Philippines as he embarks on a grueling expedition alongside a small but devoted group determined to protect the eagle. Together, they answer the question : What will it take to save a species from extinction? 
Abril 6
Engg Musika!
A Benefit Concert for the Student Wellbeing (SWELL) Program
DM Consunji Theater, UP Institute of Civil Engineering
7 p.m.
A benefit concert organized by the UP College of Engineering (UPCOE) and the UP Engineering Research and Development Foundation, Inc. (UPERDFI), it aims to raise awareness and support for the SWELL Program to provide students with facilities and services for life coaching, peer support, meditation and relaxation - to overcome stresses and foster an outlook of optimism. The concert features Department of Science and Technology Sec. Boy de la Peña; Dean Divas - Usec Rowena Cristina L. Guevara; Aura Matias and Babylin de Leon; UPERDFI President Chato Calderon; Professors Rhod Gonzalez; Benito M. Pacheco; Glen Tabios, Ji Reyes; Sean Palmiano; UPCOE staff and many more. 
Abril 6
El Fin de la Guerra Civil (The End of the Civil War)
Videotheque, UPFI Film Center
Instituto Cervantes Manila in collaboration with the Embassy of Spain in the Philippines and the UP Film Institute present the following films : "La Colmena" (4:30 p.m.) and "Pa Negre" (6:30 p.m.). La Colmena (The Hive) : Based on a 1943 novel of the same title from Camilo Jose Cela, takes place in the cafe La Delicia narrating the lives of a wide variety of characters from all walks of life who are trying to survive in a poverty-stricken Madrid after the Civil War. Pa Negre (Black Bread) : In the harsh post-war years' Catalan countryside, 11 year-old Andreu comes across the bodies of a man and his son in the woods. When the authorities try to pin the blame on his father, the boy find out the real killer. The experience opens Andreu's eyes to the world of adults who live on lies. The screenings are free and open to the public on a first-come, first served basis. 
Abril 11
University Student Council Elections/Halalan UPD2019
8 a.m. - 7 p.m.
HalalanUPD2019 is the selection of leaders of elective positions in the University Student Council (USC). The elections aims to uphold the autonomous, democratic and representative character of the USC. The University Student Electoral Board is composed of the Vice Chancellor for Student Affairs as ex-officio Chairperson, two faculty members and two student members. 
Abril 13
Teaching Vocabulary in the Second/Foreign Language Classroom Using Japanese and Chinese Examples 
Rm. 428, Palma Hall
9 a.m. - 12 p.m. 
The talk highlights the importance of vocabulary in language teaching. It presents various in-and-out-of-classroom activities to aid students in learning and remembering vocabulary words, and introduces non-textbook sources of vocabulary words and phrases that students can draw from. The event is free and open to the public, but priority will be given to language teachers. 
Abril 22-24
Worker's Institute on Labor Laws (WILL) Class 175
Isabelo Delos Reyes Auditorium, UP SOLAIR 
9 a.m. - 5 p.m. 
The seminar enables the participants to acquire basic knowledge about labor laws and legislation of the country; apply the legal processes and techniques in their respective workplaces; and understand and share experiences and practices involving labor laws and industrial relations. Seminar fee is P9,000 inclusive of meals, copy of Everyone's Labor Code, certificate of completion. Payment can be made in cash or cheque, on or before seminar schedule. 
Abril 22 - Mayo 11
Japanese for Kids & Teens
Palma Hall Pavilion 1
Japanese for Kids (8 y.o. - 12 y.o) :
Mon. - Fri., 1-3 p.m.
Japanese for Teens (13 y.o. - 16 y.o.) :
Mon - Fri., 3- 5 p.m.
The Japanese for Kids & Teens are short-term summer courses designed to teach Japanese to our young language learners (preferably ages 8 to 16). These classes will meet for 15 days. A culminating activity with friends and families of students is scheduled on the last class day. 
Abril 23
La Fashionista
Salvare : Wildlife Protection Through Innovative Fashion
The Encore at the Century Hotel, Balibago, Angeles City, Pampanga
6 p.m.
La Fashionista is the annual fashion competition wherein students can maximize their talents related to fashion. This year's competition is all about bringing boundless creativity to encourage the preservation of the animal wildlife. The contestants will strut the runway and showcase pieces that feature the frolics and colors of the wild. Designers will create exquisite designs inspired by endangered species to promote awareness of their maltreated conditions while at the same time, being fashionable. 
Abril 27
The 5th CSSP Graduate Students Research Conference
CSSP Wellness Center, Palma Hall Pavilion 1 
8:30 a.m.
The CSSP Graduate Students Research Conference (GSRC) aims to bring together graduate students engaged in social science and philosophy research. CSSP GSRC is a venue where they can share insights and findings from studies conducted for their graduate courses, or from the theses or dissertations that they are currently working on. Dr. Peter Sy from the UP Department of Philosophy and Dr. Steven Rood, a fellow-in-residence at the Social Weather Stations and the former Philippine Country Representative of The Asia Foundation will be the conference plenary speakers. 
Abril 27
2019 UP Korean Speech Contest 
Rm. 400, Palma Hall
1-5 p.m.
A continuing tradition of the UP Department of Linguistics, the contest is on its 11th installment, commemorating the 70th anniversary of the bilateral relations between the Philippines and the Republic of Korea. The contest aims to promote the Korean language to UP students, as well as the students of the Asian Language Extramural Classes of the Department. It also aims to engage the students to think about and discuss the concept of "unity" using the Korean language. There are two categories for the contest : (1) for beginner learners, and (2) for advanced learners, including students who have been to Korea for an exchange program.

Nemenzo is 11th UPD Chancellor  by Anna Regidor

Prof. Louise Jashil R. Sonido, an assistant professor at the College of Arts and Letters Department of English and Comparative Literature, was appointed as the new Vice Chancellor for Student Affairs (VCSA) on July 1, 2020.

I have devoted all my life to UP. I have thought long and hard about the life and future of this University, and I treasure this opportunity to lead and shepherd the energy and talents of our faculty, REPS, staff and students toward realizing our ideals, values and dreams as Iskolar ng Bayan, Iskolar para sa Bayan.

Since the establishment of the University of the Philippines Diliman (UPD) as a constituent university of the University of the Philippines System (UP) on Apr. 23, 1985, it has generated various publics, who have continuously motivated UPD administration to perform actively in relation to UP's mandate as the national university.

Introduction by Ruth Jordana L. Pison
When Fidel R. Nemenzo, DSc assumed his post as chancellor of the University of the Philippines Diliman (UPD) on March 1, 2020, he was prepared to move the University towards achieving its goals in the context of a volatile global economy and an extremely interconnected world.
A distinctly Tatak UP education in a time of the pandemic by Loren Agaloos and Jean Aaron de Borja
Academic excellence has always been at the center of the University of the Philippines (UP) strength and pride.

column3: Dean Crisostomo A. Ortigas Correspondence / Letters
column9: Brainy Bosses : More Scientists Leave Their Labs, Take Over Top Posts in Industry : Many Succeed But Some Bog Down in Finance or Spend Too Much Time on Design by Dr. Varian Deserts His Desk IN The Wall Street Journal. Vol. XXXVII, No. 134, April 24, 1957.
(1962, May 18) Upgrade physics instruction in the Philippines. 273 Santa Teresa Street. Stanford, California
column10: Dear Cris : Thanks for your good letter. I have just had a talk with the subject of our correspondence. He is very definitely interested in the possibility of helping you upgrade physics instruction in the Philippine high schools, and I am sure he would welcome a letter from you about the kind of activities that might be involved. Address him thus : Mr. Keith Harworth, 99 Victoria Drive, Atherton, Calif. USA. I do not have a copy of the information I gave you about Mr. Harworth but I think him competent to teach physics to either teachers or students, design fixed equipment for science departments, design apparatus for physics instruction, personally carry out all kinds of fabrication opreations, instruct shop personnel in the use of hand and machine tools, and do ordinary business or personnel management. If he comes to the Philippines he will bring a wife and a son of high school age. Mrs. Harworth has some training and experience in social welfare and she told she might like to pursue a master's degree in that field if it is offered at U.P. If Harworth gets into your field of work I think he should be on some kind of payroll, if only for regularity of status and administration. I think I told you that he does not exactly need to be paid. He is not wealthy by the standards of Philippine millionaires but he would be rated quiet well off by comparison with U.P. faculty standards. Mr. Harworth's chief occupation since the war has been the manufacture of an instrument which he invented and developed for detecting metal in the stomachs of cows. I am told that cows have a bad habit of eating barbed wire and other metallic tidbits. It doesn't inconvenience them but tends to reduce their milk production, so dairymen are continually calling on veterinarians to remove the foreign matter. So here is where Harworth's diagnostic apparatus come in. It is a sort of pocket-sized radar. Harworth is now tired of this business and looking for change. It he comes to the Philippines he will get one.

column9: (1962, April 29) Promotion and engagement of physics teaching. 273 Santa Teresa Street. Stanford, California
column10: I presume that title is still ok; people call me professor even though I was fired three years ago on the theory that men become automatically senile at the age of sixty-five. Actually I was ready for a change and have had some interesting experiences since brushing off the chalk dust of Standard lecture halls. About six weeks ago I wrote to Professor Magno a letter which I thought might bring him some welcome aid, and which seemed at least worthy of a routine reply. Apparently it wasn't. I have now heard that you are engaged in the promotion and improvement of physics teaching so I feel that you might have some interest in the matter that I communicated to Magno. I am saving myself labor by enclosing here a carbon copy of the earlier letter. Please let me know if there is anything in it that you would like to follow up. Now I know that Americans are exasperating creatures to direct and work with, but I assure you that this fellow is a better-than-average American. Last night Mary Rose and I went to delightful party in the Orinda home of Don and Sally Hansen. You would have liked it. Other guests were the Bromleys, the Holloways, the Ganes, and the Ralph Smiths. You can imagine what the talk was about. Don played Philippine records and projected Philippine slides. Everybody suffered a little nostalgia. I am presently putting in several hours a day as a Consultant at Lockhead Space and Missiles, right here on the Stanford lands. I try to help them with the x-ray spectroscopy of space and have a laboratory in which to test out and use the instruments we devise for such work. When they drop me or the work gets uninteresting Mary Rose and I will try some more globe-trotting. Mary Rose sends greetings to Teresa. Sincerely, Paul Kirkpatrick.

column9: (1962, September)
column10: Dear Sigma Pi Sigma Member : Enclosed is your copy of The Radiations which gives you news of the recent activities of the Society and also an advance story on some of the serious problems we will consider at our seventh National Convention to be held at the University of Kansas on October 22-24. Your attention is called especially to the story on page three which will give you some further information concerning the current financial situation of the Society. You will note that last year members contributed more than $6,000 to the Society. Since these contributions accounted for about one-third of the Society's budget, such "alumni dues" are not only gratefully received, but are actually essential for the continued maintenance of our organization and services. It is the hope of the Executive Council that you will want to continue your support of Sigma Pi Sigma. Will you please send your contributions 1962-63 to the Headquarters Office as soon as possible. Your contribution, deductible for income tax purposes, will be used to support the publications of the Society as well as other services we are trying to provide for our chapters and members. You can also be of real help to our Society if you will provide us with up to date information concerning yourself. For this purpose a form and return envelope are enclosed. If you wish to receive a copy of the new edition of our Information Booklet, a membership card (wallet size), or a certificate of sustaining membership (for contributions of $5.00 or more), please indicate these items on the information form. A personal note from you would be greatly appreciated, too. Sincerely yours, Marsh W. White.

column9: (1963, April)
column10: Dear Member : In October 1962 we sent you the enclosed letter and information form, together with an issue of The Radiations dated October 1962. You were asked to return the completed form so that we could update our files with your personal data. Unless we have made a clerical error we think you have not replied to our letter. We are most anxious to hear from you and to know that we have your correct address in our files. During the coming summer we hope to send you another issue of The Radiations, with recent news concerning the activities of the Society. Although we have no desire to annoy you with repeated communications we feel that we should at least go to this additional effort and expense to get in touch with you. So we urgently ask you to let us know whether you received our October communication. In December 1962 a special Post-Convention issue of The Radiations was issued. We will be glad to send you either or both of these recent issues, upon request. We think you will be particularly interested to read about the activities of the national convention held at the University of Kansas October 21-24. Some actions taken by this convention should be of lasting value to the Society and thus will increase the worth to you of membership in the organization. We will appreciate an early reply from you. Sincerely yours, Marsh W. White

column9: (1962, October 29)
column10: Mr. President, In accordance wtih your memorandum to facilitate the liquidation of all dollar aid commodities received under ICA-NEC counterpart projects, I would like to submit the following report. So far 13 projects have been inventoried, liquidated and transferred to the University. 10 projects have not been liquidated including the present U.P. Development Project for reasons hereafter stated. The following are various problems that are encountered in the process of liquidating the dollar commodities under these projects : 1. No complete records are available from the offices of the project directors of receiving units. 2. Responsible employees and officials who were then custodians of records of these properties are now either retired or are no longer in the employ of the University. It is difficult to get in touch with them and secure their assistance. 3. No complete records as to distribution of these commodities were made by former project directors or their assigned employees. 4. Some receiving units did not record properly the commodities they received. 5. Difficulties were encountered in locating records at NEC or at ICA. 6. NEC records are incomplete as found out by the U.P. team sent to NEC to reconstruct records. There are no receiving reports of recipient units available. 7. Some of the reconstructed invoices from NEC do not describe the books delivered. They only indicate the code numbers. 8. Some items, especially books, do not have unit prices. 9. Both from NEC and AID, only the total dollar values of PA's and PIO/C's of these commodities (supposed to be given to U.P.) are available but not the list of items under each PA's or PIO/C's. Without the list of items we cannot immediately proceed with the inventory. We have to reconstruct first the list of items under each PA or PIO/C. The liquidation of these properties is rendered very difficult by the fact that the Property Division was not involved in all the transactions. All the equipment coming under various projects from 1952 up to 1960 were delivered direct to the different units of the University through their respective project directors who took charge of distributing said items. In addition, all papers, documents, invoices, and other supporting papers were in the custody of the project directors who negotiated directly with NEC-ICA but did not furnish the Property Office with duplicate copies of the documents. The central administration of the University therefore has no record of such equipment. Considering the volume of these unliquidated dollar commodities and the scanty records available, coupled wiht the fact that all these project directors and some employees concerned are already out of the service of the University, may I take this occasion to request that additional personnel be appointed, specifically to carry out this liquidation work. The present personnel of the Property and of the Business Offices are not sufficient to carry out the job in a shorter time in spite of extra efforts on their part. Respectfully, Jose C. Campos Jr. Business Executive

column9: (1962, November 16) Philippine Association of Science Teachers c/o Bureau of Public Schools, Manila
column10: Prof. Cristostomo Ortigas, College of Engineering, University of the Philippines, Quezon City. Dear Prof. Ortigas : A national conference and convention of science teachers sponsored by the Philippine Association of Science Teachers, the Bureau of Public Schools, the Bureau of Private Schools, and the UNESCO will be held in Manila on December 5-8, 1962. The theme is "Improving the Teaching of Science for Developing the Skills of Inquiry and Creative Thinking." In this connection, the Board of Directors unanimously approved to invite you as a discussant in physics on the subjects : 1. How effectively do the present classroom procedures assist in developing the skills of inquiry and creative thinking? What are the earmarks of a good classroom procedure that develop critical thinking? How are the skills of inquiry and creative thinking developed in the new curriculum materials such as the PSSC, BSCS, CBA, etc.? 2. How effectively are we attaining our objectives in our choice and utilization of science equipment and facilities? This particular part of the program is scheduled on Thursday, December 6, 1962 from 3:00 to 5:00 p.m at the Manila High Schools, Intramuros. Knowing your keen interest in the promotion of science in the Philippines, it is hoped that you will not fail us and will accept this invitation. Your participation will, indeed, contribute immensely in shaping the course of thinking in the conference. Very truly yours, Aurelio Juele, Vice President for and in the absence of the President

column9: (1963, April 16) Victorino Salcedo High School, Sara, Iloilo
column10: Dean Crisostomo Ortigas, Physics Training Program, U.P. Diliman, Q.C. Dear Sir : With the school year 1962-63 over, I'm hereby submitting an annual report of my work in the Victorino Salcedo High School in Sara, Iloilo as substitute Physics Teacher for Mrs. Lilia M. Tugbang. The Victorino Salcedo High School (VSHS) is a typical town high school with approximately a little more than 500 students enrollment. I was given 2 Physics and 2 Geometry classes in the fourth and third years respectively. From time to time I was made to substitute in teaching General Math and Arithmetic to first and second year students in cases where the subject teacher had to leave for athletic meets as coach. This lasted for a period of 2 to 3 weeks at most. In most instances, this practice disrupted some of our class activities in Physics. So I had to resort to remedial measures. Extra classes were held on Saturday mornings for the 2 Physics classes. And when I had just my regular load, my off periods were also utilized for Physics and Geometry. The first extra classes were actually basic Mathematics classes, inasmuch as 3/4 of the seniors could hardly add fractions nor do algebraic transposition. These extra classes paid off handsomely in the end. With the Math part minimized, the solutions of problems in Physics became easier to cope with. It is with extreme pleasure that I can look back to those Saturday classes when students came, not out of threats or fear of the teacher but out of a genuine desire to learn. Perhaps, to a large extent, this display of a willingness to learn, had accounted for my enthusiasm in teaching. In my student days in U.P., I had the good fortune of being under a professor who made me feel that coming to class was an enjoyable experience rather than a "must". I tried to make my students feel the same way that I felt when I went to that particular professor's class. To break the monotony of the day's lesson, I tried to inject humor and refresher songs in between periods and the class even performed some calisthenics to relieve cramped muscles. I have found out that these worked to advantage and the few minutes that is "lost" is actually on the over-all a "gain", for it made the students' minds more receptive after the brief respite. The students were encourage to ask sensible questions which finally led them to associate daily occurences with physical principles being studied. In so doing, what otherwise seemed so theoretical and abstract began to take form and with it the analysis of the problems became clearer and easier to handle. As much as possible I introduced a new lesson with a demonstration experiement which led students to think on how it was done and why it gave out the results which they saw for themselves. At first they were apt to conclude that the experiment was form of "magic"; for example, why is it that water on a beaker spurts when a tuning fork is placed on the surface after being struck with a mallet? Later on however, they began to think of the results as effects explainable in the light of physical principles. And little by little by they began to know why earthen jars "perspired", why we see rainbow colors in gasoline-spotted roads, why natural occurences which we usually take for granted in the absence of a reliable explanation. To add variety to our class activities, we held outdoor experiments. Field trips were also organized. The most prominent of these was the educational trip we had in Iloilo City where we visited industrial plants, a radio station, a printing press and a university where students were shown the science facilities of the school. To reach Iloilo City at 8:00 A.M. we had to leave Sara as early as 3:00 A.M. and although the education trip was quite taxing, the group of 120 seniors and 8 faculty members that came back that evening of the same day were gratified to know a little bit more of the common knowledge of how coke is made, how newspapers are printed, how radio broadcasts come about and the like. My teaching experience in Sara was not without difficulties however. One of the pressing problems which I encountered was the lack of textbooks and reference materials. Roughly for every 4 to 5 students only 1 book was available. To top this, in our library, there was only 1 reference book for Physics and 2 obsolete laboratory manuals. To minimize the difficulty of teaching, I had to resort partly to outlining the course of study to the students. In this connection, a "Borrow-a-Book" program was initiated. Now this consisted of 3 reference books I took along from Manila plus the materials gathered in U.P. like Prof. Asperilla's "Physics for Filipino Students", Ralph Wilson's UNESCO experiments, and the PSSC materials. The students could borrow these books during the day or loan them for overnight and even week-end uses. To further offset the setbacks of inadequate textbooks, I finally resorted to utilizing the brighter students to tutor the weaker ones. The performance of the latter determined a certain percentage of the student-teacher's grade. To keep up the enthusiasm of the students, I gave out small prizes at the end of each grading period to the team whose marks improved best regardless of their previous grades. These measures however cannot altogether substitute for the inadequacy of books. Since it would be highly expensive to supply schools of this kind with complete texts, I think the problem will be diminished in size if efforts towards acquiring better reference materials will be revitalized. Aside from inadequate textbooks, another major problem which confronted me was the absence of essential laboratory apparatus. The school did not even have to its name a single thermometer or spring balance, to mention a few. We used improvised materials as substitute devices; e.g. we used milk and juice cans as beakers and overflow cans, oil cans with triangular cuttings at the sides as tripods, layers of sand, gravel, and charcoal and cotton placed layer by layer in bottles as filters and the like. What we could not possibly substitute for, we bought with funds coming out of the meager allowances of the students. I tried to make the students feel that this project was theirs as much as it was mine by trying to chip in a part of my salary in the same spirit in which the students gave away a part of their allowances. We were able to raise P60.00 and with it, we bought 2 thermometers, graduated cylinders, beakers, rubber tubing, wire gauzes, alcohol lamps and test tubes. Not one of these devices was broken or even marred by scratches at the end of the year. It just shows how much the students cared for them, fully knowing that the devices were theirs. These devices were left to the school as a donation of class '63 for the use of the coming years. The school celebrated its "Community Day" (it should aptly be termed High-School Day) last Feb. 26. In this connection, the Physics and Geometry classes under me sponsored a joint exhibit of science projects. With the help of Peace Corps members stationed in the Sara Elementary School, I was able to secure a UNESCO book containing interesting projects which could be fashioned out of local and easily available materials. The projects meant work but the students looked to them with eagerness. My training in wood shop and welding and soldering did me much good, as students came to ask how parts of their devices could be joined best and what materials to use. After we labored till dusk in the school grounds to finish the projects as I forbade the students to bring home their projects since it would tempt them to let others do their projects and it would thus defeat the purpose for which they are being made. I must admit that the projects were launched wiht a bit of pessimism and agitation on my part since most of them were new to me in the sense that I've never made them themself. But I tried to conceal from the students whatever misgivings I had and I must say in all modesty that what came out during the exhibits was beyond me. It was a fine exhibit, if I must say so myself. The students were able to make miniature electric buzzers, telegraph key and sounder, electric quizzers, elecric doorbells, steam engins, kaleidscopes, musical bottles, a weather house, hair hygrometer, a miniuature street lighting system, revolving lamps fashioned like an aquarium operating on the principle of convection currents and many more, including a fireless cooker, sundials, and the like. For the first time in the history of the school, an exhibit of that kind was put up. Townpeople and teachers from neighboring towns flocked the exhibit room and although the day left us, students and teachers alike, worn out with fatigue, I think I still have to meet a more rewarding experience. For one thing, that event taught me never to underestimate the ability of the young. Indeed they store a tremendous capacity for invention and quite a lot of amazing ingenuity. These projects were later on donated by the students to the science room of the school for posterity's sake. This year witnessed the absence of the national exams given to the fourth year high school students by the BPS. In its place, the Division Office of Iloilo gave instead a division test similar in type to that of the national exams. The highest score obtained by my students in Physics was a rating of 92% which tied with the highest score of the Iloilo Provincial High School for the first time. Comparison of medians obtained by the different public schools in Iloilo is not yet possible to this date as the results are still under the study committee. According to Mr. Jose Dator, science supervisor for the Division of Iloilo, the results will be released not later than the opening of the school year. Only then can I reliably comment on my students' performance but I am optimistic about them. This about sums up the work I have done in the Victorino Salcedo High School. Whatever success I've had in the projects I initiated, I owe mostly to the unselfish support of our lady principal, Miss Fortunata Alcantara, to the members of the faculty, to the students whose devotion I feel I'll find hard to meet again, to the generous townpeople of Sara who on every occasion of my needs lent a helping hand without much of fuss or ado and to the undeniable blessings and guidance of God. I have no regrets for having been assigned to Sara, although I must admit that I came to the place with great apprehension not knowing a single soul. And yet funny as it may seem, I left the place I hardly wanted to go to in the first place, with a feeling almost akin to pain, for it meant uprooting the roots I have steadily sunk through its grounds with the passing of days, weeks and months. But such is the nature of my job as a member of the Mobile Task Force. I can only look forward with the hope that my next assignment will have in store for me such fond memories that I now keep of the Victorino Salcedo High School of Sara, Iloilo. Respectfully yours, Lydia C. Lopez (Mobile Task Force 1962)

column9: (1964, January 22)
column10: Mr. President, Prof. Crisostomo A. Ortigas, College of Engineering, is due to retire in a month's time. My office cannot issue him clearance for his property accountability because as Project Director of NEC-Counterpart Projects from 1953-1959, he could not account for several items delivered to his custody. His failure to account for many of the equipment under NEC-ICA projects prevents the completion of the full inventory of all NEC-ICA property in the University of the Philippines. As a consequence, the formal turnover of these properties to the University cannot be done. On his own request, I recommend that Prof. Ortigas be authorized to deal with NEC-AID and assist Mr. Arsenio Talingdan in this unfinished work so that this matter may be concluded as soon as possible. Respectfully yours, Jose C. Campos Jr., Business Executive, Approved : Jan. 24, 1964. Carlos P. Romulo, President, University of the Philippines.

column9: (1964, February 3)
column10: Memorandum for : Mr. Arsenio P. Talingdan, Management Analyst and IBM Coordinator, University of the Philippines. On my recommendation, Prof. Crisostomo A. Ortigas was designated by President Romulo to confer with NEC-AID on the property accountability of various ICA projects. He is given full authority to negotiate with NEC-AID on this matter insofar as it concerns him as former Project Director. He shall keep you informed of developments working independently of your office. Jose C. Campos, Jr. Business Executive.

column9: (1964, March 4) 1st Indorsement
column10: Respectfully returned to the President, University of the Philippines, with the following comment : I am endorsing the recommendation of Prof. Crisostomo A. Ortigas to give him authority on condition that he works with Mr. Talingdan who was designated as Chairman of the U.P. Committee at a conference with NEC-AID personnel last year. This work on the liquidation of NEC-AID property was entrusted by the President to the undersigned in June, 1962. Personnel assigned to do this had no records or papers to start with because the Property Office had no records of NEC-ICA properties received by the University except those received when the Executive Vice-President was made Deputy Project Director. They had to reconstitute records from documents available only at NEC and ICA offices. Subsequently Prof. Ortigas turned over a partial list which was not complete. The inventory work was done with the sanction of the Dean and the assistance of personnel of the College of Engineering. The help of Prof. Ortigas was sought through the Dean but we did not hear from him until January, 1964 when he saw me at the Property Office and he was about to retire. I agree with Prof. Ortigas that it takes a technical man to gather all the data in this particular project. In the present case, his assistance if given early enough, would have facilitated matters. It was my own idea to draft Prof. Ortigas after his retirement and request for his voluntary services so that these projects could be liquidated. I had to do this without the knowledge of the Dean of the College of Engineering. Of the several NEC-ICA projects in the University, this is one of those which cannot be cleared because our personnel report shows the University cannot account for property worth some P600,000. I am glad to know that Dean Ortigas has finally taken an active part in the liquidation of this project to which only he holds the key. Jose C. Campos, Jr. Business Executive.

column9: (1964, January 6)
column10: Professor Crisostomo A. Ortigas (Thru the Dean, College of Engineering) University of the Philippines. Dear Prof. Ortigas : This is just a reminder of the provision of law [C.A. 186, Sec. 12 (e), as amended] regarding compulsory and automatic retirement which in your case will be on February 2, 1964--your 65th birthday anniversary. C.A> 186, Sec. 12 (e) provides : "(e)...It shall be the duty of the employer concerned to notify each such employee under its direction of the date of his automatic separation from the service at least sixty days in advance thereof." Very truly yours, Onofre D. Corpuz, Secretary of the University

The Chairman, National Economic Council, Manila. Attention : Mr. Simbulan, Fiscal Comptroller II, Dear. Mr. Simbulan : On behalf of the University of the Philippines, the undersigned hereby requests that a meeting amongst NEC, AID and UP be held at your earliest convenience for the purpose of taking action on the liquidation of counterpart projects in Diliman. When we left your  office on 6 February, we carried the impression that such a meeting might be held during the last week of March if UP can submit before 15 March an inventory approximating the value of dollar commodities received under different PA's and PIO/C's in Diliman projects. We are delivering to you today an inventory on Form 41-A in the amount of $1,275,969.98. This amount does not as yet include a large number of books, the numerous lists for which we are still processing. We are requesting that we be allowed to utilize March 14 (Saturday) and March 15 (Sunday) for completing booklists on Form 41-A as well as for segregating Industrial Pharmacy equipment from those of the Department of Chemistry, which are mixed up in PA 2283 and PA 3034 in this $1,275,969.98 inventory. The completion of book listing and rearrangement of PA 2283 and PA 3034 will be delivered to you on or before 19 March. Hoping that this request for a March meeting will meet with your approval, I am. Truly yours, C. A. Ortigas, Former Project, Director (1953-1959)

Dean Crisostomo A. Ortigas, University of the Philippines, Diliman, Quezon City. Dear Dean Ortigas : This is to extend to you an offer to work with us here at Central Philippine University in the development of our Science and Math teacher training program. Your designation would be Coordinator of Science and Math Teacher Training. In this connection, you would work in coordination with our existing teacher training program headed by Dr. Nelson. You would also work closely with the Director of Instruction, Dr. Ruiz. You would be free to develop this program in consultation with the Administration as you saw fit and within the financial limitations of the University's budget. You would also be asked to carry some teaching load with possible assignments in the Math and Physical Department and in the College of Engineering. May we request you to allows us to for the moment to hold in abeyance the question of compensation until we can think it through more fully. Your service would begin at the opening of the regular school year in August 1964. Sincerely yours, Joseph T. Howard, President

column9: (1964, August 5)
column10: Prof. Crisostomo A. Ortigas, Central Philippine University/ Dear Prof. Ortigas : This is to notify you of your appointment as Teacher Training of Central Philippine University on the following terms and conditions : 1. You will perform the duties and responsibilities pertaining to your above-named office and take charge of such co-curricular and extra-curricular responsibilities as the Administration may assign to you, during the following periods : August 1, 1964 to June 30, 1965. Provided, that this appointment shall not become effective for each succeeding period, as specified above, unless you shall have presented to the Administration, within the month immediately preceding the starting date of said period, a medical certificate from the University physician, or his authorized representative evidencing your physical and mental fitness to continue handling the above-mentioned full-time responsibilities for the said entire period. 2. On the basis of the foregoing full-time services, you will receive a basic monthly salary of P600.00, payable in semi-monthly installments during the effective periods of this appointment. Should it become necessary, during the term of this appointment, to revise or change your basic monthly salary in accordance with existing University policies, the appropriate rider/s embodying the terms and conditions mutually agreed upon will be attached hereto as a part of this appointment. 3. You will be entitled to the following privileges : (a) Vacation pay, at your basic monthly salary rate, for the months of May and June, on the condition that you will have rendered continuous and uninterrupted full-time service during the entire school year; otherwise, such vacation pay shall be reduced in proportion to the length of service actually rendered during the said school year. (b) Insurance benefits, if you join the existing University group insurance plan. (c) Existing Social Security benefits. (d) Free tuition for your children, who are single and undergraduate, if they study in the University, in accordance with the following arrangement : one child, free tuition; the second child, 2/3 free tuition; the third child, 1/3 free tuition; thereafter, no free tuition. This number shall be increased to include an additional child, at free tuition, for each five years of your successful full-time service to the University. (e) With the written approval of the Administration, one-half free tuition for one subject of three (3) units if you pursue a graduate course or a professional law course; or free tuition for one subject of three (3) units if you pursue an undergradute course. The approval of this privilege by the Administration shall be based on the condition that the course you will take is in your field of work. (f) Hospital privileges at the Iloilo Mission Hospital in accordance with the existing agreement between said Hospital and the University. (g) Certain leaves with pay in accordance with the existing policies of the University, upon written approval by the Administration of a proper application therefore. 4. You will give full-time service to the University, agreeing not to teach in, or be connected with, any other school or to engage in outside business or occupation without the consent of the Administration. (5) You are expected to know and observe strictly the ideals and principles of the University, among which are : (a) High standard of personal conduct and moral character, in family relationships and in personal and business dealings with others. (b) Responsibility for efficient service, as shown by punctuality and regularity in office attendance and in making reports, efficiency in the performance of office and other responsibilities, and continued professional growth and attitude. (c) Unreserved loyalty to the Administration of the University and to colleagues, as expressed in the spirit of cooperation and in the interest of safeguarding the University's welfare, improvement and growth. 6. You are constantly to keep in mind the purpose of the University - "mental and moral instruction, and the manual industrial training of young men and young women, under influences which strengthen Christian faith and build up Christian character." Your exemplary conduct in and outside the classrooms is a part of such influences. 7. This appointment will automatically terminate, without need of notice, at the expiration of the period specified in No. 1 of this appointment, unless sooner terminated by the Administration for any of the following reasons : your refusal or failure to comply with any of the obligations stated in No. 4; or your failure to observe the ideals and principles of the University, among which are enumerated in No. 5; or when a national or financial crisis does not permit the normal operation of the University or the department in which you are connected; or for any other justifiable cause. 8. This appointment may be terminated by resignation under exceptional circumstances and for good and valid reasons shown; however, no resignation will be considered unless it is submitted at least two (2) months before its date of effectivity, and it shall be always subject to the acceptance of the Administration to be given in writing. If the foregoing terms and conditions are acceptable to you, please indicate your acceptance of this appointment by signing your name on the proper space below and returning the original and a duplicate copy to the Office of the President. This appointment will not be complete as a contract unless the aforementioned copies duly signed by you, are received by our Office within 15 days from the date hereof. Sincerely yours, Joseph T. Howard, President

Prof. Crisostomo A. Ortigas, P-1, Area I, U.P. Campus Diliman, Quezon City. Dear Dean Ortigas, I'm sure you have already received our telegram confirming that we are willing to make your base pay P600.00 per month. This will be flat rate compensation and anything over six units of teaching will be compensated at P60.00 per three unit subject which we pay for graduate level "overloads." This will verify that your services will be directly under my office. The concept of "Coordinator" is that you will work with not under the Directors because your project will over-lap into several different fields. It also makes it possible for me to work out this appointment without clearing with the University Board of Trustees since their policy is that "no administrative office may be appointed without official Board action." Not that I would anticipate their objections but they are not meeting until the last of August and I want this finalized now, if possible. As regards quarters, I'm sorry that actions have already been taken which make the empty mission residences unavailable. One will be used by the Fulbright professor, one by the new classes we are opening in the Elementary (if they materialize), and the other by a visiting professor for the Seminary who is due in September. I repeat my regrets that none are available. Dr. Catedral was in the office this morning and said their place is still available if you are interested. Otherwise, we might be able to try to work out something with some other faculty member on the campus if you were interested. Your "Two-phase" development of the program sounds most acceptable to us. I should like to add that we would like for you to work with the "follow-up" program we plan for the NSDB students who were here this past summer. Also it is our hope that you might give us guidance and assistance in applying for Foundation grants which could be used to develop this program. We are aware that for the 1964-65 year this would be difficult as most organizations of this sort have already written their budgets for said year but we should like to be "working on it" all along with your experienced hand at the helm. May we be pleased to have your definite confirmation soon relative to your acceptance of the position of Coordinator of Science and Math Teacher Training. Looking forward to your early favorable reply, I remain. Sincerely yours, Joseph T. Howard, President

column9: (1964, August 14)
column10: Prof. Crisostomo A. Ortigas, Central Philippine University, Iloilo City. Dear Prof. Ortigas : In connection with the contract which we are holding for you, we are requesting you to get a physical examination at Mission Hospital which according to our routine procedures requires the following : "... a medical certificate from our University Physician stating that you have undergone a thorough physical and medical examination, including a chest x-ray (not just a flouroscopy) and that you have been found to be physically fit to assume full teaching and other responsibility." As soon as you can present this certificate, you will be eligible to receive your contract. Sincerely yours, Joseph T. Howard, President
