column9: (1964, December 15)
column10: Prof. Crisostomo A. Ortigas, Central Philippine University, Iloilo City. I find your proposed TTTFC Project challenging and practical even exciting as I believe it is set up to meet a crying need and also to develop the potential of manpower in this country in a unique way. No such program exists in this part of the Philippines. Far from exceeding the bounds of CPU policy, you have given substance to her traditions and educational dedication by formulating this project. Ford Foundation thinks big! They are not interested in peanuts. You have thought big. I'm sure you are prepared to give details if they are requested. Sincerely yours, Joseph T. Howard, President

column10: Prof. Crisostomo Ortigas, Central Philippine University, Iloilo City. Dear Prof. Ortigas : I have already given you verbal notice of your appointment as Director of Research of Central Philippine University but I herewith quote to you the action of the Board of Trustees so that it might be a matter of record with you. Their resolution was as follows : "CPUT 651.22-11 VOTED : That Prof. Crisostomo Ortigas be appointed Director of Research of Central Philippine University in accordance with the recommendation of the President." A job description of this position will be forthcoming and your assistance in its preparation will be greatly appreciated. This will also inform you that you are now an official member of the Administrative Commission of the University. Said Commission has regular stated weekly meetings at 7:00 in the Conference Room every Wednesday morning. You will be a valued and valuable member of said Commission, I am sure. We look forward to a happy and productive relationship with you as our Director of Research which, as I indicated to you verbally, will include the work you are already doing in connection with Math and Science Teacher Training plus other research development. Sincerely yours, Joseph T. Howard, President

Dear Prof. Ortigas, I am pleased to inform you that the Madrigal Singers of the Conservatory of Music, University of the Philippines are coming to Iloilo City on 26 October 1964 to sing at the University of San Agustin Auditorium at 7:00 o' clock in the evening. Willys jeep 4 wheel drive 4 cylinder canvass cover cost P14,500.00, same brand 2 wheel drive canvass cover cost P10,800.00 all new can be bought from Delta Waters, and Embassy Waters dealer for Toyota & Willys. Professor C.A. Ortigas, Area, I, P-1, University of the Philippines Diliman, Philippines. Dear Professor Ortigas : Several weeks ago, I answered your interesting letter of May 25, 1962. As I am not now certain I addressed it correctly, I fear perhaps you are waiting for my reply. Let me know whether or not you have received my letter, for if you have not, I want to send you the information you requested. Yours very truly, Keith Harworth, 99 Victoria Drive, Atherton , California, U.S.A.

Prof. Melicio Magno, Department of Physics, University of the Philippines, Quezon City, Phil. Dear Professor Magno : Although we have not met I have followed your career for several years and often wonder about the progress of the work which you and your colleagues are not performing at the University of the Philippines. The present letter is prompted by this interest. I remember your Department as chronically understaffed and overworked; possibly the situation is better today, but if you are presently in need of qualified manpower you may be interested to read on. A friend and former student of mine - a male American forty-seven years of age - has decided to move with his family to the Philippines, even though he has no definite occupational plans. He is a little tired of the culture which has always surrounded him and he wants a change. He says California is getting overpopulated, overmechanized and overconventionalized, and he pictures the Philippines as a place where he might live a little more closely to nature, enjoy a change in the human environment, and experience some warm weather, which he likes. No, he is not running away from a bad past or anything of the sort; he is an individualist who finds it natural to respond to his drives and impulses. I may say that there is more than wanderlust to his promptings. He has always worked hard and is in a position to spend the rest of his life as he pleases, and what pleases him is not the life of leisure but the exercise of his considerable abilities in activities useful to other members of his species. A remunerative but essentially unproductive job would not now appeal to him in the least, but some project - perhaps poorly compensated - which could yield him the feeling of beneficial accomplishment is what will appeal to him. As to his job qualifications, he is practically a Ph.D. in physics, having completed the Stanford course work and research required for the degree. His dissertation has been written, accepted, and bound, and an approved abridgement of it may be found in the Physical Review. He does not possess the degree because war came along and prevented his taking the final oral examination. This is an original and resourceful man who has made most of his money manufacturing and marketing his own inventions. He is an expert machinist and is skilled at electronic design and repair. A quarter of a century age he had a job as teacher of physics at the University of Hawaii. He has never forgotten his enjoyment in this brief experience as a teacher, and I feel sure that he was a good one. He has given me permission to lay his qualifications before you, though he has little idea right now as to where in the Philippines he would like to live and work. Because of my interest in UP I should like to see him go there. He could bring you some skills and abilities which the Department once needed badly and which are not easy to find or afford in the Philippine market. Please let me know as promptly as possible if you would like to have this prospect communicate with you or if you would like to receive any more information about him from me. Do pass my warmest greetings to all of my old friends in the Department. Very sincerely yours, Paul Kirkpatrick

Dean Crisostomo A. Ortigas, University of the Philippines Diliman, Quezon City. Sir : I take pleasure in informing you of your appointment as Member of the Science Working Group under the Office of the President. The Science Working Group is composed of the following : Ledr Marcelino E. Calinawan, Jr., Chairman; Dr. Manuel V. Arguelles, Member; Dr. Joaquin Marañon, Member; Dr. Patrocinio Valenzuela, Member; Dean Crisostomo A. Ortigas, Member; Dr. Mariano P. Ramiro, Member; Dr. Mariano P. Ramiro, Member; Mr. Hugo Fernandez, Member; Mr. Manuel C. Herrera, Member; Mr. Dominador Reyes, Member; Mr. Ramon A.D. Hermano, Member (Executive Secretary). The group shall perform the following functions : 1. To recommend ways and means of effecting an integrated and coordinated science effort for the country; and 2. To recommend ways and means of promoting the active participation of private sectors in research and utilization of products of research. This group is hereby authorized to call upon any department, bureau, office, agency or instrumentality of the government including government-owned and controlled corporations, for such assistance or information as it may need in the performance of its functions. The group will submit its report and recommendations as soon as practicable, and thereafter shall cease to exist. On behalf of the President of the Philippines and on my own, permit me to express appreciation for the valuable service we hope you would extend to the Science Working Group. Very truly yours, Marcelino E. Calinawan, Jr., Chairman, Special Assistant to the President on Science Coordination

column9: (1961, October 5)
column10: Mr. Crisostomo Ortigas, College of Engineering, University of the Philippines Diliman, Quezon City. Dear Mr. Ortigas : I have the honor to inform you that, at the meeting of a Committee concerned with the launching of the Philippine Academy of Science and Humanities, you have been named member of a Special Committee to nominate the first members of the Academy. The members of this Special Committee are : Dr. Paulino J. Garcia, Chairman; Chief Justice Cesar Bengzon; Minister Emilio Abello; Mr. Eugenio Lopez; Mr. Herminigildo B. Reyes; Mr. Crisostomo Ortigas; Dr. Salvador Araneta; Mr. Carlos Fernandez; Mr. Jose P. Bautista. As Chairman of the Committee, I would like to invite you to a luncheon meeting at Minister Abello's Office at the Philam Life Building on Isaac Peral on Friday, October 13 at 12:15 p.m. On Wednesday, October 18, there shall be another luncheon meeting of a bigger committee to determine the final structure of the Academy. It shall be held at 727 Herran at 12:15 p.m. Enclosed herewith are the minutes of the first three meetings of the Academy, its Tentative Constitution and Criteria of Membership. Kindly fill up the attached form according to your good pleasure and have it sent to Miss Emerita S. Quito, Secretary of the Committee, c/o National Science Development Board, Port Area, P.O. Box 3596, or telephone 3-64-76 local 34. With assurance of my warm personal regards, I am. Sincerely yours, Paulino J. Garcia, M.D. Chairman

column9: (1961, August 22) 1st Meeting. Minutes of the First Meeting of the Committee on the Proposed Philippine Academy of Science and Humanities, held on April 22, 1961 at 727 Herran.
column10: Members present : Dr. Paulino J. Garcia, Chairman; Fr. Isidro Katigbak, O.P. represented by a Dominican Father; Fr. Horacio de la Costa, S.J.; Dr. Otley Beyer; Justice J.B.L. Reyes; Mr. Carlos Fernandez; Mr. Felix B. Bautista; Miss Emerita S. Quito, Secretary of the Committee. Others present : Vice-Chairman Casimiro del Rosario; Commissioner Florencio A. Medina; Commissioner Canuto G. Manuel; Dr. Arsenio C. Regala; Dr. Frank CoTui; Atty. Manuel C. Herrera. At 4:45 p.m. the Chairman, Dr. Paulino J. Garcia, called a meeting to order. Each provision of the tentative By-laws (see attached copy) was discussed. A Committee on Style was set up to put the By-laws in order. This Committee was made up of Justice J.B.L. Reyes as chairman, and of Fr. Horacio de la Costa, S.J., Atty. Manuel C. Herrera & Miss Emerita S. Quito as members. It was also agreed that Dr. H. Otley Beyer be consulted regarding the criteria of membership. Telegrams were to be sent to Dr. Detlev W. Bronk, President, National Academy of Science of the United States and Prof. T.M. Cherry, President, Australian Academy of Science for similar information. The meeting was adjourned at 6:45 p.m. Minutes of the Second Meeting of the Committee on the Proposed Philippine Academy of Science & Humanities, held on September 14, 1961 at 727 Herran. Members present : Dr. Paulino J. Garcia, Chief Justice Cesar Bengzon, Fr. Francisco Araneta, S.J. Dr. Antonio Isidro, Undersecretary of Education Jose Tuazon, represented by Dra. Liceria Soriano, Mrs. Geronima Pecson, Mr. Emilio Abello, Mr. Carlos Fernandez, Miss Emerita S. Quito. Dr. Paulino J. Garcia called the meeting to order at 4:15 p.m. and promptly requested Chief Justice Bengzon to preside. Each provision of the revised tentative By-laws (see attached copy) of the Academy of Science & Humanities was taken up. Some changes in the make-up of the Academy were effected viz. that the division of the Academy be left to the members to decide. The Committee agreed to meet on September 27, 1961 at a luncheon meeting at 727 Herran.

column9: (1961, September 27) Minutes of the Third Meeting of the Committee on the Philippine Academy of Sciences and and Humanities, held at the NIST Conference Hall, 727 Herran on Wednesday, September 27, 1961 from 12:00 to 3:00 p.m.
column10: Members Present : Dr. Paulino J. Garcia, Chairman; Chief Justice Cesar Bengzon; Justice J.B.L. Reyes; Rev. Fr. Francisco Araneta S.J.; U.P. Vice President Antonio Isidro; Minister Emilio Abello; Dr. H. Otley Beyer; Mr. Jose P. Bautista; Miss Emerita S. Quito, Secretary of the Committee. Guests Present : Dr. Henri Marcel GAUSSEN, Member Correspondent de l'Academie des Sciences de France; Dr. Lawrence Fuchs, Peace Corps. Others Present : Mrs. Rolsainda C. Garcia, Dra. Josefina Magno, Miss Josefina Constantino and Mr. Ramon Hermano. The meeting was begun by a short talk of Dr. Gaussen on the French Academy of Science, a copy of which is hereby attached. He was followed by Dr. Lawrence Fuchs who commented on the momentous occasion and thanked the Chairman for having invited him to be an "eyewitness to history." The members of the Committee then proceeded to go over the revised Tentative Constitution of the proposed Academy. Dr. Isidro initiated steps to formalize the organization of the Academy so that it may not be duplicated by another group. A preamble was suggested by Fr. Araneta. A committee to nominate the first members of the Academy was set up. It is composed of : Dr. Pauline J. Garcia, Chairman; Chief Justice Cesar Bengzon; Crisostomo Ortigas; Hemenegildo B. Reyes; Salvador Araneta; Eugenio Lopez; Emilio Abello; Jose P. Bautista; Carlos Fernandez. An NSDR Committee to aid in making available data needed by this aforementioned Committee was set up by Chairman Garcia : Emerita S. Quito; Josefina D. Constantino; Rosalinda C. Valenzuela; Dolores Magnayo. It was also agreed that Universities be circularized in order that they may send in their suggestions. The next meeting was tentatively set for Wednesday, October 18, 1961.

column9: (1959, September 16)
column10:
Dr. Paulino Garcia
Chairman, National Science Development Board
Herran, Manila

Dear Dr. Garcia : 

This is in connection with your invitation for me to represent the University of the Philippines in the meetings of your Board. As I told you, I find it difficult to be present in your meetings on account of the number of engagements that I have to attend to. I am a member of ten boards and committees outside and chairman of one dozen or more committees within the University. But I thought I could be present once in a while in your Board, not however as a member but as a visitor perhaps. The reason is that the law, R.A. No. 2067, does not make a member. In its Section 5 it provides that the "Board shall be composed of a chairman; a vice chairman...and the following members : the chairman of the National Research Council of the Philippines, the commissioner of the National Research Council of the Philippines, the commissioner of the National Institute of Science and Technology, the commissioner of the Philippine Atomic Energy Commission, the director of the Office of National Planning of the National Economic Council, a representative from the University of the Philippines to be designated by the President of the University, one member representing industry, one member representing scientific and/or technological associations or societies, one member representing agriculture, and one member representing education."
From this provision it is quite clear that I have no right to be present as member of the Board because it would be the height of absurdity if I appoint myself as representative of the University. Strictly speaking, it is even illegal. 
However, I have decided to appoint Dr. Enrique T. Virata, Executive Vice President of the University of the Philippines as the representative of the University in the National Science Development Board. 
I have also designated Dean Crisostomo Ortigas to continue as our project director; and in this connection, the University of the Philippines Industrial Research Center, Project 2.1, needs the contemplated assistance for its full implementation. I hope you will give it your usual attentive and kindly consideration. 
Cordially yours, 
V.G. Sinco
President

column9: (1959, August 12)
column10: Dean C.A. Ortigas, College of Engineering, University of the Philippines, Quezom City. Dean Ortigas : I was very happy to meet you after the talk of Father Glover at U.P. Monday. On my return here, I looked for that book by Hopf which Father Glover recommended. Since we are not using it at present, we feel that it may be of help to you for the members of your very select group in Partial Differential Equations. By separate mail, I am sending this to you. Science is such an exacting disciplines and there is so much need of it in our modern world that every means of cooperating in the furtherance of science should be taken. I hope that you will find this book of some help to your interested class. I know that Father Glover before his departure yesterday for the States was concerned that you have access to this book. Sincerely yours, J.J. Hennessey, S.J. Director

Dean Crisostomo Ortigas, National Science Development Board, Herran, Manila. Dear Dean Ortigas : In behalf of the National Committee on the observance of the National Science and Technology Week, please accept my deep appreciation and gratitude for your inestimable cooperation, without which the week would not have been as successful as it was. Believe me when I say that it is only through your unfailing assistance that the great task of scientific development can be pursued to its full realization. It is with profound optimism that I look forward to the prosecution of proposals made during the Week for scientific and technological advancement. I have high hopes that with our joint efforts, the significance of the week will be translated into tangible terms for science and country. Very sincerely, Paulino J. Garcia, M.D., Chairman, National Committee 1959 National Science and Technology Week

column9: (1960, January 25)
column10:
Dean Crisostomo Ortigas, College of Engineering, University of the Philippines, Diliman, Quezon City
Dear Dean Ortigas : Please accept my deep thanks for the assistance and support you and your office readily extended the NSDB and the PAEC in connection with the laying of the cornerstone of the Philippine Atomic Research Center in Quezon City last January 22nd. In spite of the limited time allowed for planning and arrangements, the final ceremonies - thanks to you and many others - achieved its basic purpose, which was to convey to the public the significant role our reactor project would play in Philippine scientific growth. I can say in all sincerity that without your generous cooperation the complete success of the cornerstone - laying program would not have been possible. Sincerely yours, Paulino J. Garcia, M.D., Chairman

Dr. Vincent G. Sinco, President, University of the Philippines Diliman, Quezon City, The Philippines
My dear Dr. Sinco : After a somewhat long business trip, I am back in Chicago and I hasten to express my deep appreciation for the many courtesies that you, Dean Ortigas, and Vice President Virata extended to me during my stay in The Philippines last March. Both Mrs. Gollong and I look back with pleasure to the evening we spent together and hope that it will not be too long before you revisit Chicago so that we may have the opportunity to entertain you and your wife. Upon my return to the States, I learned that ICA/Washington has contracted with the National Science Foundation for the provision of a Science Advisor to the National Science Development Board. From Washington, we learned that the initiation of the industrial research program needs to await the report of this advisor. We can only hope that this additional delay will be short so that, finally, the long-contemplated program at the University of the Philippines may be initiated. Because of your own intense interest in the program, we intend to keep you informed of developments as we learn about them here in the States. In turn, should certain aspects occur which you deem of interest to us, we would appreciate learning about them. Both Mrs. Gollong and I would like to extend our warmest personal regards to you and Mrs. Sinco. Sincerely, Paul B.W. Gollong, Director of International Research

Dean C.A. Ortigas, College of Engineering, University of the Philippines, Diliman, Quezon City, The Philippines. My dear Dean Ortigas : You can imagine my surprise to learn upon my return to the States that in spite of the numerous conversations held between NSDB and USOM/Manila, ICA/Washington contracted with the National Science Foundation for the services of a science advisor to the NSDB. His short-term assignment, seemingly, will be for survey purposes, at the end of which a report will be issued. This, of course, is different from the understanding I had in my discussions with Dr. Garcia. Be that as it may, we have been informed that action on the industrial research program will be taken by ICA after issuance of the science advisor's report. This, of course, makes for additional delay. We can only hope that the delay will be short so that, finally, the long-discussed and long-awaited industrial research program at the University of the Philippines can be started. There isn't much we can do from this end except to keep abreast of developments. It really is a pity that the program, which could have been well under way, still is up in the air. It was good to see you and Mrs. Ortigas again. Both Mrs. Gollong and I look back with the fondest of memories to the time we spent together in the Philippines. Sincerely, Paul B.W. Gollong, Director

column9: (1958, November 3)
column10:
Dean C. A. Ortigas, College of Engineering, University of the Philippines, Diliman, Quezon City, Philippines
My dear Dean Ortigas : Last Friday (October 31)  we had the very great pleasure of a visit by Dr. Sinco and Dr. Abelarde. This visit was doubly appreciated because both gentlemen had a very tight schedule and yet managed to take time off for a special visit to Chicago and to Technology Center. Their stay in Chicago, albeit brief, did permit us to show them the nuclear reactor and the general layout of the Center, as well as to have them meet various members of our staff, including Dr. H. A Leedy, Director, and Dr. C.E. Barthel, Assistant Director. In the evening, Mrs. Gollong and I enjoyed their company immensely. A lot of ground was covered in the short time and I do feel that the discussions were most helpful in presenting to the two gentlemen what the Armour Research  Foundation is and how it could be effective in the Industrial Research Center program. In turn, their visit permitted us to get a firsthand impression of their views as well as of Dr. Sinco's obvious outstanding abilities. It is thus that we thank you that you made the suggestion of such a visit as you did in your letter of October 4. Now we can only await developments from ICA. With warmest personal regards. Sincerely, Paul B.W. Gollong, Manager

column9: (1958, November 18)
column10: Dr. Vicente G. Sinco, President, University of the Philippines, Diliman, Quezon City, Philippines. My dear Dr. Sinco : By now you probably have returned to the Philippines. We trust the Colombo meeting in Seattle was both fruitful and interesting. Certainly, the continued participation by the Member Nations in plans for the general development of the countries is very vital. It was indeed a pleasure to have you and Dr. Abelarde here although, perforce, your visit had to be brief. We hope that the quick glance at our Technology Center, coupled with the several meetings, gave you a picture of our mode of operations. As you know, these facilities and staff would be available in any joint undertaking on the Industrial Research Center program. It is now a matter of action on the part of the International Cooperation Administration as well as by pertinent Philippine government offices. Considering the many past delays, it is essential that action is taken at the earliest possible moment to initiate the program. We, of course, continue to maintain a close contact with Washington but seemingly, the basic final decision still rests in Manila. But in any case, if we can assist in some manner in bringing about the necessary action, we would be more than pleased to do so. May we ask that you be so kind as to extend our warmest personal regards to Dean Ortigas, Dr. Juan Salcedo, Dr. Ignacio S. Salcedo, and Dr. Clemente. Sincerely, Paul B. W. Gollong, Manager

column9: (1958, October 14)
column10: Dean C.A. Ortigas, College of Engineering, University of the Philippines, Diliman, Quezon City, Philippines. My dear Dean Ortigas : I had just returned from Washington, where I had met with ICA regarding the industrial research project in the Philippines, when your letter of October 4 arrived. At this point I must admit to a certain state of confusion in my mind. On one hand, Washington informs me that nothing will now be done on the Industrial Research Center program until such time that the survey requested by the NSDB has been carried out and the recommendations arising therefrom have been approved. Washington is just now approaching the U.S. National Science Foundation for its recommendations as to personnel that might be able to carry out the survey. It is not the intention of Washington to use a contractor for this work. On the other hand, I gather from your letter that there is no intent to hold up on the Industrial Research Center program nor that the NSDB intends to disturb the program, and that the survey program will go on apace. Where final action on the entire matter rests, I don't know. In any case, it does seem as if all we can do from this end is to await developments. It is only a pity that seemingly there are additional delays. One other thing that may be of interest to you. Although your letter seems to indicate that the Armour Research Foundation is under consideration by the National Science Development Board, Washington definitely is talking in terms of issuing a general bid request to eight or more organizations. In my estimation, this somewhat a pity because in all humility I do feel that over the course of the last seven years since we first became interested in the general state of industrial research in the Philippines, we have accumulated a wealth of information and experience in the Philippines which could be of such value in undertaking any program. Too, the cordial and strong relationship we have had the pleasure of developing over the several years could be of important service in the conduct of the program. Of course, should NSDB make a specific request for ARF, then USOM/Manila as well as Washington would enter into a negotiated contract with us. Do you think that NSDB would consider such a request, or have they already expressed such preference? With respect to the visit of President Sinco, it would indeed be a pleasure for us to see him here. Therefore, we are extending to him an invitation to visit us here at Technology Center during his stopover in Chicago, as per copy of our letter to him attached. Incidentally, you may be interested in reading the new brochure that we have just issued for this Department.

column9: (1958, September 12)
column10: Dean C.A. Ortigas, College of Engineering, University of the Philippines, Diliman, Quezon City, Philippines. My dear Dean Ortigas : Imagine our dismay to learn this week from ICA/Washington that there is further delay in the Industrial Research Center program. Seeminly, your Government has requested that a survey first be conducted. This survey, according to our understanding, would deal with industrial research generally, the position of various Philippine entities within the overall program, etc. We are not quite certain of the full details, of course, but we are disappointed that there is a further delay, one which could well be for six months or more before the Industrial Research Center program is considered. Thus we wonder if you could shed further light on the matter so that we know what the present possibilities are for any kind of participation by the Armour Research Foundation. We might add here that we could very well conduct such a survey, especially in view of the fact that back in 1951 your Government first approached us with respect to such a survey. It was only because of certain political changes that program was not initiated. Too, as you know from our many discussions and from the material we have sent to you, we have conducted numerous similar surveys elsewhere, several of them leading to specific research centers. We, ourselves, favor the concept of one organization being responsible for not only the conduct of the survey, but also the implementation of the recommendations arising therefrom. We would very much appreciate hearing from you at your earliest convenience. Sincerely, Paul B. W. Gollong, Manager
Biography of Dr. Christopher E. Barthel Jr., Assistant Director, Armour Research Foundation of Illinois Institute of Technology

column9: (1957, November 26)
column10: Dean C.A. Ortigas, College of Engineering, University of the Philippines, Quezon City, Philippines. My dear Dean Ortigas : Recently we have heard from Mr. C.D. MacGillivray, USOM / - Manila, regarding the Industrial Research Center. As you probably have heard, the responsibility for any assistance given by USOM to the Center, has been transferred from the Education Division to the Industry Division, where it properly belongs. Mr. MacGillivray said that he had a few talks with you on this matter and that he had a copy of the preliminary plans for the Center which look alright for a starting point. Further, it is our understanding that USOM has included in its tentative aid plan for fiscal year 1958 an adequate dollar figure for services such as we might provide. At present, the overall budget is now under consideration by the National Economic Council, as well as USOM's own Program Office, and it is difficult to say what the outcome will be. We hope that with your own intense interest in the program and the strong efforts you are making to implement it, a decision might soon be reached. If you feel that there is anything we can contribute to bring about action, please let us know. Warmest regards, Sincerely, Paul B. W. Gollong

Mr. Paul B.W. Gollong, Manager, Armour Research Foundation of Illinois, Institute of Technology, 10 West 35th Street, Chicago 16, Illinois
Dear. Mr. Gollong : Dean Ortigas of the State University mentioned your desire to know who from the Philippines are attending the International Industrial Development Conference Co-Sponsored by Time-Life International and the Stanford Research Institute. Calalang who was officially designated representative of an organization invited to the conference, it is our impression that these gentlemen are going at the invitation extended to them individually by the sponsors of the conference. I would like to take this opportunity to inform you that I am a member of a team of eight officials from private and government agencies that will go on an ICA sponsored trip to the U.S., U.K., Continental Europe and Japan on an 8-10 week observation of the development and implementation of product standards. The trip has been scheduled to start in October, 1957. Should our itinerary bring us close to the Chicago area, I hope to be able to call on you at ARF. My personal regards, Sincerely, Hugo B. Fernandez, Deputy Director

Dean C.A. Ortigas, College of Engineering, University of the Philippines, Diliman, Quezon City, Philippines. Dear Dean Ortigas : With the passing months, I fear, perhaps wrongly, that interest in the Industrial Research Center will wane. Although it is pleasing to read from your letter of May 7th that practically all parties involved in this program are supporting it, it would seem to me that early action is necessary if a program is going to be realized. Fortunately, you are directly on top of the situation, and thus the program probably has a better than even chance of being initiated. Thinking that you might be interested in another article from the "Wall Street Journal", relating to science, the April 24th issue speaks about scientists and their ventures into industry. The clipping is enclosed. I had a touching letter from Mrs. Magsaysay in which she expressed her appreciation for our words of sympathy. Our sincere thanks for this good gesture. Sincerely, Paul B.W. Gollong, Manager

Dean C. A. Ortigas, Director, Industrial Research Center, University of the Philippines, Quezon City, Philippines
My dear Dean Ortigas : Shortly, Dr. R. F. Humphreys, Assistant Director of the Armour Research Foundation is leaving for Burma to advise on the Atomic Energy Program of that country. En route, he will stopover in Manila, arriving June 5th on PAA Flight No. 833. He will stay at the Manila Hotel until the 7th and then proceed to Burma. column9: Biography of Dr. Richard F. Humphreys, Assistant Director, Armour Research Foundation of Illinois Institute of Technology
column10: Dr. Richard F. Humphreys, assistant director of Armour Research Foundation of Illinois Institute of Technology, Chicago, is recognized as one of the nation's leading atomic scientists. In his capacity as assistant director, Humphreys is responsible for the development of new areas of technical research at the Foundation. These new areas are being developed in the nine research departments that comprise Armour Research Foundation, an independent, not-for-profit organization which annual conducts approximately $12 million in sponsored research for industry and the government. It was largely through Humphreys' efforts that the Foundation was able to enter the nuclear reactor field and to install the world's first reactor for industrial research. As a scientist, Humphreys has gained a widespread reputation as an expert in the areas of ultracentrifugation of isotopes, cyclotron research, and nuclear reactions. Humphreys joined the Foundation in 1949 as assistant manager of the physics research department and became manager in 1951. Prior to 1949, he was an associate professor of physics at Yale University where he served on the faculty for nine years. He was acting director of the Sloane Physics Laboratory at Yale and also served as consultant on underwater sound research for industyr and the government. Humphreys has co-authored a textbook on "First Principles of Atomic Physics," and has published papers in Physical Review, Review of Scientific Instruments, and numerous other scientific journals. A Rector scholar at DePauw University, Greencastle, Ind., he was awarded a bachelor of arts degree in physics in 1933. He received his master's degree from Syracuse University in 1936 and his doctorate from Yale University in 1939. Active in civic affairs, Humphreys is chairman of the radiological defense committee of the Chicago Civil Defense Corps and has served as a member of the isotopes advisory committee to the health commissioner. He is a director and past president of the Physical Club of Chicago. His professional memberships include the American Physical Society, American Association for the Advancement of Science, American Association of Physics Teachers, and Sigma XI. A native of Greenville, O., he attended high school at Martinsville, Ind., where his mother, Mrs. R.T. Humphreys still resides. Humphreys lives at 3101 S. Wabash Ave., Chicago

Dean C.A. Ortigas, Member, Interdepartmental Committee on Atomic Energy, Manila. Dear Dean Ortigas : During my absence to attend the 10th World Health Assembly in Geneva, you are hereby designated Acting Chairman of the Interdepartmental Committee on Atomic Energy. You should preside over the meetings of the Committee and sign all communications pertaining to the Committee's activities, except routine correspondence which may be signed for the Committee by the Commitee Secretary. The subsequent meetings of the Committee may continue to be held in the Office of the Secretary of Health. Very truly yours, Paulino J. Garcia, M.D.

Dr. Paul B.W. Gollong, Manager, Armour Research Foundation of Illinois Institute of Technology, Chicago 16, Illinois, U.S.A.
Dear Dr. Gollong : Thank you for your letter of March 28, 1957 and for two copies of The Frontier, a publication of the Armour Research Foundation of Illinois Institute of Technology, Spring, 1957 and for a clipping from the "Wall Street Journal", Chicago, Tuesday, March 19, 1957 entitled "The Idea Mills". The article on the Idea Mills shows the infinite potentialities of scientific research. I have read the article entitled "Fulfilling the Undelayable" describing the brief but successful work of the Armour Research Institute in the development of some industrial products especially the conversion of Bogota's garbage into compost at a very little cost to the sponsoring country. Very interesting are the projects on the production of : 1) an all-steel plow using local material; 2) the preparation of potato flour from surplus potatoes to extend scarce wheat supplies; 3) utilization of huge tonnages of waste coffee pulp; 4) the bacterial conversion of non-edible agricultural wastes into cattle feed; 5) the protection of low-cost wooden housing from Colombia's vicious termites. I will try to confer with Dean Ortigas of the U.P. College of Engineering and get more information from him about the research program developed in January between him and the ARF. Sincerely yours, MV. Arguelles, Executive Director

Dean Crisostomo Ortigas, Chairman, Executive Committee Special Scholarship Committee, University of the Philippines Campus. column9: (1956, December 20) The Special Scholarship Committee
column10: The Acting President, University of the Philippines, Quezon City. Sir : The Special Scholarship Committee created by the Executive Committee of the University Council has the honor to submit the following report : I. The Committee enthusiastically voted to express appreciation for Regent Sibal's interest in the university's scholarship program as follows : "Appreciation is hereby expressed by the Committee for the interest of Regent Sibal in the university's scholarship programs". II. The Committee decided to recommend to the President and the Board of Regents the creation of National Scholarship Fund in the amount of P30,000 annually, to begin with, which shall be awarded to the deserving and gifted youths. The scholarship award shall cover the necessary expenses for board, lodging, transportation, tuition and other university fees estimated at the rate of P1000 a year for each scholar. The scholar shall be selected by competitive examinations and other measures which may be adopted for the purpose. The scholarship shall be open to high school graduates from both public and private schools all over the country. The principal aim of this scholarship is to enable the University to help implement the constitutional provision which requires that "The State shall create scholarships in arts, science, and letters for specially gifted citizens". III. The Committee approved to recommend to the President to make representation with the National Science Board for the allocation of P200,000 to finance primarily the scholarships of students interested in the advanced studies of sciences and mathematics. IV. The Committee resolved to recommend to the President and the Board of Regents the establishment of a system of Faculty Fellowship by which members of the faculty, particularly the promising ones shall be authorized to take advanced courses and degrees in certain departments of the University. While holding such fellowship, the faculty shall be relieved of all his teaching assignments, while receiving full salary like the fellows studying abroad. Where the services of the instructors are indispensable the fellow may be given a teaching assignment not exceeding 6 units a semester. Depending upon the quality of his academic performance the said fellowship may be renewed every year. At the completion of his course the fellow may be given the privilege to travel and observe abroad as a measure to round out his professional training. V. The Committee decided to recommend the creation of a Scholarship Committee of the University Council which shall formulate policies and implement the rules governing scholarship which may be adopted by the Council. VI. The Committee decided to recommend the maintenance of the present system of awarding College and University scholarships. VII. The general rules for non-UP scholarships recommended by the Registrar and approved by the Committee on Student Progress were reviewed, and the Committee decided to recommend the following amendments : A. Amend paragraph (1) to read as "Extra-mural scholarship includes all scholarships financed from funds other than those coming from the U.P. budget." B. Delete the last paragraph of No. (2); C. No. (4) to read as : "Whenever a scholarship is for more than one semester, continuation or renewal of the award or grant shall be allowed only if the awardee obtained an average of at least '2.5' with no grade lower than '3' in any subject in case the awardee is an undergraduate. If he is a graudate student, the minimum grade is '2'." D. Change "private" to "extra-mural" in No. (6); E. No. (8) to read as : "Administration of the above rules should be entrusted to a Committee on scholarship to be created." F. Delete Nos. (7), (9) and (10). Very respectfully, Dr. Patrocinio Valenzuela, Member; Dr. Leopoldo Uichanco, Member; Crisostomo A. Ortigas, Chairman; Dr. Antonio Isidro, Member

column9: General Rules for Non-U.P. Scholarships Recommended by the Registrar and Approved by the University Council Committee on Student Progress
column10: 1. Private Scholarships include all scholarships from private or non-University of the Philippine grants. All private schoalrships shall be administered by the Commitee on Student Progress. Proposed Change : Extra-mural scholarship includes all scholarships financed from funds other than those coming from the U.P. budget. 2. Unless otherwise specified by the donor, scholarships shall be awarded to candidates who obtained the highest general average grade derived from their class standing in the U.P. where applicable and their grades in the competitive examinations given for the purpose, in accordance with the following weights : Class standing...2, Competitive examination...1. The subjects of and the manner of conducting competitive examinations shall be prescribed by a committee for the college concerned, the members of which shall be appointed by the Dean from the said college with the representative of the College to the Committee on Student Progress as Chairman. Personal interview by the College Committee may be required. Proposed Change : Delete the last paragraph. 3. Scholarship may be awarded to freshmen after completing one semester work in the U.P. 4. Whenever a scholarship is for more than one semester, continuation or renewal of the award or grant shall be allowed only if the awardee has obtained no lower than "3" in any of this subjects in case the awardee is an undergraduate, or"2" if he is a graduate student. Proposed change : Whenever a scholarship is for more than one semester, continuation or renewal of the award or grant shall be allowed only if the awardee obtained an average of at least "2.5" with no grade lower than "3" in any subject in case the awardee is an undergraduate. If he is a graduate student, the minimum grade is "2". 5. Stipends or allowance shall be paid out to awardees in installments of not more than what corresponds to one semester. 6. Whenever a student or applicant or awardee is entitled to more than one private scholarships, he shall be made to choose only one. This, however, shall not prevent him from holding a University or College scholarship without any financial benefit. Proposed Change : Change "private" to "extra-mural". 7. All other rules, whether general or specific in nature, shall be subject to the approval by the President of the University and by the Board of Regents. Proposed change : repeal. 8. All moneys with regard to scholarships shall be deposited with the University and disbursements therefrom shall be made only upon approval of the Chairman of the Committee and of such other officials of the University whose approval is usual and necessary in the regular course of disbursement from the University funds. Proposed change : Administration of the above rules should be entrusted to a Committee on scholarship to be created. 9. Specific short name for each of the scholarships, to distinguish one from the others, shall be formulated by the Commitee. Proposed change : Repeal. 10. All negotiations for scholarship grants from private donors shall be held with the donor or his representative by the Chairman of the Committee of Student Progress or his authorized representative. Proposed Change : Repeal.

Dr. Paul B. W. Gollong, Manager, Armour Research Foundation, Technology Center, 10 West 35th Street, Chicago 16, Illinois. Dean C.A. Ortigas, Director, Industrial Research Center, University of the Philippines, Quezon City, Philippines

column9: (1957, January 28)
column10:
To : Dean C.A. Ortigas
From : P. B.W. Gollong
Subject : Research Advisory Services for the Development of the Industrial Research Center of the University of the Philippines 
As a result of various conferences and discussions, I am pleased to present the following modification to our preproposal of September 21, 1956 which covers the subject services : Objectives of the Program. It would seem that the objectives of the modified program remain the same. If desired, they may be incorporated into the project proposal you intend to present to the National Science Board. Proposed Plan. The "preferred plan" is indicated as the one most suitable to the task to be undertaken under the modified program. That is, five-year, three-phase program, encompassing the Center as a whole. Personnel Requirements. The personnel required under the modified program is similar to that set forth under the "preferred plan", except that their nomenclature may be changed to Advisory Technologists, headed by a Resident Project Leader. The Resident Project Leader would be in charge during the term of the program, preceding the rest of the field staff to undertake the first phase of the three phase plan. Not later than six months after his arrival he would be followed by two Advisory Technologists, these to be complemented by a minimum of three others not later than one year after the initiation of the field work. Thus, the minimum staff requirements for the field work seem to be 6 men for a total of 312 man-months, for a five-year program (or 168 man-months, for an initial three-year program). It is to be noted here that although not strictly an item under personnel requirements, the matter of an initial orientation visit by you or some designated, top-caliber member of the Center to the Armour Research Foundation should be considered. Such a visit, prior even to the assignment of the Resident Project Leader, is extremely desirable and is an aspect designed to bring about the fullest understanding of the facilities and staff available to the program, as well as to provide first-hand knowledge of the manner in which research institutes operate elsewhere. Financial Requirements. The dollar cost estimates under the modified program are the same as those established under the "preferred plan". As to the local costs, the U.P. Stanford Contract, could readily serve as a model.

column9: (1956, December 7)
column10: Dear Dean Ortigas : As per our telephone conversation this morning, I am sending you herewith a mimeographed copy of Republic Act 1606. I have supplied the other members of the Committee copies of same. Very sincerely yours, Vicente Bobis, Assistant Registrar

column9:
Republic Act No. 1606
An Act to promote Scientific, Engineering and Technological Research, Invention and Development
column10:
Republic Act No. 1606
An Act to promote Scientific, Engineering and Technological Research, Invention and Development
Be it enacted by the Senate and House of Representatives of the Philippines in Congress assembled : 
Section 1. In order to promote scientific, engineering and technological research, invention, and development, the Government shall, in accordance with the provisions of this Act : (a) Grant scholarships in science, engineering and technology for especially gifted citizens; (b) Grant bonus and other financial aid to brilliant and deserving scientific, engineering and technological researchers; (c) Give additional compensation to scientific, engineering and technological researchers employed in the Government who, for financial reasons, engage in teaching or in private business after office hours, subject to the condition that upon receiving such additional compensation, they shall no longer engage in teaching or in private business but shall devote themselves after office hours to scientific research and studies : Provided, That the faculty and other personnel of the University of the Philippines engaged in scientific engineering and technical research who are not authorized to teach outside or engage in private business shall be entitled to the additional compensation equivalent to that received by officials and personnel of other branches of the government of equivalent rank; (d) Employ the services of recognized scientists, engineers and technologists for the purpose of training promising young scientists or researchers; (e) Acquire all the necessary scientific, engineering or technical equipment and supplies which may be needed for conducting resaerches : (f) Enter into contract with private persons, firms and corporations in order for them to undertake any scientific, engineering and technological research, invention and development that can not be undertaken in the various laboratories, bureaus, offices, agencies and instrumentalities of the Government; (g) Extend aids to research, travel grants; send participants to international conferences or congresses; hold International Scientific or Engineering and Technological Conferences and Congresses in the Philippines; and establish exchange of scientific, engineering and technological information with other countries; (h) Establish an industrial research and training center in the University of the Philippines; (i) Allocate funds provided in this Act, over and above existing funds, to all existing and new research institutions of the government, for their expansion, improvement and establishment and for originating and continuing researches therein; and with the following research agencies designated to undertake special research projects and requirements along the line laid down in the programs outlined in appendices "A", "B", "C", "D", "E", "F", "G" and "H" which are hereby made integral parts of this Act and/or such changes as the Board may from time to time decide : 1. The University of the Philippines Industrial Research and Training Center : (a) Textile Pilot plant, (b) pulp and paper pilot plant, (c) coconut products and by-products pilot plant, including fuel, and (d) industrial research building; (2) The National Scientiic and Industrial Research Institute (formerly Institute of Science and Technology) : (a) improvement and expansion of the scientific library and laboratory facilities, equipment and personnel, (b) electrometallurgy pilot plant, and (c) ceramics pilot plant; 3. The University of the Philippines, College of Pharmacy (in cooperation with the College of Medicine, University of the Philippines and the National Museum) : industrial development of drugs from  Philippine medicinal plants; 4. The Science Foundation of the Philippines (in cooperation with the National Education Board) : promotion of science consciousness among the students and the general puiblic; 5. The University of the Philippines, College of Agriculture in cooperation with the Department of Agriculture and Natural Resources) : (a) rice and corn breeding, (b) coffee and cacao varieties improvement and control of their diseases and other plant, and (c) livestock and poultry improvement; 6. Institute of Nutrition : (a) intensification of nutrition researches and (b) nutrition surveys. 7. Department of Health, Public Health Research Laboratories : (a) Anti-biotics (in cooperation with the Bureau of Soil Conservation, College of Agriculture and College of Pharmacy, University of the Philippines), (b) tetanus toxoid, (c) human rabies immune plasma, (d) plasma expanders, and (e) preparation of plasma fraction and gamma globulins. 8. Other new and necessary research projects. (j) Perform other activities that will promote scientific, engineering and technological research, invention and development. As used in paragraphs (b) and (c) of this section, the phrase "scientific, engineering and technological researchers" refer to those undertaking studies or investigations to acquire or discover new knowledge, principle, process or information relating to science, engineering or technology. Sec. 2. The administration of the program provided for in this Act shall be vested in a Naitonal Science Board which is hereby created under the Office of the President to be composed of one representative each from the following : the National Research Council of the Philippines to be designated by the Executive Board of said council; the University of the Philippines to be designated by its President; the Science Foundation of the Philippines; the Institute of Science and Technology; the Philippine Association for the Advancement of Science; the Philippine Confederation of Professional Organizations; the Department of Agriculture and natural Resources; the Department of Health; the Department of Commerce and Industry; manufacturing industries; and the National Economic Council to be appointed by the President with the consent of the Commission on Appointments. The presiding officer of the Board shall be appointed by the President from among the above eleven members with the consent of the Commission on Appointments : Provided, that he is not the chairman of any other government board or council. The President shall, upon recommendation of the National Science Board and subject to the confirmation of the Commission on Appointments, appoint an executive director on full-time basis. The National Science Board is authorized to : (a) Organize such technical and secretarial staff as may be required to implement the provisions of this Act, for which the total sum of sixty-one thousand six hundred twenty pesos is hereby appropriated for the following to be taken from the sum appropriated in section three hereof : I. Personal Services. 1. One Executive Director - P12,000.00; 2. One Assistant Executive Director and Secretary of the Board - P6,600.00; 3. One Stenographer - P2,760.00; 4. Two Typists at P1,800.00 - P3,600.00; 5. One Messenger - P1,440.00; 6. One Janitor - P1,440.00 and 7. For salaries of expert, technical and other necessary office personnel employed by contract or otherwise - P18,000.00. Total for personal services - P45,840.00. II. Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses - 1. Travel expenses of personnel - P1,000.00; 2. Supplies and materials - P5,280.00 and 3. Sundry expenses - P5,000.00. Total for personal services - P45,840.00. II. Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses. 1. Travel expenses of personnel  - P1,000.00; 2. Supplies and materials - P5,280.00; 3. Sundry expenses - P5,000.00. Total for maintenance and other operating expenses - P11,780.00. II. Furniture and Equipment. 1. Acquisition of fixed assets - P4,000.00. Total for funiture and equipment - P4,000.00. Grand Total - P61,620.00. (b) Coordinate the various researches being undertaken by the different bureaus, offices, agencies and instrumentalities of the government and synchronize them with the activities authorized to be undertaken by this Act in order to prevent unnecessary duplication; (c) Release funds provided for in this Act exclusively for the purpose enumerated in Section One hereof; (d) Supervise, regulate and otherwise administer the research projects and activities financed from the funds under this Act; and (e) Constitute for all legal purposes, the Board of Directors with all the duties, powers and prerogatives of a Board of Directors, unless otherwise provided for in this Act. Sec. 3. To carry out the provisions of this Act there is hereby appropriated out of any funds in the National Treasury not otherwise appropriated, or from the proceeds of any bond issues authorized by law, beginning for the fiscal year nineteen hundred fifty-seven, the sum of four million five hundred thousand pesos to constitute a special fund to be known as the Scientific Engineering and Technological Research Fund for the first year and there is hereby appropriated two million three hundred eighty thousand pesos for the second year, and two million two hundred fifty thousand pesos each year for the thiord, fourth and fifth years : Provided, that any unexpended appropriation for any particular year shall not revert to the General Fund. Any amount in the form of grants, donations or aids that may be received by the Philippine Government for scientific, engineering and technological research, invention, and development, which shall be exempted from all taxes shall also form part of this Fund. Sec. 4. The Scientific, Engineering and Technological Research Fund shall be expended by the National Science Board created under Section Two, exclusively for the purposes enumerated in Section One hereof. Sec. 5. The National Science Board shall submit to the President of the Philippines, the President of the Senate and the Speaker of the House of Representatives, not later than thirty days from the opening of Congress, a yearly report on the progress made on the various projects financed under this Act, the program of projects the Board will undertake the following year, and a financial report on all its operations. Sec. 6. This Act shall take effect upon its approval. Approved, August 23, 1956. Report of the Committee on Science and Technology were eliminated due to lack of space. A true copy from the Official Gazette, Vol. 52, No. 11, September 15, 1956.

column9: (1956, November 7)
column10:
The Acting President, University of the Philippines, Quezon City. Sir : I have the honor to inform you that the Committee on Student Progress considered at its meeting today your amendments to the proposed rules for Non-U.P. scholarships. The Committee decided to accept two of your amendments : (1) to award scholarship for freshmen after completing one semester work in the University of the Philippines, and (2) to empower the Chairman of the Committee on Student Progress or his authorized representative to negotiate for scholarship grants from any private donor or his representative. Regarding your suggestion to give some recognition to students deserving scholarship but not in need financially, the Committee believes in reviving the old practice of giving honor students scholarship pins during a conversation in their honor. The complete rules as amended and recommended by the Committee on Student Progress appear below. Very respectfully, Juan C. Canave, Chairman. 
General Rules For Non-U.P. Scholarships recommended by the Registrar and approved by the Committee on Student Progress
1. Private Scholarships include all scholarships from private or non-University of the Philippines grants. [All private scholarships shall be administered by the Committee on Student Progress.]
2. Unless otherwise specified by the donor, scholarships shall be awarded to candidates who obtained the highest general average grade derived from their class standing in the U.P. where applicable and their grades in the competitive examinations given for the purpose , in accordance with the following weights : class standing...2; competitive examination...1. [The subjects of and the manner of conducting competitive examinations shall be prescribed by a committee for the college concerned, the members of which shall be appointed by the Dean from the said college with the representative of the College to the Committee on Student Progress as Chairman. Personal interview by the College Committee may be required.] 3. Scholarship may be awarded to freshmen after completing one semester work in the U.P. 4. Whenever a scholarship is for more than one semester, continuation or renewal of the award or grant shall be allowed only if the awardee has obtained no lower than "3" in any of his subjects in case the awardee is an undergraduate, or "2" if he is a graduate student. 5. Stipends or allowances shall be paid out to awardees in installments of not more than what corresponds to one semester. 6. Whenever a student or applicant or awardee is entitled to more than one private scholarships, he shall be made to choose only one. This, however, shall not prevent him from holding a University or College scholarship without any financial beneift. [7. All other rules, whether general or specific in nature, shall be subject to the approval by the President of the University and by the Board of Regents.] [8. All moneys with regard to scholarships shall be deposited with the University and disbursements therefrom shall be made only upon approval of the Chairman of the Committee and of such other officials of the University whose approval is usual and necessary in the regular course of disbursement from the University funds.] [9. Specific short names for each of the scholarships, to distinguish one from the others, shall be formulated by the Committee.][10. All negotiations for scholarship grants from private donors shall be held with the donor or his representative by the Chairman of the Committee of Student Progress or his authorized representative. ]

column9: (1955, December 14)
column10: Mr. William R. Weems, Professor & Adviser in Engineering, Seoul National University c/o/ U.N. Economic Coordinator, APO 301, San Francisco, Calif. Dear Mr. Weems : Dean Bailey and I contacted each other o nthe eve of my departure on a four-day trip; otherwise this letter would have reached you several days earlier. Anyway I hope that you can find the time to visit our College in Diliman, Quezon City, sometime during the month of January or February so that we will profit by the assistance that you may be willing to extend to us in the preparations and/or in the discussions in the connection with the Engineering Convention that will be sponsored by the University of the Philippines early in February. The object of the above-mentioned convention is to bring together the College of Engineering, the industries, and government corporations and offices, for the purpose of finding ways and means of helping one another. Such topics as improvement of curricula, regulation and placement of graduates, and industrial research cooperation will be taken up. I understand from Dean Bailey that all your expenses will be paid by your office in Korea. It is on this understanding that I am extending this invitation to you without previous consultation with our finance officers here in our school. If I am wrong in assuming this wise, please overlook the error and advise me accordingly. Yours truly, C. A. Ortigas, Dean

column9: (1954, December 9)
column10: Professor Jacob P. Den Hartog, Head, Dept. of Mechanical Engineering, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Cambridge, Mass. Dear Professor Den Hartog : On p. 31, Technology Review, Nov. 1954. Congratulations! By this time you must be a grandfather. Congratulations again; and warm regards to Mrs. Den Hartog and the rest of the family. Mrs. Ortigas and I expect to be grandparents by April;--our oldest son got married 1 July this year. We have contract with Stanford University by which we get the services of Professors in Engineering in U.S. through Stanford University. Prof. E.L. Eriksen of the University of Michigan is the head of the Stanford team. He has often spoken of you with fondness. Sometimes he refers to my former professor as a flying Dutchman. This morning we enjoyed looking at your picture together. Hunter Rouse was here as consultant for our Hydraulics Laboratory. He spent two weeks with us. I hope you could come to help us on the Stanford team before our contract expires in June 30, 1957. I have been using your Mechanical Vibrations as a text in a senior course entitled Dynamics of Machines and Foundations. The boys like the course and the textbook although they consider both as tough. Wishing you a Merry Christmas and a Happy 1955. Very sincerely yours, C.A. Ortigas, Dean (since June 1954). N.B. Memories of our visit to your country home at Lake Winnepessaupe still abide with me.

column9: (1953, December 11)
column10: Prof. Crisostomo Ortigas, College of Engineering, University of the Philippines, Diliman, Quezon City. Dear Prof. Ortigas : Upon learning that you are appointed the new Dean of our Alma Mater, College of Engineering, University of the Philippines, our association thru its Board of Directors takes pride in extending to you our heartfelt congratulations and sincere wishes for a successful deanship. We also wish to take this opportunity to invite you to be our guest in our annual convention which will be held on the 19th of December at the Aristocrat Air-Conditioned Restaurant in the form of a luncheon meeting. Trusting that you will honor us with your presence, we beg to remain. Very sincerely yours, For the PSME C.L. Navarro, Secretary

Dear Mr. Prentics : I have the pleasure to forward herewith for the consideration of the Mission the counterpart budget for the Rehabilitation of the U.P. A special budget in the amount of P35,930 chargeable against the appropriations under paragraph 2, section 1, page 969 of Republic Act No. 906 may be approvede to cover the administrative and overhead expenses of the Departments of Mining and Geology. Beginning with this fiscal year, overhead expenses of the Department of Chemical Engineering will be paid out of the regular appropriations of the College. Also attached herewith for the consideration of the Mission is the draft of the agreement on this project. Sincerely yours, Placido L. Mapa, Chairman, F&RB-AA-BGV-et
