column9: (1953, October 14) Counterpart Project No. Agreement on the Rehabilitation of the U.P. College of Engineering
column10: 1. Description of Project : To afford a better coordination and a more effective implementation of all activities aimed at the rehabilitation and improvement of the educational facilities and instructional staff of the U.P. College of Engineering, the Mining School Rehabilitation (Counterpart Project No. 20), the Rehabilitation of the U.P. College of Chemical Engineering (Counterpart Project No. 30) and the Improvement of the Instructional Facilities of the U.P. College of Engineering (Counterpart Project No. 65), have all been merged into one under this project. During FY '54 activities will be directed towards the acquisition, receipt and installation of needed laboratory equipment and towards the construction of a chemical engineering laboratory building. This laboratory builidng will house the bigger pieces of laboratory equipment now causing congestion in their present locations. 2. Financing : Budgetary requirements of this project will be financed by withdrawals from the Counterpart Fund Special Account, in conjunction with regularly appropriated funds of the U.P. College of Enginering, and counterpart funds provided for the Improvement of Instructional Facilities of the U.P. College of Engineering Project (Counterpart Project No. 65, the budget for which will expire on September 30, 1953. The University of the Philippines will provide P100,000 out of its regular funds to cover its share in the cost of the chemical engineering laboratory building. 3. Counterpart Project Budget : The counterpart budget for this project will be for the period July 1, 1953 to June 30, 1954 in the amount of P188,000, as shown in detail in the attached budget of expenditures. These funds will not be used for payment of obligations incurred prior to the effective date of this agreement. 4. Operating Procedure : An initial release of funds to the Dean, College of Engineering, U.P., equal to the amount budgeted for the first quarter (July 1 to September 30, 1953), is authorised at the time of approval of this agreement. Subsequent releases of funds will be made by PHILCUSA in accordance with need and upon submission of monthly financial reports by the project director in accordance with the provisions of Section 6 hereof. Funds released to the project director will be deposited in the Philippine National Bank in a current account designation "University of the Philippines (Counterpart Project No.__)". Withdrawals will be made by checks signed by the Dean, College of Engineering, U.P. as project director and countersigned by the Comptroller of the University. No obligations will be incurred subsequent to June 30, 1954. Payment of outstanding obligations will be made within 60 days from June 30, 1954. Any funds then remaining will be returned to PHILCUSA and redeposited in the Counterpart Fund Special Account. A final report on the project will be submitted to PHILCUSA and to the MSA Mission Controller not later than 90 days after June 30, 1954. 5. Property Acquisition and Disposition : All items of equipment and/or furniture and fixtures acquired with counterpart funds will be properly labelled with the joint MSA / PHILCUSA emblem, and a permanent record of each item showing the cost, location, vendor's name, and procuring voucher number will be maintained. All equipment acquired by the project and all supplies not consumed will be turned over to PHILCUSA upon completion of their use or completion of this project for such utilization or disposition as PHILCUSA and MSA/STEM determine. Properties and funds of this project will be subject to physical examination and the books of accounts and other records including property records being maintained by the project will be subject to audit and examination by PHILCUSA and the MSA Mission Controller, at such reasonable times as they may desire to make such examination. 6. Reporting Procedure : Monthly reports of receipts, disbursements and outstanding obligations or commitments, on forms prescribed by MSA/PHILCUSA, together with a trial balance, will be submitted by the project director to PHILCUSA and to the MSA Mission Controller not later than 10 days following the close of each month. Complete instrucitons for the preparation of these reports are contained in the Manual "Reporting Requirements for Counterpart Fund Projects", copy of which was furnished each project director and accountant. These "Reporting Requirements" are hereby made a part of this agreement by reference. Quarterly reports of project progress and status will be submitted by the project director to PHILCUSA and to the MSA Mission not later than the 15th day following the close of the quarter. 7. Date of Effectivity : The effective date of this agreement is the date the agreement is signed by the Chief of Mission, MSA/STEM and the Chairman, PHILCUSA, or the first day of the budget period, whichever is later.

column9: (1952, September 29)
column10: Dr. C.A. Ortigas, Professor and Head, Department of Mechanical Engineering, College of Engineering, University of the Philippines, Quezon City, Philippine Islands. Re : University of the Philippines Research Center. My dear Dr. Ortigas : Thank you indeed for your kind letter of September 23rd, which has given us more of an insight on the problems confronting you in the establishment of a Research Center at the University of the Philippines. We are particularly pleased to hear that you will visit the United States in the near future. May we extend a hearty invitation to you to come to Technology Center and to get a first-hand impression of the Foundation's extensive facilities and to meet some of our staff. We see from your letter that you probably will visit Iowa City and should that be the case, it would not be too much of a trip to come to Chicago. If we can be of any assistance in your program, please let us know. May we say that we have a very excellent relationship with the Mutual Security Agency and, specifically, with Dr. Renne of the Mission to the Philippines. Sincerely, Paul B.W. Gollong, Chief, Asia and Far East Operations, International Division

column9: (1952, September 3)
column10: Dr. C.A. Ortigas, Department of Mechanical Engineering, College of Engineering, University of the Philippines, Quezon City, Philippine Islands. Re : University of the Philippines Research Center. My dear Dr. Ortigas : It has been several months since we sent you certain literature which you requested for use by you in connection with a projected organization of the Research Center at the University of the Philippines. Not having heard from you since your letter of June 23, acknowledging receipt of this literature, we wonder what the status of the Research Center is. We would be interested in offering to you our extensive services and experiences in the international research field. May we hear from you whether we can place our staff and facilities at your disposal in organizing and setting up the projected Research Center. Very sincerely yours, P.B.W. Gollong, International Division

column9: (1952, September 23)
column10: Dr. P.B.W. Gollong, International Division, Armour Research Foundation of Illinois Institute of Technology, Technology Center, Chicago 16, Illinois, U.S.A. Dear Dr. Gollong : Your letter of September 3 manifests a kind interest for which I am deeply grateful. God knows we need help from everywhere; but at the same time we also know that to afford help from some sources, the party at the receiving end needs to put up considerable sums of money beyond our means at present. However, our hopes have not as yet "evaporated to dryness." The University of the Philippines has recently instructed me to proceed to Iowa City on the Mutual Security Agency Technical Assistance program. Arrangements for this projected trip have not been approved yet but it appears that final approval by MSA/Washington is forthcoming, My mission in Iowa will be to "finalize" the tentative preliminary specifications for the equipment of a modern Hydraulics Laboratory for which MSA has set aside about $140,000. I hope you will allow me to see you in case some piece of good luck should enable me to be in the premises of your great foundation. Sincerely yours, C.A. Ortigas, Professor and Head, Dept. of Mechanical Engineering

Dr. C. A. Ortigas, Head, Department of Mechanical Engineering, College of Engineering, University of the Philippines, Quezon City, Philippines. Dear Dr. Ortigas : Thank you for your letter of June 5 and the interest shown in the Armour Research Foundation of Illinois Institute of Technology and its Industrial Research Activities in numerous areas of the world. Perhaps the following brief resume, together with certain publications, covering the services, facilities and experiences of the Foundation, will give you the required material to be used by you in conneciton with the projected organization of the Research Center at the University of the Philippines. The Armour Research Foundation of Illinois Institute of Technology is a not-for-profit, non-endowed scientific organization established in 1936 to offer to industry and the public a confidential, impartial research and experimental engineering service. The sixteen years have seen a steady, continous growth in the activities of this non-governmental, public service organization until today it is one of the largest industrial research institutes in the United States. The Foundation has research appropriations estimated at a volume in excess of P9,000,000 for the current year and a full-time staff of over one thousand scientists, engineers and their assistants working in virtually every field of technology. The laboratories and operations of the Foundation in the many fields of science--ceramics, minerals, chemistry, metals, cellulose, physics, are just a few--have been continually supported by the research appropriations of the numerous industrial concerns, individuals, governmental agencies, trade associations and other organizations for which its services are performed. The investigations at the Foundation are conducted under what has become known as the Armour Plan for Industrial Research, in which the combined experience, thought, and effort of the entire scientific staff is brought to bear upon each problem for its earliest possible solution. The Foundation, through its International Division, has carried on industrial research programs in other countries since 1942, furnishing to other nations the same research and engineering service available in the United States. The Division is uniquely equipped with technicians already familiar with many of the specialized requirements and problems of research in other countries. To augment the usefulness of this background, the entire Foundation staff and facilities are available, as well as the Illinois Institute of Technology, one of the leading technological institutes in the Midwest, the Institute of Gas Technology, and the Central Research Laboratory of the Association of American Railroads, all located here at Technology Center. In addition, the Division has close and harmonious relationship with leading construction firms, engineering consultants, management consulting companies, and a multitude of manufacturing corporations, and can call on these specialized outside services when required. The Division has conducted broad techno-economic surveys of entire countries, determined specific ways to aid existing industries through research, assisted in utilizing indigenous raw materials, suggested new industries and future research possibilities, assisted in the establishment and operation of research and development laboratories in other countries. For example, the Division currently is assisting in the operation of an industrial research laboratory in Mexico City, which it established in 1946. The scientists at the laboratory are working on projects that will have significant effects on various phases of the economy of Mexico--projects covering work on tannin materials, fibrous projects, utilization of waste fiber, coal and charcoal, vegetable oils and fats. The Division also is currently carrying out fibrous and tanning materials research programs in Central America and in several of the Caribbean Islands and has been conducting a study for the United States Technical Cooperation Administration in selected South American countries on indigenous non-metallic construction materials. Under the Technical Cooperation Administration program it also is fulfilling research and engineering assistance requests from India and Pakistan in the industrial fields of refractories, foundries, cellulose and paper, small scale industries, and others. At the request of the Mutual Security Agency, the International Division recently conducted a survey of the status of the State Industrial Research Institute of Burma. As a result of the survey, the Division was asked to submit a proposal for the reorganization and expansion of the Institute. The proposal has already been accepted in principle. Of specific interest to you might be our work in the Philippines. Based on correspondence and a field trip to Manila by the writer, two proposals were prepared. One proposal, submitted to the Office of Economic Coordination, covers the services of the Foundation to cooperate with the Office of Economic Coordination in the furtherance of Philippine industrial projects. The other proposal deals with the reorganization and program development of the Institute of Science and Technology. Both proposals have been accepted in principle. To give you a more detailed picture of the Armour Research Foundation and the Illinois Institute of Technology, we are sending by third class airmal several pieces of literature : Annual Report, Ten Years of Progress, Export of Know-How, El Plan Armour, Mexican Report, and To Our Sponsors. If you require extra copies of these publications, pl;ease let us know. We are adding your name on our mailing list to receive our technical bulletins. The reports on the vairous Technical Cooperation Administration projects are confidential and have, as yet, not been released by our sponsor. However, if you require any other general information about our work under the various technical assistance programs, please do not hesitate to write us. Very sincerely yours, Paul B.W.Gollong, International Division

column9: (1953, August 26)
column10: Dear Mr. Crucillo : In reply to your letter of August 24, 1953, concerning the designation of the Dean of our College of Engineering as Project Director of the combined counterpart projects Nos. 20, 30, and 65 for FY 1953-54, I should like to suggest that Professor Crisostomo A. Ortigas, Head of the Department of Mechanical Engineering, be designated as such instead of Dean of the College; because Professor Ortigas has been the one doing the work and is more familiar with the various projects. column10: Sir : A copy of your memorandum circular dated August 7, 1953, designating certain members of the faculty as project directors of counterpart projects nos. 20, 30, 31 and 65 was received by this Office. Please be informed , however, that the counterpart budgets of Projects Nos. 20, 30 and 65 for FY 1953-54 have been consolidated into one counterpart budget for the Rehabilitation of the U.P. College of Engineering. PHILCUSA now considers these three projects as one project and desires that there should be only one project director. Very respectfully, For the Executive Summary : Signed : Cornelio V. Crucillo, Technical Adviser (Coordinator of Projects)

V. Summary Description of Project and Project Activities
A. Summary
This project proposal reccommends utilization of $630,000 of FY 1957 Technical Cooperation funds for a new project which undertakes: 1. to provide technical assistance in the establishment of an Industrial Research and Training Center in the University of the Philippines for a period of from three to five years; 2. to provide the necessary machineries and apparatus to equip the Industrial Research Center laboratories and the pilot plants assigned by R.A. No. 1606 to the University of the Philippines College of Engineering. It is proposed that this amount be expanded over a period of from 3 years to 4 years. The Technical Assistance is to be sought from outstanding research organizations in the United States, such as : The Armour Research Foundation, Arthur D. Little & Co., Inc. The Batelle Memorial institute. It is recommended that a team of 3 from these research foundations be imported to the Philippines to carry on what might be termed the 2nd and 3rd phases of what has already been done by the research team consisting of R.Norris Shreve, Raymond H. Ewell, and Thomas W. Adler, which was imported in the early part of 1956 to undertake a survey of industrial research and development in the Philippines in accordance with FR 4E200 dated May 13, 1954. About $130,000 is estimated to pay for these services (T.A.) and the remaining $500,000 will be spent for commodities as follows : $100,000 in the 1st year; $250,000 in the 2nd year; $150,000 in the 3rd year. B. Project Administration. The Dean of the College of Engineering of the University of the Philippines will serve as Project Director of this project. It is aimed to provide some pilot scale organization which will serve as a small scale model for the big Scientific, Engineering, and Technological Research Institution that is contemplated in R.A. No. 1606. Republic Act 1606 which became effective in August, 1956 provides for the establishment of an Industrial Research and Training Center in the University of the Philippines. Laboratory and pilot plant equipment as well as technical assistance will be needed for the implementation of RA 1606. C. Justirication for U.S. Assistance. Equipment needed for the Physics Laboratory and Industrial Research pilot plants and service laboratories is not available in the Philippines. Technical knowledge and skill necessary to set up and use such facilities must also be provided from U.S. aids. Thru and by the Quirino-Foster agreement, the Philippines and the United States under the leadership of the latter have launched a large scale economic and industrial development programme. The success or failure of this adventure is the responsibility of both governments. In the industrial research sector, the Philippines last month, approved Republic Act No. 1606 appropriating some 4 million pesos for financing 4 specific projects which by said act were assigned to the University of the Philippines. The peso resources set up by this Act need to be reinforced by dollar commodities and services not available in the Philippines. The resulting situation that will arise in the Philippines due to failure to receive U.S. aid for this industrial reserach program in the University of the Philippines would be comparable to the predicament of a ship that had been launched into a wide uncharted sea without the benefit of sufficient fuel, adequate crew and properly calibrated navigation instruments. The U.S. is in a position to supply the necessary reserve fuel in terms of dollar commodities; loan the additional crew in terms of technical assistance personnel ; and furnish the needed instruments. This aid needs to be extended immediately in view of the fact that the ship has been launched already - launched hopefully and jointly by the two governments. 
VI. Objectives of this Project
A. Basic Problem 
At the beginning of the UP-SU Contract it was found necessary to change the curriculum from 4 year to 5 year program. This was necessitated by a national law requiring all students in institutions of higher learning to take 12 credit hours in Spanish. There was a serious shortage of teaching staff which meant that every faculty member was overloaded. The College of Engineering had lost all its laboratory equipment during the war. Much new equipment had been ordered through the aid of PhilCUSA and MSA and was beginning to arrive for installation. Nearly all faculty members were young and had few years of experience. No graduate courses were possible or available and there was no time for the instructional staff to improve or do research work. 
B. Principal Objectives
The objectives of the contract members in Engineering was to aid in any way possible to improve the existing conditions : 
1. To help out in the teaching of technical courses;
2. To revise the 4-year curriculum to a 5-year up-to-date curriculum; 
3. To emphasize and encourage the appointment of additional teaching staff;
4. To train some of the young faculty men by offering graduate courses leading to a master's degree in engineering science. 
5. To encourage the establishment of an Industrial Research Center at U.P. (In October, 1954 an Industrial Research Center in the College of Engineering was established and was recently enlarged by the provisions of Republic Act 1606. )
C. Expected Benefits and Significance to Philippine Development. 
The College of Engineering is now equipped with excellent laboratories in most of the departments, and it is now possible to establish contact with industry in projects of research as well as technical aid. Laboratory equipment is essential to the teaching of good engineers. Cooperation with industry is also now developing rapidly and at the second Engineering Conference to be held in March, 1957 an advisory committee will be appointed to further this development. For the future it is vital to the continued growth of industry in the Philippines that a strong technical school be available for training technical personnel and for conducting the research and development necessary to the installation and day-by-day operation of the industrial plant of the nation. 
VII. Detailed discussion of progress to date and future work plans and schedules. 
A. Progress to Date
In the three years of operation of the UP-SU Contract in the College of Engineering, development of the college has been steady and marked. In all areas, staff, curricula, equipment, and morale, progress hsa exceeeded reasonable expectations for the short period of time of the operation of the contract. 
All curricula in the college have been modernized and changed from inadequate four-year curricula to five-year curricula. In addition, a new curriculum in chemical engineering has been established and curricula in sanitary engineering and industrial engineering are being formulated. The growing recognition of the college by the Filipino community is reflected in the increased enrollment, from 628 engineering students in 1952-53 to 1080 students in 1955-56. In addition to the undergraduate courses, the college has substantially increased its offering of graduate courses leading to the Master of Science degree. The faculty of the College of Engineering has been increased from 23 in 1952-53 to 54 in 1955-56. Of these faculty members, 18 have been sent to the U.S. for at least one year of advanced study in their respective fields of engineering, thereby markedly increasing the general technical ability and training of the staff. In addition, the contact of these participants with the laboratories and research installations of the U.S. has created a capacity and interest in research that might otherwise not have developed. The laboratories for undergraduate instruction in engineering have been well-equipped during the three years of operation of the contract, with only the exceptions of the chemical engineering and hydraulics laboratories. The equipment for these two laboratories is on order and will be installed in the near future. In general, the value of laboratory equipment for the training of engineers can hardly be over-estimated, and the laboratories which have been provided through NEC-ICA aid have consequently become a predominant part of the courses work offered. The engineering library has been aided in the general program for library development and is at present excellent in all phases of engineering. Curricula in fields related to engineering or necessary to the training of engineers have been helped in the contract. Several of the faculty of the Departments of Geology, Physics, Chemistry and Industrial Pharmacy, and other related fields have been sent to the U.S. for advanced training as participants under the contract. The curricula and laboratories of the department have been modified and improved. Aid to the Department of Physics is contemplated for the coming year in the form of laboratory equipment for the teaching of undergraduates. 
Progress has been made in establishing mutual beneficial relations between the College of Engineering and the industriesof the Philippines. The creation of an Industrial Research Center in the College has served to acquaint industry with the activities of the college. At the same time the research center has successfully undertaken several research projects for both the national government and private industry, e.g. the model design of the spillway for the Ambuklao Dam. An annual engineering conference between the faculty of the College of Engineering and the engineers of industry has been inaugurated, the first such conference having been held in March, 1956. 
B. Current Work Plans
Present work plans of the College of Engineering are designed both to continue the progress made in developing the curricula and laboratories for undergraduate teaching and to extend rapidly the efforts of the staff in advanced studies and industrial research. Modern five year curricula have been instituted in all basic engineering fields in the past. In the present fiscal year, a five-year curriculum in the field of Industrial Engineering is also to be set up to meet the demand of Philippine Industry for trained graduates in this more specialized field. In addition, to provide technical training without the scientific emphasis necessary to the granting of the Bachelor of Science degree, the College of Engineering is instituting four-year curricula in the basic engineering fields. Those curricula are designed to fill the present void of technologists by giving a solid training in technical work to those incapable of or uninterested in the scientific aspects of the fields. The future Philippine industry will require many technicians skilled in the practical aspects of engineering, draftsmen, engineering assistants, and technologists, who need not be skilled in engineering theory and design. Laboratories for undergraduate teaching are progressing. Satisfactory laboratories have been developed in electrical engineering, mechanical engineering, civil engineering, geodetic engineering, and in mining and metallurgy. The chemical engineering laboratory is rapidly being equipped. Supplies and apparatus are presently being purchased and will be installed to a large extend during the present fiscal year. The hydraulics laboratory is also being equipped during this fiscal year. However, due to the delayed arrival of some equipment for the hydraulics laboratory, completion is not expected until fiscal year 1959.
Emphasis is being placed on development of the faculty in the present fiscal year as in all previous years. The capabilities and advanced work of the staff are being extended through study in leading engineering colleges in the U.S. Six of the engineering faculty members are being sent to the U.S. this year for 12 to 18 months of graduate study. The graduate studies, advanced work, and research of the College in future years will be a direct result of the studies of these and former participants. 
It is of course essential that the supporting fields to engineering physics, mathematics, chemistry, etc., be well equipped with the laboratories and staff for adequate and thorough undergraduate teaching. At the present thime the Department of Physics is almost completely lacking in laboratiory facilities and equipment. During this fiscal year a visiting professor from the Physics Department of Stanford University is cooperating with the faculty of the Physics Department at U.P. in determining the laboratory needs of the university. In the following section proposals are made to remedy the lack of these laboratories. 
In addition, a participant in Physics is presently working toward the Ph.D. degree in the U.S. A participant in the field of petroleum geology is being sent to the U.S., this year for advanced study. 
To further develop the teaching of fundamental sciences in the high schools., plans are being developed this year to attract recent science and engineering graduates into this work. Additional studies in education and science will be set up to train qualified instructors for the precollege schools. 
In line with the primary objective of developing a principal center of industrial research at the University of the Philippines, emphasis is being placed during the present year on the institution of research and research facilities. Republic Act 1606 passed by the Congress of the Republic of the Philippines established the Industrial Research and Training Center in the university. Prior to the establishment of this Center, the Industrial Research Center in the College of Engineering was already in operation, and had performed several research projects for Philippine industry. Projects are now in progress in the Industrial Research Center, for example, studies in the utilization of coir dust sponsored by PHILCOA. During the present year, heavy emphasis will be placed on the continuation and completion of these projects, while at the same time planning for the design and equipping of the much expanded Industrial Research and Training Center will be undertaken. The faculty of the College of Engineering will be dream upon heavily for this purpose. 
Of the dollar commodities requested in this document for the College of Engineering, it is proposed to utilize during FY 1957 $70,000 in the purchase of laboratory equipment for the instruction of physics and $100,.000 for the partial equipping of service laboratories and facilities in the new Industrial Research Center would include 
Analytical Chemistry Laboratiory
Instrument and Apparatus Shop
Glass-blowing Shop
Electronics Shop
Materials Stores
These facilities are necessary to all engineering and scientific research, and should be made available at the earliest opportunity. It is proposed to equip the more specialized laboratories in the following fiscal years. 
Detailed lists of equipment for the service laboratories and shops are being prepared. 
In addition, it is proposed to equip the coconut pilot plant specified in R.A. 1606 by the expenditure of $50,000 during the presnt fiscal year. 
C. Future Work Plans Utilizing Aid Requested in this PPA.
Work plans for fiscal years 1958, 1959, and 1960 are to continue the development of industrial research facilities and the training of the staff of the College of Engineering. It is proposed to send up to 30 faculty members drawn from engineering and supporting fields to the U.S. for 12 to 18 months advanced study. To further advance the training of the faculty, specialized technicians from the U.S. are requested for periods of one or two years; during fiscal year 1958, 4 technicians FY 1959, 3 technicians, FY 1960, 3 technicians. Equipping of the basic laboratories in the Industrial Research and Training Center is projected for FY 1958 and 1959. During FY 1958 it is proposed to utilize $150,000 for obtaining equipment for the following laboratories and pilot plants : Physics Research, Chemical and Biochemical Research, General Engineering Research and Textile Pilot Plant. During FY 1959, it is proposed to utilize $100,000 to equip laboratories for advanced scientific studies in specific areas of the engineering and physical science fields. Detailed specifications of the equipment items required will be submitted when planning for these laboratories is complete. 
VIII. Explanation and Expansion of Financial Plan
A. Summary of All Project Costs 
Dollars (new costs) FY 1958 - $145,000 FY 1959 - $295,000 FY 1960 - $190,000
Pesos FY 1958 - P50,000 FY 1959 - P50,000 FY 1960  P50,000

2. Explanation of Requirements
a. The dollars will be required for technical assistance in control laboratories and for buying equipment for equipping these laboratories as well as the pilot plants.
b. The pesos will be required to defray transportation, living allowances, housing, as well as clerical services and office equipment needed for the technical assistants.

Circular No. 49
To : All employers and employees
Subject : Sickness Notification System
A Sickness Notification System is hereby adopted to expedite and facilitate the payment of daily sickness allowances to employees by employers, to minimize the incidence of overpayment of such allowances and to define the respective roles of the employee, the employer, and the Social Security System in the implementation of the sickness benefit provisions of the Social Security Act. 
Effective July 1, 1964, therefore, all employers and employees shall observe the following procedures in all cases of sickness for which reimbursement will be claimed from the System, to wit :
1. As soon as the employee is unable to report for work by reason of sickness or bodily injury, he or a member of his family or his representative shall accomplish and file with his employer an "Initial Notification of Sickness or Injury" (SSS Form CLD-9 N.1, copy attached), Part II of which must also be accomplished by his attending physician. 
2. This Form CLD-9 N.1 shall be duly noted by the employer in the space provided therefor, must be sent by the employer to the Social Security System within 8 days from the start of the employee's absence. Postage in transmitting the Form CILD-9 N.1 shall be paid by the System. 
3. In the meantime, sickness allowances shall be advanced by the employer to the employee from the first day of confinement if caused by injury or acute disease, or from the eight day, if caused by a chronic disease. In any case, no sickness allowances shall be advanced until and after all company sick leaves with full pay to the credit of the employee are exhausted. 
4. Upon receipt of the accomplished Form CLD-9 N.1, the System's Medical Director or his representative shall return the duplicate copy thereof to the employer with the advice to continue paying sickness benefits to the employee for the period certified to by his attending physician, or to pay sickness allowances only for such period as may have been approved as reasonable confinement. The duplicate copy of said form shall be kept by the employer. 
5. In cases where the confinement of the employee would be prolonged beyond the period originally estimated by the physician in Part II of the Form CLD-9 N.1, the employee must file with his employer an 'Intermediate Medical Certificate' (SSS Form CLD-9 N.2), duly accomplished by his attending physician, on or before the expiration of the original period of confinement. 
6. Form CID-9 N.2 shall likewise be duly noted by the employer in the space provided therefore, and transmitted to the System within 3 days from the last day of the original period of confinement estimated by the physician in Part II of the Form CLD-9 N.1.
7. Payment of sickness allowances to the employee shall be continued for the period certified in Form CLD-9 N.2., unless the employer shall have been advised by the Medical Director, in returning to employer the duplicate copy of said form, to discontinue payment of sickness beyond the period that shall have been approved as reasonable confinement. The employer shall also keep the duplicate of this form together with the duplicate of the Form CLD-9 N.1. 
8. Upon the termination of the period of actual medical attendance as well as the recuperation period, the employer shall require the employee to submit to him a complete and final medical certificate (Part II, Form CILD-9) which will be submitted together with the Sickness Reimbursement Claim Form (Part I, Form CLD-9) and the duplicates of Forms CLD-9 N.1. and CLD-9 N.2. previously received from the System. 
Forms will be available upon request from the Social Security System. 
Upon receipt of the notices, employers are enjoined to immediately take such steps as may be necessary to establish the veracity of the information furnished by the employee regarding his state of health, so that payment of sickness allowances may promptly be effected to the employee. Failure on the part of the employee to notify his employer in the manner prescribed in the foregoing shall be sufficient cause for the employer to withold payment of sickness allowances, unless the employee shall be able to sufficiently prove his confinement within the period of three months after his illness as provided in the System's Rules and Regulations or the employer had knowledge or should have had knowledge of such illness. In such a case, the employer must submit such proof to the System's Medical Director, and shall pay sickness benefits only for such period as may be approved by the Medical Director. In the same manner, FAILURE ON THE PART OF THE EMPLOYER TO TRANSMIT SUCH NOTICES TO THE SYSTEM'S MEDICAL DIRECTOR, OR FAILURE TO TRANSMIT THE SAME ON TIME, SHALL GIVE RISE TO THE PRESUMPTION THAT A CLAIM FOR REIMBURSEMENT FILED SUBSEQUENTLY IS OF DOUBTFUL CREDIBILITY FOR WHICH THE SYSTEM DOES NOT GUARANTEE REIMBURSEMENT EITHER IN WHOLE OR IN PART. 
On the other hand, NO REIMBURSEMENT WHATSOEVER SHALL BE MADE WHERE THE NOTICES ARE NOT SUBMITTED WITHIN THREE MONTHS AFTER THE EMPLOYEE RECOVERS FROM THE ILLNESS. 
In no case, however, shall this notification system authorize employers to arbitrarily withhold or deny payment of sickness allowances to employees, where it appears that the employee have taken the steps required of them. 
(SGD) BERNARDINO R. ABES
Chairman, Social Security Commission & Acting Administrator, Social Security System

Memorandum for -
Professor Crisostomo Ortigas
College of Engineering
University of the Philippines

You may remain under the accrued leave status. 

V.G. Sinco
President

The President
University of the Philippines Diliman
Sir : 
Pursuant to the brief conference at your office Thursday noon, the undersigned has the honor of requesting U.P. President's ruling on his LEAVE STATUS. He is programming his activities for next school year and finds that LEAVE STATUS has to be taken into account. Upon termination of his deanship, the undersigned assumed that he automatically remained on the cumulative leave basis because less than a year before (on 1 October 1959( the U.P. Board of Regents appointed him Professor and Head of the newly formed department of Engineering Sciences and the U.P. President ordered in Administrative Circular No. 5 Series 1959-60 dated 18 May 1960 that...
4. All existing programs, like the UNESCO Physics Training Program, ICA-NEC programs and projects, shall remain under the direct management of Professor Crisostomo Ortigas until other arrangements are made. The undersigned shall assign any future program or project as he sees fit."
Of the changes that have taken place during the school year 1960-61, two are relevant to the subject of this letter, namely; the appointment of a new head of the Department of Engineering Sciences and the transfer of the functions of the Vice President of Academic Affairs to the Executive Vice President. 
The undersigned became aware of the first of these two while he was processing some requisition involving purchase of supplies for the Atomic Energy Laboratory sometime in October. The signature of Professor Dominador Ilio appeared over the title of "Head, Engineering Sciences". Upon inquiry he discovered the Prof. D. Ilio had been appointed to this position two months before. (This removes one reason for keeping the undersigned under the cumulative leave basis)
Upon learning about the second change, I consulted with the Executive Vice President. The paragraph of Administrative Circular No. 5 quoted above was touched upon. It was arranged that the undersigned continue to discharge the function that he was discharging when he and Dr. Isidro were jointly operating C.P. 6030-A. This arrangement was later modified, however, when the services of Mr. Jose Juinio ceased to be available to him in January 1961 due to the termination of NSDB Project 2.1. The modification consisted in the release of the undersigned from prime responsibility in connection with dollar commodities. 
I am attaching pertinent excerpts from my service record in the belief that the President might wish to glance at it while formulating the reply to this letter. 
Very truly yours, 
C.A. Ortigas
Professor of Engineering Sciences

MEMORANDUM FOR :
Prof. Crisostomo A. Ortigas
Prof. Alfredo Juinio
College of Engineering
University of the Philippines
SUBJECT : Status of the U.P. Industrial Research Center and Its Personnel
At the conference held in the Office of the President last Tuesday, August 9, 1960, at which meeting the following were present : President Sinco, Dr. Virata, Dean Baguio, Prof. Ortigas, and the undersigned, the status of the U.P. Industrial Research Center was finally threshed out as follows : 
1. The U.P. Industrial Research Center, being a creation of the Board of Regents and financed by the Board, is a unit of the University with the Dean of the College of Engineering as its head; 
2. All personnel working under the U.P. Industrial Research Center must be appointed by the Board of Regents or by the President, as the case may be, upon recommendation of the Dean, College of Engineering;
3. Any faculty member of the College of Engineering receiving appointment under the defunct National Science Board has no official standing with the U.P. Industrial Research Center unless they are appointed by the Board or by the President, as the case may be;
4. Any research project financed by any outside agency, like the NSDB, to be conducted by the staff and personnel of the College of Engineering, must be carried out under the supervision of the U.P.I.R.C. Funds for such projects must be received by and coursed through the Office of the President and the President shall designate the proper University official authorized to make disbursements therefrom.
This memorandum is intended to clarify the status of some people in relation to the U.P. Industrial Research Center. It shall be understood that all projects heretofore conducted by the staff and personnel of the College of Engineering under some authority other than that of the Board of without confirmation by the Board, shall be placed under the U.P.I.R.C
Jose C. Campos, Jr. 
Business Executive

ADMINISTRATIVE CIRCULAR NO. 5
Series of 1959-1960
TO : All Concerned
RE : ADMINISTRATIVE RESPONSIBILITIES IN THE COLLEGE OF ENGINEERING
In view of the recent change in the administrative head of the College of Engineering, and in order to clarify the assignment of administrative responsibilities in the various aspects of the work and functions of the College, the following rules are hereby promulgated for the guidance of all concerned :
1. Unless otherwise specified, the administration of all academic matters shall fall within the responsibility of the Dean of the College. 
2. The incoming Dean shall have the authority of recommending changes in the departments of the College. 
3. The U.P. Industrial Research and Training Center shall remain under the direct control of the present director. However, the assignment of the faculty members of the College to the Center or the use of the equipment of the College by the Center shall be arranged by the Dean of the College in consultation wit the Director of the Center. Any disagreement in this respect shall be submitted to the undersigned for final decision. 
4. All existing programs, like the UNESCO Physics Training Program, ICA-NEC programs and projects, shall remain under the direct management of Professor Crisostomo Ortigas until other arrangements are made. The undersigned shall assign any future program or project as he sses fit. 
5. Whenever the training of faculty members (in the Philippines or abroad) or the purchase of equipment for engineering instructional use is to be contemplated under any of the projects or programs stipulated in the immediately preceding section, the recommendation of the faculty member or the choice of equipment shall be made by the Dean of the College. In case of objection on the part of the program director concerned, the matter shall be elevated to the undersigned, whose decision shall be final. 
6. The Dean of the College is hereby empowered to issue such rules and regulations in implementation of the above regulations subject to the approval of the undersigned. 
7. Any aspect of administration in the College either adjunctive or integral, not covered by the above regulation, shall be submitted to the undersigned for definite ruling. 
8. Any existing rule or regulation in conflict with the above regulations shall be considered repealed by this administrative circular. 
V.G. SINCO
President

1) The technical management, including mechanical maintenance, repairs, and technical operation of the equipment of the radio station, shall be under the direction and responsibility of the Dean of the College of Engineering and his staff. The Radio Committee previously organized is hereby abolished. 
V.G. SINCO
President

MEMORANDUM FOR:
The President
University of the Philippines
Campus
In connection with the Memo of the Secretary of the University dated 19 July 1960, the undersigned should like to request the holding of a conference for the purpose of modifying the tone of the last statement of the aforementioned Memo in view of the circumstances enumerated below, which appear unknown to the conferees at the time of the conferences. The statement referred to is "personnel of the UP Industrial Research and Training Center appointed by the Natural Science Development Board have no standing with the University until final clarification of the status of the above-mentioned project by the Natural Science Development Board." It is feared that the tone of the last sentence might ruin the channels of cooperation amongst the various sources of assistance from which the U.P. Industrial Research Center created by the Board of Regents in 1954 has up to this time been drawing nourishment. 
The operation of the UP Industrial Research Center from 1954 to 1957 was made feasible by reason of availability of TA's, dollar commodities and peso funds from C.P.'s 30 and C.P.'s 92. The first was known as the Rehabilitation of the College of Engineering : the second, the UP-Stanford Contract. When the UP-Stanford Contract was terminated in 1957, Mr. Arnold, Chief of the Office of Technical Services in Washington D.C., recommended the incorporation of TA and Commodities in CP 92 into CP 30. And so CP 30 condinued in 1958 as CP 830; in 1959 as CP 930. In 1960 this project was expanded to include administrative aspects of UP as recommended by the Hannah team into CP 6030-A. Throughout the duration of this CP 30 projects, the UP Industrial Research Center continued to receive assistance in the form of dollar commodities, NEC pesos and TA experts. 
In 1958 the College of Engineering begun to receive assistance from the NSDB by virtue of the provisions of RA 1606 and RA 2067. This assistance has been given by NSDB Project 2.1 in the form of furnitures and office equipment, a P200,000 building, and personnel that peform various duties, such a : taking deliveries of dollar commodities that are still arriving under NEC-ICA counterpart projects, chemical analyses for industry-sponsored projects, installation of equipment; coordination and supervision of research activities, etc. In view of the foregoing, the statement to the effect that Project 2.1 personnel have no status in the University appears prejudicial to the continuation and expansion of the cooperation between UP and NSDB. 
In the last two years the progress of the coordination projects centering around Industrial Research has reached a stage where the components from CP's 30, NSDB Project 2.1, and the support from industry have grown large enough to warrant consideration of forming an autonomous unit that could be operated with little help from the regular University budget. Perhaps this matter could be explored during the conference requested above. 
C.A. ORTIGAS
NSDB Project 2.1

column9: (1964, February 24)
column10:
MEMORANDUM
To : The President
University of the Philippines
Subject : Second Progress Report
As a result of the first progress report submitted on 11 February pursuant to the 31 January letters to NEC and ICA authorizing the undersigned to deal with NEC-ICA in connection with the alleged dependence of his clearance on the liquidation of counterpart projects, the acting president issued on 17 February a memorandum requesting various deans and department chairmen and property officers to extend full cooperation to the undersigned. That memorandum yielded as of 21 February the following findings :
Audio-visual Findings
1. From interviews with persons who are in the know, the undersigned gathered that at a meeting held in June 1963 for the purpose of liquidating NEC-ICA properties at Diliman UP presented an inventory amounting to $600,000. ICA advised UP to present in the next few weeks an enlarged inventory and keep ICA informed from time to time of the progress of work. Up to last week ICA had not heard from UP. 
2. During the week ending 21 February the undersigned saw vast amounts of ICA commodities sitting conspicuously in the laboratories of Chemistry, Engineering, Industrial Pharmacy, and Physics valued at no less than $567,890, which were not included in the inventory which UP presented at that June meeting. Some of these equipment are large, - so huge that one of them cannot be housed in a small classroom; others, so small that several pieces of them can be contained in a lady's handbag. 
Documentary Findings
1. In October, 1961 the undersigned assembled an inventory valued at $890,000 covering ICA commodities that were received for the College of Engineering. Two years later, that is in June 1963, this College submitted an inventory of $600,252.31 - nearly one-third smaller. 
2. Records also show that the group handling this liquidation operation received inventories from Chemistry, Geology, and Physics not a part of which was included in that inventory submitted at that June meeting. 
Comments
In view of the foregoing findings is it any wonder that the liquidation efforts repeatedly failed? Is it any wonder that outsiders ask: "What is U.P.'s conception of her obligations as a recipient of foreign aid commodities?" etc. 
Repeated failures such as those that befell our liquidation teams may be attributed to insensibility on the part of some members of the teams, and to insufficient technical knowledge and inadequate experience. This conclusion is supported by the following examples : 
1. Disensibility. This team is used herein to indicate absence of the spirit which pervades the proverb : "Do unto others what you would have them do unto you."
In the first half of the year 1963 department heads had to submit inventory lists for the second time because the lists that they submitted the first time had been mislaid. The persons that called for the lists did not seem to sense that there was anything wrong with it. A second example is the clearance of Dean Villa of the College of Dentistry. Up to 19 Feb. his clearance had not been issued for "lack of man-power to check the equipment" notwithstanding the fact that Dr. Villa by paying an outsider typist finished in November the list which the UP property office required of him for the purpose of checking his accountability. If the clearance clerks were not suffering from insensibility sickness the checking would have been done in November yet. 
2. Insufficient knowledge and inadequate experience. To know where to attack is essential to a successful execution of any operation. To know how much a shot misses a target is essential to the achieving of high hitting average. On the whole, the persons participating in the liquidation efforts did not know how to deploy their efforts and did not know how much nearer their weekly efforts carried them towards the ultimate goal. They did not know that PA 3064 had nothing to do with me; and that PA 2283 and PA 3034 were not part of my property responsibility in terms of my designation in August 1952 as project director. Team members have failed to come to know these after almost three years of assignment on the job! 
The foregoing catalogue of inadequacies induces one to suggest that something analogous to "blood transfusion" is needed. Some one who possesses technical knowledge and experience is needed to remedy the malfunction of the liquidation machinery and render it operative. 
There is no one in the service of the University at present whose experience is more valuable to the prosecution of liquidation efforts than that of the former project director's; and there is no one who possessses as much technical knowledge of the entire lot of commodities as he. He is, furthermore, impelled by strong motivation which stems from the following : -
1. One's satisfaction in finishing what he started. The former project director initiated and completed most of the P.A.'s (Procurement Authority).
2. The belief that his participation in the liquidation of the projects will be vindicate those who made him serve as project director. He was appointed by former Speaker Jose Yulo who was then Chairman of PHILCUSA upon the recommendation of former UP President V.A. Tan. Former FOA Director Harry Brenn gave his concurrence to the appointment.  
Recommendation
In view of the foregoing it is is requested that the authority granted to the former project director in 31 January be revised to incorporate the substance and bounds of the rough-draft which follows : -
"You are hereby commissioned to handle the UP part of the liquidation of NEC-ICA properties covered by the counterpart projects of which you were director effective to-day until 30 June 1964 or until the consumation of the transfer of the title to NEC-ICA properties from ICA to UP,- whichever is the earlier. In the discharge of your duties you will utilize to the maximum the cooperation of previous teams."
C.A. Ortigas
Professor of Engineering Sciences

column9: (1964, February 11)
column10:
MEMORANDUM TO :
Dr. E.T Virata
Acting President
University of the Philippines 
Subject : LIQUIDATION OF NEC-ICA PROJECTS 30 & 92
In connection with your letter dated 31 January 1964 authorizing the undersigned to participate in the university's effort for liquidating NEC-AID properties, he submits the following summary of the action he has taken and the findings therefrom : 
I. Action and Findings
1) In pursuance of a suggestion from the Business Executive, on 3 January I called on Mr. Arsenio Talingdan, who is coordinating above mentioned efforts. Mr. Talingdan confirmed the Business Executive's previous statement to the effect that the U.P. team assigned to perform the work required for the final turnover of NEC-ICA commodities had "bogged down". I gathered from what Mr. Talingdan said that the team assigned to gather records at NEC was withdrawn by their immediate superior. 
2) On 6 January I called at NEC to obtain first hand information that might enhance the effectiveness of my participation. NEC officials confirmed the fact that the U.P. team had asked NEC to furnish U.P. with a detailed list of NEC-ICA properties as previously mentioned by Mr. Antonio. According to NEC the problem involved in that request was solved right there and then. NEC informed the U.P. team that NEC is not in a position to furnish such list but that NEC is not in a position to furnish such list but that NEC would allow U.P. team to look at their files. NEC is not requiring U.P. to include detailed specifications in her inventory lists. For example, "Electric motor" would be enough description of an item; - it is not necessary to put "rpm", "voltage", and "accessories", etc. 
3) On the same day I called at AID to gather further information on the status of this liquidation efforts. I was informed at AID that the inventory of NEC-ICA property submitted by U.P. as of about six months ago represented only about 60% of the total value. According to the source of this information the U.P. team was advised to continue its work and keep AID informed of the progress from time to time. The same source stated that AID had not heard from U.P. since then. 
II. Further Findings
1) The files of the former project director show that the inventory made before 1960 covering only equipment received by the College of Engineering up to 1958 amounts to more than 60% of total value of equipment received. 
2) The team engaged in this liquidation possesses only a very hazy image of the overall picture of the project. 
3) The vocabulary of the members of the team is seriously inadequate to the extent that in some cases they are not aware that a piece of equipment that they are looking for is lying at their feet or is within the reach of their fingers. 
III. Psychological Observations 
The following speculation may be relevant to the liquidation efforts : it appears that the slowness of the work of the U.P. team is due to the fear on the part of the property office to face, embrace or assume the responsibility involved in connection with the NEC-ICA property because they are unable to grasp what it is. 
Recommendations
I. In order to achieve any progress, the team assigned at NEC should be reactivated as early as possible. 
II. Simultaneously a physical checkup of equipment in Diliman using records at the former director's files be made at the various departments that have received NEC-ICA equipment thru C.P.'s 30 and 92. These departments are : Industrial Pharmacy, Chemistry, Physics, Geology, Zoology and Botany, including the entire Colleges of Education, Business Administration and Veterinary Medicine. The cooperation of heads and property officers of the various departments are badly needed for this phase of work. 
III. That the former project director be commissioned to cooperate with the property office and heads of departments concerned. 
C.A. ORTIGAS
Professor of Engineering Sciences

MEMO TO
Dr. E.T. Virata
Executive Vice President
University of the Philippines

This is to place on the records what I understood from our brief conversation last week regarding the extension of my participation in the liquidation of NEC-AID properties involved in various counterpart projects. The work has suffered some unavoidable delay particularly with respect to PA's 2283 and 3034 which were issued for Chemistry and Industrial Pharmacy in 1958. The Department of Chemistry has been helping us generously but there are still about P20,000 worth of commodities that cannot be located. Maybe about one-half of these are consumable and perishable commodities. 
I had a conference with Dr. Pulido this morning. It was agreed between him and myself that further work should be done in identifying these missing equipment with respect to the probability that several errors might have been committed in marking the properties in the Department of Chemistry. For instance, it is probable that some UP-purchased equipment were, by mistake, marked ICA, while some ICA-purchased equipment were, by mistake, marked UP. The basis of the speculation rests on the fact that there is a large number of equipment present in the Chemistry laboratories which are marked Philcuss but which do not appear in the shipping documents that were reconstructed from NTC records at NEC by a team of the Property Office.
In view of the above, it is recommended that the commission of the undersigned "to handle the UP part of the liquidation of ICA properties" be extended until July 31, 1964. 
C.A. ORTIGAS
Professor of Engineering Sciences

Dr. Paul R. Gollong
Manager, Armour Research Foundation
Illinois Institute of Technology
10 West 35th ST., Chicago 16
Illinois, USA
Dear Paul : I apologize for the delay in replying to your letter of September 12. Weeks ago it was difficult to gauge what the authoritie involved on our side are intending to do about the industrial research program. Last week, however, indications began to take on an unmistakable pattern. 
It is now certain that in making a request for an advisor on the general aspects of research, the National Science Development Board did not intend to disturb our industrial research center program. As a matter of fact, the NSDB recommended yesterday that the Project Director of the U.P. Industrial Research and Training Center be also the Project Director of the proposed contract with Armour Research Foundation. 
I want to tell you that your interest in our efforts to advance industrial research in the Philippines is very deeply appreciated by the undersigned as well as by his colleagues. 
President Sinco, the new president of the University of the Philippines, left last night by PAA for his six-weeks visit to the United States. He told me that he will be stopping by Chicago but did not know when. I suggested to him to get acquainted with key officials of the Armour Research Foundation, particularly with Dr. Barthel and Mr. Gollong. He will spend most of his time in Washington and New York. If you should wish to get in touch with him, maybe you could obtain his forwarding address from the Philippine Embassy in Washington or the Philippine Consulate in Chicago. 
Our warmest regards to Mrs. Gollong and you. 
Very sincerely, 
C.A. ORTIGAS
Dean

column9: (1958, September 12)
column10:
Dean C.A. Ortigas
College of Engineering
University of the Philippines
Diliman, Quezon City, Philippines
My dear Dean Ortigas :
Imagine our dismay to learn this week from ICA/Washington that there is further delay in the Industrial Research Center program. Seemingly, your Government has requested that a survey first be conducted. This survey, according to our understanding, would deal with industrial research generally, the position of various Philippine entities within the overall program, etc.  
We are not quite certain of the full details, of course, but we are disappointed that there is a further delay, one which could well be for six months or more before the Industrial Research Center program is considered. Thus we wonder if you could shed further light on the matter so that we know what the present possibilities are for any kind of participation by the Armour Research Foundation. 
We might add here that we could very well conduct such a survey, especially in view of the fact that back in 1951 your Government first approached us with respect to such a survey. It was only because of certain political changes that program was not initiated. Too, as you know from our many discussions and from the material we have sent to you, we have conducted numerous similar surveys elsewhere, several of them leading to specific research centers. We, ourselves, favor the concept of one organization being responsible for not only the conduct of the survey, but also the implementation of the recommendations arising therefrom. 
We would very much appreciate hearing from you at your earliest convenience. 
Sincerely, 
Paul B. W. Gollong
Manager

Biography of Dr. Christopher E. Barthel Jr., Assistant Director, Armour Research Foundation of Illinois Institute of Technology 
Dr. Christopher E. Barthel, Jr., is assistant director of Armour Research Foundation of Illinois Institute of Technology, Chicago, one of the world's largest independent research organizations. 
Born in Gonzales, La., in 1911, he received his B.S. and M.S. degrees in physics from Louisiana State University in 1932 and 1933, respectively, finishing the work with highest academic honors. After serving as an instructor of physics at Washington and Lee University, Lexington, Va., he pursued further graduate work at Iowa State College and received a Ph.D. degree from that institution in 1940. 
In July, 1941, Barthel joined the staff of the Naval Ordinance Laboratory in Washington, D.C. He was placed in charge of the Magnetic Models Section, one of the largest and most active operations of the Laboratory, and later was appointed to a three-man committee for planning buildings, grounds, facilities, and equipment for a multi-million dollar permanent plant for the Laboratory.
He also served as personnel advisor to the officer-in-charge and as director of personnel for the Laboratory. After the war, Barthel led the research activities of a large number of scientists and engineers at the Laboratory, including the organization, staffing, and directing of research programs in the electronic computing and controls and acoustics fields. 
For his diversified scientific and administrative activities with the NOL during the war he was awarded the Navy's highest civilian honor, the DIstinguished Civilian Service Award for outstanding contributions to the war effort. 
Barthel joined the staff of Armour Research Foundation in 1947 as assistant chairman of physics research, and five months later was appointed chairman of the department. He directed its operation and management until 1950, when he was appointed assistant director of the Foundation, to plan for and direct the recruiting of staff members, classification of positions and salary administration, and to serve as counsel to staff members on personal and professional problems. 
In 1953 Barthel was appointed director of a program for the development of the Union of Burma's scientific and engineering research, under a new contract between that nation and Armour Research Foundation. Under his direction, legislation was prepared and passed by the Burmese parliament for the establishment of a research board to plan and administer scientific activities, a research council to advise the government, and the Union of Burma Applied Research Institute to conduct research aimed at the industrial and economic development of the country. 
He also directed the formation of the Union of Burma Atomic and numerous other professional and honorary groups. In addition, he has served as president of the Chicago Technical Council; engineering advisor of the Encyclopedia Britannica; a member of the board of directors, chairman of the board, and president of the National Electronics Conference; member of the electronics committee of the Research and Development Board of the National Military Establishment, and chairman of the 1957 National Industrial Research Conference. 
Barthel edited two volumes of the Proceedings of the National Electronics Conference and has written numerous articles and given addresses on technical subjects, research management, personnel administration and Burma. 
Barthel resides with his wife and two children at 1135 Jeffrey Court, North, Northbrook, Ill.
