column9: (1959, February 25)
column10:
Message for Philippinensian
I am happy to note that "The Dignity of Man" has been chosen as the theme of the 1959 Philippinensian. To the staff of this publication - congratulations for this choice : 
The dignity of man is not separable from the dignity of labor. The U.P. College of Engineering has always insisted that her students observe the dignity of labor - both manual and intellectual. The undersigned believes that efforts dedicated to the learning of new knowledge and the acquisition of useful skill shape the life of any student into a pattern within which he or she achieves the highest form of fulfillment of the noblest aspirations of mankind under any regime. 
C.A. Ortigas
Dean

column9: (1959, January 27)
column10:
The Dean
College of Engineering
University of the Philippines
Diliman, Rizal
Dear Sir/Madam :
We have this honor to inform you that we need your message for the 1959 PHILIPPINENSIAN. This year's theme is "The Dignity of Man". In this connection, we would be extremely grateful if, in writing your message, you would lay particular emphasis on the relation of your college to this theme--how the graduates can work toward dignifying man, how they can dedicate themselves to the fulfillment of the nobler aspirations of man. 
Likewise, kindly send us a list of the Faculty of Instruction of your college with their respective degrees, honors, and schools attended. 
May we expect your message before February 20, 1959?
Respectfully yours, 
MIGUEL A. ENRIQUEZ, JR.

Dr. Jose C. Locsin
Chairman
National Economic Council
Manila 
Dear Dr. Locsin : 
The U.P. College of Engineering has a visitor who is very desirous of calling on you. He is Mr. Paul B.W. Gollong, Manager of the International Division of the Armour Research Foundation, who is stopping here in Manila for a few days on his way to review their operations in Burma in connection with the Institute of Applied Research of the Union of Burma which was established through contract between the Government of Burma and the Government of the United States. It is contemplated that the call, if granted, will take place on Wednesday afternoon, March 18. 
The undersigned will deeply appreciate a reply at your earliest convenience, - by the bearer of this note, if possible.
Very truly yours, 
C.A. Ortigas
Dean

President Teodoro Evangelista
Far Eastern University
Quezon Blvd., Manila 
Dear President Evangelista : Pursuant to our discussion at the meeting of the National Science Development Board on April 10, 1959, Dr. Byerly contacted Dr. Brings on April 15, 1959. Dr. Brings expressed his desire to teach the course E.S. 201 (Advanced Mathematical Methods in Engineering), one of the courses in the University of the Philippines Atomic Energy Training Program leading to the degree of Master of Engineering (Atomic Energy). This course would meet from 4 to 6 P.M., Monday through Friday or other times as arranged between Dr. Brings and the students. The course will start on Monday, April 20, 1959 and continue for six weeks. 
Upon your concurrence of this matter, we will proceed to procure a temporary professorial lecturer's position for Dr. Brings for the Summer session. In addition, I have already sent a letter to the National Science Development Board for compensation for Dr. Brings. Approval of this interest for financing under Project 2.1 will provide compensation for Dr. Brings services. 
Sincerely yours, 
C.A. ORTIGAS
Dean
(5 copies)

Dr. Thomas H. Hamilton
Vice President for Academic Affairs
MICHIGAN STATE UNIVERSITY
East Lansing, Michigan
U.S.A.
Dear Dr. Hamilton : It is with deep gratitude that I hereby acknowledge receipt of the two reports on MSUO curriculum. These reports will certainly be of great help in the updating of our curriculum in line with the general education program. With warm regards, I am 
Yours sincerely, 
C.A. ORTIGAS
Dean

Dr. C. A. Ortigas
College of Engineering
University of the Philippines
Diliman, Quezon City
Dear Dean Ortigas : Please find enclosed two reports on MSUO curriculum. I hope you will find them helpful. 
Sincerely, 
Thomas H. Hamilton
Vice President

Dr. Thomas H. Hamilton
Vice President
Michigan State University
East Lansing, Michigan
U.S.A.
Dear Dr. Hamilton : I received this morning two envelopes each containing "The Meadow Brook Seminars on Higher Learning". Thank you very much for your thoughtfulness. The material that you sent at an earlier date referred to in your letter did not arrived at all. I wonder if it was different from the one that arrived this morning. I suspect that material might be the engineering program proposed for MSU-O. If it were, then it is something that we would like to have very much. Mrs. Ortigas as well as many of your friends here are in good health; they are joining me in extending to you their warmest. 
Sincerely yours, 
C.A. ORTIGAS
Dean

Dr. Thomas Hamilton
Vice President
Michigan State University
East Lansing, Michigan
U.S.A.
Dear Mr. Hamilton : 
The work on the general education program in the University of the Philippines has reached such a stage that we in the College of Engineering feel optimistic about putting into effect in Diliman this coming June a 5-year B.S. program in engineering similar to the MSUO curriculum which was evolved from the seminars of distinguished educators represented by the President of the California Institute of Technology, a distinguished professor of Yale University, the Dean of Engineering of MIT, the Dean of Engineering of MSU, etc. 
I should like to renew my request for the above-mentioned materials. Thanking you in advance for your kind attention, I am 
Yours truly,
C.A. ORTIGAS
Dean

Prof. Wyeth Allen, Chairman
Department of Industrial Engineering
College of Engineering
UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN
Ann Arbor, Michigan
Dear Professor Allen :
I am sorry that we missed each other when you stopped by on your way home from Tokyo. Here's hoping that some day we shall see living a very active life with the young people on the Waseda contract. 
I will be glad to meet Dr. Schuck. I shall lookl out for him and inform him about your letter and my reply to you. 
With best wishes, I am 
Yours sincerely, 
C.A. Ortigas
Dean

Dean C.A. Ortigas
College of Engineering
University of the Philippines
Quezon City, Philippines
Dear Dean Ortigas : Mrs. Allen and I stopped in Manila for a day and a half on our way home from Tokyo early this year in order to attend a University of Michigan affair. I was quite disappointed not to see you but I found out from out from your office that you were away at that time. 
As you probably know, the Boy Scouts are having a big event in your country this summer and Dr. Arthur Schuck, Chief Executive in the United States, will attend. Dr. Schuck is a very good friend of mine and I have given him your name and hope that you have a chance to meet him. Anything you can do for him will be much appreciated. 
As you probably know, when Professor Ericksen returned, I gave him a half time job helping take care of the Japanese professors who came on our Waseda contract. He is certainly a fine man on this kind of work and the men just love him. Some day, when you have a few minutes time, I would be interested in knowing how you are getting along with the Industrial Engineering Department. 
Sincerely,

Mr. Paul D. Summers
Director of I.C.A.
Manila
Dear Mr. Summers :
I deeply appreciate your having given us a choice of three alternate dates, July 5, July 7, and July 13. Of these three, President Sinco indicated that he would be free on July 13. The time, however, that is being suggested by him is 5:30 p.m. The idea is that if we start 5:30 p.m. there would be longer time for conversation. He also indicated his desire to invite your two top men in the Education Division - Dr. Myers and Dr. Hoshall.
Please let me know if the time and date are acceptable to you so we could make arrangements for sending out invitations. 
Sincerely yours, 
C.A. ORTIGAS 
Dean

Mr. Gabino A. Mendoza
Executive Secretary
PHILIPPINE CHAMBER OF TECHNOLOGY
516 Roman Santos Building
Plaza Goiti, Manila
Dear Mr. Mendoza : 
Pursuant to your letter of July 25, I have the pleasure of accepting the invitation of the Philippine Chamber of Technology to join said Chamber as a regular member. Although at the present time I do not see how my joining the Philippine Chamber of Technology would help this Chamber, it is hoped that acquaintance with its operations will suggest ways and means for doing so in the near future. Please indicate further steps necessary toward becoming a regular member. 
Yours sincerely, 
C.A. ORTIGAS
Dean

Mr. C.A. Ortigas
Dean, College of Engineering
University of the Philippines
Diliman, Quezon City
Dear Mr. Ortigas : 
We have your letter of July 22, 1959. In answer to your inquiry, kindly be informed that you were proposed for membership by the Chamber's Membership Committee itself, composed of Mr. Arturo V. Tanco as chairman and Messrs. Felix D. Maramba and Florencio Tamesis as members. Their proposal was circularized to all our members and approved by our Board of Directors. Since your membership proposal was made as it were, by the Chamber itself, it was thought that your invitation should be made by someone representing the Chamber as a whole. With regard to the category of membership that you have been invited to, kindly be informed that it was thought of our Membership Committee and of our Board of Directors that you would be invited to join as a regular member. We apologize for not having made the above clear in our original letter. And may I reiterate once again our hope that you shall accept our invitation. 
Sincerely yours,
GABINO A. MENDOZA
Executive Summary

Mr. Gabino A. Mendoza
Executive Secretary
PHILIPPINE CHAMBER OF TECHNOLOGY
516 Roman Santos Building
Plaza Goiti, Manila
Dear Mr. Mendoza : 
This is to acknowledge receipt of your letter of July 18, 1959. After a cursory reading, I found the following lines which are relevant to your letter, namely lines 110 and 111 o page 4 of your By-Laws : "..., shall he be invited in writing by the proposers." I am wondering if this had been deliberately over-looked by you when writing the above-mentioned letter.
Also on page 2 of your Constitution there are described different categories of your organization. I should like to be informed to what category of membership I am being invited, - whether sustaining or regular. 
Thank you for kind letter, I am 
Yours truly, 
C.A. ORTIGAS
Dean

Engineer Crisostomo Ortigas
Dean, College of Engineering
University of the Philippines
Diliman, Quezon City 
Dear Sir :
In the name of the Board of Directors of the Philippine Chamber of Technology, we are happy to invite you to join us in this Chamber as a member. 
A small group of technologists, a list of which is attached herewith, has pioneered in the organization of this Chamber with members from all lines of the technological profession, but limited to those who have successfully occupied responsible positions in the practice of their respective professions for at least 10 years. With the experience of such members not only in technology, but also in management, we have felt that this Chamber can contribute effectively in the development of our national economy. 
We expected no overlapping in contributions or services with existing technological organizations, because ours would be generated from a broader point of view. There can be no confict with UTOP. As a matter of fact the creation of this Chamber was initiated and approved by UTOP. UTOP members are associations, each of which cover one particular technological profession. The members of this Chamber are individuals, and, they cover a wider variety of technological services. 
Our Constitutions and By-Laws provide that cooperation shall be the basis of our relationship with other technological societies, not competition. 
Actual performance in the past couple of years has confirmed all our close expectations and we are satisfied. We have found many national issues and problems falling within the purview of the Chamber. We can see that the ways and system of technologists in the solution of problems, which are based on fundamentals, logic and facts, can contribute substantially in discussions of economic issues. 
Satisfied that the Chamber has a definite role to play in the development of our national economy, the members and our Board of Directors have decided to expand our membership, which is by invitation. You have been proposed for membership by the Membership Committee, and we are all happy to invite you to join us in this public service. 
Enclosed please find copies of our Constitution and By-Laws. May we request you, should you decide to accept our invitation, to kindly send us your curriculum vitae. Many thanks. 
Very truly yours, 
GABINO A. MENDOZA
Executive Secretary

column9: Constitution of the Philippine Chamber of Technology
column10:
PREAMBLE
We, technologists of the Philippines, and others engaged in or specially dedicated in the technological advancement of the country as related to the improvement of social and economic life; recognizing that our country is underdeveloped in industry and in agriculture; recognizing further the necessity of an organized and concerted effort in the field of tehcnology in order to help the government and the country in successfully carrying out its scientific, industrial and agricultural development; in order to promote cooperation and understanding among ourselves; insure mutual assistance in the furtherance of our common undertakings; encourage and maintain the highest standards in the practice of technological professions; and help in the development of technical and vocational man-power, do hereby organize ourselves into a non-profit association and promulgate this Constitution : 
ARTICLE I - NAME
The name of this association shall be the "PHILIPPINE CHAMBER OF TECHNOLOGY".
ARTICLE II - OFFICE AND SEAL
Section 1. - Office. - The principal office of the Association shall be in the City of Manila, Philippines.
Section 2. - Seal. - A circular seal with the words "Philippine Chamber of Technology, Manila, Philippines - 1956" shall be the corporate seal of the association which shall be in the custody of the Corporate Secretary. 
ARTICLE III - PURPOSES
The objectives and purposes for which the association is organized are as follows : 
a) To foster better understanding, relationship and cooperation among those engaged in the practice and advancement of technology, in solving their common problems. 
b) To unify into one strong and solid organization highly experienced technologists for the purpose of advancing the application of technological sciences in the development of industries and agriculture, and of developing sufficient and adequate technical and vocational man-power. 
c) To develop and maintain high standards of service and of ethics in the practice of technological professions and vocations, and to undertake, whenever called upon, services of arbitration and adjudication on cases involving technology and industrial management. 
d) To secure legislation as well as reforms and amendments to existing laws affecting their common interests and to accomplish the purposes of the association.
e) To acquire, maintain and /or operate solely or in association with any institution and/or government agency, such service facilities as a pilot plant or plants, testing or research laboratories, etc., for the benefit of members of the association and for technological advancement in the Philippines. 
f) To establish, or to cooperate in the establishment and recognition of, standards of manufacture, design and construction, installation, assaying, testing, procedural methods, safety, and such other technical standards as may contribute to and promote :
(1) progress in production and marketing
(2) safety to life, health and prosperity
(3) conservation and efficient utilization of natural resources
(4) public confidence, and 
(5) orderly advancement in the development of our national economy. 
g) To cooperate and work with local or foreign Chambers, technological associations and other non-profit civic organizations pursuing objectives and purposes of the same or similar nature as those of the association.
h) To acquire a land site for its office building by purchase, donation, lease or otherwise, and to construct thereon a building or buildings suitable for offices and other activities of the association.
i) To promote such other objectives and purposes analogous or incidental to the above-mentioned purposes and objectives for the general progress and welfare of the Philippines and its inhabitants. 
ARTICLE IV - MEMBERSHIP
Section 1. - Membership. - Membership shall be classified into (1) Regular members, (2) Charter members, and (3) Sustaining members. 
a) Regular members - These shall include all those persons who organized the association and those admitted into the association.
b) Charter members - These shall be composed of those members who signed this Constitution previous to incorporation. 
c) Sustaining members - These shall include individuals, institutions, civic organizations, and commercial firms interested in technological advancement but otherwise ineligible for regular membership. 
Section 2. - Qualifications - Regular members shall possess all of the following qualifications : 
a) Graduate with technological science degree from a credtable university or technical institute, or a technologist who is duly registered and licensed by government to practice his particular profession;
b) Recognized success in the practice of his particular technological profession in responsible positions for not less than ten (10)( years;
c) Good moral character, good personal business, and professional reputation, and possessed with the ideal of service as the basis of worthy enterprises.
Section 3. Classification. The regular members of this association shall be classified in accordance with their respective technological professions. 
ARTICLE V - BOARD OF DIRECTORS
The Philippine Chamber of Technology shall be goverrned by a Board of Directors composed of nine (9) members who shall be elected for a term of one year. 
ARTICLE VI - AMENDMENTS
Amendments to this Constitution may be proposed by the Board of Directors or by a majority vote of the members at any regular meeting but such proposed amendments may only be approved after each and every member shall have been furnished with written copies of the full text of the amendments not less than one (1) month previous to the next regular meeting and only after they have been fully discussed in said regular meeting. All the members shall be furnished with ballots on which they will vast their votes o the amendments within thirty (30) days after the date of the regular meeting in which the proposed amendments were discussed. An affirmative vote of two-thirds of all the Regular members shall be required to approve any amendment and to incorporate the same in this Constitution. Immediately after an amendment is thus approved, all members shall be notified accordingly in writing. 
APPROVED AND ADOPTED, this 7th day of September, 1956, in the City of Manila, Philippines.

column9: By-laws of the Philippine Chamber of Technology
column10:
Article I - Name
The name of this association shall be the "PHILIPPINE CHAMBER OF TECHNOLOGY".
Article II - Office and Seal
Section 1. - Office. - The principal office of the Association shall be in the City of Manila, Philippines. 
Section 2. - Sea;l. - A circular seal with the words "Philippine Chamber of Technology, Manila, Philippines - 1956" shall be the corporate seal of the association which shall be in the custody of the Corporate Secretary. 
Article III - Purposes
The objectives and purposes for which the association is organized are as follows : 
a) To foster better understanding, relationship, and cooperation among those engaged in the practice and advancement of technology, in solving their common problems. 
b) To unify into one strong and solid organization highly experienced technologists for the purpose of advancing the application of technological sciences in the development in the industries and agriculture, and of developing sufficient and adequate technical and vocational man-power. 
c) To develop and maintain high standards of service and of ethics in the practice of technological professions and vocations, and to undertake, whenever called upon, services of arbitration and adjudication on cases involving technology and industrial management. 
d) To secure legislation as well as reforms and amendments to existing laws affecting their common interests and to accomplish the purposes of the association. 
e) To acquire, maintain and/or operate solely or in association with any institution and/or government agency, such service facilities as a pilot plant or plants, testing or research laboratories, etc., for the benefit of members of the association and for technological advancement in the Philippines. 
f) To establish, or to promote the establishment and recognition of standards of manufacture, design and construction, installation, assaying, testing, procedural methods, safety, and such other standards as may contribute to and promote :
(1) progress in production and marketing
(2) safety of life, health, and property
(3) conservation and efficient utilization of natural resources
(4) public confidence, and 
(5) orderly advancement in the development of our national economy. 
g) To affiliate with local or foreign Chambers, associations, and other non-profit civic organizations pursuing objectives and purposes of the same or similar nature as those of the association. 
h) To acquire a land site for its office building by purchase, donation, lease or otherwise, and to construct thereon a building or buildings suitable for offices and other activities of the association. 
i) To promote such other objectives and purposes analogous or incidental to the above-mentioned purposes and objectives for the general progress and welfare of the Philippines and its inhabitants. 
Article IV - Membership
Section 1. - Membership. - Membership shall be classified into (1) Regular members, (2) Charter members, and (3) Sustaining members.
a) Regular members - These shall include all those persons who organized the association and those admitted into the association. 
b) Charter members - These shall be composed of those regular members who signed this Constitution previous to incorporation. 
c) Sustaining members - These shall include individuals, institutions, civic organizations and commercial firms interested in technological advancement but otherwise ineligible for regular membership.
Section 2. - Qualifications - For eligibility to regular membership in the association, the proposed member must possess all of the following qualifications : 
a) Graduate with technological science degree from a creditable university or technical institute, or a technologist who is duly registered and licensed by the government to practice his particular profession;
b) Recognized success in the practice of his particular technological profession in responsible positions for not less than 10 years; 
c) Good moral character, good personal, business, and professional reputation, and possessed with the ideal of service as the basis of worthy enterprise. 
Section 3 - Classification. 
a) The regular members of this association shall be classified in accordance with their respective technological professions.  
b) The technological classifications shall be limited to the following : 
(1) Agriculture
(2) Architecture
(3) Chemistry
(4) Civil Engineering
(5) Electrical Engineering
(6) Mechanical Engineering
(7) Mineral Engineering
(8) Surveying
(9) Miscellaneous other technological professions
c) The number of regular members under any one technological classification shall not exceed 20% of the total number of members of the association at any particular time. 
d) Minor classification under each technological classification may be created by the Board of Directors. 
Section 4. - Admission 
a) No direct application for membership shall be considered. A new member may be proposed as provided for herein and, only after the proposal for his admission is approved by the Board of Directors, shall he be invited in writing by the proposers. 
b) No proposed member shall be considered for admission unless he is sponsored by three Charter members; provided that, after the second year of the association's existence, he may be sponsored by any three Regular members who have been such members for at least two(2) consecutive years.
c) The admission of a proposed member shall be referred to all the regular members in writing and objections from regular members submitted in writing to the Board of Directors within 30 days from date of mailing the corresponding notice shall be considered before the Board takes action. A unanimous approval by all the members of the Board shall be required for the admission of a proposed member. 
Section 5. - Termination and Reinstatement of Membership. 
a) Automatic termination - Any membership shall automatically terminate : 
(1) upon failure of the member to pay his semi-annual fee three months after it is due. 
(2) upon suspension of revocation of his professional license by the government
(3) upon conviction of the member by any court of justice for any crime, immorality, or fraudulent transactions. 
(4) upon death or resignation of the regular member. 
b) Expulsion - Any member may be expelled by vote of five (5) members of the Board of Directors after charges are preferred against him under oath in writing and a fair hearing had upon the same before the Board of Directors :
(1) when he makes improper and illegal use of the name of the Philippine Chamber of Technology for his personal material gain, or that of his family; 
(2) when he violates the code of ethics adopted by the corporation, provided however, that any member may be expelled for any other cause by the unanimous vote of all the members of the Board of Directors. 
c) Reinstatement - Any member, whose membership had been terminated for failure to pay his dues, for suspension or revocation of his professional license, or by virtue of court conviction under Section 5 :
(1) may be reinstated by a vote of 5 members of the Board of Directors provided that, in the second case, the suspension or revocation of his professional license is lifted, and in the last case, a higher court of justice had reversed the decision of conviction, and provided, further, that, in all cases, he pays all fees and accounts in arrears up to the time of termination of his membership. 
Section 6. Honorary Membership - The Board of Directors may, upon approval by unanimous vote of all its members, confer an honorary membership in the association upon any individual who has made an outstanding achievement or contribution to advancement in the field of science and technology in the Philippines. 
ARTICLE V - FEES AND PRIVILEGES
Section 1 - Entrance Fee. An entrance fee of P100.00 shall be paid by each Chapter member previous to incorporation and each Regular member shall pay the same amount as entrance fee upon approval of his membership. 
Section 2 - Membership Fee. 
a) Each Charter, Regular and Sustaining member shall pay a membership fee of P50,00 semi-annually payable in advance, provided that a new member, upon approval of his membership, shall pay this fee corresponding to the semester in which the membership was approved. 
b) The membership fee may be revised in the future upon approval by two-thirds (2/3) of all the regular members of the association. 
Section 3. - Privileges
a) Only Charter and Regular members are entitled to vote and be voted upon, without limitations. 
b) All members shall be entitled to all services and privileges that the association may provide for. For this purpose, each Sustaining member which is an institution or a corporation, shall designate as its representative, one of its executives who has discretionary powers within said institution or corporation. 
c) Sustaining members may participate in discussions and deliberations but cannot vote beyond committee levels and cannot be voted upon for any position above that of a committee member. 
ARTICLE VI - BOARD OF DIRECTORS
Section 1. - Membership. - The Philippine Chamber of Technology shall be governed by a Board of Directors composed of nine (9) members who shall be elected for a term of one (1) year. Immediately after their election, the members shall elect from among themselves, a Chairman, a First Vice-Chairman, Second Vice-Chairman, a Corporate Secretary, and a Treasurer; provided, however, that no director who had been elected and who had been elected and had served as Chairman in the last preceding two years, shall be re-elected Chairman. 
Section 2. - Election - The members of the Board of Directors shall be elected by secret ballot in a regular or special meeting of the association not later than in June of each year, provided, that there is a quorum in said meeting by a duly elected Nominating Committee. 
Section 3. - Nominating Committee. The Nominating Committee, to be composed of nine (9) members, shall be elected in a regular meeting not later than three months before date of election of the members of the Board of Directors. Each group of regular members present in this meeting belonging to one technological classification shall elect one member of this Committee from said technological classification.
Section 4. - Qualifications of Nominees. A nominee shall be a Charter member or a Regular member of not less than two years membership and who must have previously served as Chairman of any committee or sub-committee. He must be a member in good standing and has no record of termination of membership for any cause. His written acceptance of the nomination and his affirmation that he will serve dedicatedly if elected, shall previously be secured. 
Section 5. - Tenure of Office. - All members of the Board of Directors and all officers appointed by said Board shall take office and assume their respective duties and responsibilities on the first day of July following their election or appointment, for one year or until successors have been elected and have qualified. 
Section 6. - Three (3) consecutive unexcused absences of any member of the Board of Directors shall automatically terminate the term of office of said member, in which case the Board of Directors shall elect in the next regular meeting a new member to replace him. 
ARTICLE VII - OFFICERS
Section 1 - The officers of the association shall be the following : 
a) President, who shall be the one elected as Chairman of the Board of Directors;
b) First Vice-President, who shall be the one elected as First Vice-Chairman of the Board of Directors;
c) Second Vice-President, who shall be the one elected as Second Vice-Chairman of the Board of Directors;
d) Treasurer, who shall be elected by the Board of Directors from among its members;
e) Corporate Secretary, who shall be elected by the Board of Directors from among its members;
f) Executive Secretary, who shall be elected, at the option of, and by the Board of Directors, who may or may not be a member of the association; 
g) Auditor, who shall be elected by the Board of Directors and who may or may not be a member of the association;
h) Other officers and employees whose positions are created by the Board of Directors shall be appointed by the President with the approval of the Board of Directors. 
Section 2. - Compensation. - No member elected or appointed to any office or position in the association except that of Executive Secretary, shall receive any compensation. The salaries and compensation of officers and employees shall be fixed by the Board of Directors. 
ARTICLE VIII - STANDING COMMITTEES
Section 1. - Major Committees. To attain the purposes and objectives of the association, there shall be created the following standing major Committees : Committee on Club Affairs, Commitee on Industrial Development, Commitee on Agricultural Development, Committee on Technical Man-Power Development, Commitee on Community Development, Committee on Fuels & Power Development 
Section 2 - Minor committees may be created by the Board of Directors under each major committee in Section 1, which shall include Membership Committee under the Committee on Club Affairs, to be composed of Charter members and/or Regular members whose qualifications shall be the same as those for nominees for the Board of Directors in Article VI, Section 4. 
Section 3. - Each major committee shall be headed by a member of the Board of Directors, provided however, that the Corporate Secretary shall be designated as the Chairman of the Committee on Club Affairs. 
ARTICLE IX - DUTIES OF OFFICERS
Section 1. - President. - The President shall be the executive officer and the only spokesman of the association. He shall preside at all meetings of the association and of the Board of Directors. He shall be responsible for the execution of all policies, resolutions, and actions of the Board of Directors and of the membership in meetings of the association. He shall direct the activities of the association towards the attainment of its purposes and objectives in accordance with its Constitution and By-Laws and with the specific policies and resolutions of the membership and of the Board of Directors. He shall sign all checks and approve all disbursements of funds of the association. 
Section 2. - First Vice-President. - The First Vice President shall act as President in case of inability, sickness, absence or any otehr temporary disability of the President and shall perform such other duties as may be delegated to him by the President. 
Section 3. - Second Vice-President. The Second Vice-President shall act as President in case of inability, sickness, absence or any other temporary disability of both the President and the First Vice-President and shall perform such other duties as may be delegated to him by the President. 
Section 4. - Corporate Secretary. - The Corporate Secretary shall be the custodian of all records, papers, and other documents of the association; he shall take minutes and prepare resolutions at all meetings of the members and of the Board of Directors; he shall perform such other duties and functions as may be delegated to him by the President or by the Board of Directors in the execution of the policies and of all resolutions of the Board of Directors and of the membership of the association in the furtherance of the objectives of the association. 
Section 5. - Executive Secretary - The duties of the Executive Secretary shall be those of the Corporate Secretary which are delegated to him by the Board of Directors thru recommendation of the Corporate Secretary. 
Section 6. - Treasurer. The Treasurer shall be the financial officer of the association and as such he will be the custodian of all funds and properties of the association; he shall countersign all checks and disbursements funds of the association. The Treasurer shall make an annual report of the finances of the association and such other reports as the Board of Directors may require him to make. 
Section 7. - Auditor. The Auditor shall audit and examine the receipts of all monies and disbursements of funds of the corporation and shall see to it that disbursements are properly carried out in accordance with these By-Laws, and with the resolutions of the Board of Directors and of the membership of the association. The Auditor shall make an annual report to the Board of Directors and the members of the association of the state of finance of the association and such other reports as the Board of Director may require of him. 
ARTICLE X - PERMANENT VACANCIES 
Section 1. - Members of the Board of Directors. - In case of death, removal, resignation or permanent vacancy for any cause in the membership of the Board of Directors, the same shall be filled by the Board of Directors, provided that such new member shall hold office only until the expiration of the term of the Director he has replaced. 
Section 2. - President. - In case of death, removal or resignation of the President, the First Vice-President shall take over the office and assume the duties and responsibilities of President. 
Section 3 - First Vice-President. In case of death, removal or resignation of the First Vice-President or of his assumption of the office of the President, the Second Vice-President shall take over the office and assume the duties and responsibilities of the First Vice-President. 
Section 4. - Second Vice-President. In case of death, removal or resignation of the Second Vice-President or of his assumption of the office of the First Vice-President, the Board of Directors shall immediately elect a new Second-President from among its members after which a new member shall be elected to fill the vacancy left in the membership of the Board of Directors, who shall hold office only until the expiration of the term of office of the member elected to the Second Vice-Presidency. 
Section 5. - Any permanent vacancy created in any office of the association shall be filled in immediately either by the Board of Directors or by the President with the approval of the Board of Directors, as the case may be, in exactly the same manner as is stipulated in these By-Laws for that office. 
ARTICLE XI - MEETINGS
Section 1. Regular meetings. There shall be regular monthly meetings of the association to be held on the first Saturday of each month at 12:30 p.m. in the principal office of the association, or at such other day, time and place within the City of Manila as may be fixed by the Board of Directors. 
Section 2. Board meetings. The Board of Directors shall hold regular meetings every month on the second Monday of each month at the principal office of the association or at any place and at such time of the day as the Board of Directors may decide. 
ARTICLE XII - SPECIAL MEETINGS
Section 1. A special meeting of the membership of the association or of the Board of Directors may be called by the Corporate Secretary oat the instance of the President or at the instance of any 3 Directors, or at the instance of any 10 members, who shall so inform the Corporate Secretary in writing, provided that, all the members of the association or the members of the Board of Directors, as the case may be, shall have been notified not later than 10 days previous to the date of the meeting. 
ARTICLE XIII - PUBLIC AFFAIRS
Section 1. - This association shall not endorse or recommend any candidate for public office and shall not discuss at any meeting the merits or demerits of any such candidate. 
ARTICLE XIV - AMENDMENTS
Amendments to these By-Laws may be proposed by the Board of Directors or by a majority vote of the members at any regular meeting but such proposed amendments may only be approved after each and every member shall have been furnished with written copies of the full text of the amendments not less than one (1) month previous to the next regular meeting. All the members shall be furnished with ballots on which they will cast their votes on the amendment within 30 days after the date of the regular meeting in which the proposed amendments were discussed. An affirmative vote of two-thirds (2/3) of all the Regular members shall be required to approve any amendment and to incorporate the same in these By-Laws. Immediately after an amendment is thus approved, all members shall be notified accordingly in writing. 
Approved and adopted this 31st day of August, 1956, in the City Manila, Philippines

column9: (1959, August 12)
column10:
Mr. Roel Ortigas
Xavier University
Cagayan de Oro City
Misamis Oriental
Dear Roel : 
We have had some interesting visitors in the last term; - all of them physicists of considerable renown. One of them was a Jesuit father who lectured on Quantum Mechanics. He gave an excellent performance. He is here on special tour of the National Bureau of Standards on the subject of propagation of waves. He returned to the United States yesterday but he will be back next year to help establish an observatory at Loyola Heights. 
The new calendar year of the UP, end to end, is undergoing some revisions on account of the congestion of students' subjects which stems from the fact that its length is only 160 days a year as against the old which was 200 days long. The revision will consist in the addition of one more than term which will result in 204-day calendar. In spite of the congestion, however, many students preferred the end to end calendar to the old calendar as it prevents them from procastinating. 
How is your Hamilton performing? It seems somewhat slow when we got it from the repair shop; - about one minute a day I think. 
We are still having Fathers O'Brien and Ortiz at least once a week. 
Affectionately yours, 
C.A. ORTIGAS

Radio Propagation Physics Division
NATIONAL BUREAU OF STANDARDS
Boulder, Colorado 
U.S.A.
Dear Fr. Glover :
I have just received an air-mail envelope containing the Iospherical Radio Propagation. I am grateful to you for your thoughtfulness and promptness in supplying my needs. Last week I received from Fr. Hennessey the Introduction to Differential Equations in Physics, which I have found very useful in my course, Engineering Science 202, Mathematical Methods in Engineering. 
I hope to be able someday in the near future to reciprocate. I look forward with anticipation to the time when you will be spending your life in the Philippines as our "next door neighbor."
Sincerely yours,

column9: (1959, August 20)
column10:
Dr. C. A. Ortigas
Dean, College of Engineering
University of the Philippines
Quezon City, Philippines
Dear Dr. Ortigas
When I had the pleasure of meeting you last week at the Physics Department, you mentioned that you were interested in a book that would give something about the propagation of radio waves. At that time I think I said something about a book that is put out here at the National Bureau of Standards. This is not the best in the field, but I must say that I found some of the chapters rather helpful, and the references do give a good introduction to the field. I hope that you find it useful. 
Permit me to say how much I enjoyed my brief stay at U.P. last week and how much I look forward to being able to return to the Philippines and to be your "next door neighbor" in the years to come. I personally feel that science and technology has a big role to play in the economic development of the Philippines, and I am quite interested in making whatever small contribution I can to this worth-while effort. 
Sincerely yours, 
Francis Glover, S.J.
Radio Propagation Physics Division

column9: (1959, December 1)
column10:
Dean Crisostomo Ortigas
College of Engineering
University of the Philippines
Diliman, Quezon City
Dear Dean Ortigas : 
Mrs. Barbara Yap Angeles of Davao City filed an application for the accreditation of her Harvardian Colleges as a University. In an investigation made by the Director of Private Schools, his report was adverse to the application. Mrs. Angeles has impugned this report on several grounds which you will find in the records among them, prejudice on the part of those who prepared the report. She also claims that the deficiencies pointed out by the investigators have been remedied. It is, therefore, desired by this Department that a new set of investigators be appointed to make a thorough review of this case. 
In a speech delivered by His Excellency, the President of the Philippines, at the Far Eastern University on January 29, 1959, he said the following : 
"How this constitutional guarantee to freedom of education may find more flesh in the development and operation of private institutions of higher learning is the challenge that should be met and exploited today, in response to our pressing need for good men and true, alive and alert to our responsibilities and potentials as a free people.
"...We might have the University of the Philippines to serve as touchstone in the enforcement of this system. Until such a system is developed and established, I am afraid we shall continue to be tolerant with the operation of a government agency of manifestly limited resources and the application of what is generally deplored as an educational straight-jacket."
We have, therefore, thought of appointing the following as the new committee to restudy the merits of this application and we have decided that this committee should compose of the following:
1. Dean Alfredo Morales, Graduate College of Education, U.P.;
2. Dean Crisostomo Ortigas, College of Engineering, U.P.;
3. Prof. Juan Canave, College of Education, U.P.
This Department would deeply appreciate it if you could take this assignment and advise the Secretary on this matter. 
Furthermore, we are also considering new criteria for the accreditation of Universities, and in time will appoint a committee to study this matter and in line with the policy enunciated by the President inthe above-mentioned sppech, we will designate members of the U.P. Faculty to this committee, of whom you will be one. 
Kindly acknowledge receipt of this letter and I hope you will formally accept this designation. 
Truly yours, 
JOSE E. ROMERO
Secretary of Education

column9: (1960, January 6)
column10:
Dr. Paul B.W. Gollong
Director of International Research
Armour Research Foundation of Illinois Institute of Technology
10 West 35th Street, Chicago 16
Illinois, U.S.A.
My dear Paul:
I was indeed very happy to hear from you again. Please accept our best wishes for you and Mrs. Gollong for the New Year; - also for the prosperity of the ARF.
I am still hoping, but with my fingers crossed, that the key people in our country that have anything to do with industrial research would eventually see the wisdom of placing the Industrial Research Center under the University of the Philippines. This is of course a wish that has a very small probability of being fulfilled, since at the present moment the big shots of the Industrial Research Center activities under the National Institute of Science and Technology.
When are you coming to Manila again? Please don't forget to look us up. Mrs. Ortigas joins me in sending our warmest regards to the Gollong family. 
Very sincerely yours,
C.A. ORTIGAS
Dean

column9: (1959, December 30)
column10:
Dean C.A. Ortigas
College of Engineering
University of the Philippines
Diliman, Quezon City
The Philippines
My dear Dean Ortigas:
Recently, a copy of Dr. William Watson's report, "Science in the Philippines, 1959", weas sent to me. After reading it thoroughly, I must come to the same conclusion reached by Dr. Watson; namely, that he did not have sufficient information on the Philippines prior to his field work nor enough time on the assignment to develop a really thorough and encompassing report on science and the Philippines. I understand that he will return so that this initial work can be expanded. 
It is interesting to me to read that Dr. Watson makes quite clear the role that the U.P. Industrial Research Center must play. With this further verification of the need for such a center, I hope that the long-planned program eventually will get under way. 
Please accept my best wishes for a most healthy and satisfying New Year.
Sincerely, 
Paul B.W. Gollong
Director of International Reseearch

The President
University of the Philippines
Sir :
Pursuant to the brief conference at your office Thursday noon, the undersigned has the honor of requesting U.P. President's ruling on his LEAVE STATUS. He is programming his activities for next school year and finds that LEAVE STATUS has to be taken into account. 
Upon termination of his deanship, the undersigned assumed that he automatically remained on the cumulative leave basis because less than a year before (on 1 October 1959) the U.P. Board of Regents appointed him Professor and Head of the newly formed department of Engineering Sciences and the U.P. President ordered in Administrative Circular No. 5 Series 1959-60 dated 18 May 1960 that..."4. All existing programs, like the UNESCO Physics Training Program, ICA-NEC programs and projects, shall remain under the direct management of Professor Crisostomo Ortigas until other arrangements are made. The undersigned shall assign any future program or project as he sees fit." 
Of the changes that have taken place during the school year 1960-61, two are relevant to the subject to this letter, namely; the appointment of a new head of the Department of Engineering Sciences and the transfer of the functions of the Vice President of Academic Affairs to the Executive Vice President. 
The undersigned became aware of the first of these two while he was processing some requisition involving purchase of supplies for the Atomic Energy Laboratory sometime in October. The signature of Professor Dominador Ilio appeared over the title of "Head, Engineering Science". Upon inquiry he discovered the Prof. D. Ilio had been appointed to this position two months before. (This removes one reason for keeping the undersigned under the cumulative leave basis). 
Upon learning abou the second change, I consulted with the Executive Vice President. The paragraph of Administrative Circular No. 5 quoted above was touched upon. It was arranged that the undersigned continue to discharge the function that he was discharging when he and Dr. Isidro were jointly operating C.P. 6030-A. This arrangement was later modified, however, when the services of Mr. Jose Juinio ceased to be available to him in January 1961 due to the termination of NSDB Project 2.1. The modification consisted in the release of the undersigned from prime responsibility in connection with dollar commodities. I am attaching pertinent excerpts from my service record in the belief that the President might wish to glance at it while formulating the reply to this letter. 
Very truly yours, 
C.A. ORTIGAS
Professor of Engineering Sciences

University of the Philippines
PHYSICS TEACHERS TRAINING PROGRAMME 
Quezon City

Dean Patrocinio Valenzuela, Vice-Chairman
UNESCO Commission of the Philippines

Dear Dean Valenzuela :

In response to our note of March 4 and in accordance with our subsequent conversation in connection with the said note I am submitting to you the following comments :

The report of Dr. Ralph C. Wilson, specialist in science education, dated January 1959 embodies most of the findings of the three other specialists that preceded him. The recommendations of Dr. Wilson as well as those of the other three touch on similar or related ideas such as training of teachers, apparatus and teaching aids, curriculum and teaching materials, etc. The paucity of materials and inadequacy of the preparation of the teachers particularly those assigned to teaching physics classes have been underscored in Prof. Wilson's report as well as in the abstracts of the reports previously prepared by the specialists that preceded him. The undersigned has not come across any refutation of the findings of the UNESCO specialists; it appears that their observations are adequately justified. In particular they are borned out by my personal experience with the Bureau of Public Schools teachers that have enrolled in the NEC-AID physics teachers training program in the University of the Philippines at Diliman during the last three years. This conclusion has been amply corroborated by other teachers, officials and citizens who feel deep concern with our education system. 
The UNESCO specialists that have come for science education assistance were not familiar with our way of life and the idiosyncrasies of our people. Therefore their recommendations can be no more than suggestive in nature. However, I hasten to add, that the report deals with problems that are so urgent in nature that the sequel step should be undertaken by us without further delay. 
I believe that UNESCO Philippines with the active cooperation of the Bureau of Public Schools, University of the Philippines, together with moral and financial support of the National Science Development Board and the National Economic Council is in a very strong position to push through a dynamic plan that can start operating for producing physics teachers in adequate number not later than August 1964. This prediction is based on the experience which we have acquired in connection with the NEC-AID physics teachers training program at the University of the Philippines. The undersigned further believes that a significant advantage will be gained by an early move on the part of the UNESCO Commission to take advantage of this physics teachers training program by employing it as a launching pad for mounting a large scale Five-Year Physics Teachers Training Program designed to make the universities produce physics teachers in sufficient number starting right away. 
The urgency of the Five-Year Plan mentioned above may be underscored by repeat here again that less than five per cent of teachers teaching physics in the Bureau of Public Schools are physics trained. Furthermore, the universities in the Philippines are not producing physics teachers and no signs are visible that physics teachers in adequate number will be produced in the next ten years. THe undersigned hereby offers his humble services to collaborate with an ad hoc group formed by UNESCO to formulate details of the Five-Year Plan referred to above. 

Yours sincerely,
C.A. ORTIGAS
Programme Management

column9: (1964, January 6)

The President
University of the Philippines
Thru the Executive Vice President

Dear Mr. President :

This letter concerns the Physics Teachers Training Program and my impending retirement which will take place on 2 February 1964. The letter is being written because of some peculiar aspects of the contents of the training program. The undersigned has been involved in its operation (management and instruction) since 1960 at which time it was known that said program would phase out at the end of the school year 1964. The peculiar aspects referred to above may be summarized as follows : 

1) The materials being used for the physics course of the Bureau of Public Schools teachers enrolled under this program are those prepared by the Physical Science Study Committee (PSSC) of the United States. The undersigned is now teaching this course due to shortage of manpower in the Department of Physics. 

2) Another peculiar aspect of the program is handling of Education 299 (Seminar) by the undersigned due to unavailability of professors. In Education 299, the goal is for the teachers under training to complete an adaptation from PSSC materials that would be suitable for use in their classes when they return to their posts. An analogous adaptation was prepared by a previous group of teachers last summer. That adaptation has generated considerable interest in high schools in the Philippines both private and public. This year's adaptation is expected to be superior to last summer's adaptation because about three times as long a period is available for the incubation requirements of modernized concepts and new approaches which characterized the training program.

Should UP find itself confronted with delay in arranging for the takeover of these two courses, the undersigned stands ready to continue handling these two courses until the end of this semester or until his replacement is found, whichever is the earlier. 

Yours truly, 

C.A. ORTIGAS
(Programme Management)
Professor of Engineering Sciences
