The President
University of the Philippines
Thru the Executive Vice President

Dear Mr. President :

In connection with any compulsary and automatic retirement which will go into effect on 2 February 1964 (about three weeks from now) I should to bring to your attention, for the purpose of seeking advice and soliciting help, a bottleneck which holds up the processing of my retirement papers at the Property Office. Mr. Antonio, officer-in-charge of the office believes that he cannot sign my clearance papers until an inventory of dollar aid commodities received from USA is completed in the form satisfactory to NEC. Further , he said he cannot proceed to make this satisfactory inventory list until NEC furnishes his office with a detailed list of the names of the equipment. I learned about this in a conference with Mr. Antonio and his assistant yesterday. They also made me understand that they do not expect that the NEC list is forthcoming, and that they cannot take another step forward in the absence of such list. 
I became involved in the dollar commodities from USA because at the time that most of the equipment for Engineering, Education, Business Administration, Industrial Pharmacy, Geology, Chemistry and Physics were coming in, I was the project director of USOM-NEC projects at Diliman, by designation by Dr. Vidal A. Tan, then U.P. President. 
It appears that the delay mentioned above will last for several years (perhaps forever in the event that the two parties do not agree on a course of action.) I therefore beg of you to find an avenue of action by which Mr. Antonio's objections may be waived.
Should my services be deemed of any value to you for the purpose of breaking the impasse between our property office on the one hand and the NEC counterpart personnel on the other, I stand ready to give them. 
Yours respectfully, 
C.A. ORTIGAS
Professor of Engineering Sciences

The President
University of the Philippines
Campus
 
Sir : 

These comments are being submitted in compliance with your order of 11 March, which was received 13 March. 
I. Records Were Not Lacking.
The following quotation from the Third Progress Report, which will be ready by Palm Sunday, should dispel all doubts regarding availability of ample records at the Business Executive's office : "Using no other records than those in the possession of the property office earlier than 1963, the task of assembling an inventory covering dollar commodities in the numerous P.A.'s under C.P. 20, C.P. 30, C.P. 65, and C.P.'s 430, 530,...930 got under way on 29 February 1964. On 13 March, 1964 an inventory exceeding $1,275,000.00 was completed and submitted to NEC. A meeting amongst NEC, AID, and UP has been scheduled to be held on 31 March tentatively. 
II. Dr. Herrera, Mr. Velasco, Mr. Talingdan
The Business Executive mentioned the roles played by Dr. Herrera, Mr. Velasco and Mr. Talingdan in the two previous liquidation efforts. I am withholding my comments on these until after interviews with these three gentlemen. In the meanwhile I limit myself to wondering why I, who has been eager all the time to participate in the liquidation efforts was not invited to join then or earlier; and to speculating why those three clearks allegedly assigned to Dr. Herrera too six months to find out that there were no records to start with. 
III. 1952-1953 vs. 1953-1959
The absence of commodities received by Chemistry, Geology and Industrial Pharmacy from the June 1963 $600,000.00 inventory casts doubts on the existence of "complete records of 1952-1953 operation in the property office" as claimed by the Business Executive. In any case it became apparent in 1953 that the efforts involved in handling increasing volumes of dollar commodities were beyond the capabilities of our property office. It was decided therefore that this office enter the operation at the time the end-user is opening the crates at which time memorandum receipts are to be prepared. [Representatives of property and/or auditing offices witness the opening of crates].
The end-user acknowledges accountability to UP by memo receipts; and accountability to the project director, by Form 131. UP acknowledges accountability to NEC-ICA via Form 131 executed by the project director. 
IV. Property Responsibility and Jurisprudence
Responsibility for the vast amounts of incoming properties loomed high in our thoughts in 1953. The principle that was adopted in connected with this problem may be outlined as follows : 
1) UP as a recipient of foreign aid is accountable to NEC, which in turn is accountable to USOM, Manila. USOM, Manila is accountable to ICA, Washington.
2) In each of the above institutions there is a network of accountability peculiar to its organizational pattern. 
3) In UP the pattern of network accountability to NEC-ICA may be described as follows : 
a) The project director answers to NEC on behalf of UP. In answering UP's accountability the project director depends on the property office, which is supposed to hold memo-receipts for equipment, etc. 
b) In the event that such a memo receipt had not been duly accomplished or had been mislaid, the project director and/or the property office representative seek the assistance of the end-users. The P.A. number under which the equipment had been procured serves as a guide as to who is the end-user of the equipment in question. The end-user exhibits the equipment or proves its existence by some other means. 
c) The end-user is doubly accountable; first to the property office, via memorandum receipt; and the second to the project director via Form 131. However when either of these two accountabilities is satisfied, the other is also satisfied automatically. In any case the ultimate criterion for the satisfaction of accountability is the physical existence of the property under consideration. Thus when a professor satisfies the accountability to the property office for a piece of equipment such as a Diesel Engine, the accountability of any other person to any other officer in connection with the Diesel Engine is automatically satisfied. 
d) The accountability of the project director is incidental to his function; i.e., it automatically ceases as soon as someone else takes over his functions. Otherwise, the situation would be incongruous in the sense that many people would still remain accountable for properties in C.P.'s 430, 530, ....930 since at one time or another, Dr. Sinco, Dr. Virata, Prof. Campos, Dr. Tentamatay, etc. served as project directors. 
Yours truly, 
C.A. ORTIGAS
Professor of Engineering Sciences

The President
University of the Philippines Campus
Sir : 
In pursuance of the reservation stated in my letter of 20 Feb, 1964 to the effect that I rejected the proposition "that the failure of U.P. to obtain the title to ICA equipment is due to the failure of C.A> Ortigas to account for many ICA equipment received while he was project director" I hereby renew my request in writing that the remaining 50 percent of my cumulative leave be released and that a deed of clearance be issued to me on the ground that all such equipment had been previously acounted for at least once; some, twice; and others, thrice. The following facts and precedents show that all ICA equipment in question had been duly accounted for by their custodians and end-users. 
Facts and Precedents
(1) When Dr. Jose Feliciano, head of Geology Department died his ICA property accountability passed on to Dr. Mateo Tupas. When Dr. Arsenio Gonzales succeeded Dr. Tupas he assumed property accountability, which included equipment from P.A.'s 2265, 3029, 3047 and 6518. Thus Dr. Gonzales considers himself accountible for ICA properties received by Geology department thru the project director. 
(2) When Prof. Enrique Ostrea relinquished the headship of Mining department to Prof. Roman Ramos the latter assumed property accountability including the ICA property purchased for the mining department thru P.A.'s 2265, 3029, 3047, and 6518. This accountability passed on to Dr. Perfecto Guerrero who succeeded Prof. Ramos. Thus like in the Geology department the property responsibiulity has been accounted for twice. 
(3) When Dr. Amando Clemente, head of Chemistry department retired his property accountability passed on to Dr. Salcedo, who in turn passed it on to Dr. Tentamatay, and finally to Dr. Pulido who now assumes the property accountability for ICA equipment received by Chemistry department thru P.A.'s 2283, 3034 and 3049. Thus properties were accounted for thrice in this case. 
(4) Part of the properties received thru P.A.'s 2283, 3034 and 3049 were properly accountability of Dean Valenzuela and this accountability passed on to Dr. Alfredo Santos as a matter of course. 
(5) Similar transfers ran smoothly as mattesr of course for properties procured for Engineering. Thus property accountability of C.A. Ortigas as former project director is a myth particularly because these equipment are here in our laboratories. The department heads say they are here and I believe them because I have seen most of them with my own eyes.
Conclusion
A team of historians employed to process the records will draw the following conclusions :
(1) "The failure of the 1961 liquidation efforts for the transfer of the title to NEC-ICA properties to U.P. was due to the failure of the project director at that time Dr. V.G. Since to account for said property. The project director then did know what should be done and failed to recognize the fact that he did not know. 
(2) The failure of the second liquidation efforts made in 1963 failed because U.P. submitted an inventory, which was nearly $300,000 lower than the inventory submitted in 1961. [The writer of this letter believes that President Romulo present Project Director is not aware of this decrease].
(3) The 1964 efforts being exerted now stand on excellent chance of succeeding because of the participation of C.A. Ortigas, who is familiar with most of the properties involved in the counterpart projects being liquidated. The records indicate that he was invited to participate in this third efforts but was excluded in the first and in the second."
In the foregoing I have not only demonstrated that property accountability is the responsibility of the heads of the departments using the equipment but have also located and identified the causes of the failure of the liquidation efforts in 1961 and in 1963. 
Therefore I reiterate that U.P. cease to hold up my clearance. 
Very truly yours, 
C.A. ORTIGAS
Professor of Engineering Sciences

The President
University of the Philippines Campus

Subject : Third Progress Report 

Dear Mr. President :

On 6 March 1964 the undersigned was commissioned "to handle the UP part of the liquidation of NEC-AID properties..." To prepare himself for the obligations anent this commission he consulted records at all levels. Amongst his findings there are two which should be cast in sharp focus and rendered in bold relief because they affect reputations of individuals as well as the integrity of the state university. The enumeration of these findings is hereunder placed after the statement of performance in order that their bearing on the liquidation efforts may be discerned rightly. 
Performance
Using no other records than those in the possession of the property office earlier than 1963, the task of assembling an inventory covering dollar commodities in the numerous P.A.'s under CP 20, CP 30, CP 65 and CP 430, 530...930 got under way on 29 February 1964. On 13 March 1964, an inventory exceeding $1,275,-000 was completed and submitted to NEC. A meeting amongst NEC, AID and UP has been scheduled to be held on 31 March tentatively. [The preceding paragraph was quoted in advance on 16 March in response to President Romulo's request for comments on the letter of the Business Executive].
On 19 March additional sheets of For 41-A coveirng over $26,000 worth of ICA books and periodicals were forwarded to NEC. Recognition is hereby recorded in favor of Mr. Fidel Camaniag of the property office, who contributed about 30 per cent of these additional materials. 
At the present time we are in the midst of segregating equipment in PA 3034 and PA 2283 into two groups; namely the Chemistry group and the Pharmacy group. PA 3034 and PA 2283 represent about $200,000 issued in FY 1951-1952 and FY 1952-1953 to CP 30. I was not the project director of this counterpart project; I believe Dr. Virata was the project director. 
Findings
The findings referred to in the first paragraph of this report may be classified under two headings; namely, inability to read the score and propensity to make untrue statements. The following illustrations are presented. 
I. Inability to read the score. 
During the months of January and February four persons in the various offices of the Business Executive informed the undersigned on different occassions in a tone of finality that the UP team who had been going to NEC to gather records needed to continue going there before they could construct the list of items for which he was being held accountable. For the purpose of safeguarding the reputation of other employees of said offices, I hereby record the names of the aforementioned persons as Professor Jose Campos, Jr.; Business Executive; Mr. Arsenio Talingdan, Management Analyst and IBM Coordinator; Mr. Carlos Antonio, In-charge Property Division; and Mr. Adriano Cabel, Chief Inventory Section. The behaviour of these four officers of the UP proves that they did not know the score of the liquidation efforts in June 1963 and proves further that they had not learned how to read the score from June 1963 to January 1964. For as stated in the first paragraph of this letter the inventory for $1,275,000 of ICA commodities was based on the records that had been in their hands earlier than June 1963.
The Business Executive was right in saying that money was wasted in sending the UP team to NEC in the sense that they spent time gathering records for PA 2265, PA 3029, PA 3047 and PA 6518 and perhaps PA 2324, which records have been always in the files of the College of Engineering (not in Ortigas' file). Again an example of inability to read the score. 
One more illustration. On 14 August 1962 Prof. Campos wrote : "As of now, the remaining stumbling block for the finalization of this turnover is the uncooperation of the College of Engineering." The real stumbling block as recently revealed is the ICA property that went to Chemistry and Industrial Pharmacy in 1952 and 1953.
II. Propensity for Untrue Statements
(a) On 9 March the Business Executive wrote that the property office had complete records of the commodities received in 1952 and 1953. However, on 29 October 1962 he wrote : "All the equipment coming under various projects from 1952 up to 1960 were delivered direct to the different units of the university thru their respective project directors who took charge of distributing said items."
It is obvious that the two statements mentioned above cannot be both true. Since I was not a project director during FY 1952 and FY 1953 I requested in writing for a loan of the "complete records" that was referred to in 9 March statement because such records would be useful for the segregation of ICA Chemistry commodities from the ICA Pharmacy commodities. Mr. Cabel of the property office informed my emissary that there were no other records than the PA 2283 and PA 3034 and PA 3049 folders, which were obtained from NEC around 1962.
(b) On 4 March 1964 the Business Executive wrote : "This work on the liquidation of NEC-AID was entrusted by the President to the undersigned (Prof. Campos) in June, 1962. The help of Prof. Ortigas was sought thru the Dean but we did not hear from him until January 1964..." This statement is false. I never heard from the Business Executive nor from the Dean. 
Recommendations
The inability to read the score together with the propensity for untruth amy be only a mechanism of defense which results from a face-saving struggle for survival. If this be the case then the sooner assistance is extended to Prof. Campos, Mr. Talingdan, Mr. Antonio and Mr. Cabel either separately or collectively the better it will be for the university. For this purpose, I recommend that : 
I. A corps of technical assistance volunteers (TACV) be constituted immediately to guide the above enumerated persons in the performance of their duties in connection with NEC-AID properties. The nucleus of thsi corps shall include the present project director or deputy director of C.P.'s 6030, 6130, 6230 and6330, the former project director of C.P. 20, C.P. 30 and C.P. 65, and the former project director of C.P.'s 430, 530...930. (The above mentioned nucleus involves so far only two persons : President Romulo or Vice-President Virata and Prof. Ortigas).
II. To enable this TACV to render assistance quickly and effectively the Business Executive shall furnish said TACV with copies of the lists of NEC-ICA properties covered by the transfer of accountability in the following cases : 
1. Geology Department 
(a) From Dr. Feliciano to Dr. Tupas
(b) From Dr. Tupas to Dr. Gonzales
2. College of Pharmacy
(a) From Dean Valenzuela to Dean Santos
3. Physics Department
(a) From Prof. de la Cruz to Dr. Magno
4. Chemistry Department
(a) From Dr. Clemente to Dr. Salcedo
(b) From Dr. Salcedo to Dr. Tentamatay
(c) From Dr. Tentamatay to Dr. Pulido
Yours truly, 
C. A. ORTIGAS
Professor of Engineering Sciences

The President
University of the Philippines Campus
Dear Mr. President :
Last month the undersigned submitted a request for the issuance of a deed of clerance in his favor on the basis of precedents. He maintained then as he continues to maintain now that it is not right for the Business Executive's office to hold him responsible for NEC-ICA properties that had been accounted for by end-users to the satisfaction of the B.E.'s office during the years prior to President Romulo's regime. Without retreating from this position he hereby renews that request on the basis of justice. 
Seen from the affirmative side of the proposition that foreign aid is mutually beneficial to U.S. and R.P. as conceived in the Quirino-Foster Agreement signed by President Quirino on behalf of R.P. and Mr. William C. Foster representing President Harry S. Truman on 14 November 1950 the University of the Philippines is honor-bound to acknowledge its accountability to AID-NEC for properties received under counterpart projects operated by her. The Business Executive must have become acutely aware of his inability to account for NEC-AID properties last June when AID informed UP that UP's accountability exceeds by far the $600,000 inventory that UP was submitting for consideration at that time. Instead of requesting my assistance right there and then he abandoned the liquidation efforts for seven months; and in January 1964 he held up my clearance. This is an act of injustice; becauase the paucity of their inventory was the consequence of the negligence of his office. If the B.E. has asked me to help him at the time that President Romulo assigned to him the task of liquidating the NEC-ICA counterpart projects which President Sinco had taken over, the title to the properties would have been transferred to UP within the summer of 1963. How is this estimate arrived at? ANSWER : In less than three weeks in March, an inventory covering all PA's under CP 20, CP 30, CP 65, CP 430, 530, 930 was assembled in Form 41-A. In other words, the work that the B.E.'s office neglected to do during four years of Tan regime, the entire Sinco regime and the first year of Romulo regime could have been made up within one summer last year or earlier. 
In view of the foregoing, I hereby again reiterate in the name of fair play that the commander-n-chief of the University of the Philippines grant my previous request, which is detailed as follows : 
(1) Release the second half of my commutation leave; and 
(2) Issue a deed of clearance in my favor. 
Yours truly, 
C.A. ORTIGAS
Professor of Engineering Sciences

The President
University of the Philippines Campus

Dear Mr. President :

I shall be at the Central Philippine University from 15 May to 27 June for the National Science Development Board - Asia Foundation - supported summer institute for upgrading high school physics teachers in the Visayas. This information is being furnished in connection with my participation in the liquidation of NEC-AID equipment, which operation is not yet completed; the delay being caused by difficulties encountered in the location of and accounting for some $20,000 worth of commodities received under PA 2283 and PA 3034. 
The personnel of the chemistry department started to trace the whereabouts of the above mentioned articles in April, but the problem still remains unsolved. There are about $20,000 worth of commodities duly acknowledged to have been received by the University but they cannot be located in the premises of the Department of Chemistry. Perhaps one-half of these are supplies and perishables. On the other hand, there are many pieces of equipment in the Department of Chemistry which are marked with ICA-PHILCUSA emblems and bear PHILCUSA property numbers, but these equipment do not appear in the original invoices and shipping documents. 
In a conference between the head of the chemistry department and the undersigned yesterday, it was agreed that an ad hoc inventory team consisting of members from the chemistry department and members from the Business Executive's office to match records against existing equipment in the chemistry laboratories which bear the markings of PHILCUSA or FOA but whose names and descriptions do not appear in the original invoices might find the key to the solution of this ticklish problem. It is probable that some pieces of equipment purchased by the University of the Philippines' money were wrongly labelled PHILCUSA while some pieces of equipment donated by AID were labelled erroneously as UP Property. This suggested solution is a simple operation but is difficult to get it started, hence this appeal to the commander-in-chief of the University.
Recommendation
I. It is recommended that an ad hoc committee such as described above consisting of two members from chemistry and two from property and supply office be formed and instructed to start the work soon. The lists of equipment referred to are in the possession of Dr. Pulido and the details of the operation of this team had been threshed out by Dr. Pulido and myself to the extent that my physical presence in the premises will not be needed in the next six weeks. It is also recommended that the commission of the undersigned "to participate in the liquidation operation" be extended to July 31, 1964 inasmuch as there are still other things to be done with respect to other procurement authorizations. 
II. It is further recommended that the University of the Philippines put her records straight with respect to the involvement of various UP personnel in the counterpart projects. Permit me to illustrate : - As indicated in one of his letters to the President the Business Executive was under the impression that the undersigned was the project director of CP 30 (Rehabilitation of the School of Chemistry) the counterpart project to which were issued PA 2283 and PA 3034 in Fiscal Years 1952 to 1953. We have been informed by the AID-Education Division that the director of said project was Executive Vice President Virata from the time it was established to the time that part of the project was incorporated wiht CP 430 (Rehabilitation of the UP College of Engineering) which went into effect legally in October 14, 1953 when pertinent documents were signed by the director of USOM and the Chairman of PHILCUSA. Dr. Vidal A. Tan, then UP President, recommended Prof. C.A. Ortigas to be the project director of CP 430 (Rehabilitation of the UP College of Engineering) on August 26, 1953. True copies of the letter of President Tan to Mr. C.V. Crucillo, Mr. Crucillo's letter, and that of the Agreement on the Rehabilitation of the UP College of Engineering are hereto attached. 
The undersigned respectfully requests that the University of the Philippines request AID and/or NEC for further documentation that might help the various offices of the University to obtain a clear picture of the liquidation problems. 
Yours truly, 
C.A. ORTIGAS
Professor of Engineering Sciences

column9: (1953, October 14)
column10:
COUNTERPART PROJECT NO. 430
AGREEMENT ON THE REHABILITATION OF THE U.P. COLLEGE OF ENGINEERING
1. Description of Project :
To afford a better coordination and a more effective implementation of all activities aimed at the rehabilitation and improvement of the educational facilities and instructional staff of the U.P. College of Engineering, the Mining School Rehabilitation (Counterpart Project No. 20), the Rehabilitation of the U.P. College of Chemical Engineering (Counterpart Project No. 30) and the Improvement of the Instructional Facilities of the U.P. College of Engineering (Counterpart Project No. 65), have all been merged into one under this project. 
During FY '54, activities will be directed towards the acquisition, receipt and installation of needed laboratory equipment and towards the construction of a chemical engineering laboratory building. This laboratory building will house the bigger pieces of laboratory equipment now causing congestion in their present locations. The engineering laboratory building provided for in the Improvement of the Instructional Facilities of the U.P. College of Engineering (Counterpart Project No. 65) is expected to be completed by the end of the second quarter. In view thereof, large machinery and equipment will have to be installed. 
2. Financing : 
Budgetary requirements of this project will be financed by withdrawals from the Counterpart Fund-Special Account, in conjunction with regularly appropriated funds of the U.P. College of Engineering, and counterpart funds provided for the Improvement of Instructional Facilities of the U.P. College of Engineering Project (Counterpart Project No. 65), the budget for which will expire on September 30, 1953. The University of the Philippines will provide P100,000 out of its regular funds to cover its share in the cost of the chemical engineering laboratory building. 
3. Counterpart Project Budget
The counterpart budget for this project will be for the period July 1, 1953 to June 30, 1954, in the amount of P345,000 as shown in detail in the attached budget of expenditures. These funds will be used for payment of obligations incurred prior to the effective date of this agreement. 
4. Operating Procedure :
An initial release of funds to the Dean, College of Engineering, U.P., in the amount of P90,000 for the first quarter (July 1 to September 30, 1953), is authorized at the time of approval of this agreement. 
Subsequent releases of funds will be made by PHILCUSA in accordance with need and upon submission of monthly financial reports by the project director in accordance with the provisions of Section 6 hereof. 
Funds released to the project director will be deposited in the Philippine National Bank in a current account designated "University of the Philippines (Counterpart Project No. 430)."
Withdrawals will be made by checks signed by the project director designated by the President of the University of the Philippines and countersigned by the comptroller of the University. 
No obligations will be incurred subsequent to June 30, 1954. 
Payment of outstanding obligations will be made within 60 days from June 30, 1954. Any funds then remaining will be returned to PHILCUSA and deposited in the Counterpart Fund-Special Account. 
A final report on the project will be submitted to PHILCUSA and to the USOM Comptroller not later than 90 days after June 30, 1954. 
5. Property Acquisition and Disposition
All items of equipment and/or furniture and fixtures acquired with counterpart funds will be properly labelled with the joint FOA/PHILCUSA emblem, and a permanent record of each item showing the cost, location, vendor's name and procuring voucher number will be maintained. All equipment acquired by the project and all supplies not consumed will be turned over to PHILCUSA upon completion of their use or completion of this project for such utilization or disposition as PHILCUSA and FOA/STEM determine. 
Properties and funds of this project will be subject to physical examination and the books of accounts and other records including property records being maintained by the project director will be subject to audit and examination by PHILCUSA and the FOA Mission Controller, at such reasonable times as they may desire to make such examination. 
6. Reporting Procedure :
Montly reports of receipts, disbursements and outstanding obligations or commitments, on forms prescribed by FOA/PHILCUSA, together with a trial balance, will be submitted by the project director to PHILCUSA and to the FOA Mission Controller not later than 10 days following the close of each month. Complete instructions for the preparation of these reports are contained in the Manual "Reporting Requirements for Counterpart Fund Projects", copy of which was furnished each project director and accountant. The "Reporting Requirements" are hereby made a part of this agreement by reference. 
Quarterly reports of project progress and status will be submitted by the project director to PHILCUSA and to the FOA Mission not later than the 15th day following the close of the quarter.
7. Date of Effectivity
The effective date of this agreement is the first day of the budget period. 
(SGD). HARRY A. BRENN
Director, USOM
(SGD). PLACIDO L. MAPA
Chairman, PHILCUSA

Fourth Progress Report
The President
University of the Philippines Campus

column9: (1953, August 26)
column10:
Sir :
A copy of your memorandum circular dated August 7, 1953, designating certain members of the faculty as project directors of counterpart projects nos. 20, 30, 31 and 65 was received by this Office. Please be informed, however, that the counterpart budgets of Projects Nos. 20, 30 and 65 for FY 1953-54 have been consolidated into one counterpart budget for the Rehabilitation of the U.P. College of Engineering. PHILCUSA now considers these three projects as one project and desires that there should be only one project director. Under the new counterpart agreement which PHILCUSA has submitted to FOA, the Dean of the College of Engineering is designated project director of the combined counterpart project. 
Very respectfully, 

For the Executive Secretary :
Signed : CORNELIO V. CRUCILLO
Technical Adviser
(Coordinator of Projects)

The President
University of the Philippines Campus

Dear Mr. President :
Last month the undersigned submitted a request for the issuance of a deed of clearance in his favor on the basis of precedents. He maintained then as he continues to maintain now that it is not right for the Business Executive's office to hold him responsible for NEC-ICA properties that had been accounted for by end-users to the satisfaction of the B.E.'s office during the years prior to President Romulo's regime. Without retreating from this position he hereby renews that request on the basis of justice. 
Seen from the affirmative side of the proposition that foreign aid is mutually beneficial to U.S. and R.P. as conceived in the Quirino-Foster Agreement signed by President Quirino on behalf of R.P. and Mr. William C. Foster representing President Harry S. Truman on 14 November 1950 the University of the Philippines is honor-bound to acknowledge its accountability to AID-NEC for properties received under counterpart projects operated by her. The Business Executive must have become acutely aware of his inability to account for NEC-AID properties last June when AID informed UP that UP's accountability exceeds by far the $600,000 inventory that UP was submitting for consideration at that time. Instead of requesting my assistance right there and then he abandoned the liquidation efforts for seven months; and in January 1964 he held up my clearance. This is an act of injustice; because the paucity of their inventory was the consequence of the negligence of his office. If the B.E. had asked me to help him at the time that President Romulo assigned to him the task of liquidating the NEC-ICA counterpart projects which President Sinco had taken over, the title to the counterparts would have been transferred to UP within the summer of 1963. How is this estimate arrived at? ANSWER : In less than three weeks in March, an inventory covering all PA's under CP 20, CP 30, CP 65, CP 430, 530,...930 was assembled in Form 41-A. In other words, the work that the B.E.'s office neglected to do during four years of Tan regime, the entire Sinco regime and the first year of Romulo regime could have been made up within one summer last year or earlier. 
In view of the foregoing, I hereby again reiterate in the name of fair play that the commander in chief of the University of the Philippines grant my previous request, which is detailed as follows : 
(1) Release the second half of my commutation leave; and 
(2) Issue a deed of clearance in my favor 
Yours truly, 
C.A. ORTIGAS
Professor of Engineering Students.

Dr. Joseph T. Howard
President, Central Philippine University
Iloilo City

Dear Dr. Howard : 
This is to reply to your offer to the undersigned to join Central Philippine University as "Coordinator of Science and Math Teacher Training." I understand that an employment such as stated in your letter is customarily covered by a contract. It is therefore suggested that the following three points be spelled out in the contract : 
I. Manner of Compensation : This question is being mentioned first because on 2 July we agreed to hold it in abeyance and recently I heard that you were about to write me about it. It has come to my knowledge that directors, deans, etc. in Central Philippine University are given flat-rate compensation as dean or as director and an additional compensation for teaching assignments depending on the number of units carried. May I say that this system is to my liking because it will enable me to limit my teaching load to the extent that my performance as coordinator may be maintained at peak levels? 
II. Relationship Between the Coordinator on the one hand and Directors on the other. This point has particular reference to the clause "you will be free to develop this program in consultation with the Administration as you saw fit and within the financial limitations of the University's budget." As regards this mandate, I propose that it be stated in the agreement that the Coordinator of Science and Math Teacher Training will be responsible to the President only and not subject to 'overrule' by any CPU Administration officer other than the President. 
III. Quarters. The undersigned hereby requests that he be provided living quarters in one of the mission houses at a rate to be determined by Central Philippine University. The living quarters may be part of a house or the whole house. He strongly believes that his work as coordinator can be best performed if he lives within the campus and in a house which will provide an academic atmosphere. I have been in some of your mission houses and there I felt this kind of atmosphere. 
COMMENTS
(Not part of agreement)
Two Phases. The Science and Math Teacher Training Program may be divided into two phases, namely : the development phase and the operation phase. At the present time there is no science and math teacher training program in CPU; therefore the work must start with planning and development. During the undersigned's tour of duty with NSDB Summer Institute at Central Philippine University last summer a one-year curriculum designed to convert holders of B.S. pre-medic into physics teachers was drafted by Dr. Cathedral, Prof. Sornito and the undersigned. Perhaps this curriculum will provide the starting point of the development of the Science and Math Teacher Training Program. One encouraging aspect of this one-year curriculum is the inclusion of four courses (two in mathematics of 3 units each; and two in physics of 3 units each) which were offered in the NSDB Summer Institute. Complete syllabi for these four courses could be worked out during 1964-65 while they are being taught; - as a start in the implementation of the program. 
Sincerely yours, 

C.A. ORTIGAS

The President
University of the Philippines Campus
Dear Mr. President : 
This morning, officials of NEC, AID and UP held a meeting at the Board of Regents room. The undersigned attended this meeting upon the invitation of the U.P. Business Executive. 
One of the items discussed was the transfer from AID to UP of the dollar commodities that were acquired under Procurement Authorizations issued during the time when the undersigned was Project Director. The transfer was not made during this meeting; - there are things yet to be done by NEC and ICA on the one hand and the University of the Philippines on the other. It was apparent from the pace of the deliberations during the meeting that the undersigned's assistance to the early transfer of these commodities is still needed. In view of this, I hereby request that my commission to participate in the liquidation efforts be extended until such efforts are terminated. 
Truly yours, 

C. A. ORTIGAS 
Professor of Enginering Sciences

column9: (1964, September 17)
column10:
General Carlos P. Romulo
President, University of the Philippines
Diliman, Quezon City
Dear Mr. President :
In view of the fact that Dr. Virata has already been issued clearance by the University of the Philippines as revealed to Mrs. Ortigas by Mr. Antonio of the Business Executive Office the undersigned, who retired six months earlier, hereby requests that a clearance be issued to him likewise. 
It will be recalled that the Business Executive held up my clearance because of his state of helplessness with respect to the liquidation of NEC-AID dollar commodities, which were issued to UP from 1951 to 1960. This situation gradually cleared up to the extent that by July 30, 1964 it was accepted that the remaining mess lay in the dollar commodities requisitioned for chemistry department and the College of Pharmacy and Geology department before the middle of 1953. The project director of the counterpart projects up to then was Dr. Virata, which can be verified from the records of AID. The Business Executive erroneously held me accountable for these commodities because the progressive percentage receipts for commodities from these (pre-1953) projects were constantly and consistently carried in my quarterly and annual reports to NEC since FY 1954 in accordance with the form prescribed by that office.
At any rate the impediments to the issuance of clearance were the Chemistry-Industrial Pharmacy "mess". These impediments vanished when clearance was issued to Dr. Virata. Believing that I am entitled to an equally fair treatment, I reiterate my request that UP issue my clearance at its earliest convenience so that I may be relieved of inconvenience which has been forced upon my way of life since 2 Feb. 1964. 
Very truly yours,

C.A. ORTIGAS

column9: (1964, October 28)
column10:
Dean Francisco Nemenzo
College of Arts and Sciences
University of the Philippines
Diliman, Quezon City

Dear Dean Nemenzo :
This is a follow-up of our conversation last Friday (23 Oct. 1964). 
I am convinced that the delay of my clearance stems from the absence of earnest concern and enlightened sensibility on the part of the preceding Business Executive coupled with the lack of competence on the part of UP officials that former President Sinco placed in the offices that he created and accentuated by the fear of said offcials to bring the matter to the attention of the President lest the seriousness of their incompetence be discovered. 
The facts of the matter may be summarized as follows : I was retired on 2 Feb. 1964. My clearance has been held up since then by Business Executive Jose Campos, Jr., who unjustly alleged that the repeated failures of his attempts to secure the title to dollar equipment obtained from ICA thru various counterpart projects was due to my inability to account for properties received by the UP under my tour of duty as project director. 
The records now available in the University at NEC and ICA show that properties received thru procurement authorizations (PA's) issued in the counterpart projects of which I was project director have been accounted for. It is true that there are properties that have not been duly accounted for but these were requisitioned in counterpart projects which other persons were directing, or helping to direct. Some of these persons are still in the payroll of the University. Among them may be mentioned Dr. Enrique Virata, Dr. Augusto Tenmatay, Mrs. Lolita Asuncion, Prof. Enrique Astrea, Prof. Roman Ramos, and Prof. Arsenio Gonzales. 
It might be relevant to mention at this moment that the work of adjusting prices of properties and pin-pointing continuing responsibilities for existing equipment is going very slowly of lack of attention on the part of the officials using said equipment. Now this failure to adjust prices and pin-point responsibilities by the mere process of signing memorandum receipts is not a legitimate reason for holding up the clearance of a professor who has retired nine months  before and who was expended a large part of this nine months helping UP straighten out the confusion in its liquidation efforts without any compensation. I am pouring out these sentiments to you in the belief that as Acting Business Executive you might be able to cause this case to be given due attention. 
Let me mention further that Dr. Jose Feliciano, Dr. Amando Clemente and Dr. Ignacio Salcedo had been cleared of accountability for these dollar equipment for which I am being now unjustly held accountable. This action automatically transfers the accountability of the equipment to the property officers of the University, who issued the clearances to these gentlemen? May not this transferred responsibility be the real reason why they are holding up my clearance? If this is so then they are guilty of a serious misbehavior. 
I understand that GSIS has sent to UP a request for my clearance. I wonder if the GSIS request has reached you already. This appears to be a proper stimulus for following up my case. 
Sincerely yours, 
C.A. ORTIGAS

column9: (1964, October 30)
column10:
HON. GIL PUYAT
Member, Board of Regents
University of the Philippines

Dear Sir : 

This is an appeal to you as member of the Board of Regents to intercede for Mr. Crisostomo A. Ortigas so that he may secure his clearance from the University of the Philippines. 
By virtue of the Retirement Law he was retired from the service on February 2, 1964. A requirement for the release of retirement gratuity is a statement of clearance from the University of the Philippines (Form 420) and which has been signed by the Dean of the College of Engineering, the U.P. Librarian, the Legal Officer and heads of other units recommending the issuance of clearance. The Property Custodian of UP, has, however, up to this time refused to sign this form. Prof. Jose Campos, past Business Executive, in a letter to the President, explained that Mr. Ortigas could not be given his clearance because he "failed to account for NEC-ICA equipment issued to him." 
I shall attempt to explain the background of this stinging remark of the Business Executive. 
From 1953 to 1959, Mr. Ortigas was Project Director of NEC-ICA sponsored projects for the rehabilitation of the College of Engineering. As such, dollar commodities were sent directly to him for him to distribute to end users such as the Department of Geology and Department of Chemistry. The heads of these units in turn acknowledged the receipts of these commodities in proper documents and thus assumed responsiblity for them. Reports of the disposition of the commodities were periodically sent to the sponsoring agencies and copies of these reports were sent to the Property Division, U.P. 
In July, 1960, Mr. Ortigas was furnished a copy of a letter of Prof. Jose Campos, then Secretary of the Board of Regents, to Dr. Jose Locsin, Chairman of NEC. In this letter he notified Dr. Locsin that in accordance with the policy laid down by the Board of Regents, the President of the University of the Philippines was to be the Project Director of all NEC-ICA projects. The letter terminated Mr. Ortigas' duties as Project Director. 
Shortly after this, NEC-ICA projects were terminated and U.P., NEC and AID tried to negotiate for transfer of the assets to the University of the Philippines. But up to now no such transfers have been accomplished becauase of discrepancies in the inventories of the three agencies involved. 
It is on the strength of this failure that the clearance of Mr. Ortigas is being withheld. So Mr. Ortigas requested the President to allow him to reconstruct the inventory of this equipment. The President allowed him to do this and by March, 1964, Mr. Ortigas was ready with an inventory that was submitted to NEC. 
In July, 1964, Mr. Ortigas was invited by Prof. Campos to sit at a meeting of NEC, AID and UP representatives. At this meeting the three agencies again differed in their inventory values. NEC submitted the smallest, UP next and AID the biggest. UP was sixty-two thousand dollars ($62,000) behind AID. Mr. Ortigas requested AID representative to identify in terms of document numbers the missing items. But up to now nothing has been heard from AID.
The situation has reached an impasse. It is improbable that these three offices, the NEC, AID and UP will ever be able to reconcile their records with one another. They have been trying to do this the past three years. The difference in the figures may be due to accounting systems or interpretations of items. For instance, part of the sixty-two thousand dollars ($62,000) difference in AID and UP records is an amount of thirty-five thousand dollars ($35,000) for the Graduate College of Education. Dr. Antonio Isidro, not Mr. Ortigas, was involved in the College of Education. The UP Property Custodian upholds the opinion that there should be no clearance until AID accepts our inventory report. 
We believe that the problem needs the intervention of high authorities of the University. This is a question that transcends the scope of the Property Custodian. For it is not a matter of the Property Custodian having found missing equipment. The question involves differences of inventory values which is quite distinct from a physical check up of equipment. 
Mr. Ortigas has written a number of letters to the President requesting for the release of his clearance statement. But he appears to be waiting for the recommendation of the Property Custodian. 
Thank you for your very kind attention. 
Very truly yours,
TERESA Z. ORTIGAS
(Mrs. Crisostomo A. Ortigas)

column10:

Member, Board of Regents
University of the Philippines

column9: (1964, November 11)
column10:
Dean Vicente Abad Santos
Officer-in-Charge
U.P. President's Office
Dear Dean Abad Santos : 
Attached herewith is the statement of "clearance" issued in favor of Crisostomo A. Ortigas by Mr. Carlos Antonio. The last sentence in this document, "This certification does not include the NEC-AID commodities transferred to the University through him as Project Director" nullifies the purpose for which the statement has been issued. It is no clearance at all. 
There has been no transfer of commodities through Mr. Ortigas as Project Director. No transfer has been made because no agreement has been reached as to the value of the equipment. 
The equipment received by Mr. Ortigas for the University as described in his documented reports have been found and located by a team appointed by Mr. Antonio himself. The heads of the different units have already acknowledged the existence of such equipment. 
The reservation made by Mr. Antonio in his statement of clearance seems to be caused again by the fact that AID has not accepted our reports. We reiterate again athat his failure to reach an agreement should not be held against Mr. Ortigas. This was the basis of our appeal to Regent H.R. Reyes and Regent Encarnacion Alzona which was endorsed to you. 
At our conversation last Thursday, November 5, 1964, it was my understanding that at a conference you called where the Dean of the College of Engineering, the Accountant, the Auditor, the Property Custodian and Regent Alzona were present, it was agreed that the report of Mr. Ortigas was to be accepted by the University and that was to be made the basis of the inventory of NEC-ICA equipment received by Mr. Ortigas. 
May I appeal to you to please straighten out this matter of clearance? It has dragged on and on - nine months now! We have suffered already too many indignities being shoved from office to office; being charged with neglect which cannot be substantiated.
Very truly yours, 
MRS. CRISOSTOMO A. ORTIGAS

column9: (1964, November 24)
column10:
Dr. Carlos P. Romulo
President
University of the Philippines
Thru the Business Executive

Dear sir : 
This letter is on behalf of Professor Crisostomo A. Ortigas (retired, February 2, 1964), who is now in Central Philippine University at Jaro, Iloilo. 
The purpose of this letter is to appeal to you for the release of a property clearance statement in favor of Mr. Ortigas. This has been pending for the tenth month now. The clearance is being withheld on account of problems encountered by the Property Division with respect to the liquidation of dollar commodities received by the University from the National Economic Council and the International Cooperation Administration now Agency for International Development (AID). 
Mr. Ortigas was director of some NEC-ICA projects from 1952 to 1959. In 1960 such a designation was transferred to the President, University of the Philippines. 
On May 2, 1961, the property clerk of the College of Engineering, Mr. Gorgonio Velasco, submitted to the Dean, College of Engineering, inventory lists of ICA-NEC equipment, dollar portion, for the period June 30, 1952 to December 31, 1959, broken down as follows : 
ICA-NEC...$890, 343.06 -- 75 pages, 4 copies each Counterpart Project 420 peso portion...P16,265.71. Counterpart Project 430 to 930 peso portion..88, 242.99 (Engineering File #7.011-A)
In March, 1964, an inventory covering all Procurement Authority papers under counterparts 20, 30, 65, 430, 530...930 was assembled in Form 41-A.
The Property Division, therefore, had ample time to study these inventory lists, to check if not to honor them. 
We therefore request, Mr. President, that (a) The University accept the inventory list submitted by Mr. Ortigas, and (b) The deed of property clearance in favor of Mr. Ortigas.
Very truly yours, 
TERESA Z. ORTIGAS
(Mrs. Crisostomo A. Ortigas)

column9: (1964, December 29)
column10:
Rev. Brother A. Philip
De La Salle College
Green Hills, Mandaluyong
Dear Brother Philip :
This letter has been composed in pursuance of the suggestion made by Dean Waldo S. Perfecto last week. That suggestion was, I believe, unavoidable in view of the large number of conversations that have been between De La Salle top brass and the undersigned; - three with the Provincial, two with the President, two with the Academic Vice - President, two with the Chairman of Natural Science, and one with the Chairman of Physics. Dean Perfecto suggested that I write down a resume version of my interpretation of those pleasant conversations. I gather that De La Salle College is inviting me to join her staff on the hope that my experience and qualifications will help her attain some of her immediate goals in connection with her desire to see a stable and dependable Philippines. My concept of a fruitful role at De La Salle College is indicated by the block which I have inserted in the chart that Dean Perfecto furnished me. That chart, which now bears the insertion, is inclosed herewith. Said insertion is intended to indicate that the Coordinator of Science and Mathematics Teacher Training is directly responsible to the President (solid line) and has indirect relationships (dotted and dashed lines) with the Deans of the Graduate School and of Engineering as well as the Chairmen of Physics, Mathematics, and Natural and Engineering Sciences. This role is like that of my present position at Central Philippine University where I am under contract from 1 Aug 1964 to 30 June 1965 as Coordinator of Science and Mathematics Teacher Training with six units of teaching a semester. Central Philippine University has no Vice - President but employs instead a Director of Instructional Services to whom all deans report directly. The accompanying brochure illustrates the pattern of collaboration amongst Dean of Graduate Studies (Nelson) and the Dean of Education (Herradura), the Coordinator of Science and Mathematics Teacher Training.
In as much as Rev. Brother C. Richard had previously indicated to oversee the preparation of a proposal by De La Salle College, may I request that in drafting her proposal to the undersigned De La Salle College include the following items; namely, job description, initial and terminal dates of employment and rate of compensation? May I further request that such a proposal be mailed before February 1965 to : Professor Crisostomo A. Ortigas, Central Philippine University, Iloilo City?
With best wishes for the New Year, I am 
Sincerely yours, 
C.A. ORTIGAS
Inc : Administration Flow Chart with INSERTION
Two Central Philippine University brochures

column9: (1965, February 17)
column10:
Dean Waldo S. Perfecto 
Academic Vice President
De La Salle College 
1501 Taft Avenue
Manila

Dear Dean Perfecto : 
This is to acknowledge receipt of your letter dated February 13 as well as the telegram which preceded it by a couple of weeks. I share with you the expectation for some more exchanges of views in the near future. 
Central Philippine University has recently created the Office of Director of Research and put me in it without prejudice to my continuing as Coordinator of Science and Mathematics Teacher Training. Wish me good luck. 
Sincerely yours, 
C.A. Ortigas
Director

column9: (1965, February 22)
column10:
Dr. Patrocinio Valenzuela
Executive Secretary
National Research Council of the Phil. 
University of the Philippines
Quezon City
Dear Dr. Valenzuela :
This is to acknowledge receipt of the Addenda of the 26th February, 1965 Annual Meeting of the Charter and the Regular Members of the National Research Council of the Philippines and the accompanying registered documents. I regret to inform you that my duties at Central Philippine University require my absence from the said meeting. 
Yours truly, 
C.A. ORTIGAS
Director

College of Engineering
M E S S A G E
(Philippinensian '55) 
To the class of 1955 : Congratulations and best wishes. The success you are about to achieve will form a firm foundation of your future. You owe this good fortune to the government of the Philippines. Consequently the Filipino people are entitled to a goodly measure of the fruits of your endeavors. In order to be of assistance to the five-year program of economic development you need to nourish the urge to serve your country. The University of the Philippines has given you the training and she expects you to cherish that urge. 
In cherishing such an urge you are setting a living example to peoples of other countries - peoples who constitute the elements amongst which World Peace should reign. 
C.A. ORTIGAS
Dean

Mr. Patrick J. Selak
Manager, Nuclear Engineering Department
KAISER ENGINEERS
Kaiser Building
Oakland 12, California
U.S.A.
Dear Mr. Selak : 
I was very happy to get a package from you and was delighted to take possession of its contents. I want to thank you very much for this thoughtfulness of yours as well as for the value of its contents. 
We are waiting word from Washington with respect to their action to our nuclear reactor program. Our Congress recently passed a bill repealing the old law creating the Philippine Nuclear Commission which you heard about while you were here in Manila. We are very hopeful that everything will proceed smoothly. 
Mrs. Ortigas joins me in sending you our warmest regards to you and your family not excluding the kids. 
Sincerely yours, 
C.A. ORTIGAS 
Dean

column9: (1956, October 24)
column10:
Dr. C.E. Barthel, Jr.
Assistant Director, ARF
Technology Center
10 West 35th Street 
Chicago 16, Illinois 

Dear Dr. Barthel :

Thank you for your letter of 17 September 1956. A few days before your letter arrived 6 copies of your proposal for assistance to our industrial research center were received - with profound gratification. It will take some time before we can get action on this matter; in any case we will let you know as soon as something develops. 
Mr. Gollong dropped me a note indicating that he intends to stop by on his way back. We are looking forward to seeing him and talking to him about our local problems. 

Sincerely yours,

C.A. ORTIGAS
Dean

column9: (1956, September 17)
column10:
Dean C.A. Ortigas
Industrial Research Center
University of the Philippines
Quezon City, Philippines

Dear Dean Ortigas : 

Somewhat belatedly, I am acknowledging your letter of August 24, in which you requested information on the cost of consultation services which might be provided the Industrial Research Center by the Armour Research Foundation. In spite of the delay in writing you, Mr. Gollong and I have been at work preparing a draft of a plan of service to the Center. This draft is nearly completed and it will certainly be in the mails within a few days.
Thank you very much for your letter of August 24. We continue to hope that we shall be working with you in the not-too-distant future.

Cordially yours, 

C.E. Barthel, Jr. 
Assistant Director

column9: (1956, August 24)
column10:
Dr. C.E. Barthel, Jr.
Assistant Director, ARF
Technology Center
10 West 35th Street 
Chicago 16, Illinois 

Dear Dr. Barthel :

Thank you for your letter of 7 August 1956. This reply was purposely put-off hoping that the research bill would become law -- and the hope was fulfilled yesterday.
The first problem we have concerns control laboratories in which, I believe, your institute can help us. The question that arises naturally is : How much will the consultative services cost us? Proposals which do not carry cost estimate will not be acted upon by our superiors here. 

We are looking forward to welcoming Mr. Gollong. 

Cordially yours, 

C.A. ORTIGAS
Dean

column9: (1956, August 7)
column10:
Dean C.A. Ortigas
College of Engineering
University of the Philippines
Quezon City, Philippines

Dear Dean Ortigas : 

I was indeed delighted to receive your letter of August 1st in which you announced that your law-making bodies had approved the research bill designed to give strong support to the development of research and technology in the Philippines. Science and technology are growing at such a rapid rate at the present time that no country can afford to lag. In my humble opinion, therefore, the decision of your law-making bodies will have a profound effect on the development of the economy of your fine country. 

Of course, I was equally delighted to hear that your group had convinced the Standford group that technical assistance for the support of an expanded research center should be sought from the Armour Research Foundation rather than from the Stanford Research Institute. You know of our intense enthusiasm for research in general and for the Philippines in particular, and we at the Foundation continue to hope that we shall have an opportunity to assist in the not-too-distant future in the development of science and technology in the Philippines. 

Mr. Gollong, Manager of our International Department, plans to be in Manila sometime in January, 1957. He will certainly want to speak with you then about possible ARF assistance to your research center. In the meantime either Mr. Gollong or I will welcome the opportunity to provide you with any information that you might require to assist you in the initiation of a program of ART participation in the expansion of your research center. 

Again please convey my warmest greetings to my friends in your College of Engineering and elsewhere in the University.

Cordially yours,

C.E. Barthel, Jr. 
Assistant Director

column9: (1956, August 1)
column10:
Dr. C.E. Barthel, Jr. 
Assistant Director
Armour Research Foundation of Illinois Institute of Technology 
10 West 35th Street
Chicago 16, Illinois

Dear Dr. Barthel :

The day your letter of 20 July arrived was a happy one for us. The research bill we spoke about during your short visit at our campus was passed by our law-making bodies. It is now awaiting the approval of President Magsaysay, who we believe will not veto it inasmuch as he certified to its urgency for the special session. 

The members of the Stanford group and our group have been talking about possible technical assistance from ICA as well as aid in the form of dollar commodities. I think our group have convinced the Stanford group that technical assistance should be sought from ARF rather than from SRI. 

With warm regards, I am

Yours cordially, 

C.A. ORTIGAS
Dean 

P.S. Dean Valenzuela is sending his best regards and is saying that he had already sent you by surface mail some of the bulletins of the National Research Council.

Dean C.A. Ortigas
College of Engineering
University of the Philippines
Quezon City, Philippines

Dear Dean Ortigas : 

I am so very enthusiastic about the value of scientific and engineering research on the economy of a nation that my discussions with you and your colleagues on June 26th were indeed a pleasant occasion for me. These discussions and others which I held with members of the United States Operations Mission to the Philippines, and with the staff of the Institute of Science and Technology, indicate conclusively that the Government of the Phlippines also recognizes the value of research to its development. The details of the planning for your Industrial Research Center seem to be well under way and we of the Armour Research Foundation wish you outstanding success in your program. I am sure that my visit with you on June 26th convinced you of the sincere and continued interest of the Armour Research Foundation in the development of science and engineering research in the Philippines and we hope that we shall have an opportunity to serve in your country in the near future. 

Again, it was certainly a pleasure to meet with you and your people at the time of my short visit to Manila. I hope that you will be so kind as to convey my greetings to Dean Valenzuela and to Professor Ericksen. 

Cordially yours, 

C.E. Barthel, Jr. 
Assistant Director

Dr. Paul B. W. Gollong
Manager, A.R.F.
10 West 35th Street
Chicago 16, Illinois

Thank you for your letter of 20 June and a copy each of "Partners in Research," English version and Spanish version of the draft agreement, etc. Your letter arrived late in the forenoon of Tuesday, 25 June, and Dr. Barthel showed up early in the afternoon. Our "conference" lasted at least five hours and we enjoyed every minute of it. The antecedents of the pronoun "we" include Dean Valenzuela of the College of Pharmacy, Dr. Santos (Professor of Industrial Pharmacy), and Prof. Eriksen (Head of the UP-Stanford Contract professors). I wonder if Dr. Barthel enjoyed the conference as much as we did. He was accompanied by Mr. Alder (ICA staff/Manila) and his son. The Alders left Dr. Barthel to our mercy within the first hour. 
During that day, 25 June, the Senate of the Philippines was discussing the Research Bill which will provide

Dr. Paul B.W. Gollong
Manager, A.R.F.
10 West 35th Street
Chicago 16, Illinois 

Dear Dr. Gollong : 
Thank you for your letter of 20 June and a copy each of "Partners in Research," English version and Spanish version of the draft agreement, etc. Your letter arrived late in the forenoon of Tuesday, 25 June, and Dr. Barthel showed up early in the afternoon. Our "conference" lasted at least five hours and we enjoyed every minute of it. The antecedents of the pronoun "we" include Dean Valenzuela of the College of Pharmacy, Dr. Santos (Professor of Industrial Pharmacy), and Prof. Eriksen (Head of the UP-Stanford Contract professors). I wonder if Dr. Barthel enjoyed the conference as much as we did. He was accompanied by Mr. Adler (ICA staff/Manila) and his son. The Alders left Dr. Barthel to our mercy within the first hour. 
During that day, 25 June, the Senate of the Philippines was discussing the Research Bill which will provide us with funds for expansion of research operation in our College. The copy of this bill, which we furnished Dr. Barthel will be considerably altered only in wording but not in spirit. Many minor amendments were proposed by enthusiastic senators and most of them were accepted. 
We learned a lot from reading the published report as well as from the briefing which Dr. Barthel so generously gave us. 
Yours truly, 
C.A. ORTIGAS
Dean

Dean C.A. Ortigas
College of Engineering
University of the Philippines
Quezon City, Philippines

Dear Dean Ortigas :
In view of your interesting letter of June 12th, we cabled our Dr. C.E. Barthel to make certain to see you during his all-too brief stopover in the Philippines. By now he will have had the pleasure of seeing you and discussing with you mutual points of interest. We will be anxious to have Dr. Barthel's report upon his return to Chicago. 
Meanwhile, we are pleased to send you copies of a draft agreement, both in English and in Spanish, similar to the ones we used in the Mexico, Colombia and Burma programs. Naturally, each contract must be tailored to the specific use and to the indigenous conditions. Obviously, we could not send you a direct copy of our agreement with Burma, but we believe that the enclosed draft is indicative of the type generally used. 
Further, we are enclosing a copy of "Partners in Research", which sets forth the methodology of operation of the Foundation, together with a reprint on the operation in Mexico and a copy of a recent newsrelease on the Colombia opreations.
Please be assured of our continued interest in assisting in the creation of an effective research program in the Philippines. If we can aid in any fashion, please do not hesitate to let us know. 
Sincerely, 

Paul B.W. Gollong
Manager

Mr. Paul B.W. Gollong
Manager, Armour Research Foundation of Illinois Institute of Technology
10 West 35th Street, Chicago 16
Illinois, U.S.A.

Dear Mr. Gollong : 

Thank you for your 5 June letter and the three enclosures : Burma Applied Research Program, ARF Research Reactor and AMA Getting the Most From Research and Engineering. We are making preparations for contacting Dr. C.E. Barthel Jr. 
There is considerable interest among our legislators to appropriate a sizeable amount of money for research during this coming special session which is scheduled to start 20 June. A copy of your agreements with Burma might help us draft a reasonably effective program of research in the Philippines. Would you be so kind as to suggest a quick way of getting such copy?
Thank you in advance. 

Sincerely yours, 

C.A. Ortigas
Dean

Dean Crisostomo A. Ortigas
College of Engineering
University of the Philippines
Quezon, Philippines

My dear Dean Ortigas : 

From our mutual friend, Dr. Ignacio S. Salcedo, we have learned that he kindly transmitted certain correspondence and material to you that we had send to him. Because of the study tour that he made in the United States and because of his association with the University of the Philippines, we felt that the material might be of use to him and to the University. 

Since we sent him this material, we have accumulated certain other brochures which we think might be of use to the University, particularly to the Industrial Research Center, of which you are a Director. The brochures deal with research and engineering and with our research reactor which was recently dedicated. 

We continue to believe that the Armour Research Foundation may be of assistance to the Industrial Research Center of the University of the Philippines, as it has been to other similar institutes elsewhere in the world. Most indicative of the type of service we can render is the program in Burma, in which we are providing advice and assistance in the areas of research administration, project conduct, program development, and like activities related to the expansion and strengthening of the local Institute. Believing that the details of this particular program may be of interest to you, we are pleased to send you a copy of the Burma Report. The Government of the Union of Burma kindly has permitted us to make limited distribution of this report. 

Incidentally, our Dr. C.E. Barthel, Jr., Director-General of the Burma Institute will be in Manila the latter part of June. We hope that he has the opportunity of seeing you and discussing with you his activities. 

Sincerely, 

Paul B.W. Gollong
Manager

Dear Mr. Trinidad : 

It is indeed a pleasure to inform you that our return trip from your very hospitable "guest house" to Manila was negotiated very smoothly and we enjoyed every kilometer of it. Our party stopped for a few minutes at the Forest Reservation in Itinao to enjoy its cool and refreshing atmosphere and at Cagayan de Oro to read the historical market of the Capitol building relating to the laying of the corner stone by President Quirino. We arrived in Cebu on schedule. Mr. Rodriguez of the CEPOC entertained us at the guest house until it was time for us to go to the airport. Our plane landed in Manila on schedule. 

Professors Eriksen, Bromley, and Festin join me in saying "Thank you again."

Please extend our warm regards to Mr. Pascual, Mr. Serrano, Mr. Flores, and the other boys. 

Yours sincerely, 

C.A. ORTIGAS 
Dean

Sincerely yours,

column9: (1955, December 16)
column10:
Dr. Patrocinio Valenzuela
College of Pharmacy
University of the Philippines
Campus

Dear Dean Valenzuela :

I am sorry to report to you my failure to contact the Alumni Association at Cebu. The president of the Cebu Chapter is in the U.S. However, I met Mr. Cabreros in CEPOC.

As regards our visit to Iligan, I am happy to say that we met most of our U.P. alumni at the Fertilizer Plant, Steel Plant, and the Power Plant, and the Carbide Plant. Our alumni there are completely out of touch with the U.P. I think it might be a good idea to send them the Collegian and the Campus Journal regularly. These and other U.P. publications will establish good public relations with our alumni.

The journey was uneventful. Prof. Festin and I left Manila at 6:30 A.M., Friday, 9 December by PAL Convair, which landed on schedule (9:00 A.M.) at Cagayan de Oro where we transferred to another plane for Iligan, to join Profs. Eriksen and Bromley who left Diliman the previous Monday. The four of us left Iligan by National Power car at 6:20 A.M. Sunday to return to Cagayan de Oro to catch the plane to Cebu. The automobile made the distance in three hours over a considerably bumpy road. Our plane arrived in Cebu at noon Sunday. We spent Sunday afternoon looking for U.P. alumni in vain. Early Monday morning we left for a visit to CEPOC, where Mr. Rodriguez entertained us at the guest house. Soon after lunch we left for home and arrived here in Diliman about half past six. 
I repeat the journey was uneventful but we enjoyed every minute and every kilometer of it. 

Sincerely yours,

C.A. ORTIGAS
Dean

Philippine Association of Civil Engineers
Quezon City

Please let me know at your earliest convenience if this counter-proposal is acceptable to you. 

Sincerely yours,

In the name of the "PHILIPPINE ASSOCIATION OF CIVIL ENGINEERS" known as "PACE", I am requesting your help in writing an article about the "Progress and Trends of Engineering Education in the Philippines and other countries". Dear Sir : 

We are now working on the Philippinensian '55 because of our desire to issue the university annual as early as week after commencement exercises. 
The theme of the 1955 Philippinensian is "Dedication of the UP Youth to the Cause of World Peace". We, therefore, forward this request for your graduation message which we hope will primarily center on the aforementioned theme. 

We would greatly appreciate it if you could send us your message on or before December 10, 1954. 

Thank you. 

Respectfully, 

ARTURO C. MOJICA
Associate Editor 

MESSAGE 
To the class of 1955 : Congratulations and best wishes. The success you are about to achieve will form a firm foundation of your future. You owe this good fortune to the government of the Philippines. Consequently the filipino people are entitled to a goodly measure of the fruits of your endeavors. All that you need to do to be of assistance to the five-year program of economic development is to nourish the urge to serve your country. The University of the Philippines has given you the training and she expects you to cherish such an urge.

M E S S A G E
(Philippinensian '55)

In order to be of assistance to the five-year program of economic development you need to nourish the urge to serve your country. In cherishing such an urge you are setting a living example to peoples of other countries - peoples who constitutes the elements amongst which World Pleace should reign.

A few weeks ago, Mr. Gollong, the writer of this enclosed letter was visiting us in Diliman, Quezon City. He spoke very highly and very appreciatively of the leadership of the late President Magsaysay. Now he is conveying through the undersigned his deep sense of loss over the tragic accident. I am therefore forwarding this letter to you as a token of appreciation on my part of the sympathy expressed by the Armour Research Foundation. 

Very truly yours,

C.A. ORTIGAS

I have for acknowledgment your letter of 30 September requesting for an autobiography and picture. column9: (1958, September 30)
column10:
Dean Crisostomo A. Ortigas
College of Engineering, U.P.
Quezon City

Dear Sir : 
We, the students of the Philippine Normal College, presently taking Natural Science 2 under Prof. Garzon, would like to request that you kindly furnish us at your earliest convenience your autobiography together with your picture. We are planning to compile the autobiographies and pictures of the first one hundred outstanding Filipino scientists as a part of our class project. 
Really, there is a felt need in pushing through this project because according to Prof. Garzon, who was one of the members of the Screening Committee on Science Talent Search, which was sponsored by the National Science Board through the cooperation of the Bureaus of Public and Private Schools, the University of the Philippine and the Philippine Normal College, the students who were interviewed were totally ignorant about the splendid achievements of our Filipino scientists. They blamed the local newspapers and magazines for not publishing their work and the seeming apathetic attitude of our institutions with regard to this matter. 
Spurred and motivated by the observation and comment of our professor, our class thought of this project in order that we might be able to disseminate in one way or another our outstanding Filipino scientists. May we, therefore, enlists your whole-hearted cooperation and support in order to make our project a reality?
Hoping for your kind attention and help regarding our request. 

Very truly yours, 

NATURAL SCIENCE CLASS
III-G2

By:

EDISEO MENDOZA
President, Natural Science Club

CONTENTS NOTED : 

DIONISIO P. GARZON
Professor, Natural Science

My dear Dean Ortigas :

You can imagine my delight in learning from your letter of May 14 that action finally has been taken on the industrial research program. I, personally, must admit that at times I lost hope that anything would ever result out of the concerted efforts of our friends in the Philippines and of ourselves. It is, of course, some seven years since the initial interset in an industrial research program was expressed,. 

We understand from conversation with Washington that bid requests for the program will shortly be issued. Although we do not know whether our own bid will be suitable, we deeply hope so, especially in view of the close interest we have for so long maintained with Philippine developments in this area.

column9: (1957, December 2)
column10:
Congratulations to Tau Alpha on its twenty-fifth anniversary. It is a matter of first-hand knowledge to the writer that members of Tau Alpha have developed and cherished remarkable loyalty to one another.
This loyalty can be a potent factor in the development of high norm of conduct amongst the citizens of the Republic of the Philippines. The members of the Tau Alpha Fraternity possess the ability for utilizing this loyalty to enhance the usefulness of the University to the growth of the Filipino Nation. I hereby urge the resident members and the Alumni members of Tau Alpha to put up a performance that can be classified under a category that may be termed nation-wide rather than merely frat-limited.

column9: (1957, August 26)
column10:

Manager, Armour Research Foundation
Technology Center, 10 West 35th St.
Chicago 16, Illinois
U.S.A.

Dear Dr. Gollong :

I have received your letter about the Conference to be sponsored by the Stanford Research Institute and the Time International Magazine together with the materials enclosed therewith. I inquired from ICA and NEC but they did not seem to be familiar with this project. So I asked Mr. Fernandez of the Industrial Development Center to help me contact the different offices involved with the request that he communicate with them directly and extend your invitation to each of them on behalf of the Armour Research Foundation. I believe that this was the most efficient way of letting these different companies hear of your hospitality. 

Best regards to you, Dr. Bartel, Dr. Nelson, Dr. Cieflo and others who were so kind to the undersigned during his visit in your laboratory.

Cordially yours, 

C.A. ORTIGAS
Dean

Dr. Paul B. W. Gollong
Manager, Armour Research Foundation
Technology Center, 10 West 35th St. 
Chicago 16, Illinois
U.S.A.

Dear Dr. Gollong : 

Thank you for your letters of 3 May and 22 April about Dr. Humphreys and Dr. Ewell respectively. 
I shall look forward to seeing Dr. Humpreys in our Campus and shall congratulate Ray as soon as I can get around to it. 
Things are going slow about the pesos from the National Science Board although moral support from NEC and ICA/M has been repeatedly indicated. 

Sincerely, 

C.A. ORTIGAS

Quezon City, Philippines

My dear Dean Ortigas :

Shortly, Dr. R.F. Humphreys, Assistant Director of the Armour Research Foundation is leaving for Burma to advise on the Atomic Energy Program of that country. En route, he will stopover in Manila, arriving June 5th on PAA Fllight No. 833. He will stay at the Manila Hotel until the 7th and then proceed on to Burma. 

Although his stay will be brief, I believe that a meeting with you would be most interesting. Thus, I have asked him to contact you upon his arrival. A brief biography on him is enclosed. 

Sincerely, 

Paul B.W. Gollong
Manager

Dean C.A. Ortigas
