Quezon City, Philippines

My dear Dean Ortigas : 

In the "C & EN" issue for April 15th, I see that Ray Ewell has been named Vice Chancellor for Research and Professor of Chemistry at the University of Buffalo with the appointment effective in September, 1957. As you know, Ray, formerly Assistant Director of the National Science Foundation, has been a Consultant to the India Ministry of Commerce and Industry in New Delhi under The Ford Foundation. I thought you might be interested in this news. 

Best regards, 

Paul B.W. Gollong

column10: The United States Agency for International Development's (USAID) Global Center for Environment, Energy and Environmental Training Program has developed the Best Practices Guide Series to provide technical information on the topics of power sector reform and regulatory practices. This series of guides is adapted from coursework that was designed to develop technical leadership capacity in energy development and greenhouse gas emissions reduction that are both friendly to the environment and beneficial to economic growth. This guide is for regulatory staff members, members of regulatory bodies government officials and professional interested in or working on establishing or restructuring the power sector, particularly those involved with regulation or establishing or restructuring regulatory functions. It provides regulatory decision-makers and professionals with enhanced knowledge and procedures necessary to start up and run an efficient and effective regulatory body. Through a contract with the Energy Group at the Institute of International Education (IIE), USAID's contractor for the Technical Leadership Training Program, The Regulatory Assistance Project (RAP) has prepared the Best Practices Guide : Implementing Power Sector Reform.

column3: The Hanoi University of Technology
column10:
The Hanoi University of Technology (HUT) was established in 1956. Over the last 40 years, HUT has been continuously growing and making considerable contributions to the industrialisation and modernisation process of the country. 
A large number of scientists and highly qualified engineers, who obtained various academic degrees of BE, MSc and Ph.D. at HUT, have been working effectively in various fields of political, cultural and social life, and science & technology development throughout the country. The obvious achievements in training and research undertaken at HUT and their application to production activities, have contributed significantly to the flourishing national technologies. 
Over the last decade, Vietnam has embarked upon a radical and comprehensive process of economic and social reform. Based on 40 years of tradition, HUT has been creative and dynamic in this process and has become a centre of excellence. It should continue to contribute more and more to socio-economic development, industrialisation, and modernisation of the country. 
The development of HUT has been linked with strong support from the Government, great efforts by HUT staff and students from generation to generation, and especially with wholehearted assistance from international organisations and individuals. Our friends and colleagues in the world have supported us not only in building up infrastructure and facilities but also in teaching staff development. Over the years, international co-operation programmes have brought to the university effective staff with high academic standards and competence in training, research, and management activities, which has accelerated the process of integration of HUT into the university community within the region, and all over the world.

column9: Introducing a Computer System into the Civil Engineering Company by Jose Ma. de Castro
column10: Three case studies of engineering design/consultancy firms are discussed, to provide practical insights to civil engineering firms who are planning to computerize. It is observed that top management persons must be conversant with computer solutions of engineering problems. At least one full-time engineer must be detailed to the program development. The firm must choose the software first and then the computer system. Because the computer field is changing so rapidly, the system must be chosen that would pay for itself in three to five years.

column9: The 1985 Mexico Earthquakes : Their Implications for Metro Manila by Jose Ma. de Castro
column10: The paper presents initial information available regarding the 1985 Mexico earthquakes : the tectonic setting, the geotechnical characteristics of Mexico City, the types of structures that were damaged, and the types of foundations. A comparison is made with the setting of Metro Manila. Some recommendations are given from the structural engineering point of view, to help prevent similar damage from taking place in the Philippines.

column9: Preparing for the Big One in Metro Manila : A Proposed Program of Lifeline Preparedness by Jose Ma. de Castro
column10: The concept of lifelines is reviewed. To define the setting, both the political makeup of Metro Manila and its geotechnical condition are first reviewed. Among the lifelines considered are : transportation, water, energy and communication lifelines. A plan to provide reasonable assurance that these lifelines are functional after the major earthquake event is proposed. Roles of different entities, both government and private, are suggested. The proposed preparedness plan is discussed in two parts : post-earthquake activities, and development of public policy in anticipation of a major earthquake.

column9: Load Resistance Factor Design Philosophy by Jose Ma. de Castro
column10: The publication by the American Institute of Steel Construction of the specifications for Load Resistance Factor Design (LRFD) signaled the acceptance of LRFD as a co-equal of the traditional Allowable Stress Design (ASD) in the design of steel structures and indicated that, in time, LRFD would be the predominant design method for structural steel. Although LRFD has already been in use as strength design for reinforced concrete since 1956 in the USA, and since 1986 in the Philippines (first adopted in the 3rd Edition of the National Structural Code of the Philippines for buildings), the philosophy behind the LRFD method of design is not yet fully appreciated by the local structural engineering profession. Because of the many implications of LRFD both for the practitioner and for building code writers, this paper presents a brief discussion of the philosophy and background of the LRFD method of design.

column9: Implications of Welded Beam-Column Joint Failures during the 1994 Northridge Earthquake by Jose Ma. de Castro
column10: Contemporary wisdom states that a welded steel moment frame (WSMF), connected by means of Uniform Building Code (UBC) standard prequalified welded connections, is a superior structural system because of the ductility provided and is thus relatively invulnerable to earthquake damage. The 17 January 1994 Northridge Earthquake quickly proved that such frames were not only not invulnerable but were instead very susceptible to damage. This paper provides a catalog of different types of damage found in WSMF's, discusses some possible causes, and then presents some solutions proposed by different organizations and entities in the United States, some of which had been tested in laboratories under simulated earthquake loading. Finally, different implications are examined.

column9: Analysis & Design of Soil Nail System : Derivations by Jose Ma. de Castro
column10: The block of soil-nailed material is assumed to act as a rigid body for external forces. The retained soil is assumed to have a horizontal surface, with surcharge loads that are uniformly distributed. Earthquake forces are not explicitly considered. Both the external stability of the soil-nailed block, and the internal stability of the nails against pullout from the soil, are considered in the derivation of required nails.

column9: Key Elements to Consider in Revising the Codes of Ethics of Filipino Engineers by Manuel C. Belino
column10: This paper gives a preliminary analysis of the five (5) Codes of Ethics of Filipino Engineers and enumerates some key elements that must be considered in revising these Codes. The key elements are : underlying principles, scope and form, marginally relevant elements, and concerns for contemporary issues. The concerns for contemporary issues include promotion of safety, health and public welfare; concern for the environment; respect for intellectual property rights; and, liberalization of professional services.

column9: The 1997-1998 El Niño Episode and Metro Manila's Water Supply by Reynaldo B. Vea
column10: The Metropolitan Waterworks and Sewerage System (MWSS), the water franchise for the Metro Manila area, had to implement several measures to cope with the effects of the El Niño episode on the quantity and quality of the water supply. An explanation of the El Niño phenomenon is given as well, to emphasize the need of incorporating this knowledge into the process of strategic planning for water supply development.

column9: Computation of Nonlinear Wave Field in Reflective Harbors by Eric C. Cruz
column10: A computational model is presented that applies to waves in harbors of any shape. A set of equations for nonlinear dispersive wave evolution is used with a damping term for energy dissipation at structure discontinuities and boundary conditions on the velocities at structure faces to model the coupled interior and exterior wave fields. The boundary condition is developed using a volume flux resolution method and allows for alternate ways of prescribing the condition for the direction of the reflected wave. The effect of structure damping is shown by a diffraction simulation and the applicability of the boundary condition for partial reflection is verified by comparing with the result of a laboratory experiment.

column9: Using Tensorial Biomorphometrics in Quantitative Growth Analysis of Prenatal Rat Eyes by Andre S. Publico
column10: Developments in geometric morphometrics, image acquisition, and geometrical information extraction technologies have made the invetigations of forms and form changes in biological intra- and inter- population settings increasingly practical. Presented in this paper is a reference-frame invariant morphometric method for handling, not only landmark data, outline data or interlandmark distances representation, but also the more general cases of multiboundary and zoned biological structures. Form change is considered as a large deformation of continuum from an initial to a final configuration. The kinematics is modeled as displacement funtions that satisfy the Laplace equation subject to Dirichlet boundary conditions. These problems are solved using the boundary element method. Growth analysis of prenatal rat eyes by this tensorial biomorphometrics obtained results that agree with documented qualitative descriptions.

column9: Effects of Chemical Composition and Diameter of Binders on the Interfacial Transition Zone in Concrete by Nathaniel B. Diola
column10: The effects of chemical composition and the diameter of binders on the ITZs (Interfacial Transition Zones) between model aggregates and mortar were investigated. To achieve variety in binder chemical composition, three types of cement : ordinary Portland, high alite, and high belite cements were used together with fly ash and blast furnace slag. Limestone powder and ordinary Portland cement were sieved and classified into three diameter ranges for the investigation of the influence of binder diameter. It was found that the amount of C3S + C3A in the binder affects the formation of ITZ. Also, it was shown that the presence of fine reactive particles does not necessarily lead to improvement in ITZ or mortar properties. The effect of diameter of binder and some ways of improving the ITZ and mortar strength are also discussed.

column9: Commercial Production of 10,000 psi Concrete for the Pacific Plaza Towers Project by Francis C. Felizardo, Oliver S. Elec, Jose R. Ofilan, Jr., Maricor M. Cervera
column10: From August 1998 to April 1999, more than 12,000 cu.m. of 10,000 psi (10K) concrete was succesfully delivered to the Pacific Plaza Towers (PPT) Project, the first Philippine project to specify 10K concrete. Although producing high strength concrete is a technical challenge, the raw materials for producing 10K concrete are readily available. Moreover, the ideas behind its mix design, production and performance measurement are both simple and accessible to concrete producers with a good technical organization. The PPT Project proved that reliable and cost-effective 10K concrete is now available in the Philippines.

column9: Graphical Post-processing of Finite Element Analyses by Mark H. Zarco
column10: A procedure for post-processing finite element results is presented. The procedure uses graphic entities to generate mesh, contour and vector plots in data exchange format (DXF) for viewing and further processing using cad software. An algorithm for generating contour plots from finite element analyses results is also presented. The contour plotting procedure ues an interpolation scheme that is consistent with that assumed by the finite element formulation. In cases where the physical quantity being plotted is discontinous across element boundaries, a global smoothing procedure is employed to obtain the required nodal values. Illustrative examples are presented to demonstrate the capabilities of the proposed procedure. The resulting post processing procedure is general enough to apply to wide range of problems, and provides a convenient means of generating graphical representations of finite element analysis results.

column9: Technique for Calculating Vertical Soil Stresses Underneath Foundations with Curved Boundary by Victor A. Pulmano
column10: A simple computational technique is proposed for evaluating the vertical soil stresses underneath foundations with curved boundary. By parametric mapping of the local and Cartesian coordinates which define an eight-node square domain and an eight-node domain with curved boundary, respectively, the integral expression of the Boussinesq solution over a loaded domain with curved boundary is transformed into an expression which can be easily integrated using the Gaussian integration scheme. Numerical example is presented to demonstrate the performance of the proposed technique.

column9: Green's Functions for Clamped Beams by Salvador F. Reyes
column10: In the analysis of frames using general-purpose commercial software, obtaining the value of the bending moment function M(x) and transverse shear V (x) at any section x of a non-prismatic beam or a beam subjected to uncommon combinations of loading can be cumbersome and not too accurate. To provide an alternative, this note derives Green's functions for bending moment and shear in the clamped beam with or without a hinge release at some point on the axis. The task of finding shear and bending moment functions under any loading condition is reduced to a straightforward numeric integration process that may be easily implemented on currently available personal computers.

column7:
UP REPSA, Inc. is duly registered with the Securities and Exchange Commission
The UP REPSA, Inc. is a non-stock, non-profit organization duly registered with the Securities and Exchange Commission on September 9, 1997. Its members consist of research, extension and professional staff of the University of the Philippines System. 
UP REPSA is an organization whose members come from the research, extension and profssional staff of the entire UP system. 
UP REPSA's objectives are : 
* to organize the research, extension and professional staff of the University of the Philippines in the pursuit of common scientific, economic, social and political interest; 
* to safeguard the rights and to advance the welfare of the REPS, as well as to work for the re-orientation of science for the people through sustained education activities among science and technology workers and the people as a whole; 
*to work for the development of scientific programs suited to the needs and interests of the common people;
*to make a unified stand on issues, problems and events of national/sectoral concern;
*to make proper sectoral representation in various policy making bodies of the University
UP REPSA Activities
Since establishment, the following activities have been held : 
*Election of Officers for the System and for each Autonomous Unit.
*Holding of the First UP-REPS Conference last October 15-16, 1998 at the D.L. Umali Hall of UP Los Baños, with the theme "Gearing UP-REPS Toward the 21st Century"
*Submission of the "Proposals for New Guidelines in Determining Rank and Salary Grade of UP-REPS" to the UP Administration.

column10:
Japan International Cooperation Agency (JICA) in cooperation with Institut Teknologi Bandung held the first South East Asian Seminar/workshop on Higher Engineering Education Network at the Institut Teknologi Bandung, Indonesia February 25-27,1997, aiming to exchange information, human resources, education and researches in the region. Around eighty delegates from six countries, Indonesia, Malaysia, Philippines, Singapore, Thailand and Japan attended the seminar/workshop. However, the frame, the scope, the functions, and the activities of this Network were not definitive yet at that moment. So, at the workshop, all the participants intensively discussed and debated those topics. 
This proceeding includes all the contents presented at the seminar/workshop as well as the agreements and consensus's reached through the individual communications with each country delegate after the seminar/workshop, which are in the executive summary. The executive summary describes the conclusions derived from three commission meetings on February 26 and the conclusion session on February 27, and the agreements and consensus integrating the individual communication results with each country delegate.

(2002, July 30) MEMORANDUM NO. DCB 02-031
column10:
TO : Deans and Department Chairs of Concerned Colleges/Units/Departments
FROM : Demetria C. Bongga
Vice Chancellor for Research and Development
SUBJECT : 12th BIWAKO Prize for Ecology
DATE : 30 July 2002 
1. The Shiga Prefectural Government in Japan is inviting candidates to apply for the 12th Biwako Prize for Ecology in accordance wtih the rules stated in the attached prize invitation guidance pamphlet. The Biwako Prize provides research grants to individuals engaged in the study of ecology or related sciences dealing with aquatic and/or watershed environments. The prize is awarded annually to an individual who has achieved academically or socially significant research and who is expected to continue to advance this research at a similar level of accomplishment.
2. Nominations shall be made through channels. Deadline for submission of nominations (2 copies) to OVCRD is September 15, 2002. 
3. Details about the 2002 Biwako Prize for Ecology and the nomination procedures are found at the attached announcement and at the following webpage : http://www.ovcrd.upd.edu.ph/dcbmemo31.htm 
Please disseminate the invitation to your students, faculty and research staff.

column9: (2002, July 1) Re : Nomination of Candidates for the 2002 Biwako Prize for Ecology
column10:
Dear Sir/ Madam :
The Shiga Prefectural Government in Japan has consistently promoted programs that further humanity's sustainable coexistence with environment. In 1991, the Shiga Prefectural Government established the Biwako Prize for Ecology in its desire to play a greater role in environmental conservation and to make a greater contribution to the international community. 
The Biwako Prize is aimed at supporting individuals engaged in the study of ecology or related sciences dealing with aquatic and/or watershed environments. The prize is awarded annually to an individual who has achieved academically or socially significant research and who is expected to continue to advance this research at a similar level of accomplishment. 
On the 12th occasion of the awarding of the Biwako Prize for Ecology, we consider it essential to seek further development of this prize by continuing our commendation of high-level researchers as appropriate to the purpose of the award. To more accurately reflect the deepening and expansion of the research content of ecological studies, we have decided to revise our system and extend the screening period from three months to over six months, and change the management/commendation cycle for the prize from the present one year to two years, thereby improving the selection and management activities for this award. 
Related to this, we hereby invite candidates to apply for the 12th Biwako Prize for Ecology in accordance with the rules stated in the enclosed prize invitation guidance pamphlet. We look forward to receiving many recommendations of suitable candidates. 
The announcement of prize recipients will be made in May 2003 and the award ceremony will be held on July 1st, 2003 ("Lake Biwa Day," the environmental conservation day for prefectural residents as set by the Basic Environmental Ordinances of Shiga Prefecture).
We would also appreciate it if you could notify related organizations or research bodies under your jurisdiction regarding the invitation of candidates to apply to become recipients of the Biwako Prize for Ecology.
I would be very grateful if you would circulate the information on the Nomination of Candidates for the 2002 Biwako Prize for Ecology among academic societies, research institutes, and related organizations in your country to encourage the nomination of candidates for the prize. I would be also appreciated if you could notice the applications term is until October 30, 2002. 
Thank you very much for your attention to this request.

Yours sincerely, 

Yoshitsugu Kunimatsu
Governor
Shiga Prefecture

Dear Sir/Madam :

We, the students of the University of the Philippines College of FIne Arts are celebrating our annual FINE ARTS WEEK, on February 19 to 23, 2001 with the theme ArtReach : On Common Ground. This is a weeklong festivity, featuring art workshops, exhibits, art talks and fora, film showings and intramurals. ArtReach intends to bring together individuals from different fields. This year, we go beyond the range of the visual arts by reaching out and delving into various areas of specialization, such as literature, music and dance. 

Workshops on photography, printmaking, caricature, beginner's airbrush, and body casting will be conducted two weeks before the Fine Arts week proper in order to allow the active participation of students, staff and faculty members. The registration of the different workshops is from January 15 to February 2, 2001. 

We also like to invite you to the Student-Faculty and Connecting Lines Exhibits, wherein artworks of students, faculty members and guest artists are displayed and opt for sale.

Dear Engr. Atanacio

Greetings from the Bank of the Philippine Islands!

This year, we are celebrating the 10th year anniversary of the BPI Science Awards and we would like to express our heartfelt thanks to your university for being our partner in this undertaking. 

The celebration of this milestone in our effort to encourage students to excel in these specialized fields is made more meaningful since it coincides with our commemoration of the centennial year. We, in the Bank of the Philippine Islands, are hopeful that our modest efforts in recognizing the achievements of outstanding students in science and engineering will contribute to our shared task of nation-building. 

We would like to invite you to the 1998 BPI Science Awards ceremonies for UP DIliman on February 11 (Wednesday), 9:00 am at the College of Science Auditorium. The guest of honor during the awards will be Fernando Zobel de Ayala.
Again, we express our sincerest appreciation for your cooperation. We look forward to seeing you during the awards rites and to working with you again for the continued success of the BPI Science Awards.

column9: (1998, January 27)
column10:
Dean Edgardo G. Atanacio
College of Engineering
UP Diliman

Dear Dean Atanacio, 

Warm Greetings!

The University Center for Women's Studies is co-sponsoring with the National Centennial Commission-Women Sector and the Women's Studies Association of the Philippines the National Symposium on "History Makes Women/Women Make Herstory" on March 9-10, 1998 at the Bahay ng Alumni. This is in collaboration with the Department of History, CSSP and the Women and Development Program of CSWCD.

The objectives of the National Symposium are : 

- To examine the critical issues stemming from the gender-blindness of Philippine historiography;
- To provide an alternative framework for viewing women's roles and contributions in the making of Philippine history; 
- To explore various feminist methodologies, including historiography, in the weaving of a Philippine 'herstory";
- To attempt a feminist reconstruction of Philippine history (focusing on the centennial);
- To encourage the making of regional/local "herstories" in tandem with the national one;
- To showcase women's historical creativity through exhibits, cultural presentations, and bazaars; and
- To orient and prepare the participants for the forthcoming international conference in December 1998. 

We cordially invite you and the members of the faculty to participate in this historic affair. Enclosed is the symposium brochure which describes various activities you may be interested to join. 

The symposium can accomodate only 500 participants. We will therefore register participants on a "first come, first served" basis. 

Please address all inquiries and communications to the University Center for Women's Studies at UP Diliman. Our telephone numbers are 920-68-80 and 920-53-01 local 4931. Esel Laxa-Panlaqui or Wanet Lacsamana will gladly take your call. 

Very truly yours, 

SYLVIA H. GUERRERO
Director

column10:
The BPI Science Awards
Shaping the future through science

The Bank of the Philippine Islands (BPI) believes that the Development of Science and Technology is vital in the advancement of any country.

Along this line, BPI, through its Foundation, has created a program geared to recognize young men and women from all over the Philippines who excel in the specialized fields of science. Conceived in 1989, the Annual BPI Science Awards serve as encouragement for budding scientists and researchers to strive towards helping the country make technological breakthroughs. 

PHILOSOPHY

Science and Technology are the real engines of progress. History and experiences point to the fact that the modern world is shaped by advances in these fields. Outstanding science students are worthy of recognition in view of their potential contributions to industry and nation building. 

OBJECTIVE

The objective of the awards is to recognize and give incentives to students who excel in specialized fields of science, namely, Mathematics, Physics, Engineering, Chemistry, Biology and Computer Science. An awardee must have consistent scholarly academic records in his chosen field and must be recommended by the school. 

PARTICIPATING SCHOOLS 

The awards are given to the top 3 junior and senior students of the following universities : 
- Ateneo de Davao University
- Ateneo de Manila University
- De La Salle University
- Silliman University
- St. Louis University Baguio
- University of the Philippines Diliman
- University of the Philippines Los Baños
- University of San Carlos
- University of Santo Tomas
- Xavier University

column10:
Background
To participate in the 1983 observance of the Diamond Jubilee of the University of the Philippines and to celebrate the Golden Anniversary of its founding, the U.P. Chapters of the Honor Society of Phi Kappa Phi extended an invitation to the U.P. Chapters of Pi Gamma Mu International Social Science Honor Society and Phi Sigma Biological Honor Society to sponsor a joint activity on campus. 
The first cooperative effort was a convocation honoring outstanding senior held January 21, 1983 at Malcolm Hall, College of Law. This was envisioned to become a regular annual undertaking.
It is hoped that recognition of exemplary seniors will help achieve the objectives of these societies, encourage the awardees to continue to strive for excellence in all their endeavors, and serve as inspiration for emulation by other students. 
PI GAMMA MU
The International Social Science Honor Society
- The International Honor Society of Pi Gamma Mu is a non-profit corporation organized to promote social science. it was founded in 1924 by Dean Leroy Allen of Southwestern College and Dean William A. Hamilton of the College of William and Mary. It has more than 160 active chapters worldwide. 
- The Philippines Alpha Chapter, the first in Asia, was established in the University of the Philippines on June 27, 1932 with an initial core of 11 members. It celebrated its Golden Jubilee in 1982 and by then its membership had grown to more than 6,000. The honor society counts among its membership the cream of the graduates of the University. 
- The Society's motto - COGNOSOCIETIES VERITATEM ET VERITAS VOS LIBERABIT - is translated, "Ye shall know the truth and the truth shall make you free."
- Pi Gamma Mu endeavors to inspire in its honored members social service to humanity and life interest in the study of human association. It seeks also a synthesis of all branches of social sciences into a philosophy of human society and human welfare. 
THE HONOR SOCIETY OF PHI KAPPA PHI
An International Honor Society
- The first honor society to recognize superior scholarship in all fields of study and to elect into membership the highest ranking students from all branches of learning. 
- Founded at the University of Maine at Orono in 1897 by three state university presidents to fill the need for an interdisciplinary honor society. 
- Chapter No. 045, established at the University of the Philippines in 1933 by 14 scholarly leaders, four of whom eventually became presidents of the University. 
- The motto of the Society - PHILOSOPHIA KRATEITO PHOTON ("May the love of learning rule mankind") - express a goal rather than an accomplishment. 
- The primary objective - the recognition and encouragement of superior scholarship in all academic disciplines, fosters an abiding love of learning. 
PHI SIGMA
Biological Honor Society
- Devoted to the promotion of research and academic excellence in the biological sciences. 
- Charges its members to assume leadership in all activities appropriate to a biologist, to maintain high scholarship, to promote active research among its members, and to acquaint others with the results of all worthy achievements in biological fields. 
- Founded at the Ohio State University, Columbus, Ohio, 17 March 1915. 
- Alpha Chi Chapter installed at the University of the Philippines, 12 March 1949; Dr. Herbert E. Warfel, installing officer, with officers and members drawn from the U.P. Biological Society, College of Arts and Sciences. 
- The society's motto - ALETHEIA EK TES GES - is translated, "Truth shall spring out of the earth and righteousness shall look down from heaven" (Psalm 85:11)
- Member, American Association of College Honor Societies and American Institute of Biological Sciences; associate member, American Association for the Advancement of Science.

Dear Dr. Atanacio : 
The Commission on Higher Education (CHED) will conduct an evaluation of colleges and universities in the National Capital Region (NCR) in relation to their application for government authority to operate programs. To undertake this task, the Commission is seeking the assistance of experts from the academe, industry and professional associations. These experts shall be organized as Quality Assessment Teams (QATs) in the different clusters of disciplines. 
In this regard, since UP is a center of excellence in the field of engineering education, we would like to request for assistance from your institution by allowing some of your faculty to be involved as members of NCR-QAT which will evaluate colleges and universities offering engineering programs. SUBJECT : CONSTITUTION OF A SELECTION COMMITTEE FOR THE GAWAD CHANSELOR FOR OUTSTANDING INVENTION AND INNOVATION

As part of the celebration of the Diliman month in February 2001, we will be launching the Gawad Chanselor to recognizing the outstanding accomplishments of our faculty, staff and students. In this regard, please constitute yourselves into an Ad Hoc Committee, with Dean Edgardo G. Atanacio as Chair, to select the OUTSTANDING INVENTION AND INNOVATION OF U.P. DILIMAN. 

First, Second and Third Prize will be given during the Recognition Ceremony to be held on February 23, 2001.

column9:
Proposal on Teaching Effectiveness Course for Engineering Faculty 
September 29, 2000
column10:
Target Date : 5 Saturdays commencing October 28, 2000
Course Philosophy :

This course is based upon the philosophy that : good teaching practices can be taught and can be learned, i.e., a "real" academic scholar is both a good teacher and a good researcher; in a competitive marketplace, those who demonstrate the ability to cogently represent their research and the research in the field (the same skills of a good teacher), will be the favored candidate for the future professorate; teacher and student both, have a responsibility to provide an optimal learning environment; the current research on, and philosophies of, teaching and learning can be translated into sound practice; students and individuals, can profoundly influence the success of the classroom experience; and faculty from all disciplines have something of value with which to engage in dialogues for learning; and that close collaboration with the discipline is essential for effectively learning teaching skills that match the language and culture of the discipline. 

Course Goals:

College and university professors traditionally have been better prepared in their content area than the practice of teaching. With sound practice and the current, competitive marketplace in mind, this course is designed to assist future applicants to the professorate to : 

- Cogently represent their research, the research in the field, and their content (the same skills required of a good teacher), thus being the favored candidate for the professorate;
- Combine their content knowledge with the theory and practice of sound teaching; 
- Acquire an advanced level of skill and understanding of the philosophy and practice of teaching and learning in the college and university setting; and 
- Carry this skill and understanding to every campus on which they have teaching appointments, becoming advocates for the advancement of good teaching. 

Course Objective : 
Through classroom discussions, assignments, and exercises, students will be able to demonstrate:

- Familiarity with the diversity of the individuals, i.e., the faculty and student population, student development and student learning; 
- A skill level and knowledge of a number of instructional methods
- A skill level and knowledge of organizational skills and classroom management
- Familiarity with resources and technology available to enhance teaching and learning; 
- Personal philosophy of teaching and scholarship;
- Familiarity with the role of the faculty member in the university and college setting

Teaching and Learning Strategies :
The underlying philosophies for teaching and learning in this course include active learning, critical reflection and experiential learning. A variety of instructional methods including lecture, teamwork, class discussion and interaction, guest speakers, reflective writing and reading, video, technology and the internet will be used to model good teaching practice. The participants will have the opportunity to practice and reflect on the course material and class discussions in a real setting, that is, experiential learning. 

Course Syllabus: 
1. Introduction to the Course
- Individual introductions and expectations
- The syllabus - course policy and practices
- Assigning teams for facilitation of weekly discussions

2. The College Student
- Student Development
- Learning styles and knowledge acquisition
- Motivation

3. What makes a good teacher?
- What makes a good teacher?
- The first day of class
- Diversity in the classroom

4. Instructional Methods
- Active learning
- Lecturing
- Teams
- Discussions
- -Questioning
- -Case study
- -Laboratory games
- -Role play
- -Simulation
- Writing and reading 
-- Experiential learning
-- Individual instruction
-- Field trips, study tours

5. Organizational skills
- Being prepared
- The syllabus
- Time management

6. Ethics
7. Classroom management
--Academic integrity
--Discipline and problem situation

8. Evaluation of teaching
-- Instructor and course evaluation
-- Feedback
-- Teaching Portfolio

9. Testing and Grading
-- Learning assessments formats
-- Grading

10. The faculty role
-- Teaching, Tenure and Promotion
-- Research and teaching connection
-- Guest : Vice Chancellor for Academic Affairs or the Chancellor herself
-- Responsibilities outside the classroom
-- Mentoring

11. Teaching Resources
-- Teaching tools, media and technology
-- The internet
-- Campus and community services

12. Course wrap-up
-- Feedback
-- Dialogue about the course
-- Course and instructor evaluation

Budget : 
The budget for this activity is estimated at one hundred twenty five thousand pesos (P115,000) broken down as follows : 
Honoraria for resources speakers 
P1000/hr X 8 hr/day x 5 days    P40,000
Lunch and Snacks
P200/pax X 40 pax                      P80,000
Photocopying/Documentation       5,000
TOTAL                                            P125,000

IX. Matters for Confirmation of the Board

The Board DEFERRED action on the following, pending clarification of the provision in the Memorandum of Agreement as specifically stated in letter a below.

The Board would like to find out if this includes appointment of UP faculty/academic personnel to administrative positions at Mapua. 

A. Memorandum of Agreement entered into by and between U.P. Diliman and the Mapua Institute of Technology (MIT).

Project : Faculty Exchange 

Particulars : 

a. Upon request of Mapua Institute of Technology, U.P. Diliman shall permit its faculty from the College of Engineering, College of Education, and other colleges as may be needed, to serve at MIT on a part-time basis in such capacity as may be requested by them and subject to the rules and procedures agreed upon. (underscoring ours)
b. Faculty members of U.P. Diliman serving at MIT shall be given reasonable compensation by the latter institution, in accordance with its salary administration plan, policies and rules; provided that faculty members and/or academic personnel given teaching assignments at MIT shall be appointed threat to a rank equivalent to that held by said faculty member and/or academic personnel in U.P. Diliman. 

Duration : From 15 June 2000 to 14 June 2003

Date Signed : 10 June 2000

CERTIFIED CORRECT : 

MARTIN V. GREGORIO
Secretary of the University and of the Board of Regents

MEMORANDUM OF AGREEMENT

KNOW ALL MEN BY THESE PRESENTS :

This Memorandum of Agreement, made and entered into this 10th day of June, 2000 at Diliman, Quezon City, Philippines, by and between :

The UNIVERSITY OF THE PHILIPPINES DILIMAN, a State Institution of higher learning, created and operating pursuant to Act. 1870, as amended, with principal office at Quezon Hall, U.P. Campus, Diliman, Quezon City, herein represented by its Chancellor, DR. EMERLINDA R. ROMAN, hereinafter referred to as "U.P. DILIMAN";
and
MAPUA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY, a private non-sectarian institution, organized and operating in accordance with laws of the Republic of the Philippines, with principal office at Muralla St., Intramuros, Manila, herein represented by its President, DR. REYNALDO B. VEA., hereafter referred to as "MAPUA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY".

WITNESSETH : that

WHEREAS, one of the current thrusts of UP DILIMAN is to provide advanced instruction in engineering and to give professional and technical training to deserve students of the country;

WHEREAS, MAPUA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY is a private non-sectarian educational institution founded more than 75 years ago by Don Tomas Mapua;

WHEREAS, it is a policy of U.P. DILIMAN to extend assistance, consistent with its purpose and without prejudice to its needs and programs, to other institutions of learning in order to further the interests of higher education in the Philippines; 

WHEREAS, the current thrust of MAPUA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY is consistent with U.P. DILIMAN's mission of helping attain quality education; 

WHEREAS, MAPUA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY desires and seeks assistance from U.P. DILIMAN;

WHEREAS, U.P. DILIMAN has faculty members and/or academic personnel who possess the necessary qualification and are willing to undertake services for the assistance sought; 

NOW, THEREFORE, for and in consideration of the foregoing premises, the parties hereto hereby agree as follows :

1. On request by MAPUA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY, U.P. DILIMAN shall permit its faculty member and/or academic personnel from the College of Engineering, College of Education, and other colleges as may be needed, to serve in the former on a part-time basis in such capacity as may be requested by MAPUA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY, and subject to the rules and procedures stipulated in Annex "A" which is made an integral part hereof. 

2. Faculty members and/or academic personnel of U.P. DILIMAN serving in MAPUA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY shall be given reasonable compensation by the latter institution, in accordance with its salary administration plan, policies and rules; Provided that, the faculty membre and/or academic personnel given teaching assignments at MAPUA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY shall be appointed thereat to a rank equivalent to that held by said faculty member and/or academic personnel in U.P. DILIMAN.

3. MAPUA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY shall furnish U.P. DILIMAN and the college where the faculty/staff belongs with a copy of the appointment issued, or contract of employment executed, as the case may be, for each faculty member and/or academic personnel.

4. As needed by, and on request of, U.P. DILIMAN, MAPUA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY shall permit its faculty member and other academic personnel to serve in the former on a part-time basis with compensation as may be agreed upon by both parties, in such capacity as may be desired by U.P. DILIMAN.

5. All Dean, Directors and Heads of other principal units of U.P. DILIMAN and MAPUA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY shall be informed of this Agreement in its entirety; Provided that, faculty members and/or academic personnel whose services are sought shall be apprised of this Agreement before their written consent is required. 

6. Only Graduate Students of MAPUA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY shall have access to U.P. DILIMAN library facilities, subject to U.P. DILIMAN's library rules and regulations. The library shall be open to the Graduate Students only on Wednesdays, for room use only, upon presentation of a referral letter from the MAPUA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY librarian, and a valid ID from said students. 

7. It is understood that the activities enumerated herein shall be decided through consultation with the concerned U.P. DILIMAN college/unit, and that the assistance extended to MAPUA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY by U.P. DILIMAN shall be subject to the availability of faculty members and/or academic personnel and other resources. 

8. This Agreement shall be deemed modified accordingly insofar as it may be affected by new policies, rules and procedures or amendments to existing ones that may be adopted by either institution, provided that the other institution shall be notified thereof within thirty (30) days from its issuance. 

9. This Agreement shall be effective upon completion of the signatures of both parties and shall remain in full force and effect for a period of three (3) years beginning 15 June 2000 to 14 June 2003, unless terminated by mutual consent or as the exigencies of the services in either institution may require; Provided that, in the latter instance, the party initiating termination hereof shall notify the other at least thirty (30) days in advance in order that pending programs are not prejudiced. 

10. This Agreement may be renewed or amended for such period and under such terms and conditions mutually agreed upon by the parties in writing. 

IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the parties have hereunto set their hands on the date and at the place first above written.

column9: Rules and procedures to govern the involvement of members of the faculty of the U.P. Diliman in Mapua Institute of Technology
column10:
A. RULES
The following Rules shall govern the involvement of members of the faculty of U.P. Diliman in MAPUA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY and the faculty of MAPUA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY in U.P. Diliman without prejudice to such other rules as may be promulgated as the need arises : 
1. Specific authorization in each case from the Office of the Chancellor of U.P. Diliman is required before a faculty member may teach in MAPUA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY. 
2. Each authorization shall be for a period not exceeding one (1) semester, exclusive of any renewal which shall in no case go beyond the term of the Memorandum of Agreement. 
3. The maximum permissible load of a faculty member who will teach in MAPUA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY shall not exceed six (6) units (i.e. course credits) per semester.
4. Service in MAPUA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY shall be rendered outside the regular office hours of the faculty member in U.P. DILIMAN.
5. It is to be understood that the involvement of a faculty member in MAPUA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY shall not interfere with or prejudice his or her regular and other responsibilities, ongoing projects or programs. Corollary to this, therefore, the permission granted may be withdrawn by the Office of the Chancellor of U.P. DILIMAN if the exigencies of the service so require. An advance notice of at least thirty (30) days shall be served to MAPUA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY before such a withdrawal shall take effect. 
6. No authorization shall be issued without the occurence of the dean and department chairperson concerned and the written consent of the faculty member whose services are being requested. 
B. PROCEDURES
The following procedures shall be followed in the processing of requests (initial or renewal) for services of faculty members of U.P. DILIMAN.
Step 1 - MAPUA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY prepares the request for services addressed to the Chancellor of U.P. DILIMAN through channels, specifying, among other things, the following : 
a. Name of faculty member whose services are sought;
b. Nature of projected assignment or involvement, including course title and number of units;
c. Projected service hours; and 
d. Approximate duration (i.e. , the inclusive dates) of projected assignment 
The requests should be forwarded directly to the Chancellor of U.P. Diliman or the department where the faculty member belongs. The request should be submitted at least thirty (30) days in advance. 
Step 2 - Chairperson of Department - With the needs of the service in mind, the Chairman endorses the request, together with the written consent of the faculty member concerned, to the Dean. However, if the chairperson feels the services of the faculty member cannot be made available, he should communicate directly with MAPUA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY accordingly and need not forward the request to the Dean. 
Step 3 - Office of the Chancellor - Also with the needs of the service in mind, the Chancellor of U.P. Diliman approves or disapproves the request and subsequently informs MAPUA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY of the action taken.

Office of Extension Coordination

Dr. Amelia P. Guevara
Vice-Chancellor for Academic Affairs
U.P. Diliman

column9: (2000, March 10) Summary Report on the Results of the Memorandum of Agreement with the Mapua Institute of Technology by Jonathan L. Salvacion, Dr. Eng
column10:
The original and main intent behind the Memorandum of Agreement with the Mapua Institute of Technology (MIT) was to enable an exchange of faculty members between the U.P. College of Engineering and MIT. It took effect between June 15, 1999 and June 14, 2000. 
As an immediate result of the MOA, the author was able to teach a graduate course (Advanced Mass Transfer) for one semester following the signing of the MOA. However, no other faculty member of the College is known to have taught in Mapua during that semester up to the present. 
As a result also of the MOA, problems concerning faculty members of MIT which are enrolled in the Ph.D. program of the U.P. Department of Chemical Engineering were easily resolved. Advising was facilitated because of frequent contacts between faculty members of both sides. Two Ph.D. students whose research projects were discontinued because of the termination of the Engineering and Science Education Program (ESEP) and the second-ment of their U.P. adviser to the DOST were able to change their research direction and/or adviser. Although the direct benefits of this may be seen to accrue mainly on the side of MIT, the main benefits to the U.P. Department of Chemical Engineering is the continuation of research projects led by U.P. faculty members and a new linkage. Indeed, the MOA fostered a much better atmosphere for collaboration between the Departments of Chemical Engineering of both institutions. It is therefore hoped that in the future, a new MOA can be signed extending to joint research projects that can be undertaken between the faculties of U.P. and MIT.

This form was supposed to have been returned to us after accomplishment as we gave Mr. David instructions to furnish as a copy after seeing the University Legal Counsel and after notarization of the Promissory Note. We likewise informed the College, through Ms. Remedios Santiago and Mr. Alfonso Julian about these developments since the college was in a better position to communicate with Prof. David. 

We took it to mean therefore, that Prof. David had been making arrangements with the University Legal Counsel regarding the settlement of her contractual obligation. However, when Dr. Jonathan C. Salvacion came over to our Office to inquire about Prof. David's status, we learned from the Office of Legal Services, that there was no Promissory Note filed with their office. We have likewise attached a copy of the steps/procedures involved in the normal processing of reneging fellow's settlement of his contractual obligation.

Since Prof. David has now been appointed Senior Lecturer in your college, we would appreciate your immediate action to clarify her contractual obligation status. 

Very truly yours, 

MA. BELINDA V. LOPEZ
Director

Standard Operating Procedures on the Processing of Resignations/Dropping From the Rolls of Fellows with Contractual Obligations 
HRDO :
1. HRDO (EBRS) receives resignation letter of fellow (with endorsements of the Dean and Department of Chairman), or recommendation from dropping from the rolls. 
2. EBRS checks with the Scholarships Section if the fellow has a contractual obligation. If the resignation letter is unaccompanied by a duty accomplished University clearance, the fellow is requested to submit this requirement before acceptance of the resignation and clearance is granted. 
3. HRDO requests for a statement of actual fellowship expenses incurred from the Accounting Office. 
4. HRDO computes total contractual obligation upon receipt of certified statement of expenses from the Accounting Office. 
5. If there is a donor/sponsoring agency (i.e., Fulbright-PAEF, Colombo Plan, Bilateral Programme, etc.) a statement of expenses is likewise requested from the agency and added to the computation of the contractual obligation statement. 
6. The fellow's contractual obligation is indicated on the University clearance form. If there is a contractual obligation, the University Legal Officer will not sign the clearance. 
7. HRDO informs and furnishes the fellow/unit or his appointed representative a copy of the contractual obligation statement and gives instructions to communicate with the Legal Office to facilitate the processing of the fellow's clearance papers. 
8. The Legal Office secures the fellow's files/records from the HRDO for study and evaluation and preparation of demand letter. 
9. Upon grant of clearance by the University Legal Officer when the fellow has made arrangements to settle his contractual obligation, the resignation paper will be processed by the HRDO and the corresponding notification of acceptance of the resignation will be issued addressed to the fellow, copy furnished the unit through the Dean or Head of Unit.

Office of Legal Services :
1. The Legal Office secures the fellow's fellowship folder from the HRDO for study/evaluation of the case. 
2. Informs the fellow or his representative of the options/mode of payment of the contractual obligation based on 980th BOR ruling, dated August 29, 1985.
3. Prepares the necessary contract on the manner of payment as indicated in a Memorandum of Undertaking/Promissory Note the fellow is required to execute with the University Legal Office, before the Legal Officer signs the clearance. 
7. The fellow is given instructions to remit payment to the Accounting Office for which he will be given an official receipt acknowledging the payment which is deposited to the Reneging Fellows fund. 
8. OLS furnishes HRDO a copy of the Promissory Note or certification on the necessary arrangements made by the fellow regarding the settlement of his/her contractual obligation.
9. If neither the fellow nor his sureties could be contacted, the Legal Office will request the Department of Foreign Affairs to put the fellow's name on watch for cancellation of his passport if ever he/she is abroad. 

Head Unit/College : 
1. The dean/unit head endorses resignation letter or recommendation for dropping from the rolls of the reneging fellow.
2. Upon receipt of statement of contractual obligation, unit informs the fellow to make arrangements for the settlement of his contractual obligation to facilitate processing of his clearance papers.

column9: FAG INNOVATION AWARD 2002
column10:
FAG Innovation Award 2002
FAG KUGELFICHER FOUNDATION

FAG Kugelfischer Georg Schafer AG, one of the world's leading rolling bearing manufacturers, established the FAG KUGELFISCHER FOUNDATION on the occasion of its 100th anniversary in 1983.
Its object is the promotion of science, research and teaching in the field of scientific engineering related to bearing technology.
With its foundation, FAG Kugelfisher Georg Schafer AG breaches the gap between industry and science by awarding the ideas, visions and goals of those working at the universities, in research and business.
It is the goal of the foundation to promote the innovative potential and support competitive performance.

In serving its aims, the FAG KUGELFISHER FOUNDATION offers the FAG INNOVATION AWARD for outstanding treatises in the fields of Product Innovation/Enhanced Manufacturing/Market Strategy

column9: FAG INNOVATION AWARD 2002
column10:
FAG INNOVATION AWARD 2002
FAG KUGELFISCHER FOUNDATION

Conditions...Conditions of entry
Treatises from the fields of Product Innovation, Market Strategy and Enhanced Manufacturing that are related to bearing technology can be submitted. We refer to the enclosed examples of topics. 
The treatises may have been drawn up by individual natural persons or by teams. In the case of teams consisting of natural persons, the team has to nominate an authorized person to represent the team against FAG KUGELFISCHER FOUNDATION in all actions. 
If the treatise has been drawn up in the context of a scientific institute, a company or a similar institution, the corresponding consent with the participation in this competition is required. 
The treatises may be submitted in German or English and should not have been completed more than three years before the application deadine. 
Applications are to be directed to the FAG KUGELFISCHER FOUNDATION by the announced application deadline.
Documents...The following documents must be submitted along with an application :
- Fully completed application form
- Curriculum vitae
- Scientific-engineering treatise (in copy)
- Short description of the treatise contents
- Expert assessment on the treatise
- Teams : Authorization
Address...The documents should be sent to : 
FAG KUGELFISHER FOUNDATION
Georg-Schafer-Strasse 30
D-97421 Schweinfurt
The contents of the submitted treatises will be treated confidentially. The applicant agrees that the submitted treatise be passed on to the founder for inspection and for utilization, should the occasion arise. 
The foundation and the founder will handle the submitted treatise with care. However no liability will be accepted for its loss, damage or unauthorized usage by third parties.
The awarding of prizes will be decided by the foundation committee of the FAG KUGELFISCHER FOUNDATION. The judges' decision is final.
We reserve the right to renounce on the awarding of prizes if no prize-worthy treatise has been handed in. In the case of several equally good treatises, a division of prizes is possible. 
The conditions of entry are accepted as binding on submitting the application.

Examples of themes
Product Innovation
- Development of physical / mathematical models for describing the kinematics and dynamics in rolling bearings. 
- Tribology in rolling contact

Enhanced Manufacturing

Market Strategy

column9: 1998 Outstanding Architect of the Year
column10:
A brief biography of Architect Philip H. Recto 
1998 Outstanding Architect of the Year
awarded by the Professional Regulation Commission (PRC) 
Republic of the Philippines during the Philippine Centennial Year Celebration

Architect Philip H. Recto was born in Manila on July 24, 1947. He obtained his Bachelor of Science degree in Architecture from the University of the Philippines where he was a College Scholar and ranked 2nd place in the graduating class of 1970. A diligent student, he was also the Vice-President of the College of Architecture & Fine Arts Student Council.
Arch. Recto's early professional training included an apprenticeship with the office of Felipe M. Mendoza & Associates where he accumulated a wealth of practrical experience, especially in detailed contract documentation; and then with the renowned firm of Leandro V. Locsin & Associates where he honed his design prowess while participating in significant projects such as the Cultural Center of the Philippines. 
In 1971, just a month after passing the board examinations, Arch. Recto obtained his first major commission - an integrated textile knitting factory project in Singapore. Due to the varied technical nature of the project, Arch. Recto decided that a move to the island-state for the duration would be valuable as it would allow him to devote full-time to the project and also serve to expand his professional horizons in the global scene. Thus began a 5-year stay in Singapore where he gained invaluable experience in the design and supervision not only of industrial buildings but also modern high-rise office buildings, residential condominiums, schools and churches. 
After completion of the textile factory project and desiring to broaden his experience in other building types, Arch. Recto joined Ang Kheng Leng & Associates, one of the oldest and biggest architectural firms in Singapore with branch offices in Kuala Lumpur & Penang, as an executive architect. There he was entrusted with many prestigious projects, including the 50-storey International Plaza, the tallest building in Singapore at the time. At this high point in his professional career and equipped with considerable foreign experience and professional maturity, Arch. Recto decided to return to the Philippines and set up practice on his own turf. 
Having returned to Manila at the end of 1975, Arch. Recto set up an architectural partnership with long-time classmate and good friend, David SyCip, Jr., early the following year. In 1978, he established his own professional practice where he went on to carve a niche in the design of functional, no-frill condominium buildings, school buildings and high-rise office buildings. 
Since the beginning, Arch. Recto has always believed in honesty, integrity and diligence as the foundation of service in gaining clients' trust and confidence; and he has inculcated such values in his associates and staff. His unblemished record and high ethical conduct are hallmarks in the building industry today. 
A well-known personage, Recto served as President of the United Architects of the Philippines (UAP) Makati Chapter for two terms (1983 & 84); Chairman of the 11th Architects' National Convention in 1985; and UAP National Vice-President in 1987. He was a member of the 1984 Inter-Professional Conference Committee which undertook detailed review of UAP Document 301 - General Conditions; and was the head of the committee that prepared UAP Document 700 - Standard Forms for Architectural Practice, both used extensively by practicing architects today. 
A prolific interest, Recto has designed numerous landmark structures ranging from first-class office towers to compact, high-rise residential buildings, among which are the PSBANK Tower in Makati; the Philippine National Oil Co. (PNOC) Bldg. in Fort Bonifacio; the 54-Storey One San Miguel Avenue in Ortigas Center; Sunette Tower, Primetown Century Tower and Century Citadel Inn, all in Makati; the State Investment Trust Bldg. in Ortigas; Gedisco Tower in Manila and the JG Summit Corporate Tower in Mandaluyong City. Other notable works include Manila Electric Co. (Meralco) Training Centre in Antipolo; SM City in Xiamen, China; Byron EDSA Hotel in Mandaluyong City; Faith Academy Campus Redevelopment in Cainta; Biblical Seminary of the Philippines in Valenzuela; Hope Christian High School in Manila; Grace Bible Church in Nagtahan; Steniel Manufacturing Plant in Cagayan de Oro City and the Toyota Showroom & Service Center in the Manila Bay reclaimed area. 
Rectos' outstanding achievement in the field of design was recognized when the rank of Fellow was bestowed upon him by his peers in 1986. This is the highest rank in the professional organization of architects. Later, he was invited to be a life member of the exclusive ASEAN Association for Planning & Housing representing the Philippines. He also received numerous awards for his meritorous services to the architectural profession. Dubbed "Designer par excellence" by Construction Magazine (March 1989), his name was acknowledged in VIP's in the Philippine Business (1988), Business & Professionals in the Philippines (1988-1989), ASEAN Who's Who and Who's Who of the World (1998).
When Philippine Centennial Year was celebrated in 1998, Arch. Philip Recto received one of the highest recognitions for a Filipino architect - the prestigious "Outstanding Architect of the Year" award from His Excellency, President Fidel V. Ramos, and the Professional Regulation Commission (PRC), the government agency mandated to license and regulate the practice of Filipino professionals, including 14,000 architects. 
A well-respected professional, Arch. Recto is also generous in sharing his expertise with various government agencies. He was appointed by former President Corazon C. Aquino as a Trustee of the Philippine Technological Council in 1987. He sat as a member of the National Building Code Review Committee (Architecture Sub-committee) at the Department of Public Works and Highways, and acted as a Consultant to the Construction Industry Authority of the Philippines on the formulation of Uniform General Conditions for Private Construction Contracts. He is a founder and director of the Society of Philippine Accredited Consultants and served as Chairman of the Architecture Committee of the Philippine Chamber of Commerce and Industry. He is a sought-after resource speaker on topics concerning high-rise building design, the National Building Code and architectural practice. 
Aside from professional organizations, Recto is actively involved in several religious, educational and civic organizations. He is the Chairman of the Board of Palawan Hope Christian School; a board member of Hope Christian High School, Manila; a deacon of United Evangelical Church of the Philippines and a Paul Harris Fellow of the Rotary Club of Manila. 
Arch. Recto is married to Ester Chiong whom he met in the course of their respective businesses. Prior to their marriage in 1977, Ms. Chiong was a top business executive at a well-established property development company. Today, she is Philip Recto's supportive partner in managing the family's business affairs. Arch. & Mrs. Recto are both devout Christians who believe in the importance of incorporating Christian values and teachings into all aspects of daily life. They generously support a variety of Christian ministries. 
- By Mary B. Holland
PS BANK TOWER
This architecturally-acclaimed 28-storey office building houses the head office of Philippine Savings Bank, one of the country's largest banks and a member of the Metrobank Group. Situated in a location which can be seen from almost all directions, the elegant building's multi-faceted design presents an interesting view and becomes a converging sculptural focal point from all angles. Crowning this impressive building is a space frame structure serving as the communication tower. Illuminated at night, it presents a spectacular sight which is visible for miles and makes it a familiar landmark in Makati City. 
ONE SAN MIGUEL AVENUE
Located at the gateway to Ortigas Center, this 54-storey office building is one of the tallest buildings in the country. With its dramatic exterior in a unifying combination of glass, granite and aluminum, this imposing structure incorporates the country's first double-deck passenger elevator. Sky terraces grace the intermediate floors and the rooftop features a skylounge and roofdeck gardens. Two radio rooms occupying the topmost part of the building with powerful communication antennae complete the prestigious archiectural landmark.

Dear Dean Atanacio, 

GREETINGS!

We are pleased to report to you the activities of our organization since the last General Membership Meeting in February 1999. 

1. ASEAN Automotive Project. We have submitted our final report on our year-long engagement with the Australian Pacific Economic Corporation Committee (AUSPECC) last August 2000. This project is a regional study of the ASEAN Automotive Industry with SATMP as project leader. The results of the study can be viewed through www. asean-auto.org, a web-site that SATMP developed and maintains. Limited copies of this report may be requested from the SATMP secretariat. 
2. NRE Contest. As part of our mandate to advocate environment - friendly technologies, the Philippine National Oil Company engaged our services to manage a nation-wide contest on New and Renewable Energy System. This almost year long engagement culminated with the awarding of prizes to the winners last May 2000. We are happy to report that the Department of Energy would extend commercial funding support to the three winners of the contest. The results are posted on our web site. SATMP produced a souvenir CD to document the contest. Copies of the CD may be requested from the SATMP secretariat. 
3. Analysis of Technical Options for Mitigating Environmental Emissions from the Urban Transport System in Selected Asian Countries. SATMP is part of six-Asian-megacity study on greenhouse gas mitigation being coordinated by the Asian Institute of Technology based in Bangkok. The project is ongoing and will be completed mid-2001. 
4. DAP Yearbook on Technology, Competitiveness and Economy. The Development Academy of the Philippines (DAP) has engaged SATMP's services to manage the development and publication of DAP's Yearbook which henceforth will be an annual activity. The project started this year and will run through 2001. 
5. Technology and Exports Competitiveness. The Philippine Exporters Confederation of the Philippines (PHILEXPORT) has engaged our services to undertake several studies on the above subject, viz. IPR, Innovation, Technology Management, etc. The results were published in a compendium by SATMP and PhilExport. Limited copies of this report may be requested from the SATMP secretariat. 
In addition to the above, SATMP has also conducted local seminars/workshops as part of our advocacy agenda on technology management (see attached list of policy forum and conferences). 
We look forward to be ablt o expand to other technology related activities in the coming years. But as before, the strength of the organization depends on your continuous support. 
Enclosed are membership renewal form and statement of membership dues for your updating. Thank you and best wishes.

Sincerely, 

MAGDALENO B. ALBARRACIN, JR. D.B.A.
President

The National College of Public Administration and Governance, University of the Philippines, (NCPAG-U.P.), together with the Institute for Aerospace Survey and Earth Sciences (ITC) in the Netherlands, is instituting a new area of specialization, Spatial Information Management, in the Master of Public Administration degree Program. This program will be implemented with the support of the U.P. College of Engineering (Department of Geodetic Engineering), the College of Social Sciences and Philosophy (Department of Geography), the School of Urban and Regional Planning (SURP), and the National Center for Transport Studies (NCTS). This new specialization aims to develop a corps of decision makers, administrators, and technical personnel knowledgeable in the theory and practice of Public Administration and Governance, and in the application of information and communications technology (ICT), specifically, Geographic Information Systems (GIS) to their work as public servants. The GIS technology will provide the government and society with an effective platform for addressing public administration concerns in hazard and disaster management, fiscal administration, land administration, public service delivery, election administration, national security, environmental security, and public information management, among many other applications.

The new course program has secured the approval of the Scientific Council of the ITC. Meanwhile the proposal is now undergoing the curricular approval process in the University and we hope to start in the second semester of Academic Year, 2001-2002. The MPA-SIM Program initially targets to accept a maximum of twenty students. The SIM specialization requires students to complete a total of thirty-nine (39) units within a two-year period. Students who have successfully defended their thesis proposals would be given an opportunity to stay for a maximum of three months at the ITC in the Netherlands where they will write and defend their theses. 

In this connection, may we invite you to a roundtable discussion on the MPA-SIM program on April 25, 2001 (Wednesday), 9:00 AM, at the Dean's Conference Room, National College of Public Administration and Governance, University of the Philippines. This roundtable discussion will hopefully provide a basis for determining the needs of government in terms of spatial information management so that a more responsive MPA-SIM Course can be provided by the NCPAG.

We hope that you will help us bring the curriculum closer to your particular need as an institution. We are intent on making the program strategy appropriate for developing the capacity of your staff to better meet challenges of our increasingly technological world. 

Truly yours,

LEDIVINA V. CARINO
Dean

Dear Sir : 

Attached herewith is a copy of the proposal of DLRC to establish a Computer Classroom and Review Center at the College of Engineering. As mentioned in the proposal we will need a 4 bay classroom or two regular size rooms. The creation of the center will greatly benefit engineering students as this geared towards the improvement of the services offered by DLRC1. 

We are hoping for your favorable response to our request. 

Sincerely yours,

Jaime Y. Hernandez Jr. 
DLRC Faculty Coordinator

Proposal for the establishment of a DLRC Computer Classroom and Review Center at the College of Engineering, University of the Philippines Diliman Campus

Project Description

Goal : To establish a self-sustaining, non-profit computer classroom and review center at the College of Engineering in U.P. Diliman.

Objective : 
1. To give underprivileged students opportunities to utilize the benefits of technology.
2. Tutor students on computer basics, as well as in their academic subjects free of charge.

Methods : 
How : The center will offer several services such as low-cost computer rental on word processing, computer software, internet, and printing. Free tutorials from our student volunteers, will help students with computer and internet literacy. We will also offer free tutorials from our volunteer faculty members in various academic subjects. 

When : With the approval of the Dean of the College of Engineering and U.P. Administration, and the availability of startup funds, we hope to open the center on the second semester of school year 2001-2002.

Where : The center will be established at the College of Engineering. 

Our Staffing and Administration

DLRC Core Group :

DLRC Director : 
Dr. Lydia R. Arcellana

DLRC Faculty Coordinator : 
Prof. Jaime Hernandez Jr. 

Guidance Counselors : 
Claribel Amihan
Rosalinda de Mesa

U.P. Faculty Volunteers :
Ronald John Galiza
Kristine Joy Carpio
Giovanni Tapang
Khrisna Alcantara

Staff : 
Mary Jane V. Cenas

Student Assistants :
Rich de Guzman
Melinda Mejia
Sarah Jane Mandaing
Marizelle Ramos

Volunteer Student Leaders : 
Boni Lou Arensoi
Frederick Ryan Domingo

Evaluation Procedures
We have plans to implement the following evaluation procedure : 
1. The DLRC Core Group will meet quarterly to review the following :
A. Financial records, time sheets, expenses, and pricing models to determine if we are charging the least amount for our services to students. 
B. Random surveys administered to students to determine the effectiveness of our tutoring program.
2. If required by the U.P. administration or major donor, an independent audit will be conducted and the results made available to any concerned party. 

Communication

We will make available a semi-annual report detailing our progress, as well as an annual report from the DLRC director. 

Organizational Information
The Diliman Learning Resource Center (DLRC) is directly under the Office of the Vice-Chancellor for Student Affairs (OVCSA). The DLRC exists with the following purpose and objectives : 
Purpose Statement :
To promote academic excellence among UP students (particularly freshmen and sophomore students) in a caring and nurturing environment.

Objectives :
- To promote academic excellence by training students to analyze problems, ask right questions, arrive at solutions and become self-reliant
- Encourage students to identify and maximize their potential and thus help them find their niche in society.
- To provide opportunities for the students to discern their values and evaluate these in terms of their benefit to self and society.

DLRC maintains two centers : DLRC1 is situated at the 3rd floor of the National Engineering Center (NEC) building and DLRC2 is at the back of the Kamia Residence Hall. At present, DLRC1 occupies a two bay classroom which is normally used for peer tutorials and as study center for students. The present demands of U.P. education, especially engineering students, require much use of computers and computer applications. This proposal is given in order to enhance the service provided in DLRC1 to U.P. students. 

Our Needs, Your Opportunity
Startup funding inthe amount of 2,000,000.00 pesos. This amount will cover the expenses for 30 computers, tables and chairs, 2 printers, internet equipment, multimedia projector, a copying machine, air-conditioning equipment, computer software and the renovation of a 4 bay classroom (2 bays for the computer classroom & 2 bays for the review center). The breakdown is given in the supplemental information as part of this proposal.

Thank you again for your consideration of this proposal. Should you need more information. please do not hesitate to contact us. 

Our address : 
Diliman Learning Resource Center
Kamia Residence Hall
University of the Philippines
Diliman, Quezon City 1101
Tel. No. 920-5301 loc. 6665
