The College of Mass Communication in cooperation with the U.P. Diliman Office of Initiative for Culture and the Arts is pleased to announce the 3rd U.P. DILIMAN FILM AND VIDEO FESTIVAL this February. The festival is a competition that aims to promote independent film and video production among students from various schools, colleges and universities in the country. Students may submit entries in four categories, namely, NARRATIVE, ANIMATION, EXPERIMENTAL, and DOCUMENTARY. A trophy and cash prize awaits the winner : 1st Prize is P5,000; 2nd Prize is P3,000 and 3rd Prize is P2,000.
In this connection, we would like to solicit your help in encouraging your students to join this festival. Entries may be in 16mm film or VHS Video formats not exceeding 45 minutes in running time and must have been produced not earlier than February 1, 2000. The deadline for submission of entries is on February 19, 2001 at 5:30 pm at the Film and AVC Department of the UP College of Mass Communication. For further details please call PLDT # 920-6863 or UP Trunkline 920-5301 up to 99 local 4639 and look for Fortune. 
Enclosed are the poster, contest rules, and entry forms. The entry forms may be photocopied should you run out of it. 
Thank you very much.

Very truly yours, 

DR. GIGI JAVIER ALFONSO
Chair, Organizing Committee
3rd UP Diliman Film and Video Festival

The 3rd UP Diliman Film and Video Festival Competition Mechanics
1. Entries must be student works completed not earlier than February 1, 2000.
2. Entries must have been completed while the filmmaker/video director was enrolled as a student in their respective schools and universities.
3. Entries should be in the following formats : 
FILM : 16mm
VIDEO : VHS
4. Running time for all entries must not exceed 45 minutes. 
5. All works must strictly be independent works and not produced for commercial screening or commercial circulation. 
6. Each participant is allowed to submit only one (1) entry in each category.
7. Each entry can only be submitted in one (1) category.
8. Each entry reel or tape must contain only one (1) title. 
9. Categories are the following :
Narrative (Short Feature)
Experimental
Animation
Documentary
10. Entries must include the following :
a. Duly accomplished entry form (original or a facsimile/xerox). Include the following information :
Title of film/video work
Date Production Started
Date Production Completed
Name of school and College or department of origin
Producer (s)
Scroptwriter (s)
Editor (s)
Production Designer (s)
Other production staff
Competition Category
Film or Video
Film or Video Format (see #9)
Sound Format (Magnetic, Optical or silent in case of Film entry)
Running Time or duration of film/video work (in case of Film entry please include number of film reels) 
Short synopsis of film/video work
Complete address of filmmaker or group rep
Conforme (signed by person submitting)
Conforme (signed by owner of film/video rights) 
b. Synopsis of entry
c. One (1) 5x7 inches (5R) or 3x5 inches (3R) production stills of the entry. 
11. Awards for the winning entries shall be given to the filmmaker/video director indicated in the entry form. For joint directorial works, the group must assign a single representative to act on its behalf. All official communications shall be addressed to this representative. The Organizers shall not be liable for any controversy regarding the sharing of awards among the members of the group. 
12. The decision of the Board of Judges is final. The Board of Judges reserves the right not to give awards in any category should no entry merit it. In the case of ambivalence, the jurors shall decide the category of the entry. 
13. Cash prizes for the categories NARRATIVE, EXPERIMENTAL, ANIMATION and DOCUMENTARY are as follows :
1st Prize : P5,000.00 plus trophy
2nd Prize : 3,000.00 plus trophy
3rd Prize : 2,000.00 plus trophy
14. Entries must be submitted not later than 5:30 PM FEBRUARY 19, 2001 at the Film and AVC Department of the College of Mass Communication.
15. Winners shall be notified by mail or telephone. All winners are enjoined to be at the awards ceremonies ON February 28, 2001 at 2PM at the UP-CMC Auditorium.
16. The UP-OICA and UP-CMC reserves the right to exhibit the entries, winning or not, during the festival week. The UP-OICA and UP-CMC also reserves the right not to screen entries it deems unsuitable for public screening. 
17. Winning entries shall be archived and filed by the UP-CMC Film and AVC Department in its collection/library.
18. Entries may be picked up starting March 30, 2001.

Diliman, Quezon City

Dear Dean Atanacio,

We would like to inform you about the sentiment of the faculty members of the MMME Department in connection with the request of the U.P. Engineering Society to put up a tambayan along the corridor wall which is between the IE Department and the MMME Department. 

In a consultation with the Department faculty members during our last regualr meeting dated December 13, 2000, they unanimously voted against putting up a tambayan by any organization in that area. One of the main reasons cited is the anticipated increase in the noise level reaching our Department which they believe will deleteriously affect the efficient performance of their jobs such as course preparation work considering that our Department is not sound-proof. They also point out that tambayans along the corridor make the area congested and narrows the space intended for people to walk through, especially during the times that a lot of students congregate there. A safety issue was also cited by Dr. Mena. 

We understand the dilemma of the U.P. Engineering Society, however, in view of the reasons cited, we are not in favor of their choice of location for their new tambayan. We therefore propose that a more appropriate area be located for their transfer. To quote one of my staff, lobbies and corners are okay for the purpose but not corridors. 

Thank you very much.

Very truly yours, 

ALBERTO V. AMORSOLO, JR., Ph.D.
Chairman, MMME Department

Dear Dean Atanacio : 

This is in connection with the Philippine Engineering Journal (PEJ), which is a published by the U.P. Engineering Research and Development Foundation, Inc. (UPERDFI) for the National Engineering Center. I recently was informed that the UPERDFI reduced the amount of it direct assistance for the publication of the PEJ from P120,000.00 to P60,000.00 effective 1999. Unfortunately, this amount of financial assistance is not sufficient to enable to the PEJ to come out with two issues per year. While in the last year there has been in delay in the release of the PEJ, this has mainly been due to the slow rate at which the PEJ receives articles, which I mentioned on a number of occasions to the College Executive Board. We have been able to solve this problem by more aggressively seeking out potential contributors of articles while still maintaining the quality the articles included in the PEJ. I also foresee that this will not be a problem in the near future, given the new university guidelines on faculty promotions that have publication requirements. In addition, we have also undertaken steps to streamlining the typesetting and proof reading process such that a given issue of the PEJ can be made ready for distribution within a month after all articles have been received. 

Because of the abovementioned efforts, we have been able address the delay in the release of issues of the PEJ. We recently released the December 1999 that was charged versus the UPERDFI's direct assistance for 2000. The June 2000 issue is already at the printers, with the December 2000 issue to follow shortly. However, the balance of 2000 direct assistance provided by the UPERDFI is not sufficient to cover the publication cost of these two issues since each issue costs approximately P40,000.00 to publish. The foundation has advised us to charge these issues versus our 2001 allocation, which will be in sufficient to publish the two issues. At this point, we anticipate that we will be able to release both the June and December 2001 on schedule as we already have articles for these two issues. 

For this reason, I would like to ask your assistance in requesting the foundation for additional financial assistance in the amount of P100,000.00 to cover the costs publication of the next four issues of the PEJ (June and December 2000, and June and December 2001). This amount is over and above the P60,000.00 in direct assistance the UPERDFI has already allotted for the PEJ.

May I also request from the UPERDFI, through your representation, that the level of direct assistance be restored to P120,000.00 per annum, in enable the PEJ to release two issues of the journal per year. 

Hoping for your favorable response to this request. 

Sincerely, 

Dr. Mark H. Zarco,
Faculty-in-charge, PEIS

Excerpt from the Minutes of the 1146th Meeting of the Board of Regents held on 20 October 2000

VI. POLICY MATTERS FOR APPROVAL OF THE BOARD  

F. Proposal to Establish the U.P. Diliman Center for International Studies

Overview

This report proposes the creation of a Center for International Studies in U.P. Diliman that will bring together existing area and international studie at the Asian Center (AC), College of Arts and Letters (CAL), College of Social Sciences and Philosophy (CSSP), and other units with related offerings. The goal of the Center is to promote reserach on the cultures and the interrelatedness of the social and economic systems of countries in Asia, America, and Europe. In the future, depending on the need and faculty resources, the Center may offer degree programs at the undergraduate and graduate levels. 

Rationale

An international studies program is important for its cotribution to the wealth of knowledge and to the development of well-rounded, cosmopolitan citizens. Area studies imply intimate knowledge of a given country's or region's cultural foundations arising from basic research in the history and culture, social movements, civil society, foreign relations, and migration patterns of the world's major civilizations. 

Moreover, area studies help advance national development goals. The conduct of Philippine foreign policy, for instance, benefits a great deal from the availability of a large pool of university-based specialists who can provide expert analysis in various areas of concern (e.g., culture, politics, economics, science and technology) about individual countries or a regional grouping of countries.

The Philippines is increasingly being integrated with the rest of the world not only economically but also socially, politically and culturally. Such integration, however, is not always smooth and to enable this ongoing transformation to serve development goals. It is important to have a good understanding of the determinants, processes, and consequences of rapid globalization. To do this, teaching and research in international studies, whether basic or policy oriented, undertaken by a core of country and subject specialists, are indispensable.

U.P. Diliman is well placed to provide the required teaching and research, not necessarily by creating new programs, but by integrating and strengthening existing area and international studies programs in the Diliman campus. These programs have had a long history. At present, however, they are scattered in various units and departments, resulting in high overhead costs and low synergy. A little introspection will show that n amalgamation can lead to scale economies in the use of faculty time and financial resources and to more focused academic programs.

As globalization proceeds, economic and technical cooperation between developed and developing countries, anchored on human capital buildup and technology transfers, gathers strength. In this regard, the developed countries have seen it fit to assist the developing ones in capacity enhancement through support education, training and research. U.P. Diliman will be in a strong position to attract some funds for the purpose, which are in the form of official development assistance and grants from private foundations, if it has in place a world-class international studies program. 

Aim/Vision

The Center for International Studies shall be a leading source of knowledge of the countries of the world, to enable the Filipino to gain this knowledge and meaningfully relate to the outside world. 

Objectives

1. To conduct multi/interdisciplinary cross-cultural research. 
2. To become a resource center for information on different countries. 
3. To grant degrees on studies of specific countries in order to train and develop experts/specialists on countries of the world. 
The offering of degree programs is not foreseen in the immediate future. Nevertheless, the institution of any degree program shall follow the procedure prescribed by the University. 

Research Agenda
The various fields of specialization of affiliate faculty members define the research agenda. Faculty research is mandatory. Research work has, at the minimum, a credit load equivalent to three units of teaching every semester. 
As a further incentive to research, a fund in support of academic research must be established at the Center. The fund should be sufficiently large to finance local research expenses of the faculty, including a reasonable honorarium. 

The incentive structure is designed to encourage publication in professional journals, both here and abroad. Invitation to join the Center and subsequent promotion rest heavily on the publication record of the faculty. 

Organization
Providing overall policy direction to the Center is the Coordinating Council, the chairperson of which is the Diliman Chancellor. The Council shall be composed of the deans of the College of Arts and Letters, College of Social Sciences and Philosophy, Asian Center, School of Economics and other units closely affiliated with the concerns of the Center.

It must be emphasized that the Center will not have its own complement of permanent faculty members. Rather, the Center will be open to faculty affiliates who will be assigned or detailed to the Center on a temporary or part-time basis. The idea is for the faculty to maintain their own disciplinal identity and to be assigned to the Center on a temporary basis. 

A director shall head the Center. S/He shall be assisted by coordinators, the number of which will depend on the number of area studies that are ready to be implemented. In the immediate term, for example, coordinators for a Southeast Asian Studies Program and a European Studies Program will be designated. 

Organization

Providing overall policy direction to the Center is the Coordinating Council, the chairperson of which is the Diliman Chancellor. The Council shall be composed of the deans of the College of Arts and Letters, College of Social Sciences and Philosophy, Arts and Letters, School of Economics and other units closely affiliated with the concerns of the Center. 

It must be emphasized that the Center will not have its own complement of permanent faculty members. Rather the Center will be open to faculty affiliates who will be assigned or detailed to the Center on a temporary or part-time basis. The idea is for the faculty to maintain their own disciplinal identity and to be assigned to the Center on a temporary basis. 

A director shall head the Center. S/He shall be assisted by coordinators, the number of which will depend on the number of area studies that are ready to be implemented. In the immediate term, for example, coordinators for a Southeast Asian Studies Program and a European Studies Program will be designated. 

Budgetary Requirements
U.P. Diliman shall source the funding requirements of the Center from its own budget. 

In estimating the budgetary requirements, the following guidelines will be observed:

1. Personal services

Charges against this item shall include th following :
- honoraria and RATA of faculty administrators
- salaries and honoraria of substitutes of faculty members who will be detailed to the Center
- honoraria of faculty doing work for the Center

2. MOOE

Charges against this item shall include, among others, utilities expenses, security and janitorial services, office rent, etc.

(Please see Appendix 1 for the full text of the Proposal and an estimate of budgetary requirements for the first five years.)

Board action : Approval

CERTIFIED CORRECT : 

MARTIN V. GREGORIO
Secretary of the University
and of the Board of Regents

column9: An International Studies Agenda for UPD
column10:
Introduction
The following report represents the fruition of an "idea whose time has come" : the integration, rationalization and coordination of area/international studies in UP Diliman. Its analyses and recommendations are made with the end in view of putting UPD at par with other prestigious universities in Asia and the world through the institutionalization of area/international studies programs as a distinct field of intellectual endeavor. 

It is an incontrovertible fact that the University is the pioneer in the Philippines in the field of area studies (i.e. Asian Studies) : in 1955 was created the Institute of Asian Studies, a non-degree granting establishment for research and which 1958 was "upgraded" into the Asian Center. But with the proliferation of other area-studies programs (e.g. French, or European, the latter being under the wing of the UCIDS), a certain tendency to stagnate has set in. The relevance of certain courses, if not of whole programs, is sporadically under question. There is no coordination among the various programs existing or proposed to be put up. Not least, the problem of the acceptability of area-studies has lingered in the minds of a number of faculty members in the "established" disciplines. Thus the full potential of area-studies is not tapped by the university which was among the first in Asia to venture in that field. 

Yet the age of globalization has brought great winds of change upon the national community, and with it comes the urgent challenge for UPD to be regionally and internationally relevant. This does not mean that we should adopt area/international studies programs indiscriminately and uncritically just to keep up with current trends, but it means that UPD should make efficient use of its bountiful multidisciplinary faculty resources, and further develop country experts to make it a center for the learning of culture, society, history, economic activity and political processes of, at least initially, its immediate neighbors. Well-developed and productive area/international studies centers are hallmarks of the great modern university; no nation with aspirations to greatness can afford to be ignorant of its partners in either trade or simple diplomatic relations. Indeed, it has been rightly said that one gains better knowledge of one's own society after intimate contact with a foreign one. 

The best time to embark on this project is now, with the new millennium's psychological and ideological climates auguring a more open atmosphere for this field of learning than ever before. It will not be effortless, to be sure, and "disciplinal inertia" plus budgetary constraints will create an impression of resistance. But there is growing local demand for expertise in country-or area studies, and several foreign governments and foundations are only waiting to be asked to help set up appropriate programs. UPD should realize that it has so much yet-untapped intellectual resources that could make area international studies one of its major offerings. 

I. Status Report
A. Existing courses/programs :
1. Disciplinal based
1.1. Comparative literature courses - CAL
These include courses on European as well as Asian literature
1.2. Master in International Studies - Political Science, CSSP. This degree is more oriented towards international relations. 
1.3. Courses on features of various countries in the departments of History, Geography, Linguistics, Political Science, Psychology, Philosophy and Demography - CSSP 
1.4. Art Studies : Asian and Chinese - CAL
1.5 Economics in Asia - School of Economics
1.6 Language structures of Japanese, Chinese, and Bahasa in Indonesia - Linguistics, CSSP

2. Area based
2.1 MA Asian Studies (China, Japan, Korea) - Asian Center

3. Language based

3.1. B.A. in different European languages - CAL
3.2. B.A. Linguistics, Plan C : Linguistics at Wikang East Asia (Japanese/Chinese/Korean) - CSSP

B. Existing research/data base building/networking

1. Film : CMC
2. Gender : SOLAIR and Women's Study Center
3. Music : CM
4. Public policy : NCPAG
5. International relations and security : Area and Strategic Studies : Japan Program - Law Center
6. Earthquake engineering (Japan) : College of Architecture

C. Initiatives and Programs
1. Area Studies
a. Area Studies Council - 1994
This council was set up afer several meetings and a workshop conducted in previous years. It consisted of the VC for Academic Affairs, the VC for Student Affairs, Director of the Office of Instruction, the deans and directors of CSSP, CAL, AC, ILS, SURP, CPAD, SE and 2 other representative of CSSP and AC. 
The duties of the council were to : 
1. advise the Chancellor on matters pertaining to Area Studies; 
2. develop an Area Studies program;
3. coordinate studies and research;
4. collect and retrieve knowledge to enrich area studies
b. The documents show that Area Studies :
1. 'encompasses studies, researches and expertise in the language, society, culture, particular country and/or region. The systematic learning of the language in its cultural context will be given priority";
2. "aims to develop expertise in area studies for the development of basic knowledge, the formulation of national policy and the creation of an enlightened and patriotic citizenry"; 
3. intends to conduct "the training of selected scholars";
4. intends to study cultural areas - (this was not explained fully).
c. European Studies
1. CAL - Proposed a European Studies minor for the existing BA European Languages (for both Plan A and Plan B) "to provide European language students an opportunity to know about the continents, cultural, social and political diversity" and...Produce students proficient in at least one European language other than English...conversant with the culture and political and social institutions of not just one European country, but of the entire continent as well..." Required number of courses will be 27 of the total 141 units required for BA European Languages Program as follows : 

6 units Comparative Literature
3 units Art Studies
3 units History (Kas 102)
3 units Geography (Geo 151)
9 units Political Science
3 units Music or Fine Arts

2. CSSP - Plans are underway for a "...multidisciplinary approach to the study of social/human problems, and complementary to existing special area/topic, degree, research, training, extension programs and course offerings in various Units of the University,... of area coverage (North/East/South/West/Central European countries) and a comparative perspective - with particular reference to the Philippines and Asia..."

- Special projects : Research and resource collection - research on the influence of Europe upon the Philippines and comparative studies of European and other countries. Initial plan - Country Profiles Project "...projects require joint participation among at least two European Union countries and two developing countries..."

d. Southeast Asian Studies
1. CSSP - proposal under study
2. Linkage with Southeast Asian Studies Regional Exchange Program (SEASREP)

A number of the faculty have collaborated or benefited from one or more of its components which are : 

Teaching SEAS at the undergraduate level
Post-graduate training
Language training
Staff development
Collaborative research
Conferences
Publications
Institutional development
Library resource development
Administrative networking

3. Chinese Studies
Proposals were made for : 
1. Chinese Studies Resource Office - "...to develop and promote 1) research and publication, 2) library resources, 3) intra- and inter-university linkages and exchanges pertinent to the mutual understanding of the Filipinos and Chinese peoples..."
(This was one of the recommendations of the conference/workshop held on October 25-26, 1993, sponsored by the Chiang Ching-Kuo (CCK) Foundation). 
2. Task Force on Chinese Studies (1998) - reiterated the establishment of a Chinese Studies Resource Office "to enable the UP to institutionalize a framework of bringing together on an organized, regular and permanent basis, scholars, academic and professionals in the field of Chinese Studies from various disciplines." It proposed setting up "a core group composed of research fellows, an advisory panel and a physical office".

f. Korean Studies 

A Korean Studies committee was formed in 1995 which held an Interaction Conference between the College of Arts and Letters and Han Nam University sponsored by the Korea Foundation.
In a letter to Dr. A. T. Torres then VCAA, the Vice President of Korea Foundation Mr. Young-Mo expressed their intention to support a teacher for the Korean language and the hope that courses on Korean culture, history and literature would be offered in the future. He mentioned the Foundation's program which included grants for institutional efforts to develop Korean Studies in terms of visiting professors, fellowships and book and reference materials. 
A few members of the faculty have gone to Han Nam University. This activity did not prosper because the program was for undergraduates, was too short for gaining knowledge, and the support given was not adequate. 

g. Japanese Studies

The documents show that the proposed BA Japan Studies was meant "to develop an understanding of Japanese society and culture as part of the liberal education orientation of the University and as a contribution to the human resource requirements of a globalizing world." After a long period of discussions, and meetings of successive committees which were established to study the viability of the program, it was decided to propose 2 tracks (A and B) to be managed by CSSP and CAL.

Required number of units : 147

Track A
Courses
GE courses - 42
mandated course (PI 100) - 3
language courses - 36
core courses (required for both tracks) - 33
major courses - 18
Pre-requisites - 6
electives - 9
                 147
Track B - Arts and Letters
GE courses - 42
mandated course (PI 100) - 3
Language courses - 36 
core courses - 33
major courses - 33
elective -3 
                    150

The curricula were discussed at a 3/147 UC Special Meeting on 2.26.99. The decision was to reconvene the committee to study the newly instituted courses for Japanese in the Department of Linguistics and make the necessary changes in the proposed Japanese Studies curriculum. 

Presently, both proposals track A and B with the necessary changes have been passed by the respective College Assemblies and resubmitted to the VCAA Office. 

h. American Studies

A Committee on American Studies proposed a BA American Studies curriculum in 1996. The program "...aims to provide basic knowledge of the United States of America as a nation, to enable the students to interpret trends in American society, politics, economy, industry, and technology. It also aims to develop an appreciation of American literature and the arts and to broaden the perspective of students on regional and global affairs supportive of Philippine nation building."

The committee last met in November 1996 to discuss the curriculum and request the members representing their various colleges to propose to their respective units, the possibility of offering the new courses listed in the curriculum. The proposal stipulated a total of 141 units, with 42 GE courses, PI 100, 33 core courses (18 to be instituted), and 63 other courses in American Studies (18 to be instituted). 

II. Proposed General Framework for Strengthening International Studies 
As shown by the preceding status report on existing and planned efforts towards building expertise and amassing knowledge of other countries, UPD has still a lot of ground to cover. 
The greatest effort taken to date is initiating discussions on an Area Studies Program. Apparently one reason for the failure in implementing the program can be the indecision on the scope of the 'area' concept. 
Area Studies implies the study of a group or groups of countries which are in some way identified together either geographically, linguistically, culturally, economically or by religion. Also, such studies could proceed on a disciplinal approach. Therefore studies along these lines would necessarily be comparative in nature and would demand experts on the countries involved. 
The documents show that the program aims, among other things to "...develop expertise in Area Studies for the development of basic knowledge." This contradicts the notion of area studies, if this means basic knowledge of the people and culture of the specific countries of the area. 
The discussion/proposal did not clearly spell out what was meant by 'cultural area'. It seems that this has to evolve out of the commonalities of the countries to be studies as an area. 
The other proposals/initiatives except that of Japan Studies are still in the initial stages of planning. 
The BA Japan Studies proposal is so far nearest the stage of implementation and this is the greatest and most immediate problem right now. Although the CSSP and CAL are supposed to take care of the admission to and graduation of students, no unit/body has been given the responsibility of coordinating the tracks. This problem will be compounded should the other proposals (Chinese studies, Korean studies, etc.) go for establishing a degree especially since these proposals are multi/interdisciplinary. It is necessary therefore to come up with a general framework for strenghthening and coordinating studies of other countries and the areas into which they cluster.

column9: (2000, December 4)
column10:
Edgardo Atanacio
Dean 
College of Engineering

Dear Sir : 

Greetings!

This is to file an official complaint against Mr. Donato Dela Cruz, our professor in ChE 133, Process Equipment Design I. Until now we have yet to receive our final grade for that subject. There was only a sheet of paper in which he signed his name and listed the names of those that passed. 

Listed below are instances, which serves as grounds for our complaint :

1. No classcards yet. 
2. Delayed posting of the second part of the list of those that passed. Some of us could have finished the enrollment on the first day if not for that. We had to go back for three days to find out the results to no avail. 
3. Left us hanging in the middle of the semester, since he had to work. We were supposed to have make-up classes on Saturdays but there he would never show up for any reason at all. There was no notice that we won't be having classes. 
4. Excessive absences. 
5. He crammed the fourth exam into a three-hour lecture, which ended late at night, and to think he would still arrive late. The fourth exam was just 2 days away. 
6. He moved the exam to a later date to October 20 when the last day of finals was supposed to be October 19. That was the time when there was no electricity, and the rooms were dark. Also he arrived about an hour late, and he still had not photocopied the exams, which was a total waste of time. 
7. Always arrives late on the day of the exams or make-up classes. 
8. Always goes back on his word : 
a) He told the class that we will be having just 3 exams since he was going on leave, then changed his mind and changed it back to 4.
b) We waited for our class standings/exemption list for the finals but he never showed up and said some time later, that no one was exempted from the finals. 
c) The second part of the list of students that passed, in which he said a particular date, but it never came. Then he said tomorrow, and we waited, and there was still no list. It came three days later. 

We hope this letter will reach you to shed more light upon the nature of our complaint. Your utmost cooperation and attention will be highly appreciated. 

Sincerely yours, 

Jayme Maniti

Isabella Yap

Sarah Jane Legaspi

Dear Sir : 

The CIAP shall launch its Action Agenda and present its proposed specific action plans for each action agenda item in pursuit of the Construction Industry Strategic Plan for the 21st Century (CI21) on December 04, 2000, 1:00 p.m. at the Makati Shangri-La, Ayala Avenue, Makati City. 

The CI21 is a framework of the orderly and accelerated growth of construction based on the shared industry vision of "Global players in a global environment." It was formulated in mid-1998 through a series of consultation meetings with representatives from various industry associations to set the long-term direction for all concerned sectors. The intent of CI21 is for CIAP, government agencies involved in construction, and industry associations, to use it as the basis for drawing up their own Action Agenda an/or plans and programs for the specific construction industry sectors within their areas of concern. 

Based on the CI21 and the CIAP mandate, the CIAP Action Agenda and proposed specific action plans seek to address the following three (3) major challenges : 
- Upgrading of Construction Capabilities and Resources;
- Enhancing Quality and Efficiency; and
- Ensuring Professionalism and Social Responsibility

The proposed strategies and action points will be presented during the launching to government and private participants from the various sectors of the construction industry. The participants shall be given time after the launching to comment on the proposed CIAP Action Agenda and to rank each item according to urgency and importance. These will be consolidated for discussion and finalization in a workshop to be held in January of next year. A plenary session in February 2001 is also set for the signing of agreements on specific responsibilities and commitments under the CIAP Action Agenda and Specific Action Plans. 

In this regard, we are pleased to invite you to grace the occasion as one of our special guests as well as one (1) officer/member from your agency to participate and/or represent your office in the forthcoming workshops to discuss the CIAP Action Agenda on Upgrading of Construction Capabilities. 

For confirmation and other queries, please don't hesitate to call us at Tel. Nos. 896-18-31, 896-18-33 and Fax No. 896-45-69.

We look forward to your presence and participation. 

Very truly yours,

KATHRYN JOSEPHINE T. DELA CRUZ
Officer-In-Charge

Abarro, Jr. could be given the credit in improving the Wood Wool Cement Bonded Board construction material, (DOST-Los Banos, introduced the material thru its Transfer Technology Program) by re-discovering the weed PALO VERDE (Scientifically known as Piper Arborecens) and using its excelsior as one of the main components/ingredients in producing Wood Wool Cement Bonded Board which his firm G.C. Enterprises is producing and marketing under the name ZAMBOARD (featured in 1996 Land Bank of the Philippines calendar).

This product Zamboard was submitted and tested by DOST Los Banos, Laguna (refer to xeroxed letter-result sent by Mr. Arturo A. Pablo, Chief, Composite Products Development, Forest Products Research and Development Institute, DOST, College, Laguna dated 24 May 1995 to Mr. Carlos A. Abarro, Jr.) and accredited by AITECH, Housing and Urban Development Coordinating Council, Makati Avenue, Makati City (refer xeroxed copy Inter-Agency Committee Accreditation Certificate No. 95-005 / R1 dated 24 March 1997 signed by Chairman, Dionisio Dela Serna) and likewise, found to be one of the better Wood Wool Cement Bonded Boards available in the market today. 

Zamboard is an alternative construction material that is economical and practical in low-cost and marginalized housing. Thru, innovative design, use and construction the budget conscious residents of Zamboanga City, the neighboring provinces and cities in Region IX are benefitting by using Zamboard in their house renovation, constructing low-budget houses as well as medium-cost structures and buildings.

Abarro III (Dean, College of Architecture, Western Mindanao State University, Normal Road, Zamboanga City) his elder brother, had designed, used and experimented extensively thru actual projects the construction material, Zamboard and thus, proving the material to be very promising, which could be used 80 to 90 per cent in structure components specially in low-cost housing projects as well as medium to high budget cost structure (refer to his paper, entitled : Zamboard, a very promising construction material, presented in November 1996, @The  4th International Conference for Civil Engineering, Diamond Hotel, M.M.

Zamboard 
A VERY PROMISING CONSTRUCTION MATERIAL

ARCHITECT DOMINGO A. ABARRO III

ABSTRACT

This paper is about Zamboard, a very promising alternative construction material which is made of 60% cement and 40% Palo Verde Weed mixed with suitable accelerator into two feet by eight feet panels, thickness varying from 8 mm to 50 mm. A transfer technology from DOST at Los Banos, the author in coordination with G.C. Enterprises (manufacturer) has a first information in experimenting with the product and putting its inherent characteristics, uses and other special features into actual use in search for a lower costing material for low cost and marginalized housing. Principles are discussed and introduced to show the real potential of the product and its eventual use for the benefit of the construction industry and its ultimate beneficiary, the house owner. 

1. INTRODUCTION

As the millennium fast approaches the 21st century, the population of the Philippines as well as that of the world has increased tremendously. Such is likewise the case, of the need for more of one of the basic necessities of man, SHELTER. More so when we look at the great disparity between the rich and poor wherein the latter is found wanting of the very basic need, coupled with the scarcity of raw and inexpensive materials caused by misuse, wanton destruction of our natural environment in the guise of so called "modern development" introduced by uncaring, ignorant, irresponsible, and materialistic, profit-oriented impostors pretending to be the real professionals. 

Thus, a search for an alternative construction material that would meet the following criteria :

1.1 Ease of production, less expensive (than existing ones) but possesses far superior qualities and characteristics. 
1.2 Uses waste materials that are abundant. 
1.3 Environment friendly
1.4 User friendly with existing tools and equipment and methods of construction
1.5 Flexibility in terms of use and application.
1.6 Long-lasting / stand the abuse of time and the natural elements. 
1.7 Best used for low-cost and marginalized housing but with expensive home applications through innovative, creative designs and construction methods.

Alas, Zamboanga Boards (Zamboard for short) is it!

2. ZAMBOARD (ZAMBOANGA CEMENT BONDED BOARD) DEFINED

Zamboard is made of shredded wood fibers of excelsior of Palo Verde (Scientifically known as Piper Arborecens, which grows well in lowlands as well as uplands as Zamboanga Peninsula with elevation up to 600 meters above sea level. It grows rapidly and adaptable to a wide range of soil condition, ease in plantation establishment and suitable to various and uses are salient characteristics of this weed) bonded with ordinary cement, water and a suitable cement accelerator, cold pressed under low pressure and ambient temperature. Its surface dimensions 610 millimeter wide by 2440 millimeter long and in various thickness from 8 millimeter to 50 millimeter.

3. HISTORICAL DEVELOPMENT

Wood cement composite product have been known in Europe for so many years. The Heraklith Company of Austria claims to be the original patentee of the so-called wood wool building slab. The company obtained its first patent of the material as early as 1808. 

In Germany, the first wood wool products was introduced in the market in 1921. It had wood wool from spruce, a member of the pine family as its main raw material and soil cement, its main binder. 

Wood wool industries have been well established in many European countries from many years. In Britain, the wood wool buildings slab industry was already established long before the second world war. The slabs were used mainly as structural insulating roof decking materials. Years later other countries like Netherlands, France, and Switzerland develop the product and were marketed successfully over the world as DURAPANEL. before the second world war. The slabs were used mainly as structural insulating roof decking materials. Years later other countries like Netherlands, France and Switzerland develop the product and were marketed successfully over the world as DURAPANEL.

In Asia in the early sixties, countries like Australia, Japan, Malaysia, and Singapore have already adopted the manufacture of the board until they shifted into high grade finished boards. 

Wood wool or cement bonded board was introduced in the Philippines druing the 1980's through the Department of Science and Technology (DOST) at Los Banos, Laguna which introduced the technology and the resulting product to some manufacturers in Luzon using rice stalks, rattan shavings, saw dusts and rice husks as raw materials with a short-lived success due to unavailability of the mentioned raw materials in volume quantity. My brother, Carlos Anselmo A. Abarro, Jr., a graduate of U.P. College of Agriculture at Los Banos, was introduced to the technology and product when he mated to his Alma Mater in seach for new business opportunitiesa after the family logging TLA (timber license agreement) expired and with a Lease Agreement for 500 hectares of logged-over area for Agro-industrial ventures good for 25 years. He readily accepted the technology transfer in 1995 and started searching for the raw material that would suit and give the desired qualities of a wood wool board. In the process of searching he re-discovered the great potential of the weed Palo Verde which abounds and rapidly grows in our logged-over area. Produced in our factory situated at Pamacutan, Zamboanga City, tested and tried in the laboratories of DOST at Los Banos (refer to MECHANICAL PROPERTIES OF ZAMBOARD) our new product was born and renamed Zamboanga Board or just Zamboard. 

4. MATERIAL COMPONENTS

The main components of this product is wood and cement. Wood is lightweight and can be easily machined while cement is incombustible, rigid when cured, resistant to rot, fungi and is highly resistant to dimensional changes when exposed to humidity. 

Any wood materials except hard wood can be a potential raw materials for the product. The latest discovered species of weed which has a high potential value is the Palo Verde of scientifically known as Piper Arborecens. This small evergreen tree abound in Zamboanga City particularly in Barangay Pamacutan, Sinubong, Patalon, Labuan, Limpapa, La Paz, Baluno, and Bunguiao, and it is now being utilized for the manufacture of ZAMBOARD as one of it's main ingredients, it is produced commercially in Zamboanga City by the Abarro's G.C. Enterprises and the first manufacturer to use the Palo Verde weed.

6. CHARACTERISTICS AND USES

Zamboard has a desirable characteristic, relatively lightweight, water resistant, fire resistant and decay resistant. It is durable enough to withstand adverse water conditions such as typhoon, earthquakes and heavy rains, thermal insulating acoustic properties. It can be saved, to the high humidity, the board can be used as exterior walling paneling, and as partitions in rooms with high relative humidity.

7. CONSTRUCTION APPLICATIONS 

Having the rare privilege to design and make use of our new product, Zamboard, I was able to discover that it is a promising alternative construction material to existing ones such as concrete hollow block (for exterior and interior walling) plywood (for interior, exterior sidings, ceiling, eaves, fascia as well as parapet walls a furniture) and more importantly scarce wood. 

Taking advantage of its inherent, positive physical characteristics (termites free fire retardant water resistant, earthquake resilient, thermal insulator, acoustic properties and rot/fungi/vermin proof) and other desirable features such as nailable, sawable (using ordinary carpenter's saw), gluable, plasterable and sanderable. The designer can come up with new innovative and creative design and construction methods that can lower the cost of existing low and marginalized cost of housing at the least. In bringing down the cost of low and marginalized shelters the designer/builder should be working on 3 very important areas : 

7.1. Innovative Design

7.1.1. Principles of Duality as well as multi-purpose use of areas and spaces come into play (i.e. Sala by day, dining area during meal and sleeping area by bedtime)
7.1.2. Maximum use of all available spaces i.e. Added useable area due to conversion of dropped ceiling to attics. 
7.1.3. Down scaling of house components without violating existing rules and regulations with respect to safely and comfort (i.e. Regular stairs to spiral staircase) 
7.1.4. Proper placement of sizing of fenestrations such as doors and windows and ventilation for better light and cross ventilation that would result to energy-saving and cost-cutting (i.e. Less usage of artificial lights and electric fans and the like)

7.2. Use of inexpensive materials with special characteristics and desirable features to the maximum and to designer's advantage which is synonymous to the use of Zamboard (refer to Table I - Recommended Uses of Zamboard as to Density and Thickness and Table II - Comparative Cost Analysis of Zamboard with other materials)

7.2.1 Using 1" thick high dense on 2" thick low dense Zamboard panels for exterior walls (non-load bearing) instead of 4" concrete hollow blocks with properly placed and situated reinforcing bars will cut the cost by a minimum of 15% due to lesser cost of the materials and resulting reduced size of foundation, ease of construction (being panels) not to mention lesser labor cost and savings from having thinner plaster work on surfaces (minimum 1/2", maximum 3/4" in thick plastering), interior surface of 2" thick panels could just be painted without plastering hence having a textured effect in vogue nowadays. Using 1" thick high dense panels will results to using smaller dimension wooden window jambs (from 2"x 4" maximum to 1"x 3" minimum) and door jambs from 2"x 4" maximum to 2" x 3" minimum). 
7.2.2. Zamboard 1" thick high dense for second floor or attics instead of T&G or plywood will also contribute to lower the cost plus if designed creatively the under surface of the panel can serve as the ceiling of the ground floor (considering the textured and acoustic qualities of Zamboard) cutting the cost more (no ore ceiling and ceiling joists).
7.2.3. Thermal/insulator quality and acoustic quality of our material enables us to use attics as second floor for bedrooms even in highly urbanized area (concrete jungle) where heat is a major problem not to mention the added space at a minimum cost. 
7.2.4. Zamboard will cost lesser in the long run for as it ages it will last longer for it is 60% concrete, 40% Palo Verde, termite proof, fire retardant, water resistant, earthquake resistant, rot/fungi/vermin proof thus it can be best use as fascia, interior and exterior walling. 
7.2.5. Rough texture and patterns formed on the surface of Zamboard can save the owner from using expensive textured paints - the surface and it is serves as the texture itself when finished with ordinary latex paint. 
7.2.6. When painted with water proofing paints, it can be used as roofing material as well as tiling (for walls) material for toilets. Used as roof shingles it is ideal for beach houses where rust-free roofing is desired with thermal control coming as a bonus. 
7.2.7. Versatile - can be used with steel, concrete and wood construction
7.2.7.1. As a furniture material, its natural look, plain painted or textured painted is a head turner. 
7.2.7.2 Zamboard is not just for the poor, the more expensive homes can use where its qualities and characteristics can be used to its full advantage (ie. Fascia - water-resistant, vermin/fungi/rot proof, textured ceiling).
7.2.7.3. Zamboard's limitations - not to be used in areas where you want to have a very smooth surface finished as the final finish; it has limited bending angle and a little heavier than plywood of the same thickness and size.
7.3 Innovative Construction Methods 
Low cost houses could be constructed like Lego, using pre-fabricated components such as Zamboard panels with innovative attachments, supports and reparations resulting to faster construction. Principles of quality and multi-purpose come into play-walls become dining tables, bed during the night, flooring as ceiling (underside of panel floor) roofing as walling (attic design) resulting to a truly low-cost shelter for the multitude who need it most. 
8. CONCLUSION
Zamboard or wood wool cement board is one of today's technological development substitute building material to improve traditional low cost housing construction. It is a versatile housing construction material that can be used in dry construtions, as sheathing in timber framed construction, in wet construction as a substitute for concrete hollow block (CHB). The board has many uses, but foremost among these are for walls and ceiling. 
The advantage of Zamboard is that, it is lightweight and can be easily handled, eliminating the need for any special on-site equipment erection and utilization. Transporting is easy too, due to the small size of the panel. It is adaptable to any building design and is totally impervious to termites. Ultimately, at a very much lesser cost considering its inherent qualities and desirable features, one can design and build shelter for the pauper and the king with creativity and innovation.

ALEJANDRO MELCHOR, JR.
Executive Secretary

Secretary Melchor was born in Manila on May 1, 1930. He currently resides in Wack-Wack Subdivision, Mandaluyong, Rizal with his wife, the former Rosario M. Panganiban and his children; Alejandro III, Michael, Elizabeth, and John. 
He completed all his formal schooling in the United States, ie., grade school, high school, and college. He started college as an engineering student at the University of the Philippines. He was subsequently appointed a cadet at the Philippine Military Academy in Baguio. From there he went to the U.S. Naval Academy in Annapolis, Md., where he graduated in 1952. He is also a graduate of various service schools both here and abroad. 
Secretary Melchor's record of government service has included economic planning, development and programming work at the Program Implementation Agency and the Presidential Economic Staff, establishing of our economic information reporting system, review and control of foreign-funded projects, negotiation of various loans with foreign financing institutions, assignment with the Asian Development Bank as temporary alternate Director, service in various special missions abroad, e.g., UNCTAD, bilateral negotations, attache duty in Washington, D.C., and line, staff and command assignments, both ashore and afloat. He was also Director of Studies of the UNDP - financed Transport Survey which culminated in the preparation of feasibility studies for major highway projects for which the World Bank has committed financing. Mr. Melchor has also taught mathematics at Far Eastern University, marine engineering at La Salle College and lectures at the National Defense College, the Philippine Executive Academy, and the U.P. Institute for Economic Development and Research. 
Among Secretary Melchor's awards and decorations are a Presidential Award for his services as Deputy Overall Committee Chairman for the 7-Nations Manila Summit Conference, the Navy Command Badge, the Distinguished Service Star with anahaw leaf cluster, the Bronze Cross, the Military Merit Medal, the Military Commendation Medal, the Administrative "E" Award as an Executive Officer and the Operations "E" Award as a Commanding Officer. 
Prior to his appointment as Executive Secretary on February 8, 1970, Secretary Melchor was concurrently serving as Undersecretary of National Defense and Deputy Director General for Operations, Presidential Economic Staff; Chairman, PES-DND Computer Center; Presidential Action Officer for Foreign Loan-Funded Projects; and member of the Board of the National Power Corporation. He continues to hold the last three positions and serves, in addition as Chairman, Power Development Council; part-time Governor, Development Bank of the Philippines; and Alternate Governor, International Bank for Reconstruction and Development.

Alejandro Melchor, Jr., pp. 243
column10:
Executive director of Asian Development Bank, part-time governor of Development Bank of the Philippines, and chairman-president, Philippine Aerospace Development Corporation. Born in Manila on May 1, 1930; married to Rosario M. Panganiban; three sons and one daughter. 

Early schooling in the United States, from the grade school to high school and naval academy training. U.P. engineering student when he passed examinations for cadet at Philippine Military Academy. Subsequently, he enrolled at the US Naval Academy in Annapolis, Maryland, graduating in 1952. Graduate also of various service schools in US and the Philippines Foreign leader grantee, 1972. 

Extensive record of government service includes economic planning and development with the Program Implementation Agency; later, with Presidential Economic Staff; loan negotiations with foreign banks and financing institutions; special missions abroad for the government; attache duty in Washington, D.C. director of studies of UNDP financed projects. Prior to his appointment as executive secretary in Malacañang, variously undersecretary of national defense and deputy director general of Presidential Economic Staff; chairman, National Computer Center; Presidential acton officer for foreign loans funded projects; and board member, National Power Corporation. Appointed by President Marcos Executive Secretary on February 3, 1970. Among his awards : Navy Command Badge, Distinguished Service Star, Bronze Cross, Military Merit Medal, Military Commendation Medal.

column9: Melchor Hall (College of Engineering)
column10:
The late Col. Alejandro Melchor was one of the most outstanding engineering alumni of the University. In 1925, a year after he received his civil engineering diploma, he was appointed mathematics instructor, first in the College of Liberal Arts and then at the College of Engineering where he rose to assistant professor in 1927 and served as college secretary in 1930. 
Melchor was named head of the department of engineering of the Philippine Military Academy in 1936. In this position, he worked closely with Gen. Douglas MacArthur and Lt. Col. Dwight Eisenhower in preparing the Academy's four-year curriculum. 
He took graduate courses at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology and was later assigned to the Engineering Board at For Belvoir. During the war he served as undersecretary of national defense in the government-in-exile of the Commonwealth. He was a member of the Philippine delegation to the United Nations Conference in San Francisco in 1945. 
Melchor was born in Ibajay, Capiz, on August 9, 1900. He was a member of the U.P. Board of Regents at the time of his death on September 5, 1947.

column3: Philippine Association for Scientific Conservation of Cultural Property, Inc.
column10:
PHILIPPINE ASSOCIATION FOR SCIENTIFIC CONSERVATION OF CULTURAL PROPERTY, INC. 128-13th Avenue, Cubao, Quezon City, Philippines

PASCON is a non-stock and non-profit corporation under the laws of the Republic of the Philippines. 

OBJECTIVES

1. To encourage the conservation of our country's cultural property, both movable and immovable such as important works of art, antiques, artifacts, historical structures, etc.;
2. To promote, develop and disseminate knowledge in cultural conservation;
3. To upgrade the field of conservation science; 
4. To promote and establish linkages among conservators and other individuals doing related work within the country and other parts of the world;
5. To act as a clearing house or coordinating body for the various institutions and individuals engaged in the restoration and preservation of cultural materials; 
6. To collect documents, manuscripts, books and other publications and audio-visual materials on conservation, catalogue them and make them available to the reading public;
7. To publish the PASCON Journal and PASCON Newsletter which shall contain vital information, current issues and studies in the field of conservation; and,
8. To promote greater awareness of the significance and value of preserving our cultural and historical heritage. This will be done by means of public lectures, workshops, symposia, course offerings and published essays.

FUNCTIONS

1. Conduct and support interdisciplinary scientific research as they apply to conservation and to those who work for it.
2. Undertake the publication of scientific, educational and cultural materials.
3. Raise funds from popular support and/or conduct fund-raising activities, projects and campaign programs. 
4. Receive and take possession of, and use the proceeds and income of money deposits, trust funds, stocks, bonds, and other securities of any individual, organization, association, agency, corporation or foundation, domestic or foreign, as set aside for the purpose of the corporation.
5. Fund totally or partially support, any government or private activity, project or program in the field of conservation.
6. Conduct training programs, seminars, workshops, courses and conferences on conservation and related fields, directly or through an established recognized educational institution, school or training organization.
7. Give awards, prizes, certificates, plaques, citations, or scrolls of honor, for distinction, outstanding service, and achievement in conservation and related fields. 

COMPOSITION
1. Professional working in conservation laboratories and museums. 
2. Professionals who are deemed acceptable by reason of some special contribution to conservation science. 
3. Professionals in other fields of discipline who have interest in conservation, e.g. architects, engineers, artists, history researchers, archivists, librarians, etc. 
4. Students taking courses in Museology, Archaeology, History, Anthropology, Arts, Chemistry, Geology, Architecture, and other fields of the natural sciences. 

PASCON shall be responsible for the creation, operations and coordination of the following :
1. Committee on Professional Growth
2. Committee on Trainings
3. Committee on Publications
4. Committee on Special Projects
5. Committee on Finance
6. Committee on Membership

column3: CONSAL NEWSLETTER
column10:
CONSAL NEWSLETTER

Second Announcement on 8th CONSAL
11-14 June 1990
Jakarta, Indonesia

Conference theme : New Challenges in Library Services in the Developing World

The Committee keeps imagining that 8th CONSAL will be carried out sooner than expected. Hectic days are becoming more routine than usual as the moment drew alarmingly closer and closer. Preparation would have seemed to run into a collision course had it not been tackled totally by all the Committee members, especially by the staff of the National Library who volunteered for duty at the Secretariat. 

Office hour has never been enough for doing all the administrative work, even when it has been extended to overtime. And how time does flow. Only a month and a half remains before the D-day. In any case, the Committee is confident that everything will go smoothly as expected. Should there be any deficiencies, however trivial it may be, it is indeed unavoidable for such a gathering as colossal at this one. 

The Committee estimates that about 400 participants will take part during the four-day conference. Naturally, most of the librarians and information specialists will come from countries in the Southeast Asia region, but the list at the Secretariat shows that no less than 20 non-ASEAN registrants will be involved, either as speakers or plain participants. It demonstrates that CONSAL is, a matter of fact, the assembly for librarians to strengthen and increase the already well-established regional cooperation, particularly in Southeast Asia.

The ASEAN librarians have met seven times since it was first held in 1970, to discuss and to share experiences from various perspectives of library matters, incorporating the most "traditional" topic, like cataloging and classification, to complex issues such as online bibliographic cooperation using the computer. The latter, as often encountered and conversed in international forums like CONSAL, is unmistakably growing more and more dominating. And the eighth convention, to be held next June, will not likely eliminate that very question, although information technology will not be the only subject to discuss. 

As it has been indicated in the first 8th CONSAL circular, the Committee looks forward to receiving papers on the strategy to meet new challenges in library services, as well as those on relevant topics within the range of education and training on librarianship, manpower development, and information exchanges. Besides, there is also a topic which is recently becoming actual among librarians and archive specialists, that is, library conservation and preservation. In fact, it has become one of the priorities in the program of the National Library of Indonesia, which is evident by the establishment of the National Preservation Center and by the continuing support of several developed countries on the program. 

In this second newsletter, the publisher presents the 8th CONSAL Executive Board membres (except Brunei Darussalam which is not a member yet). The names of several important figures of the Government, who act as patron and advisers, also appear in addition to the Executive and Organizing Committees of 8th CONSAL. Of significance too is a more detailed tentative program of the Congress. It is also worthwhile to note that the Congress is expected to be officially opened by President Suharto at the State Palace. This another confirmation of how keen the President's interest is in librarianship and all of its aspects, including book publishing and reading habit. His concern in these affairs had already been expressed earlier in his address on 11 March 1989, that was during the opening ceremony of the National Library building. 

Notice to all participants : Please be aware that all registration forms must be filled out and submitted to the 8th CONSAL Secretariat no later than 19 May 1990, whether the fee has been paid or not. Failure to do so may result in the participants' names not being listed as attendees to the opening ceremony at the State Palace. However, the payment of the fee may be done prior to the start of the Congress (Sunday, 10 June 1990). Just a reminder, formal attire is compulsory on the occasion of the opening.

column9: Minutes of the 25th Faculty Meeting held on 17 June 19897 at MH 410 (GE CONFERENCE ROOM)
column10:
(1) The meeting was called to order at 1:30 p.m. with Dean R.A. Garcia presiding. 
(2) Approval of minutes of the 255th Faculty Meeting
The minutes of the 255th faculty meeting held on 09 March 1987 were approved as presented. 
(3) Introduction of New or Returning Faculty Members
The CE Department introduced Mr. Joseph Raymond V. Flora, BSCE magna cum laude 1987 as a new faculty member. 
The following were introduced as returning faculty members : 
ME Department : 
Dr. Ibarra E. Cruz, returning from special detail with PNOC-ERDC
Prof. Rodolfo Casiple returning to full-time status 
ES Department : 
Prof. Angel A. Alejandrino, returning from special detail with NHRC
(4) Business Arising from the Minutes
a) Attendance 
Dr. G.T. Marzan remarked that there seems to be more members absent than present in faculty meetings. He stated that something should be done about attendance to meetings. 
b) Candidates for Graduation who have Cleared their Deficiencies.
As requested in the previous meeting, a list of candidates for graduation as of the end of second semster 1986-87 who have cleared their deficiences is included in pages 3 and 4. All of the candidates except for Mr. Enrique Manzano have cleared their deficiencies. Mr. Manzano's candidacy will be discussed at this meeting. 
c) Reiteration of the UPAA and UPAE Homecoming and Reunion Activities
The faculty was reminded that the UPAA Homecoming and Reunion Activity will be held on Saturday, 20 June 1987 8:00 a.m. to 12:00 p.m. at the Manila Hotel while the corresponding activity for UPAE is scheduled on Saturday, 11 July 1987, 6:30 p.m. at Valle Verde Country Club. Tickets for the UPAA affair cost P200 and for UPAE, P100.
d) UPAE Fourth 500 Life Members
It was announced that the life membership fee in the UPAE will still be P300 up to July 11, 1987. Those who register will now be part of the "fourth 500" life members. 
(5) Candidates for Graduation
a) Without Deficiencies
The lists of candidates for graduation without deficiencies for undergraduate and graduate levels were approved as presented. 
b) With Deficiencies
The lists of candidates for graduation with deficiencies for undergraduate and graduate levels were approved provided that they removed their deficiencies on or before 22 May 1987 with the exception of the following two candidates for BSChE whose names were withdrawn : Gener J. Lopez and Marissa R. A. Villanueva. 
Ms. E.M. David reported that they have not been able to clear their deficiency with her as of May 22, 1987. 
(6) Change of Grade/Status
The following changes of grade or status were approved by the faculty : 
Name of Student
1. Widodo
2. Jimenez, Hector
3. Frondoza, Christine
4. Vilvestre, Ingemar
5. Forteza, Carlos C. 
6. Gargantos, Gilbert C. 
(7) Other Matters
a) Revision of Curricula to Remove Spanish
i) BS Mechanical Engineering
Dr. MV. Hernandez, Jr. reported that of the 12 units originally alotted to Spanish, their department will use only six units for technical courses so as to compensate for the additional units due to the new General Education program. The total number of units becomes 174 units. 
The six units will be used as follows : 
- require ME 176 (3 units)
- include another elective with departmental approval
This proposal was approved by the faculty. 
ii) BS Metallurgical Engineering
Dr. M.U. Ordillas, Jr. stated that the 12 units originally alotted to Spanish will be used such that there will be: 
- 3 units foreign language
- 9 units electives
He also proposed that EM 10 be moved from its present place in the second semester of the fourth year to first semester of the fifth year. 
A discussion ensued during which Dr. Pacheco stated that 3 units of foreign language are not sufficient and Dr. Hernandez agreed that it is difficult to learn of foreign language in 3 units. Dr. Hernandez also added : now that some General Education units can be reduced, why add some more?
Dr. Ordillas answered that the three units of foreign language are meant to develop reading comprehension. Dr. Gopez added that based on his experience in teaching an experimental course in French in Technology it is possible for students to learn to read a foreign language in three units. Prof. M.T. Escoto, Jr. also stated that learning another foreign language could be beneficial. 
Dr. Pacheco said that with the growing volume of reading material in English, there would be little time for engieners or engineering students to read material in another language.  
Dean Mena suggested that the proposal be changed instead to 12 units department approved electives. 
After an informal conference with MetE Department staff members, Dr. Ordillas modified his proposal to 9 units department approved electives together with the proposal to move EM 10 to the first semester of the fourth year. This was approved by the faculty. 
iii) BS Mining Engineering, Industrial Engineering, Geodetic Engineering
The faculty approved the proposals to replace 12 units of Spanish with 9 units department approved electives in BSEM, BSIE, and BSGE. The proposals were given by the respective department chairmen. 
iv) BS Chemical Engineering
Dr. Wilfredo Jose gave copies of the proposed revisions to the BSChE curriculum. After some discussion it turned out that some of the revisions such as Change in Course Title and Change in Curriculum Requirements were not directly related to the abolition of Spanish. These changes cannot be made due to the proviso that at least three years after a curriculum revision is done, there should be no changes. (The BSChE curriculum was recently revised). 
Accordingly the BSChE revisions which were approved by the faculty were :
- replacement of Spanish with 9 units of deparment approved electives 
- institution of ChE 197 Special Topics, credit 3 units, may be repated for a maximum of 6 units
The Secretary was requested to check whether the BSChE curriculum required EE 3 and for EE 6 and 7. 
v) BS Civil Engineering
Prof. U.P. Ignacio, Jr. stated that the CE Department proposes to increase the number of units of CE 110, CE 131 and CE 132 from two to three units and add 6 units of department approved electives to replace Spanish. This proposal was approved by the faculty. 
vi) B Computer Science
Prof. Francisco stated that since consensus of the Computer Science Committee and additional approval by the College of Science are needed for the BCS revisions the ES Department will present its proposal for conditional approval by the faculty. 
Only 9 units will be used as follows : 
- requirement of a second special problems course (3 units)
- splitting of CS 195 (3 units) into two courses; CS 191 (3 units) and CS192 (3 units)
- addition of another elective ( 3 units)
There were no objections to the proposal. 
vii) BS Electrical Engineering
Dr. E.F. Abaya reported that the EE Department was not yet ready with a proposal. For the information of the body he added that the Department intends to use only 9 units of the 12 units originally alotted to Spanish.
b) Request for "Special Faculty Meetings"
Dr. Pacheco and Dr. S.F. Reyes suggested that informal faculty meetings be held to discuss other matters for which ordinarily there would be no time during regular meetings. Dr. Pacheco suggested institutional self-examination as a possible theme in one meeting. Dr. Reyes suggested that these "special" meetings be held regularly. Dr. Hernandez suggested that a discussion paper be presented at each of these meetings. 
It was decided that the meetings be held every second Wednesday of each month with the first meeting being held on 8 July 1987, 2:30 p.m. Dr. Pacheco was requested to present a paper on "Institutional Self-Examination" at this meeting. 
(8) The meeting was adjourned at 3:00 p.m.

ADOLFO JESUS R. GOPEZ

CURRICULAR REVISIONS OF BACHELOR OF COMPUTER SCIENCE DUE TO THE NEW RULE ON SPANISH SUBJECTS
column10:
CURRICULAR REVISIONS OF BACHELOR OF COMPUTER SCIENCE DUE TO THE NEW RULE ON SPANISH SUBJECTS 

1) Removal of twelve units of Spanish from the curriculum. 
2) Reduction on the total number of units by 3 to eliminate the need to require a summer session in the program. 
3) Increase in the number of units of electives by 3. 
4) Inclusion of 6 units of one foreign language in the list of electives.
5) Requirement of a second CS 198 : Special Problems course (requiring a total of 6 units of CS198)
6) Revision of the CS 195 : Software Engineering course into two courses by 
a) Institution of a Course
CS 191 : INFORMATION SYSTEMS DESIGN
Feasibility Study. Systems analysis and design. Construction and management of information systems. Documentation. Prerequisite : Junior Standing. Credit : 3 units. 
b) Change in Number, Description, Prerequisite and Credit

From CS 195 : SOFTWARE ENGINEERING 
Metholodogies for requirements analysis. Software design, implementation and maintenance. Testing techniques. Software system documentation guidelines. Prerequisites : Fourth Year Standing. Credit : 3 units (2 hrs. lecture., 3 hours lab.)

To CS 192 : SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
Implementation of a software project using the techniques of requirements analysis, design, testing and maintenance. Performance evaluation. Prerequisite : CS 191. Credit : 3 units (1 hr. lect., 6 hrs. lab.)

NOTE : In the last curricular revision approved by the university Council on 14 February 1987, CS 62 : Software Design and Development was revised to CS 195. The instructors of CS 62 believe that it should be a 6 unit course. It was not revised into two subjects then because there was no room for an additional subject in the program revision. 

PROPOSED REVISION OF THE B.S. ELECTRICAL ENGINEERING CURRICULUM

RATIONALE 
In the light of the removal of the 12-unit Spanish language requirement, two lecture courses and one laboratory course are proposed to be added to the E.E. curriculum to strengthen its Electronics and Computer foundation. The two lecture courses proposed to be required are now being taught as electives in the present curriculum. 
In order to satisfy the 15 unit E.E. elective requirement in the present and proposed curricula, two new courses are proposed for institution. In addition, an existing Special Projects course is proposed to be taken as an elective for up to 6 units. 
These changes will effectively reduce the total units of the curriculum from the present 182 to 176, the former total before the institution of compulsory General Education courses. 

SUMMARY OF CURRICULUM CHANGES
1. Courses are removed from the curriculum as follows :
Span. I
Span. II
Span. III
Span. 20

2. Two existing courses are added to the curriculum as follows : 
EE 123 - Electronic Circuits II
EE 162 - Switching Theory and Digital Logic Design

3. One laboratory course is instituted and added to the curriculum to accompany EE 123 as follows : EE 124 - Electronics Laboratory III

4. One existing laboratory course, EE 122, is reduced by one (1) unit to accomodate the institution and addition of EE 124. 

5. Two courses are instituted to serve as Electives as follows : 
EE 177 - Wire Communications
EE 178 - Wireless Communications

6. Up to 6 units of EE 198 (Special Projects) will be allowed as Electives.

7. The total number of units is decreased from 182 to 176. 

I. REVISION OF UNDERGRADUATE COURSES
(Change in number of units/credit)

EE 122 ; ELECTRONICS LABORATORY II
From : 2 units (6 hours lab.)
To : 1 unit (3 hours lab.)

EE 198 ; SPECIAL PROBLEMS
From : 7 hours a week (1 class, 6 lab.); credit 3 units
To : 7 hours a week (1 class, 6 lab); credit 3 units may be repeated for a maximum of 6 units

II. INSTITUTION OF UNDERGRADUATE COURSES
EE 177 : WIRE COMMUNICATIONS. - Telephony and telegraphy. Terminal equipment. Switching techniques. Traffic engineering. signalling systems. Wire transmission characteristics. 

Prerequisite : EE 171
Credit : 3 units (3 hours lecture)

EE 178 : WIRELESS COMMUNICATIONS. - Radio wave propagation. Antennas. Transmission lines and waveguides. Cavity resonators. Radio link design. Satellite communications. 

Prerequisite : EE 171
Credit : 3 units (3 hours lecture)

EE 124 ; ELECTRONICS LABORATORY III. - Construction, performance measurements and behavior analysis of pulse, waveshaping, timing and digital circuits. 

Corequisite : EE 123
Credit : 1 unit (3 hours lab.)

INSTRUCTIONS FOR REGISTRATION
Second Semester, 1987-1988
Tues., 20 Oct. - Fri., 23 Oct.
1. Go to your assigned adviser. Your adviser will write the subjects you are to enroll in . The student shall verify that the schedule of the subjects has no conflicts. Conflicts of schedule are not allowed. Never add or cross out a course in your registration forms without the consent of your adviser. 
2. After the U.P. Form 5-A is signed by your adviser, write your Student No. and your Enlistment Priority Number (EPN) at the upper right hand corner of your Form 5-A. Your EPN is the last three digits of your student number written in the reverse order. (For example, if the student number is 85-14093, the EPN is 390). Then go to 3rd floor classrooms (West Wing) for CS, ES, GE, EM and Met E classcards (classcards in ChE, CE, EE, IE and Me subjects may be obtained from the appropriate departmental office) and/or enlistment. Enlistment for non-engineering subjects will be done at Melchor Hall on Tuesday, 20 Oct. 1987. From Wed., 21 October onward, enlistment will be done at the Colleges where subjects needed are offered (see bulletin boards for enlistment schedules). All classcards must be filled out completely before they are presented for checking. 
3. Register in P.E. if you have not yet completed the required number of units. 
4. Secure the U.P. Form 5 at the 2nd floor lobby. Fill up the form and have it signed by your adviser. 
5. Proceed to the checkers at the 2nd floor lobby for checking and turn in your U.P. Form 5-A. 
6. Male students including aliens should register at the Dept. of Citizen Military Training. 
7. Present the U.P. Form 5 and CMT registration papers to the assessors at the 2nd floor lobby for assessment of fees. Special assessment - those with fees for billing, go to the first floor of the Office of the University Registrar, Benton Hall. 
8. Go to the Institute of Small Scale Industries Bldg. for the payment of fess. See that your U.P. Form 5 and classcards are stamped "REGISTERED".
9. Register for library privileges at the Eng'g Library. 
10. Deadline for submission of classcards to your instructors is on Nov. 3, 1987 (or one day after the last day for late registration). If you do not see your instructor before this day, have your classcard transmitted to him by the department chairman. 

Payment of tuition and other fees may be made in CASH, MANAGER's or CERTIFIED CHECKS only. Personal checks are not acceptable. 

ADOLFO JESUS R. GOPEZ, Dr.-Ing.
College Secretary
