SOME GUIDELINES IN CONNECTION WITH GRADUATION

1. Students who have completed all requirements for graduation (academic as well as non-academic) within the semester and who have applied for graduation accordingly, are listed as candidates for graduation as of the end of that semester. 
2. If, however, some graduation requirements are fulfilled beyond the end of the semester concerned, then the student becomes a candidate for graduation as of the end of the succeeding semester. 
3. Definition of end of semester : 
3.1 The first semester and the summer session end on the day immediately preceding the first day of advanced registration for the second semester and for the first semester, respectively. 
3.2. The second semester ends on Commencement Day.
4. All candidates for graduation must have their deficiencies made up and their records cleared not later than five weeks before the end of the last semester, with the exception of those in academic subjects and work in Physical Education and Military Science, in which the student is currently enrolled during that semester. 
The attention of graduating students should be called early enough on their graduation deficiencies including possible deficiencies in CMT and Physical Education. Regarding Physical Education, please be reminded that there are appropriate Physical Education classes in the University even for the physical defective students. Students should be reminded of their deficiencies in CMT and Physical Education even before their senior year.
5. The authority requested by the University Registrar from the University Council to add to the list of candidates up to a specified deadline refers only to students whose graduation had already been approved by the faculty. The names of these students, however, might not have been included in the list submitted to the University Council because of some deficiencies. These deficiencies pertain only to those cases where requirements had been completed before the end of the semester but the grades or results of which were inadvertently misplaced or not yet sent to the Office of the University Registrar. These requirements may be subjects already passed, removals, oral examinations, comprehensive examinations, theses and/or dissertations.
6. A fine on late application for graduation every semester of summer term is imposed as follows : P10,00 after the deadline and additional fine is P1.00 every working day thereafter, but not to exceed 30 working days. After the 30 working days shall have elapsed, no more applications will be entertained for that semester or term. 
7. No student shall be conferred any title or degree who fails to pay the required graduation fee within the specified period set by the University Registrar. Such student may, however, upon his request and payment of the necessary fees, be given a certified copy of his credentials without specifying his completion of the requirements toward any title or degree.

column9: TRANSFER, SHIFTING AND ADMISSION POLICIES (adopted by the Executive Committee, 18 September 1985)
column10:
COLLEGE OF ENGINEERING
University of the Philippines
Diliman, Quezon City

TRANSFER, SHIFTING and ADMISSION POLICIES
(adopted by the Executive Committee, 18 September 1985)

a) Transfers, shifting and admission for 2nd Bachelor's degree will be allowed in both the first second semesters subject to availability of slots.
b) Transfers from other schools into all BS Engineering programs are allowed only if the quotas are not filled up.
c) Foreign students may apply for transfer to any BS Engineering program subject to the availability of slots.
d) Eligibility for transfer to U.P. from a local or foreign school shall be decided by the University Registrar based on University Rules and Regulations.
e) Students of other UP units wishing to shift to any BS Engineering Program must have studied at least two semesters and earned at least 33 units in their original unit. To be considered for shifting a student must have no failing grade, or "4" in Mathematics, Physics and Chemistry courses. Admission of students is subject to the availability of slots in the quotas. 
f) Shifting from other U.P. units is not allowed for students who have finished their third year. 
g) Shifting within the College of Engineering is allowed subject to the approval of the Departments concerned.
h) Transfer or shifting to the B.S. Computer Science program are not covered by these policies.

3 hours a week (class); credit 3 units

2. Met E 222; ADVANCED MINERAL PROCESSING II. - Special topics in flotation and comminution theory, mineral physics, thermal decripitation, use of radioactive tracers and ion exchange resins. 
Prerequisite : Met E 127
3 hours a week (class); credit 3 units

3. Met E 231 : ADVANCED EXTRACTIVE METALLURGY I - Theory, operation and economics of modern industrial hydrometallurgical processes, including electroplatingm electrowinning, and electrolytic refining.
Prerequisite : Met E 132
3 hours a week (class); credit 3 units

4. Met E 232; ADVANCED EXTRACTIVE METALLURGY II. - Problems in roasting, smelting and refining. Pyro and electro-metallurgical processes.
Prerequisite : Met E 134 or consent of instructor - 3 hours a week (class); credit 3 units

5. Met E 235; PHYSICAL CHEMISTRY OF STEELMAKING. - Design and control of iron and steelmaking processes, with emphasis on blast furnace, basic oxygen furnace, and electric furnace. Application of deoxidizers, vacuum and inert gas processes for product purification.
Prerequisite : Met E 134 or consent of instructor 
3 hours a week (class); credit 3 units

III. COURSES IN MATERIALS
1. Met E 241 : ADVANCED PHYSICAL METALLURGY - Electron theory of metals, theory of crystal binding, solid solutions and compound formation. Phase stability. Solid state phase transformations. Microstructures and mechanical properties. 
Prerequisite : Met E 147
3 hours a week (class); credit 3 units

2. Met E 243; HEAT TREATMENT OF FERROUS ALLOYS - Types of ferrous alloys. Interrelationships among composition, microstructure, service requirements and mechanical properties of ferrous alloys. Industrial heat treatment practices. Special alloys. 
Prerequisite : Met E 146 or consent of instructor 3 hours a week (class); credit 3 units

IV. COURSES IN METALS FORMING
1. Met E 251; METAL CASTING. - Metallurgy of cast metals. Unit foundry operations. Sand testing and control. Melting and casting practices. Manufacture of special cast metals and alloys. 
Prerequisite : Met E 147
3 hours a week (class); credit 3 units. 

2. Met E 257 : DEFORMATION PROCESSING - Fundamentals of plastic deformation processing. Forging, rolling, extrusion and drawing. Tools and equipment.
Prerequisite : Met E 147 or consent of instructor 
3 hours a week (class); credit 3 units

column10:
(1) The meeting was called to order at 2:20 p.m. with Dean Garcia presiding.
(2) The minutes of the 253rd faculty meeting held on 15 October 1986 were approved with the following corrections :
a) Prof. M.T. Escoto Jr. was on special detail in Japan as USPS-NSTA researcher at the time of the meeting. 
(b) Delete the words "by the faculty", in terms 6 and 8 of the minutes. 
(3) Business arising from the minutes
a) Item 3 (a) 
The Secretary announced that Mr. J. Ofreneo already has a grade in EE 81. 
b) Curricular Revisions
Dr. Pacheco reported on the progress of the curricular revisions. For the Biotechnology course ChE 202 microbiology and biochemistry are now prerequisites. He also reported that in the revisions of the B.S. (Metallurgical Engineering) program due to the general education program the abolition of MetE 155 was not approved because U.P. Baguio still requires it in their curriculum offerings. 
He requested the Chairman of the C.E. Department to write to the School of Urban and Regional Planning regarding the institution of CE 245, in order to get feedback from that school.
c) Request for extension of residence by Gerardo A. Remulla 
The Secretary informed the body that Mr. Remulla's request for extension of residence has already been approved by the Chancellor U.P. Diliman. Mr. Remulla is presently enrolled in Shop 4. 
(4) Candidates for graduation as of the End of the First Semester 1986-87 without deficiencies
The list of candidates for graduation without deficiencies in the graduate and undergraduate divisions were approved. It was ascertained that candidates for graduation with honors were not underloaded during their period of residence. In Item VII-13 the words "cum laude" should be underlined. 
(5) Candidate for Graduation as of the End of First Semester 1986-87 with deficiencies
Before action on this list was made the Secretary requested action on the request for change of grade in EE 4 of Mr. F. Kiunisala III from 5.0 to 3.0 made by his instructor, Mr. A. Echevarria. After the reading of Mr. Echevarria's request the faculty approved the change of Mr. Kiunisala's grade in EE 4 from 5.0 to 3.0.
The faculty approved the list of students for graduation with pending deficiencies as of the end of the first semester 1986-87.
(6) Requests for Change of Grade/Status
The following requests for change of grade/status were approved by the faculty. Mr. Echevarria's request regarding Mr. Kiunisala's grade in EE 4 is included in the list. 
Name of Student
1. Henson, Marie Elaine - ES 13 ULZ12 - 5.0 - * - 2-85-86 - C. Gonzales
2. Kiunisala Frederick - EE 4 - 5.0 - 3.0 - 1 - 86-87 - Echevarria 
3. Ledesma, Lovelita - ChE 61 - 2.5 - 2.25 - 1-86-87 - E. David 
4. Limos, Rommel Jason - ChE 43 - 5.0 - 3.0 - 1-86-87 - A. Escoto
5. Neores, Diosdado L. - CS 31 - 2.5 - 2.0 - 1-86-87 - M. Vencio 
6. Patricio Claribelle - CS 31 - 2.75 - 2.25 - 1-86-87- M. Vencio
7. Thalla, Primavera - CE 11 - 4.0 - 2.5 - 1-86-87 - G. Zabat
8. Trinidad, Ma. Jennifer - CE 11 - 2.5, 4.0 - 1-86-87 - G. Zabat
9. Ulanday, Ma. Cecilia - ES 13 - 4.0 - 3.0 - 2-85 -86 - C. Gonzales
*Cancelled
Mr. Marcial T. Ocampo's request for the change of grade of Ms. Jocelyn C. Flores on the basis of absences which should be excused was read to the body by the Secretary. Mr. Ocampo argued that because three of Ms. Flores' absences were due to her employment her grade should be changed as follows : 
(11 + 3) = 14 = 30% Attendance
90 = 27% Group Work
90 = 36 % Final Exam
93% = 1.50
(old grade = 1.75)
It was pointed out that Ms. Flores present average is 2.05 and that if this change of grade is approved. She will be able to graduate with a masters degree. Dr. Pacheco stated that the change of grade is not due to any error on the part of the lecturer. Ms. Flores was still absent whether her absences were excused or not. Dr. Marzan said that Mr. Ocampo is still requesting the change of grade. Dr. Jose argued that this change of grade does not really affect Ms. Flores' graduation since she is enrolled in the second semester 1986-87 anyway. Dr. Pacheco answered that if this change of grade is approved, Ms. Flores will drop her present enrollment. He also said that if this request is approved similar requests must be approved in the future. 
Dr. M.G. Mena moved for disapproval of Mr. Ocampo's request for change of grade. Prof. M.T. Escoto, Jr. seconded Dr. Mena's motion. The faculty voted for disapproval of Mr. Ocampo's request with 20 voting for disapproval none voting for approval and the rest abstaining. 
(7) Introduction of New Faculty Member
Mr. Francis Felizardo, BS (Civil Engineering) magna cum laude 1985 was introduced to the body as a new instructor in civil engineering. His appointment was effective 1 December 1986. 
(8) Other Matters
a) The Secretary announced that the Team on Graduation Affairs of the Engineering Student Council was requesting the faculty members to go to MH 203 for picture taking this afternoon (10 December 1986) at 4:00 p.m. 
b) It was announced that the minimum contributions for the College Christmas Party scheduled on 18 December 1986, 3:00 p.m. at the 2nd floor lobby are : 
(i) Professor  P80
(ii) Associate Professor  P70
(iii) Assistant Professor  P60
(iv) Instructor  P50
These contributions are needed to subsidize gift bags for administrative personnel.
c) It was announced that the ACES GP's Comp. Soc., EMC 2 Gamma Sigma Pi and the Engineering Student Council are participating in the coming lantern parade (December 18)
(9) The meeting was adjourned at 3:20 p.m.

ADOLFO JESUS R. GOPEZ

column9: (1986, December 8) 254th ENGINEERING FACULTY MEETING
MINUTES OF THE 253RD ENGINEERING FACULTY MEETING HELD ON OCTOBER 15, 1986, ROOM 410, MELCHOR HALL
column10:
(1) The meeting was called to order at 9:25 a.m. with Dean Garcia presiding. 
(2) The minutes of the 252nd Faculty Meeting were approved with the following correction : 
In Item 7(a) the name "Leonardo Yabes" should be corrected to "Leopoldo Yabes".
(3) Business arising from the minutes
a) Mr. J. Ofreneo has no grade yet in EE 81 under Prof. J.A. Azarcon, Jr. Considering the time elapsed since this course finished, Mr. Ofreneo should already have a grade, even an "INC". Dr. Pacheco inquired as to what steps should be taken to make sure that the faculty member involved will give Mr. Ofreneo a grade. Dean Garcia replied that he would send a letter to Dr. Azarcon. 
b) The Secretary informed the body that Ms. R.A. Nisperos opted to take Speech I in the summer of 1987. Her request for extension of residence up to the end of summer 1987 has already been approved by the Chancellor, U.P. Diliman. 
c) The Secretary also informed the body that Mr. V. L. Romero, whose request for extension of residence was endorsed by the faculty during the 252nd meeting, is enrolled in Chem 28 in the first semester 1986-87.
d) The revised graduate courses in Metallurgical Engineering were approved in the last meeting of the University Council. They are now ready for implementation.  
(4) Abolition and Institution of Some EE Courses
The Secretary announced that EE 28 and 29 as presented have been endorsed for approval by the Executive Committee. The action of the Committee was carried out by means of a referendum. These courses will be offered by the EE Department for BCS students and interested EE students. 
Prof. R.G. Solis presented the proposal to institute EE 6 and 7 to replace the EE 3, 4, 5 series. He said however that EE 3 will be retained because it best meets the needs of the CE students particularly in wiring requirements. Prof. Solis also presented the proposed EE 28 and 29 courses on computer hardware for BCS students. Prof. Francisco stated that the prerequisite for EE 28 is CS 21, not CS 20 as listed in the proposal. 
The abolition of EE 4 and 5 and the institution of EE 6 and 7 were approved by the body.
The institution of EE 28 and 29 was also approved. Upon suggestion of Dr. J. Bala, these courses will be numbered as EE 8 and 9 respectively. 
(5) Biotechnology and Biochemical Engineering
Dr. Jose presented the proposal to institute three courses : ChE 174, 202 and 294. He informed the body that an ad hoc committee composed of professors from the Colleges of Home Economics, Science, and Engineering, and the Natural Science Research Institute have already discussed these courses. 
With respect to the two graduate courses being instituted they will form part of the Biochemical Engineering option in the MSChE program. The other option in MSChE is the general Chemical Engineering option which is currently being offered. 
Dr. Jose added a footnote to the Biochemical Engineering option which reads : "Students with insufficient background in biochemistry and microbiology will be required to take Chem 40 and 40.1 and Biology 120 or their equivalents." he also stated that since the graduate courses are also intended as elective courses for interested students from the other departments, the footnote applies only to students in the Biochemical Engineering option. 
When questioned about possible funding, Dr. Jose replied that this field will be considered as a priority by the PCIERD. 
The courses were approved for institution. 
(6) Revision of the BS and MS Mechanical Engineering Programs
Dr. Hernandez gave the salient features of the proposed revisions. Dr. Pacheco pointed out that the footnote of the course checklist of the revised program should read : "May be taken in English or Filipino".
The revisions were approved. 
(7) Revision of the Geodetic Engineering Program 
Dr. Marzan discussed the significant features of the proposed revision of the BSGE program. Total number of units will be 181 units, up from 177 units.
The section on institution of courses should read "Institution of Undergraduate Course" since only one course is being instituted. 
In response to Dr. Abis' inquiry regarding Remote Sensing, Dr. Marzan stated that it is being taught as part of Photo-Interpretation. In fact, the title of GE 159, which is the revised GE 59, is now "Photo-Interpretation and Remote Sensing".
The revision was approved. 
(8) Revision of the BS (Metallurgical Engineering) Program
Dr. Ordillas presented the proposal to revise the BSMetE program. Due to the increase in the number of units of the General Education Program (GEP) the total number of units in the BSMetE program was increased to 186. The revised program brings the total number of units down to 180. Dr. Ordillas also told the body that the section on institution of courses should be entitled : "Institution of a Course" since only one new course is being proposed. 
Due to similar corrections in the course lists of all the proposed revisions, the body agreed that the listing of courses (particularly those in the GEP) should be standardized. The College Secretary was requested to carry out the standardization. 
In response to a question by Dr. J. Bala, the Secretary informed the body that the following notations have been the official U.P. designations of the engineering programs for at least two years now: 
Present notation
Bachelor of Science (Chemical Engineering)
Bachelor of Science (Civil Engineering)
Bachelor of Science (Metallurgical Engineering)
Former notation
Bachelor of Science in Chemical Engineering
Bachelor of Science in Civil Engineering
Bachelor of Science in Metallurgical Engineering
The proposed revised BSMetE program was approved by the faculty. 
(9) Revision of the BS and MS Civil Engineering Programs
Prof. de Castro, Officer-in-Charge of the CE Department presented the significant features of the proposed revisions. He cited the increase in the units of the new GEP as one of the reasons which led to the revision of the BSCE program. 
Dr. Pacheco cited some corrections in the notations of courses in the GEP and requested the inclusion of the current course checklist in the proposal. 
The body approved the revised BSCE curriculum as corrected and the renumbering of the EnE courses as presented. With the exception of CE 245 all the graduate course revisions were approved. For CE 245 the CE Department was authorized to effect such changes as may be needed (even withdraw the course) after consultation with the staff of the School of Urban and Regional Planning. 
(10) Revision of the Bachelor of Computer Science (BCS) Program
Prof. Francisco presented the signficant features of the BCS program revision. He also cited the following corrections :
a) EE 38 and 29 should be replaced with EE 8 and 9, pursuant to the decisions of the body (see Item 3 of these minutes);
b) Page 11 of the proposal should be deleted,
c) IE 3 (instead of IE 31) should be placed in the list of electives;
d) The footnote of the course checklist should be "may be taken in Filipino or English".
Dr. Pacheco made the comment that 20 units in the first year, second semester might be too heavy for freshman. Prof. Francisco replied that the three units cannot be absorbed in other semesters but may be taken in the summer session. The adviser should help the student decide whether the student's load will be 20 units or just 17 units with the other three to be taken in summer. Ms. Vencio supported the latter view and stated that the adviser and the student should decide on a case-to-case basis. 
Dr. Pacheco suggested that the program be modified such that the first year second semester load would be 17 units with the three units being taken in the summer after the first year. This would allow the students to justify the summer session for scholarship purposes or to their parents, while act preventing the capable students from taking all of the 20 units during the semester. 
After some discussion the ES Department agreed to Dr. Pacheco's suggestion. Social Science II will be taken in the summer after the first year of the BCS program. 
The faculty approved the revised BCS program as modified. 
In response to Dr. Abis' inquiry, Dean Garcia stated that the institution of an MSCS program is being considered. 
(11) Action on a Request for Extension of Resilience beyond 8-1/2 years 
The request of Mr. Gerardo A. Remulla 76-01260 to take Shop 4 in the second semester 1986-87 and CMT in the summer of 1987 to allow him to obtain his BSME degree was approved by the faculty. 
The Secretary will prepare the indorsement to the Chancellor, U.P. Diliman. 
(12) Other matters
a) The Secretary read a position paper made by the personnel of National Water Resources Council (NWRC) on the request of the faculty to retain the Water Resources library and databank at the National Hydraulic Research Council (NHRC). 
b) The Secretary reminded the faculty that the deadline for the submission of grades was last Monday, 13 October 1986. 
(13) The meeting was adjourned at 11:30 a.m. 

ADOLFO JESUS R. GOPEZ

The 251st Engineering Faculty Meeting will be held on MONDAY, 24 March 1986 at 2:00 PM in Room 410.

AGENDA : 
1. Approval of the minutes of the 250th meeting. 
2. Business arising from the minutes.
3. Approval of course revisions. 
4. Approval of the candidates for graduation as of the end of the second semester, 1985-86. 
5. Other matters. 

ALEXANDER P. PARAN
Officer-In-Charge

(1) The meeting was called to order at 1:25 p.m., Dean R.A. Garcia presiding. 
(2) The minutes of the 247th, 248th and 249th faculty meeting were approved with the following corrections :

CE Dept. : Michael V. Gonzales
Rolando Orense
Andre S. Publico
EE Dept. : Aurelio Pimentel
(4) Business arising from the minutes : 
a) General Education (G.E.) Program
A system-wide G.E. Conference (248th meeting) will be held on 15 to 17 December 1985. Dr. Gopez will be attending at the request of Dean Garcia.
b) National Telecommunications Institute (NTI)
The Dean informed the Body that there is no longer a need for a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) between the U.P. and the MOTC. 
Pres. Angara and Minister Dans have already drafted a letter to the President of the Philippines to be submitted with a draft of an Executive Order empowering the U.P. Board of Regents to create the NTI and to determine the working relationship between the U.P. and MOTC. According to one of the memoranda issued by the Office of the U.P. President, the NTI will be attached to the U.P. College of Engineering. 
The papers have already been endorsed by the Prime Minister and are already at Malacañang.
Dr. G.T. Marzan pointed out that Item 5d-i (pages 6) of the minutes of the 248th has now been answered. 
Mr. E. Gonzales asked about the role of the College faculty in the creation of the NTI. Dean Garcia replied that the unit will be attached to the College but no structure nor procedure presently exist for its establishment. No details are as yet available. Establishment of the NTI will be a first experience.

column9: PROPOSED REVISION OF THE GENERAL EDUCATION PROGRAM
column10:
Appendix E, page 1
PROPOSED REVISION OF THE GENERAL EDUCATION PROGRAM
I. ORIENTATION AND OBJECTIVES OF THE REVISED GENERAL EDUCATION PROGRAM
The Revised General Education Program shall be oriented towards the development and promotion of a liberal, scientific, and humanistic Filipino culture. The Program's objectives are : 
1.1. To broaden the intellectual and cultural horizons of students by developing in them an awareness of the environment and a deeper insight into and understanding of man and man's achievements that would enable them to appreciate their Filipino, Asian, and human heritage. 
1.2 To cultivate among students a capacity for clear and independent thinking that is critical but open-minded, scientific but holistic, logical and rational, but imaginative and humanistic. 
1.3. To develop the students' capabilities to evaluate, integrate, synthesize, and even modify knowledge as well as to discriminate between values, purposes, and ends to that they could make choices and decisions with freedom and responsibility and with universal ethical values and humanist ideals in mind. 
1.4. To develop the communication skills, whether in speech or in writing, that would enable the students to express themselves clearly, coherently, effectively, and if possible, eloquently. 
1.5 To foster a nationalist commitment that would promote the cause of attaining a truly free, democratic, just, self-reliant, and progressive Filipino society. 

II. COURSE REQUIREMENTS OF THE REVISED GENERAL EDUCATION PROGRAM

The Revised General Education Program shall consist of 60 units of courses distributed as follows : 
2.1 Humanities Requirements
English and/or Filipino - 12 units
Humanities I - 3 units
Speech I - 3 units 
Foreign Language - 12 units
Subtotal - 30 units
2.2. Natural Sciences and Mathematics Requirements 
Mathematics I - 3 units
Natural Science I - 3 units
Natural Science II - 3 units
Science and Society I or Environmental Issue - 3 units
Introduction to Computers - 3 units
Subtotal - 15 units 
2.3 Social Science Requirements
Social Science I - 3 units
Social Science II - 3 units
Social Science III - 3 units
Social Science IV - 3 units
Philosophical Analysis - 3 units
Subtotal - 15 units
TOTAL - 60 units
III. REVISION OF GENERAL EDUCATION COURSES
3.1 CHANGE OF COURSE TITLE 
From : PHILIPPINE HISTORY AND INSTITUTIONS I
To : SOCIAL SCIENCE I. PHILIPPINE HISTORY AND INSTITUTIONS
3.2 CHANGE OF COURSE TITLE AND DESCRIPTION
From : MATHEMATICS I. MATHEMATICS FOR GENERAL EDUCATION. 
Fundamental principles of mathematics; applications. 
To : MATHEMATICS I. Survey of the concepts, methods and applications of mathematics.
3.3 CHANGE OF COURSE NUMBER, TITLE, AND DESCRIPTION
From : NATURAL SCIENCE I. FOUNDATIONS of PHYSICAL SCIENCE I. - Basic principles and the philosophical, cultural and social aspects of physics, astronomy and meteorology. 
To : NATURAL SCIENCE I. Foundations of physics and the earth sciences.
Prerequisite : Mathematics I. 
Credit : 3 units
IV. INSTITUTION OF GENERAL EDUCATION COURSES
4.1 SOCIAL SCIENCE II. ASIA AND THE WORLD. Survey of world civilizations with special focus on the civilizations that developed in Asia.
Credit : 3 units
4.2 SOCIAL SCIENCE III. SOCIETY AND CULTURE. - An analytical study of the institutions, processes, and practices in Philippine society.
Credit : 3 units
4.3 SOCIAL SCIENCE IV. POLITICAL ECONOMY. - Principles, theories and practices with particular emphasis on the Philippine experience.
Credit : 3 units
4.4 SCIENCE AND SOCIETY I. The nature, logic and structure of science and its interrelationships with society from a historical perspective; science and technology in the Philippines
Prerequisites : Natural Science I and II (for their equivalent) and senior standing
Credit : 3 units
4. INTRODUCTION TO COMPUTERS. Computers in society; introduction to computer programming. 
Credit : 3 units (2 lect., 1 lab.)
V. PROPOSED ABOLITION OF A GENERAL EDUCATION COURSE
5.1 Natural Science 3 FOUNDATIONS OF BIOLOGICAL SCIENCE 
a. With the exception of the tool courses, the G.L. courses may be taken by students during their 3rd, 4th or 5th years. 
b. Any combination of English and/or Filipino may be taken provided that at least 3 units of a literature course in English or Filipino must be part of the combination. 
c. Under the present las, 12 units of Spanish are required, but if the Spanish law is repealed, the 12 units of foreign language requirements would be determined by each department according to the needs of its degree program.
d. May be replaced by Mathematics 11 or 17
e. May be replaced by any of the introductory disciplinal courses in physics, geology, and meteorology.
f. May be replaced by any of the introductory disciplinal courses in biology, botany, zoology and chemistry.
g. Choise is to be specified by each department.

column9: PROPOSAL FOR THE ESTABLISHMENT OF THE NATIONAL TELECOMMUNICATIONS INSTITUTE
column10:
Proposal for the Establishment of a NATIONAL TELECOMMUNICATIONS INSTITUTE in the University of the Philippines
1. RATIONALE
National Development. The accelerated pace of industrialization and agricultural modernization during the last decade has called for a rapid expansion of telecommunication services in the country. Being essential to the country's economic development, telecom facilities must undergo major improvement and expansion. Such upgrading has been programmed to be undertaken by both the government and the private carries and it will include the integration of telecommunication backbone routes, upgrading of transmission and switching facilities, and expansion of service coverage, and the introduction of new services. 
Manpower Needs. In light of the country's developmental thrust, a greatly increased demand for trained engineers, managers, and technicians who will plan, design, construct, install, manage, operate, maintain and upgrade these facilities has been recognized by the government and industry leaders. In fact, the managerial and technical manpower requirements of the sector have already been identified and characterized in a study conducted by A.D. Little Consultants for the Ministry of Transportation and Communications (MOTC). In addition, the U.P. Foundation, Inc. also completed a study for the Ministry to establish a National Telecommunications Manpower Development Program which will provide th aforementioned manpower needs. Likewise, a 1984 UNDP/TTU survey team has confirmed that the urgent need for training will be answered through a National Telecommunications Institute. 
At present, the manpower supply and demand gap in the telecommunications sector is being exacerbated by the outflow of experienced personnel who are lured by higher paying jobs abroad. Furthermore, the existing educational and training programs are deemed inadequate to meet present and future manpower needs. They are basically uncoordinated among themselves as well as with the sector's requirements. 
Coordinated Nationwide Manpower Development Program. To come up with the engineering, technical managers and technicians in step with the industry's needs, it will be necessary to establish a broad-based educational and training program involving the industry, the national government, the University of the Philippines and other technical institution all over the country. 
R&D and Local Manufacture. The International Telecommunications Union (TTU), a UN agency, constituted in 1983 an Independent Commission for Worldwide Telecommunication Development. One of the Important recommendations coming from the commission is that developing countries, with the assistance of the industrialized countries, 1) establish R&D institutions as "sources of higher technological, supervisory and managerial training..." and 2) carry out equipment development and manufacturing activities in support of their equipment requirements.
To this end, NTI sees applied R&D for telecommunications as one of its vital tasks. Technical assistance from Japan and other industrial nations will be sought in pursuit of the goal of local manufacture. 
Future Asian Telecommunications Center. Over the long term, the same expertise and physical resources which are used to train Filipinos may be made available to the Asian community as well as the rest of the Third World as a center for graduate education and applied research. It is envisioned that with the collaboration of other units of the university the NTI can be the Philippine counterpart of the Asian Institute of Technology in the field of telecommunications. 
Since the U.P. is the leading engineering institution in the country and has the support of both the government and the private sector, it is hereby proposed that the National Telecommunications Institute (NTI) be put up in U.P. Diliman. The NTI will coordinate with the U.P. Electrical Engineering Department to provide the training opportunities, research and extension services in telecommunications and its allied fields. 
2. NATURE AND OBJECTIVES OF THE NTI
2.1 Nature of the NTI
Initially, the NTI is to be organized out of the present staff and facilities of the U.P. and of the Butel's Telecommunications Training Institute (TTI). It is expected that later, this activity will involve the training centers of PLDT and AFP COMMEL. NTI will coordinate its programs with telecommunication companies and various government agencies. Initially, training will be carried out by jointly offering the courses and sharing the facilities and staff of the centers. As a center of excellence in telecommunications, the NTI shall provide instruction, undertake research activities and provide extension services in the field of telecommunications and related areas. As such, it shall : 
2.1.1 Accord research and extension service as much importance as teaching
2.1.2 Take a leading role in the development of Philippine telecommunications through the development products, processes, services and the continuing training of personnel
2.1.3. Attract, retain and nurture highly qualified staff by providing the proper environment, facilities and incentives thereby ensuring external services, quality research outputs and training results. 
2.2 Objectives of the NTI
An expanded and improved national telecommunication network will only be possible with more qualified manpower. The NTI will, therefore, serve as the national center of excellence for the acquisition, dissemination and application of knowledge in the field of telecommunications. Specifically, it will work towards the attainment of the following objectives :
2.2.1 To provide the telecommunication industry and government with competent manpower in the various specialties of telecommunications for the successful implementation of the country's telecommunication development plans through extensive continuing education and training programs. 
2.2.2 To conduct applied technological research and development activities in all areas of telecommunications and its related fields. 
2.2.3 To assist colleges and universities, and technical schools in developing instructions and instructional materials.
2.2.4 To provide extension services related to national scientific and technological development.
2.2.5 To assist the U.P. Department of Electrical Engineering to provide quality undergraduate and graduate education in telecommunications, including broadcasting and the application of electronics and computers in communications. 
2.2.6 To provide the industry and the government with experts in telecommunications and related areas, especially to identify appropriate technology to import/adapt, and/or to evaluate those being brought/introduced here.
2.2.7 To establish an industry data bank and information system.
3. PROGRAMS AND SERVICES OF THE NTI
In pursuit of its objectives, the NTI shall carry out applied research and development programs and offer the following services : 
3.1 Educational and Training Programs
3.1.1 The initial thrust of NTI will be towards the rapid upgrading of technical manpower in the industry. Thus, the Institute will also offer short-term intensive courses leading to certificates in some specialized telecommunication subjects including : 
- Telephony
- Outside Plant Engineering
- Inside Plant Engineering
- Cable Splicing
- Line Construction
- Station Installation and Maintenance
- Central Office Equipment Installation
- Telegraphy
- Radio and Carrier courses
- Digital Communication

Other countries will be instituted later when the NTI shall have enlarged its instructional staff.

3.1.2 The NTI shall assist the College of Engineering in offering top-quality undergraduate and graduate programs leading to the following degrees :
- B S Electrical Engineering (major in Telecommunications Engineering)
- M S Electrical Engineering (major in Telecommunications Engineering)
- M S Industrial Engineering or Masters in Business Administration (major in Telecom Administration and Management)

The NTI shall also give assistance for the production of educational materials to complement its mission, e.g. textbooks, primers, manuals, films. 

This assistance shall be in the form of NTI staff who shall be allowed to lecture in the college, use of NTI facilities, and through the provision of scholarships, fellowships, research and training assistance. 

Over the long term, the NTI shall extend assistance to other engineering and technical schools in the country. 

3.2 Research Programs

The opportunities for doing meaningful applied research in telecommunications, electronics and computers are especially significant in light of country's present economic situation. The NTI shall undertake basic and applied research, the latter being directed at meeting the country's needs in the following areas :

Telecommunications. This will include research work in all aspects of telecommunications as well as related fields. Studies on the problems of the industry will also be done. Research and development activities in telecommunication may be concerned with the following specific technologies : 
- Local Area Networks for office or Factory Automation
- Locally developed and built teletype/telex machines
- CAD Computer programs for telecomm design
- Teaching Aids - e.g. PCM trainers, transmission line simulation, multiplex equipment
- Microprocessor-based communications test equipment - e.g. data analyzers, transmission line test sets
Applied Electronics. This area covers such aspects of electronic technology as computers, consumer electronics, educational and industrial equipment as these may relate to communications. 
Technology and Public Policy. There will also be research activities on the effects/impact of telecommunications and related technologies on society and public policy. 

3.3. Extension Services 

In keeping with the mission of transmitting university skills and resources to the country at large, the NTI shall also render various types of related extension services such as the following : 
- Consultancy
- Technology Assessment and Forecasting 
- Industrial Contract Research
- Public Policy Advising
- Technical Information
- Continuing Education
- Industrial Services
- Engineering Support

4. ORGANIZATIONAL STRUCTURE OF THE NTI

The NTI shall adopt the organizational structure shown in diagram A.

4.1 Administration

The NTI shall be governed by the rules and regulations of the University of the Philippines. However, policies, plans and program directions will be formulated by the Advisory Board.

The chief executive officer of the NTI shall be the Executive Director who shall be appointed by the President of the University of the Philippines upon recommendation of the members of the Advisory Board. The Executive Director shall have a term of five (5) years. 

The Executive Director shall be assisted by an Associate Director and by the Assistant Directors of the four (4) major divisions - Administrative, Extension and Support Services, Research and Training. 

4.2 The Advisory Board

The NTI shall have an Advisory Board which shall fulfill the following functions : 

4.2.1 To set the policies and program directions
4.2.2 To help in assessing programs and performance
4.2.3 To help in generating resources for its various programs
4.2.4 To help in developing linkages with various Philippine and external institutions and agencies. 
The NTI's Advisory Board shall be composed of representatives from the following entities : 
- University of the Philippines 
- Ministry of Transportation and Communications
- National Telecommunications Commission
- National Economic Development Authority

The NTI Executive Director shall be an ex-officio member of the NTI Advisory Board. THe Minister of Transportation and Communications shall be the Chairman of the Advisory Board. 

4.3 The NTI Divisions

The four (4) major NTI divisions are as follows : 

4.3.1 Administrative. This division shall manage and coordinate all non-academic affairs of the NTI such as providing internal and personnel services. It shall regulat finances, make budget allocations, and handle the procurement of supplies and equipment needed by the Institute. 
4.3.2 Extension and Support Services. This division shall be responsible for the continuing education programs, for industrial and related services, for the technical information system of the NTI, and for providing consultancy services. 
4.3.3 Research. This division shall take charge of all research and development activities as in equipment R&D in the industry and related fields as well as assist in the development of course materials for use in training. 
4.3.4 Training. This division shall undertake all the educational and training programs of the NTI. It shall also handle student affairs and provide counselling. 

5. STAFFING OF THE NTI

The initial instructional, research and extension services staff of the NTI shall be composed of faculty members of the EE Department of the University of the Philippines, trainors from the training centers of BUTEL, AFP COMMEL and PLDT and lecturers from the industry. The service of expatriate experts shall be obtained through bilateral and multilateral assistance. 

Eventually, the NTI shall have an expanded staff consisting of the following categories : 

5.1 Regular Technical Staff 

This will be composed of regular faculty members and researchers with the minimum qualification of an MS degree (or its equivalent in work experience) for those who will handle the advanced level subjects and carry out applied research work. These staff members may hold joint appointments with the College of Engineering. Another group of the regular staff will be made up of technical trainors whose qualifications shall include an engineering degree and/or extensive industrial experience. 

5.2 Supplementary Academic Staff 

This will be composed of non-regular teaching and/or research staff members who shall supplement NTI's regular technical staff from time to time. The members of this staff shall have the same minimum qualification as the regular technical staff. They may be research fellows, visiting professors, professorial lecturers or adjunct researchers. 

5.3 Auxiliary Academic Staff

This will be composed of full-time graduate students of the NTI with appointments as Teaching or Research Assistants, and with corresponding teaching, research or extension service duties. 

5.4 Technical Support Staff

This will be composed on non-technical personnel with some technical training and appointments such as research technician, laboratory technician, computer technician, electronic technician, laboratory attendant, machinist, draftsman, technical librarian, carpenter, etc. 

5.5 Administrative Support Staff 

This will be composed of non-academic personnel with appointments such as administrative officer, administrative assistant, senior clerk, accountant, property custodian, messenger, clerk-typist, etc. 

6. FACILITIES OF THE NTI

The initial facilities of the NTI shall consist of the present facilities that can be jointly used at the training centers of PLDT, BUTEL, AFP COMMEL and at the U.P. College of Engineering. The latter organizations have signified their willingness to permit equipment, instruments, and space necessary for the conduct of training to be made available to the Institute. 

However, to carry out its expanded programs effectively, the NTI will need the following infrastructure, facilities and equipment : 

6.1 Infrastructure

In addition to the existing TTI facilities in Valenzuela, Bulacan, the proposed NTI complex requires the construction of the following buildings/facilities : 
- Training center
- Administrative building
- Library
- Multi-purpose hall 
- Housing
- Sports/recreational area
- Motor pool

It is expected that a 10-hectare site can be found in the U.P. Diliman Campus, either in the proposed commercial-office complex north of Marcos Avenue or in the south section of the campus adjacent to the Science Community Complex. 

6.2 Facilities and Equipment

The NTI will also require the following types of facilities and equipment : 
- Aerial cable plant
- Underground plant
- Subscriber plant
- Tools and equipment for outside/inside plant
- Central office equipment
- Transmission system
- Telegraph/telex/data system
- Data communications equipment
- Broadcasting equipment
- Office equipment
- Shop equipment
- Teaching aids

PROPOSED REVISION OF THE GENERAL EDUCATION PROGRAM

I. PROPOSED ORIENTATION AND OBJECTIVES OF THE REVISED GENERAL EDUCATION PROGRAM

The Revised General Education Program shall be oriented towards the development and promotion of a liberal, scientific, and humanistic Filipino culture. The Program's objectives are : 
1.1. To broaden the intellectual and cultural horizons of students by developing in them an awareness of the environment and a deeper insight into and understanding of man and man's achievements that would enable them to appreciate their Filipino, Asian, and human heritage. 
1.2. To cultivate among students a capacity for clear and independent thinking that is open-minded but critical, holistic but scientific, imaginative but logical and rational. 
1.3. To develop the students' capabilities to evaluate, integrate, synthesize, and even modify knowledge as well as to discriminate between values, purposes, and ends so that they could make choices and decisions with freedom and responsibility and with universal ethical values and humanist ideals in mind. 
1.4. To develop the communication skills, whether in speech or in writing, that would enable the students to express themselves clearly, coherently, effectively, and if possible, eloquently. 
1.5. To foster a nationalist commitment that would promote the cause of attaining a truly free, democratic, just, self-reliant, and progressive Filipino society. 

II. PROPOSED COURSE REQUIREMENTS OF THE REVISED GENERAL EDUCATION PROGRAM

The Revised General Education Program shall consist of 54 units of courses distributed as follows : 

2.1 Humanities Requirements
English and/or Filipino - 12 units
Humanities I - 3 units
Speech I - 3 units
Foreign Language - 12 units
Subtotal - 30 units

2.2. Natural Sciences and Mathematics Requirements
Mathematics - 3 units
Natural Science I - 3 units
Natural Science II - 3 units
Science and Society I or Environmental Issues - 3 units
Subtotal - 12 units

2.3. Social Sciences Requirements
Social Science I - 3 units
Social Science II - 3 units
Social Science III - 3 units
Social Science IV - 3 units
Subtotal - 12 units
TOTAL - 54 units

III. PROPOSED REVISION OF GENERAL EDUCATION COURSES
3.1 CHANGE OF COURSE TITLE
FROM : PHILIPPINE HISTORY AND INSTITUTION I
TO : SOCIAL SCIENCE I. PHILIPPINE HISTORY AND INSTITUTIONS 

FROM : INTRODUCTION TO ASIAN CIVILIZATIONS
TO : SOCiAL SCIENCE II. INTRODUCTION TO ASIAN CIVILIZATIONS

3.2 CHANGE OF COURSE TITLE AND DESCRIPTION
FROM : MATHEMATICS I. MATHEMATICS FOR GENERAL EDUCATION - Fundamental principles of mathematics; applications.
TO : MATHEMATICS I. -- Survey of the concepts, methods, and applications of mathematics. 

3.3 CHANGE OF COURSE NUMBER, TITLE AND DESCRIPTION
FROM : NATURAL SCIENCE I. FOUNDATIONS OF PHYSICAL SCIENCE I. Basic principles and the philosophical, cultural and social aspects of physics, astronomy and meteorology. 
TO : NATURAL SCIENCE I. Foundations of physics and the earth sciences. 
Prerequisite : Mathematics I
Credit : 3 units
FROM : NATURAL SCIENCE 2. FOUNDATIONS OF PHYSICAL SCIENCE II. The basic concepts of inorganic, organic, and biological chemistry; the basic principles of earth sciences. 
TO : NATURAL SCIENCE II. Foundations of chemistry and the life sciences. 
Prerequisite : Natural Science I
Credit : 3 units

IV. PROPOSED INSTITUTION OF GENERAL EDUCATION COURSES
4.1 SOCIAL SCIENCE III. PHILIPPINE SOCIETY AND CULTURE. An analytical study of the institutions, processes, and practices in Philippine society. Credit : 3 units
4.2 SOCIAL SCIENCE IV. THE PHILIPPINE POLITY. Principles, theories and practices. Credit : 3 units
4.3 SCIENCE AND SOCIETY I. The nature, logic and structure of science and its interrelationships with society from a historical perspective; science and technology in the Philippines. 
Prerequisites : Natural Science I and II (or their equivalents) and senior standing. Credits : 3 units

V. PROPOSED ABOLITION OF A GENERAL EDUCATION COURSE 
5.1 Natural Science 3 : FOUNDATIONS OF BIOLOGICAL SCIENCE 

ADOPTED BY THE FACULTY OF THE COLLEGE OF ARTS AND SCIENCES DURING ITS MEETING HELD ON 31 AUGUST 1983. 

Report of the Committee on a System-Wide General Education Program

After studying and reviewing past studies and programs on the subject, the Committee on a System-Wide General Education Program decided to "start anew in a manner of speaking, to steer clear of the various special concerns which naturally stem from academic prejudices, ideological interests as well as personal biases and insecurities. Thus oriented, the Committee hoped it could be creative rather than reactive, integrative rather than specialized and isolated. It could cover global rather than narrow areas of thought, and address the total person of the learner rather than a limited number of his faculties. 

CONCEPT

General education, as conceived of by the committee, has no intention of providing the learner with any one specific orientation or philosophy or ideology. It means rather to expose him to alternative, even competing, view-points and methodologies, upon which he can make analytics differentiations and whose significance and worth as well as inadequacies and limitation he can examine and evaluate in the light of universally accepted principles. It aims to place the student in the midst of problematic settings and situations that will call into play the exercise of his total powers, such as mentioned in the list of objectives, and his use of wide-ranging data and information available from relevant fields of knowledge, and areas of human endeavor in historical perspective. It intends to bring him to a conscious exercise and practice of available cognitive styles and strategies to seek answers to questions and solutions to problems, and even hit upon new knowledge, while integrating for himself forms and interpretations, theories and explanations, decisions and policies in his studies in the arts and sciences. 

In short, the committee's conception of general education lays stress on how to think, feel, and do integratively, not what to think, feel, and do in isolated and piecemeal fashion. 

General education should train students to be able to learn how to learn and to think of themselves as individuals and as members of society. 

Under this concept, general education is that component of a university education that helps the student actualize his potentials as a thinking, doing, and feeling individual and member of society by heightening his awareness, consciousness, and exercise of the various modes of apprehending reality in all its complexity, diversity, relativity and flux. 

OBJECTIVE

1. Cultivating and integrating the intuitive and analytic faculties. 
2. Developing the abilities to discriminate between what is unique, particular, and/or concrete, on the one hand and what is general and abstract, on the other, as well as to perceive the relationship between them. 
3. Developing side by side the participant's sense of involvement and the observer's critical detachment. 
4. Developing recognition and appreciation of the interaction between what is visible and what is inward. 
5. Instilling appreciation and respect for process and the dynamic continuities and discontinuities of the past, the present, and the future. 
6. Deepening and strengthening the individual's relationship with himself, with others, with the physical world, and the cosmos. 
7. Developing a sense of and appreciation for the underlying connection between and among the empirical, logical, moral and aesthetic domains. 
8. Developing a sense of wholeness in all of human endeavor.

Outside of the enumerated objectives, themselves values basic to general education, no specification is made of values and attitudes. The committee is of the opinion that, in learning how to perceive and structure the reality both within and outside him, the learner is at the same time grasping, - in fact, more genuinely - the values and attitudes that characterize the fullness of human dignity, his full worth as a person. the committee stands by the view that the full worth of a person lies in his freedom as well as the fullest possible exercise of all his faculties. Freedom is envisioned as much a goal as a climate for learning which it is incumbent upon the university and the classroom to provide. It is the kind of freedom that allows the full exercise of one's rational powers in self-teaching, self-learning, self-thinking. It assures that the individual will be free to act judiciously and responsibly, as, in the process of growing and unfolding, he is released from the tyranny of unexamined impressions, naive preconceptions, irrelevant compulsions, and blind caprice; he is freed from mindless involvement in a problem by its transformation into a solution; he is liberated from the entrapment of mere facts to a perception of them in the light of some structured meaning. For it is only through this liberating process that he can be clear-seeing enough to grasp truth. It is this clear-eyed discernment of the truth which, in turn, will render him humble, honest, and far-seeing. And above, and best of all because then unhampered by the lettesr of self-interest, critical and fearless. 
The integrative purpose of this conception is evident in the alignment of persistent dichotomies in thinking and behaving, which, under the listed objectives are seen as continuous and interacting. Broken down, the entire conception may be grouped under three categories. 
The Over-all Concept : 
1. thinking, feeling, doing (willing)
2. individual and member of society
3. various modes of apprehending reality
4. reality in all its complexity, diversity, relativity, and flux.
The Learner : 
1. thinking, feeling, doing
2. intuitive, and analytic faculties
3. participant's sense of involvement and observer's critical detachment
4. relationship with himself, with other men, the physical world, and the cosmos
The Object of Learning : 
1. the unique, particular, and/or concrete, on the one hand and the abstract and general, on the other; the relationship between them 
2. interaction between what is visible and what is inward
3. appreciating, and respecting process and the dynamic continuities and discontinuities of the past, the present, and the future
4. connection between and among the empirical, logical, moral, and aesthetic domains
5. a sense of wholeness in all of human endeavor
RECOMMENDATIONS
I. Courses
A. Tool Courses - 12 units
English or Filipino - 6 units
Speech - 3 units
Philippine History - 3 units 
The above-mentioned tool courses are existing courses in the current G.E. program. The committee feels however, that they should be restructured so as to be supportive and reinforce the concepts and objectives of general education as stated in this report. 
B. Natural Sciencews and Mathematics - 9 units
G.E. Mathematics - the nature, logic and structure of Mathematics and its interrelationship with society from a historical perspective - 3 units
G.E. Natural Science I and II - the nature, logic and structure of natural sciences and their interrelationship with society from a historical perspective - 6 units
I. Physics and the Earth Sciences
II. Chemistry and the Life Sciences 
C. Social Sciences - 9 units
Philosophical Analysis
Logic, epistemology and ethics - 3 units
G.E. Social Science I - Social Change : Theories and Models of Social Change : An interdisciplinary course with inputs from the various social science disciplines - 3 units
G.E. Social Science II - Researches and case studies on social change - 3 units 
D. Humanities - 9 units
G.E. Humanities I - Images of Man and Reality in Literature, Comparative Study of the Various Perceptions of Man and Reality as Reflected in Selected World Masterpieces through the Ages - 3 units
G.E. Humanities II - The Arts - Images of Man and Reality as reflected in the masterpieces of art in the world - 3 units
G.E. Humanities III - Perceptions and Values in the Discourse, Methods, Products, and Results of the Arts & the Sciences - 3 units
E. Practicum - 3 units
G.E. Practicum - Development of sensitivity to people, responsiveness to their needs, and critical awareness of social realities
Total Number of Units - 42 units
III. Administration
Creation of a System-Wide General Education Center 
The Committee strongly recommends the creation of a General Education Center under the Office of the University President. We believe that general education has primacy over all aspects of education and therefore is deserving of centralized concern, decision-making, research and implementation.
The functions of this proposed General Education Center are : 
1. Selection and appointment of a core faculty.
2. Recruitment and development of faculty members.
3. Curriculum development. 
4. Preparation of instructional materials and teaching aid. 
5. Monitoring and evaluation. 
6. Research
7. Organizing and sponsoring workshops, seminars and conferences
8. Publications

The proposed organizational structure of the General Education Center is as follows :
Director
Administrative Staff
Faculty
Services
Training 
Curriculum
Monitoring

There are three anticipated problems of the Center : 
1. Faculty incentives
2. Faculty status : Permanent or affiliate (e.g. loyalty to mother unit)
3. Funding
4. Physical plant and facilities

Inclusive of Spanish subjects would depend on the repeal or non-repeal of the Spanish Law which requires students who need to take any type of board examinations to comply with a maximum of 12 units of Spanish subjects. 

A move which the autonomous universities can take for the repeal of the Spanish Law is to pass resolutions making the Spanish subjects optional. The repeal is a complicated matter since it will affect other specific provisions of the Spanish Law as well as the policies pertaining to professional organizations.

Name of Students               Course     From     To      Sem/Yr     Faculty
1. ALLENEGUE, Edwin         E.E. 69     4.0         3.0     2-83-84     C.B. San Juan 
2. LEGASPI, Dante E.           C.E. 11     2.0         1.5     2-83-84     V. B. Zabat
3. LEYRAN, Eugene R.S.      C.E. 54     4.0         3.0     2-83-84     V. B. Zabat
4. VIGILAR, Gregorio            C.E. 60     2.0         1.5     2-83-84     J. de Castro
(5) Other matters :
(a) The Dean reminded the faculty advisers that they should not allow students to enroll in more than 6 units this coming Summer Session. 
(b) The faculty discussed the proposed increase in tuition fees in the University. The following are the comments/reaction of the faculty : 
Some questions posed : 
1) What is the probability of this increase being adopted by the University?
2) What will the University do in case of mass action against the increase? 
3) How do the new rates compare with those of the private schools?
4) Are children of faculty and personnel still eligible to enjoy tuition fee discounts?
Some comments : 
1) Before we can comment we should see the whole picture first.
2) The increase should be graduated over a length of time. 
3) Some sort of double taxation-- we already pay our income tax according to brackets and now the tuition benefits are also bracketed. (i.e., tuition fee discounts diminish as the income bracket increases. 
4) Use NET income instead of GROSS income to specify the category. 
5) The rationale for the increase is to maintain academic excellence. The expected net yield of P18M is a small percentage compared with the national subsidy; therefore we cannot expect a quantum improvement in academic excellence. 
6) A suggested approach : subsidize the student with free tuition but must serve the government after graduation; those not willing to serve the government must pay the full tuition. 
7) Tuition fee discounts should not be granted outright to graduate students; financial assistance should be based on intellectual ability. The income tax return is misleading. Some graduate students may have low income but may be sons of millionaires. 

(6) There being no further business the meeting was adjourned at 4:05 p.m.

MIGUEL B. ESCOTO
Secretary

column9: (1984, January 18) Minutes of the 241st Engineering Faculty Meeting Held on December 14, 1983 and January 18, 1984, Room 410, Melchor Hall
column10:
(1) Dean Mena called the meeting to order at 2:15 p.m. The following new members of the faculty were introduced :
Ch.E. - Mr. JOAQUIN E. SAN DIEGO
C.E. - Ms. OLIVIA MARIA LAI REYES
Returnees, from Met/Mining : Dr. ADOLFO GOPEZ
Dr. MANOLO MENA
New Engineering Librarian : Ms. YOLANDA GRANDA (vice Mrs. Lopez-retired). 
(2) The minutes of the 240th meeting held on July 6, 1983 were approved with the following correction : Mr. Victorino Zabat is added to "Present" list. 
(3) Business arising from the minutes :
(a) On Item 4 : Mr. Ong was a probable candidate for graduation with honors as of the end of summer, 1983. However his "INC" in E.E 81 was not cleared on time. 
(b) On Item 6 : On the assessment of the effect of the new class schedule additional data will be gathered for the second semester; feedback from the students will also be obtained. 
(4) The faculty approved the candidates for graduation as of the end of the 1st semester, 1983-84. 
The faculty also discussed the case of Mr. Tito R. Flores, a candidate for the B.S. Met. Eng'g who was recommended by the faculty for graduation as of the end of the 2nd semester, 1982-83. However, the University Registrar objected since Mr. Flores finished only 55 units in U.P.; this is less than 50% of the total units for the B.S. Met. E. program. This student has a B.S. degree in Mining from St. Louis University when he applied to our Met.E. program. He validated some courses and re-enrolled those courses which he did not succeed in validating. He finished the requirements for the B.S. Met. in two years. It was pointed out that Mr. Flores finished 55 units out of the total of 98 units required from the 3rd to the 5th years. 
Dr. Pacheco pointed out that this case may be a dangerous precedent since students may take the first two-year courses in another school and then apply in U.P. for the last years of the program. 
Prof. A. dela Cruz said that in future cases we should be careful at the admission stage. After some discussion Dr. Ordillas moved that Mr. Tito Flores be recommended for graduation as a special case. In the votation two are "against" and the rest are "in favor". 
(5) The following requests for change of grade were approved by the faculty : 
NAME OF STUDENT
1. CAMBA, Willie -  C.E. 53  - 4.0 - 3.0 - 1-83-84 - Victorino Zabat
2. LAO, Willen S.  - E.S. 21 - 1.75 - 1.5 - Sum. 82 - 83  - Jesus Ignacio
3. MANGALILE, Reynaldo  - E.E. 72  - 3.0 - 2.5 - 1-83-84  - Alfonso Aguilar
4. PIMENTEl, Aurelio - E.E. 36 - 2.0 - 1.5 - 1-83-84 - Christian San Juan
5. TOLENTINO, Andres Fe - C.E. 21 - 3.0 - 2.5 - 1-83-84 - Fernando German

(6) The faculty considered the following curricular matters :
(a) Institution of two courses in Energy : 
Energy 101 : Introduction to Energy Engineering
Energy 197 : Special Topics 
Faculty Action : Approval  
(b) Change in Prerequisite : 
For E.S. 26 : Computer Programming
From : E.S. 21 or equivalent 
To : Math 55
For C.S. 51 : Numerical Methods and Analysis 
From : C.S. 12 and Math 55
To : Math 55 and C.S. 11 or equivalent 
Faculty Action : Approval 
(c) Proposed B.S.E.E. curriculum : 
Dr. Viray explained that the proposed revised E.E. curriculum satisfies the need to incorporate the technological advances in computers, communication, instrumentation and controls. 
Prof. F.G.L. Reyes asked why E.S. 13 is deleted from the program. Dr. Viray said that in actual practice this course is not needed; another reason for removing E.S. 13 is to make room for E.E. 13 : Introduction to Electrical Engineering so that the second year students who take this course will already have a sense of belonging to the College. 
Faculty Action : Approval of B.S.E.E. curriculum 
(d) Proposed B.S.I.E. curriculum : 
Prof. F. dela Peña explained that the present I.E. curriculum dates back to 1978 and now there is a strong need to revise the curriculum. 
The main features of the proposal are : 
- emphasis on methods engineering and use of statistical analysis
- redistribution of subject content in sequential courses
- reduction in number of required electives
- rearrangement of courses in the different year levels to expose the student to the engineering program as early as possible
- renumbering of courses according to the proposed University numbering system
At this point the Dean declared a recess (4:15 p.m.); continuation of the meeting was set for January 1984. 
The continuation of the 241st faculty meeting was held on January 18, 1984. Dean Mena called the meeting to order at 2:15 p.m. Dr. Wilfredo Jose, a returnee from the Ch.E. Dept. was introduced. 
The faculty approved the following requests for change of grade : 
NAME OF STUDENT
1. TI, Nelson Lim - C.E. 53EF - 2.5 - 2.0 - 1-83-84 - V. Zabat
2. REX, Rey R. - E.S. 12PW2 - 1.25 - 1.0 - 1-83-84 - F. Aniban
3. KHO, Stephen U. - E.S. 12PW2 - 1.5 - 1.0 - 1-83-84 - F. Aniban
4. PALMA, Elsa -- E.S. 201K - Drp. 2.0 - 2-82-83 - M. Pascual
5. FAJARDO, Stella R. - I.E. 42UV - 2.25 - 1.75 - 1-83-84 - A. dela Cruz

Discussion of the B.S.I.E. curriculum was resumed. 

Faculty Action : Approval of B.S.I.E. program. 
(e) Proposed B.S. Met. E. curriculum : 
Dr. Ordillas presented the proposed revised Met. E. program. 
Features of the new curriculum are : 
- balanced consideration of the four major branches of metallurgy : minerals engineering, extractive metallurgy, adaptive metallurgy, and physical metallurgy. 
- increased emphasis in investigative and computational skills
- reduction in laboratory units form 93 to 81
- reduction in Chemistry and Geology requirements 
- separation of laboratory courses from lecture courses (except in design courses)
- rearrangement of courses to introduce the student to metallurgical engineering earlier

After discussion it was agreed that the footnotes for the social science courses should be uniform for the different curricula being presented.

Faculty Action : Approval of the B.S. Met. E. curriculum 
(f) Proposed B.S. Mining E. curriculum : 
Mr. Forado Serrano presented the new Mining curriculum. The main features of the curriculum are : 
- institution of courses on specialized areas of mining technology
- inclusion of courses in computer programming and statistics needed for new mining courses.
- change in course description of retained mining courses to reflect up-to-date mining engineering practice
- increased number of electives required
- reduction in Chemistry and Geology requirements
- rearrangement of courses to introduce the student to the mining engineering program earlier

Faculty Action : Approval of B.S. Mining Engineering program

(g) Proposed M.S.I.E. / Diploma in I.E. : 
The I.E. Dept. proposes a two-tiered approach to the I.E. graduate program : the Diploma program and the masteral program. Non-I.E. baccalaureatte degree holders will be trained in the basics of I.E. and after completion of required courses will be awarded a diploma. These required courses are graduate courses and problem-oriented. Holders of the Diploma will be eligible for admission to the masteral program. For the Diploma program the grade-point requirement and the maximum residence (3 years) should be indicated.
The masteral program will allow for specialization in the following fields : Production Systems, Operations Research, and Information System. The student may choose a thesis or a non-thesis option.
Faculty Action : Approval of Diploma & masteral programs in I.E. 

(h) Proposed master's program in Naval Architecture and Marine Engineering :

Prof. Vea presented the proposal for this new program. Among the things he discussed are : 
- the need for the masteral program - to develop the capability in ship design and construction in order to support the need for a strong interisland shipping fleet
- assessment of the potential demand for graduates of the program
- resource requirements of the program when fully operational
- institution of new courses (14 courses) / sample programs 
- financing - proposed sources are the regular budget and the shipyard fund 
The proposed start of the program is in the second semester, 1984-85.

Faculty Action : Approval of the master's program in Naval Architecture & Marine Engineering 

(7) There being no further business the meeting was adjourned at 3:45 p.m.

MIGUEL B. ESCOTO
Secretary

One (E.E.) will probably graduate with honors (still has INC. in E.E. 81). 

5. The Dean presented to the faculty the proposed University rules on scholastic delinquency. After discussion of the proposals the faculty agreed to recommend the following : 

(a) the University Council Committee on Student Progress should inquire if all units have their own scholastic rules as per Art. 389 of the University Code (The faculty of each college or school shall approve suitable and effective provisions governing undergraduate delinquent students.)

(b) Further study by the Committee should be made particularly on the basic framework/premise that was adopted for the existing univesrity scholastic delinquency rules. 

The faculty will submit its recommendations based on additional information regarding the above items. 

6. The faculty made a preliminary assessment on the effect of the new class scheduling. Does the new system attain its objectives, such as : (a) maximum utilization of classrooms, (b) students may possibly go to classes 4 days instead of 5 days a week?

A survey made in the College for the first semester, 1983-84 shows that : (a) there was a reduction in the use of some classrooms, although there was an increase in the use of some rooms at NEC. (b) there is no marked change in the number of days that the students go to classes.  

Dr. Garcia made the following suggestions : 
(a) take the average percentage utilization of all classrooms by each day (Monday, Tues, Wednesdays, etc.) rather than by room to room comparison.
(b) take into account the number of students (size of class) using a given classroom
(c) get a feedback from the students

7. The faculty approved the request for a change of grade by Prof. Ulpiano P. Ignacio, Jr. for Mr. Marvin Garbo CE 24 from "4.0" to "3.0", 2nd semester, 1982-83.

column9: (1983, November 29) PROPOSAL TO REVISE THE CURRICULUM LEADING TO THE DEGREE OF BACHELOR OF SCIENCE IN MINING ENGINEERING
column10:
SUMMARY OF UNDERGRADUATE COURSES IN MINING ENGINEERING TO BE OFFERED BY THE DEPARTMENT OF MINING AND METALLURGICAL ENGINEERING 

E.M. 10; PRINCIPLES of MINING. History importance and characteristics of the minerals industry. Principles of mineral exploration including geology, geochemistry, geophysics, sampling, prospect drilling and statistical evaluation. Principles of mine development for both underground and surface operations. Brieg introduction to mining methods. Prerequisite : Preceded or accompanied by Geol. 11. 3 hours a week (class); credit 3 units. 

E.M. 36; UNDERGROUND MINING. Techniques, unit operations, and systems involved in underground mining. Criteria for selection of mining method. Reserve estimates, development planning, engineering drawings for development and extraction, underground haulage systems. Prerequisite : E.M. 10. 2 hours a week (class); credit 2 units.

E.M. 42; COAL MINING. - Origin, classification, and composition of coal. Reserve computations. Methods of coal extraction : surface and underground. Equipment and methods selection. Conventional and Mechanized systems. Utilization and marketing. Environmental considerations. Prerequisite : E.M. 10. 2 hours a week (class); credit 2 units. 

E.M. 45; SURFACE MINING. - Merits of surface mining. Engineering and economic factors in the selection and use of various equipment and systems in surface mining. Ore estimates, grade control, short and long range planning, cost estimation, slope stability and control, and placer operations. Prerequisite : E.M. 10. 2 hours a week (class); credit 2 units. 

E.M. 46; ROCK MECHANICS and EXCAVATION PRACTICE. - Physical properties of rocks, theories of rock substance failure, and fundamentals of rock mass and rock substance response to applied loads. Principles of the design of underground openings and pit slopes, ground support, tunneling, and other practical applications. Prerequisite : E.M. 42, 45, and E.S. 13; or consent of instructor. 3 hours a week (2 class, 3 lab) : credit 3 units. 

E.M. 152; MINE MANAGEMENT AND SAFETY. - Principles of mine administration, industrial and human engineering, project management. Causes and prevention of accidents in coal and metal mines. Mines safety regulations. Prerequisite : Senior standing. 3 hours a week (class); credit 3 units. 

E.M. 154; MINE ECONOMICS. - Methods of sampling, ore reserve calculations and statistical analysis applied to the evaluation of ore deposits. Engineering economic principles applied to mineral project evaluation including : time value of money, cash flows, depreciation, depletion, risk, various profitability measures, cost of Capital. Prerequisite : Senior standing. 3 hours a week (class); credit 3 units. 

E.M. 156; MINE PLANT DESIGN. Analysis and design of materials handling system, mine de-watering, hauling, hoisting, compressed air and other power systems. Introduction to Systems Engineering. Prerequisite : Senior standing. 3 hours a week (class) : credit 3 units. 

E.M. 157; MINE VENTILATION. Fundamentals of mine ventilation including control of gases, dust, temperature and humidity. Analysis and design of ventilation systems. Prerequisite : Senior standing. 3 hours a week (2 class, 3 lab); credit 3 units.

E.M. 170; INSPECTION TRIPS AND SEMINARS. - Visits to mines. Reports on such visits. Presentation and discussions of papers on assigned topics. Prerequisite : Senior standing. 1 hour a week (class); credit 1 unit. 

E.M. 197; SPECIAL TOPICS. Current issues and Problems in mining engineering. Credit : 3 units may be repeated for a maximum of units.

column9: PROPOSALS TO REVISE THE CURRICULA LEADING TO THE DEGREES OF BACHELOR OF SCIENCE IN INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND MASTER OF SCIENCE (INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING) AND TO INSTITUTE A PROGRAM TOWARDS A DIPLOMA IN INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
column10:
Part I
Proposal to revise the curriculum leading to the degree of Bachelor of Science in Industrial Engineering
I. BACKGROUND
The existing BSIE curriculum was instituted in 1978. By the end of School Year 1982-1983 the faculty of the Department of Industrial Engineering and Operations Research felt that there is already a strong need to make new revisions and therefore discussions have been made the result of which is this proposal. 
II. MAIN FEATURES OF THE PROPOSAL
The main features of this proposal are as follows :
1. Increased emphasis on methods engineering and the use of statistical analysis.
2. Redistribution of subject matter content in sequential courses.
3. Reduction in the number of electives required.
4. Rearrangement of courses in the different year levels to be able to integrate the student to the engineering program as early as possible.
5. Renumbering of courses in line with the proposed University numbering system.
Part II
Proposal to institute a Diploma Program in Industrial Engineering (Dipl. I.E.) and to revise the curriculum leading to the degree of Master of Science (I.E.)
I. BACKGROUND 
The graduate program in Industrial Engineering at the University of the Philippines has consistently attracted the largest enrolment at the University's Graduate Engineering Program. The accessibility of the program to students with different baccalaureate degrees appear to be the principal reason for attracting a large enrolment. On the other hand the number of graduates in the program are very few. A number of reasons could be identified. First, students with non-IE background need to take as much as 51 units to earn a degree. Second, many of the students are part-time students who find difficulty in carrying out their studies to completion. Third, many of the students are not very keen in getting the degree but are more concerned about learning the basics of IE for practical reasons. The possibility, however, that students become delinquent or uninterested in the program content cannot be discounted. 
Recent developments in the field and experiences gained in the administration of the existing IE graduate program have triggered a need to review and modify the curriculum. It is in this context that the concepts presented here are being proposed. 
II. A TWO-TIERED APPROACH
Among students admitted to the IE graduate program with non-IE background only a few are able to finish and earn a degree. Many among them, however, are able to go through the remedial courses prescribed. Notably, this category of students comprise the majority of the graduate IE students. This sector therefore cannot be ignored in trying to redesign the IE graduate curriculum. 
It is therefore proposed that a program be made available for the non-IE's in such a way that there are first trained in the basics of IE, taking into consideration the fact that they already have a baccalaureatte degree in other engineering fields or in the physical and mathematical sciences and awarding them with a diploma after completion of the required courses. The courses to be offered in this program will differ from the undergraduate courses in the sense that they will be problem oriented and students will be required to carry out some research relevant to the subject matter. They will be classified as graduate courses. A student who earns this diploma will be eligible for admission into the masteral program.
The masteral program, on the other hand, will allow for a thesis and non-thesis option. Specialization will be in the field of Production Systems, Operations Research and Information Systems.
