column9:
Esguerra, Jasper Powell S. 
BS Chemical Engineering
UP College of Engineering Recognition Rites 2013 Valedictory Address
Delivered last 29 April 2013
column10:
Opportunity Cost

Four to five years has again passed in our lives. How much difference did it make?
Distinguished guests, Dr. Adolfo Jesus Gopez, Dean of the College of Engineering Dr. Aura Matias, Associate Deans, College Secretary, and Head Librarian, department and institute heads, professors and instructors, parents and guests, fellow graduates, ladies and gentlemen, good evening. 
Many people say that college has its way of changing people's lives. And I couldn't agree more. Bago mag-college, and enrollment, isang araw lang. Sa UP, isa, isang linggo. Sa start ng semester, tao ka pa. Sa kalagitnaan, wala ng buto, sabaw na lang. At bago matapos ang sem, zombie na. Dati, masaya ka lang kapag 100/100. Ngayon, 100/300, ok na, mag-curve nawa. O, akala nyo sobrang seryoso ng speech na 'to 'no? Cinram ko alng din 'to, tulad ng madalas nating ginagawa bilang engineering students. Joke lang po. 
But seriously, we should all give ourselves a put on the back for surviving the College of Engineering adventure alive. The past years have been undeniably tough, and I'm quite sure that for the majority of us, there has always been the question, playing at the back of our minds, of whether we can make it or not. Eventually, I got to discover that it was never a question of "Can I?", but a question of "Will I?" It was never a question of our abilities, but rather a question of our determination, our passion, of how much we want it. Look, nandito na tayo, nasa kaliwang balikat na ang mga sablay, handang handa nang anihin ang ating tagumpay.
But now, what? What lies after graduation? Life after college, according to some of my friends from previous batches, seems to give more uncertaintites than our time spent here in the university. It's because after college, we're on our own, no longer limited by university rules. Wala ng maximum of six absences. Wala ng math subjects na kapag twice mo binagsak, sisipain ka palabas ng office na pinagtatrabahuan mo. Basically, there are several paths that we could take, but how do we know if we are where we're supposed to be? I mean, after four to five years of enduring exams, feasibility studies, thesis, machine exercises, problem sets, and design projects, it would be a shame if we'll not find a venue where we could channel the education we received into its envisioned purpose. And actually, that's the problem. We became too preoccupied with preparing for own future that we forgot to imbibe to ourselves who we really should be. 
This is the point when we go back to the core of UP education - an ideology that is so important because it is what should set UP Eng'g apart from other engineering schools, something that we might have overlooked when we were too busy with our individual struggles. 
I know you're already expecting this, the reminder of giving back to the country, to be integral to this speech. But because we're all analytical and critically-minded engineers in the making, I'll making this as objective and as figure-based as possible. So please lend me a few more minutes of your time. This is the last lecture you'll be hearing anyway. 
Based on a study by Edita Tan from the UP School of Economics, the government has allocated a budget of PhP 95,000 for each UP student in 2009. That's 95,000 for me, another 95,000 for my friend Diana Gernan, another 95,000 for my friend Lou Aguion, another 95,000 for Mico Panis, and the same goes for each one of you. That is on an annual basis. Assuming the figure is the same throughout our stay in the university, the Filipino people would have spent around 400,000 to 500,000 pesos per student. And because nearly 800 of us are garduating today, that would total to nearly half a billion pesos spent for our batch alone to enjoy a subsidized yet the highest quality of education available in the country. 
What is the opportunity cost? That same half a billion pesos could have been used to augment the government's Pantawid Pamilay Pilipino Program. That same half a billion pesos could have been used for DSWD's supplemental feeding program, for the DOH Health Facilities Enhancement Program, for the Department of Agriculture's National Rice, Fisheries, Livestock Program, and so on and so forth. 
The government could have allotted that for something else. Imagine the thousands of Filipino people who could have benefited more from that half a billion pesos. Then why invest on us? Simple. Because the government believes that UP students could also be a long-term solution to poverty. Dahil naniniwala ho ang taumbayan na pagka-graduate natin ng kolehiyo, mare-recognize natin na regardless of our degree, regardless of our social status, regardless of the tuition we pay, tayong lahat ay may malaking utang - not a debt that we literally pay back, but a debt that we ought to pay forward by creating changes intended to benefit the future of our motherland, a responsibility given to our hands today as we leave the university. 
And now as I speak, I recognize how ironic that at times, we still complain about our tuition, about how some of us got re-bracketed from B to A, when we do not even dare ask ourselves if we are worth every centavo that the Filipino people is spending on us. Wag naman sana natin isiping, "Balang araw, magbabayad din naman ako ng tax eh, tax na mapupunta sa budget ng Pilipinas. Eh di parang nakapagpaaral na rin ako ng UP student." Well, if that's going to be our thinking, then the greater opportunity cost would be our seats, the very space that we are occupying right now. Dahil kung hindi natin piniling mag-aral sa UP, baka may ibang nakaupo sa inuupuan natin na mas makakaintindi sa tunay na kalagayan ng ating bansa. Because in the past days, I came to realize that a UP education fails when we exit the university without the capability of thinking beyond ourselves. 
Social responsibility is such an inconvenient duty for almost all of us. Why? Because since we were young, we are already accustomed to believe that success is measured by how big we'll make it in life. Even in the real world, much focus is being given to the technical feasibility and market profitability. Only a few gives attention to social impact. 
My closest friends know how difficult it was for me to come up with this speech. That's because I was struggling to find a valuable message that I could impart to you. But maybe, all I really wanted to say is this : that if we will it, then we can make our lives after college an adventure with the goal of affecting other people's lives in pursuit of our purpose of shaping, changing, and building this nation. Oo, dahil bilang UP Engineers, taglay natin ang kakayahang hubugin, baguhin, at buuin ang bansang ito. So that when we meet 40, 50 years from now, we will not ask each other the brand of cars we own, the retirement fee we're getting, the number of children we're able to send abroad for leisure and education. Instead, we will be asking ourselves, "How much difference have you made?" Bayad ka na ba sa PhP 500,000 na utang mo?"
Marami sa atin ang nakapagpa-picture, nagpapa-picture o magpapa-picture kay oble ngayong tapos na tayo ng kolehiyo. Wala ng sumpa eh. Hindi na tayo made-delay. Sana'y wag lang natin limutin na kaya malapad na nakabuka ang kanyang mga braso ay dahil sinisimbulo nito ang walang pag-iimbot na pag-aalay ng sarili sa ating bansa. Sabi nga ni Albus Dumbledore, "Soon, we must all face the choice between what is right and what is easy," At hihiramin ko ho ito mula sa isang Eng'g org : I urge you to do the right thing. 
Forty, fifty years from now, sana'y napatunayan na nating lahat na tayo'y karapat-dapat sa edukasyong tinamasa natin dito sa UP. Maraming salamat at magandang gabi muli.

column9: (1987, September 9) Subject : College of Science "Proposed Administration of Graduate Programs"
column10:
College of Engineering
University of the Philippines
Diliman, Quezon City 3004 

Dr. PAZ G. RAMOS
Vice-Chancellor for Academic Affairs
U.P. Diliman

Subject : College of Science "Proposed Administration of Graduate Programs"

Dear Dr. Ramos :

As requested by Memorandum No. 13 dated 14 July 1987 of the Office the Chancellor we are submitting the following comments : 

1) The most heard remark was that : "Why should the College of Engineering comment on a proposal that seems to concern only the College of Science?" According to Dean Mena, since this proposal concerns the College of Science, "it is not appropriate to discuss the pros and cons of their proposal without full knowledge of the background information which made them propose such a structure."

2) When discussed at an informal faculty meeting, the consensus was that the College of Engineering has no serious objection to the College of Science proposal as long as a similar procedure is not applied or used in the College of Engineering. 

3) If pressed for further comments, the most often mentioned criticism of the proposal is that the Graduate Council as proposed should not be limited only to PhD degree holders. Several reasons have been mentioned : 
a) What if the Dean does not have a PhD?
b) This could produce an "elitist" group and is discriminating against non PhD holders.
c) In some programs there may be a lack of PhD holders. It is difficult to retain or attract engineers with PhD's.

4) Finally, based on the provisions of Article 19 of the existing U.P. Code (Article 57 of the Proposed Revised U.P. Code) the University Council has among others the power "to prescribe courses of study" and "to fix requirements for admission to any college or school of the University". The creation of a Graduate Council seems to run counter to the U.P. Code. Anyway those excluded from the Graduate Council may be at the University Council to block or delay action on the Graduate Council's proposals. 

Very truly yours, 

ADOLFO JESUS R. GOPEZ
College Secretary

column9: (1999, July 5) Proposal for the Institution of a Graduate Diploma Program in Electric Power Engineering
column10:
Department of Electrical and Electronics Engineering 

To : Jonathan Winston L. Salvacion
College Secretary
College of Engineering

From : Rowena Cristina L. Guevara, Ph.D.
Chairman

Re : Proposal for the Institution of a Graduate Diploma Program in Electric Power Engineering 

Attached herewith is our proposal for the institution of a graduate diploma program in Electric Power Engineering

If you have any questions, please do not hesitate to get in touch wiht me either by phone : 925-2957 (PLDT), 5513/5612 (U.P. local) or e-mail : gev@eee.upd.edu.ph 

__________________________________________

University of the Philippines
Department of Electrical and Electronics Engineering
German Yia Hall, Diliman, Quezon City, 1101

PROPOSAL FOR THE INSTITUTION OF A GRADUATE DIPLOMA PROGRAM IN ELECTRIC POWER ENGINEERING (Diploma in Electric Power Engineering)

A1. RATIONALE 

As the country moves towards industrialization, there is an accompanying increase in the number of companies and agencies involved in the generation, transmission, distribution, and regulation of electric power. The diversification of the electric power industry can be seen in the imminent privatization of the National Power Corporation, which has been the sole supplier of electric energy in the past, and its eventual breakup into generation and transmission corporations. Recently, Independent Power Producers (IPPs) were allowed to generate electric power and connect directly to the electric cooperatives, private utilities, and other customer. The Luzon, Visayas, and Mindanao power grids are fast becoming more complex to operate with the construction of new transmission lines and generating units. Customer requirements in power quality are also becoming more stringent. Load growth, the changing regulatory laws, and competition to supply electric power is keeping the electric utility industry in constant transition. Under these conditions, power system planning, design, and operation requires engineers and utility personnel with a wide range of power system knowledge and skills. Engineers and utility personnel may find themselves thrust into situations for which they are unprepared. The Diploma degree in Electric Power Engineering will supplement an engineer's undergraduate education with an in-depth study of the power generation, transmission, and distribution subsystems, and the interrelationships that bind them into a coordinated power system. 
A majority of those who enroll in the Master of Science graduate program of the Department of Electrical and Electronics Engineering are employed full-time. A few who are able to obtain a study leave are given only a maximum of one year to finish their studies. Still fewer are the employers who give study leaves with pay. Therefore, a large number of graduate students can only allocate one year to one-and-a-half-years for full-time study. The more rigorous requirements of a Master's degree, such as sufficient prepartion for a comprehensive examination or conducting research for a thesis, are not easily satisfied by these students due to time, work, and financial constraints. Some engineers even opt to pursue non-engineering graduate degrees in order not to be left behind in job promotions. Therefore, the Diploma program in Electric Power Engineering is being proposed in order to meet the needs of the large number students who would like to pursue a graduate degree in electric engineering but are strapped for time and resources. 

A2. PROGRAM GOALS 

The Diploma degree in Electric Power Engineering will supplement an engineer's undergraduate education with an in-depth study of the power generation, transmission, and distribution subsystems, and the interrelationships that bind them into a coordinated power system. This provides the engineer with a working knowledge of the major disciplines of electric power engineering so that, regardless of assignment, he/she will have an understanding of the contributions and effects of the various power system components. Emphasis is placed on theoretical understanding and practical problem-solving ability to prepare engineers for assignment in planning, design, maintenance, operation and management of electric power systems. 

B. AN ASSESSMENT OF THE POTENTIAL DEMAND AND CURRENT SUPPLY FOR THE GRADUATES OF THE PROGRAM

The Diploma program in Electric Power Engineering is geared towards electrical engineers who want to undertake professional development and take up a career in electric power engineering and who can only allocate one school year of study. Graduates of the program will find employment in various electric utility companies, industrial companies, consulting firms, universities and colleges, and government agencies. 
The impending approval of the Omnibus Power Bill in Congress will result in the deregulation and restructuring of the and tougher competition in the electric power industry. There is going to be a great need for a re-training of most of the electrical engineers and utility personnel of the various generation companies, transmission companies, and government agencies in order to meet the demands of the new environment in the industry. The major players are going to be the Manila Electric Company (MERALCO) and the transmission and generation companies that will be created from the present National Power Corporation (NPC). Smaller players in the new electric power industry will be composed of independent power producers, electric cooperatives, and private utility companies. MERALCO has about 1,200 electrical engineers while NPC has about 2,000 electrical engineers. With the goal of gaining a competitive advantage, electric utility companies will put a premium on electrical engineers with higher technical competencies. 
A survey of the records of the graduate students who entered the Master's program in Electrical Engineering from 1990 to 1997 showed who completed 18 units or more, did not finish the Master's program. These students should have pursued a Diploma degree. 
Therefore, the restructuring and deregulation of the electric power industry will force electric utility companies and their engineers to seek higher technical competency in order to be competitive in the new electric power market. To get a headstart over competitors, the education and training of a utility's engineers must be achieved in the shortest possible time without compromising the quality of education or training. The Diploma program in Electric Power Engineering meets these requirements of the electric power industry. 

___________________________________________

APPENDIX C
IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS OF THE PROPOSED GRADUATE DIPLOMA PROGRAM IN ELECTRIC POWER ENGINEERING

A. ADMISSION REQUIREMENTS

1. Admission into the Diploma degree program in Electric Power Engineering shall require
(a) a Bachelor of Science in Electrical Engineering degree from a recognized institution of higher learning,
(b) a grade weighted average of 2.25 or better, 
(c) English language proficiency, and 
(d) satisfaction of additional University requirements such as health clearance and other special admission requiements that may be imposed by the EEE Graduate Committee.
Proof of English proficiency is required of students whose native language is not English, except those who graduated from institutions where the medium of instruction is Engilsh. For English proficiency, a score of at least 500 in the Test of English as a Foreign Language (TOEFL) administered by the Educational Testing Service (Princeton, New Jersey, 08540, USA) is required. 
2. Each application for admission into the Diploma degree program in Electric Power Engineering must be accomplished in the official University application form and accompanied by official transcripts of records, written recommendations from two (2) former professors or experts in the field of Electric Power Engineering, and the officially prescribed application fee. 
3. All applications shall be submitted to and processed by the College of Engineering Graduate Office, referred to the Graduate Committee of the Department of Electrical and Electronics Engineering for evaluation and decision, and endorsed by the latter to the Dean for official action. 

B. GENERAL DEGREE REQUIREMENTS

To satisfy the requirements for the Diploma program, a student must : 
(a) Complete a minimum of 21 units of formal graduate courses, and
(b) Maintain a cumulative weighted average grade (CWAG) of "2.0" or better in his/her graduate courses at the end of each academic year. 

C. PROGRAM ADVISER/COMMITTEE AND PROGRAM OF STUDY
Program Adviser/Committee
Every student admitted into the Diploma program shall be assigned a Program Adviser/Committee by the EEE Deaprtment Chair. The Program Adviser/Committee shall advise, guide and evaluate the student.

Program of Study
Within the first of the student's initial year in the Diploma program, a Program of Study shall be designed by the Program Adviser/Committee in consultation with the student and on the basis of the latter's academic preparation and desired specialization. A copy of this Program of Study shall be submitted to the Graduate Office/Committee, through channels. Subsequent revisions in the Program of Study must be approved by the Program Adviser/Committee and communicated as soon as possible to the Graduate Office through the channels.

Study Load Per Semester
The normal study load for full-time students per semester shall be nine (9) to twelve (12) units of formal graduate courses. 

D. COURSE REQUIREMENTS 

Every student in the Diploma degree program shall be required to complete a minimum of 21 units of relevant courses set by the EEE Graduate Faculty Council. 

E. GRADE REQUIREMENT

Grading System

The following numerical grades shall be used in graduate courses : 1.0 (Excellent), 1.25, 1.5 (Very Good), 1.75, 2.0 (Good), 2.25, 2.5 (Satisfactory), 2.75, 3.0 (Pass), 4.0 (Conditional Failure), INC, (Incomplete), and 5.0 (Fail).

A grade of "3.0" or better is a passing grade. A graduate student earns credit for a course when he/she obtains a grade of "3.0" or better.

Cumulative Weighted Average Grade

To remain in good standing in the Diploma program, a student must maintain a Cumulative Weighted Average Grade (CWAG) of "2.0" or better in his/her course work at the end of each academic year until the completion of his/her program of study. The student's CWAG shall be computed the Program Adviser/Committee at the end of each academic year (two semesters and one summer) and reported by the latter to the Graduate Office, through channels. 

The CWAG shall be based on all courses taken by the student in the approved program of study, including those taken in compliance with the Residence Rules. 

Failure to Satisfy the Grade Requirement

A student who fails to satisfy the CWAG of "2.0" at the end of the academic year shall be disqualified from the Diploma program unless the Dean/Director decides, on justifiable grounds and upon the recommendation of the student's Program Adviser/Committee, through channels, to waive the rule. 

F. RESIDENCE RULES

One-Year Residence Before Graduation

The student must be officially enrolled at least one (1) academic year prior to the concerment of the Diploma degree. 

Regular Period for Completion

The time limit for the completion of all Diploma degree requirements shall be no more than two (2) years. The period of residence shall start from the student's first enrolment in a graduate course after admission into the diploma program and shall include all leaves of absence from the program. The regular period for completion of a graduate student transferring from another university shall be reduced by one (2) semester. 

Extension of Residence 

In exceptionally meritorious cases, extensions of residence beyond the above regular period of completion may be approved by the Dean/Director upon the recommendation of the appropriate bodies. This extension shall be for a period not exceeding one (1) semester at a time. In no case shall the extensions exceed more than one (2) year. The student granted an extension shall take 3 additional units of graduate courses in his/her discipline or area during the extension period. 

Non-Compliance with Residence Rules

Any student who fails to complete all requirements of the degree within the regular period and any approved extension thereof shall be disqualified from the Diploma program. 

Leave of Absence (LOA) / Absence without Leave (AWOL)

A student who goes on leave of absence (LOA)/absence without leave (AWOL) from the Diploma program shall seek readmission into the program form the Dean/Director, through channels.

Option to Proceed to a Master's Degree
Student in the Diploma program may opt to proceed to a Master's program upon the recommendation of the Program Adviser and approval of the Dean. 

G. GRADUATION FOR THE DIPLOMA PROGRAM

Application for Graduation

After completion of all requirements for the Diploma degree, the student shall be qualified to apply for graduation to the University Registrar through the college/unit Graduate Office. 

Conferment of Degree

After the graduation of the student shall have been recommended by the College Faculty Assembly and appropriate body, endorsed by the University Council, and approved by the Board of Regents, he/she shall be officially conferred the Diploma degree by the University.
